EDCS-09300485.translation: Titus Flavius Iustus, son of Titus, of the Quirina tribe, a veteran of the II Adjutrix Legion, from Sirmium, aged 70, arranged for this to be made for himself and his wife Julia Optata while alive, by decree of the decurions, in the consulship of Lateranus and Rufinus.
EDCS-09300485.summary: This funerary monument records the self-funded construction of a tomb by a veteran for himself and his wife, commissioned by local officials during the consulship of Lateranus and Rufinus.

EDCS-09300486.text_edition: \n Dominis nostris Diocletiano et Maximiano et Constantio nobilissimis Caesaribus\n
EDCS-09300486.translation: To our lords Diocletian, Maximian, and Constantius, most noble Caesars.
EDCS-09300486.summary: This is a dedication to the emperors Diocletian, Maximian, and Constantius, likely part of a larger inscription, dating to their joint rule.

EDCS-09301194.text_edition: \n Minervae Augustae sacrum Lucii Aurelii Valentinus et Silvester decuriones coloniae Aquincensium pro se et suis ex voto posuerunt Sabino et Anullino consulibus\n
EDCS-09301194.translation: Sacred to Minerva Augusta. Lucius Aurelius Valentinus and Silvester, decurions of the colony of Aquincum, erected this at their own expense and for their families, in fulfillment of a vow, in the consulship of Sabinus and Anullinus.
EDCS-09301194.summary: This inscription records a votive dedication to Minerva Augusta by two decurions of Aquincum, made for themselves and their families during the consulship of Sabinus and Anullinus.

EDCS-09400595.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Aeflanus Onirus nnorum XL hic situs est Marcus Aeflanus Licinianus praefectus castrorum patronus eius fieri iussit\n
EDCS-09400595.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Marcus Aeflanus Onirus, aged 40, lies here. Marcus Aeflanus Licinianus, prefect of the camp and his patron, ordered this to be made.
EDCS-09400595.summary: This tombstone records the burial of Marcus Aeflanus Onirus, aged 40, whose patron, a camp prefect, ordered the monument.

EDCS-09400597.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo S Arrius Super veteranus legionis II adiutricis piae fidelis votum solvit libens laetus\n
EDCS-09400597.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Arrius Super, veteran of the II Adjutrix Legion, pious and faithful, gladly and joyfully fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-09400597.summary: This inscription records a veteran of the II Adjutrix Legion fulfilling a vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.

EDCS-09400598.text_edition: \n ] E T R LVIL MADEIREN et Marcus Ulpius Carus optio statorum consularis sumptibus suis votum solverunt libentes merito Praesente et Albino consulibus XV Kalendas Iulias\n
EDCS-09400598.translation: [...] and Marcus Ulpius Carus, optio statorum consularis, fulfilled their vow at their own expense, gladly and deservedly. Present on the 15th day before the Kalends of July, in the consulship of Praesens and Albinus.
EDCS-09400598.summary: This inscription records two individuals, including a military officer, fulfilling a votive offering at their own expense, dated to June 17th in the consulship of Praesens and Albinus.

EDCS-09400599.text_edition: \n Aug--- Maximo II et Aeliano consulibus\n
EDCS-09400599.translation: [...] in the consulship of Maximus II and Aelianus.
EDCS-09400599.summary: This fragmentary inscription notes the consulship of Maximus II and Aelianus, likely part of a larger dedication or record.

EDCS-09400600.text_edition: \n Invicto Deo Aelius Culvisius ex volto\n
EDCS-09400600.translation: To the unconquered god, Aelius Culvisius, in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-09400600.summary: This inscription is a votive dedication to an unconquered god by Aelius Culvisius.

EDCS-09400602.translation: To Syra, the innocent, for her life.
EDCS-09400602.summary: This inscription appears to be a dedication or epitaph for a woman named Syra, described as innocent.

EDCS-09400603.text_edition: \n MV annorum XXXX hic situs est Utte Maxima ponendum curavit bene merenti\n
EDCS-09400603.translation: [...] aged 40, lies here. Utte Maxima arranged for this to be placed, for one who deserved well.
EDCS-09400603.summary: This tombstone records the burial of an individual aged 40, for whom Utte Maxima arranged the monument, honoring someone deserving.

EDCS-09400604.text_edition: \n centuria Cassi Potentis centuria Antoni Aeserni Crestius Propinquus\n
EDCS-09400604.translation: The century of Cassius Potentis, the century of Antonius Aesernus. Crestius Propinquus [erected this].
EDCS-09400604.summary: This inscription likely marks the boundaries or work of two military centuries, possibly related to a construction or land division project.

EDCS-09400608.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Caius Iulius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate II imperator III consul pater patriae proconsul et Caius Iulius Verus Maximus Augustus nobilissimus Caesar restituerunt ab Aquinco milia passuum L\n
EDCS-09400608.translation: Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the second time, emperor for the third time, consul, father of the country, proconsul, and Gaius Julius Verus Maximus Augustus, most noble Caesar, restored the milestones, 50 miles from Aquincum.
EDCS-09400608.summary: This inscription records the restoration of 50 milestones by Emperors Maximinus Thrax and his son Caesar Maximinus Daza, dating to the period of Maximinus' second tribunician power (AD 236-237).

EDCS-09400609.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Caio Iulio Vero Maximino Pio Felici Invicto Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate III imperatori V consuli proconsuli patri patriae Imperatori et Caio Iulio Vero Maximo Augusto nobilissimo Caesari principi iuventutis Augusti nostri filio Germanicis Dacicis Sarmaticis maximis cohors Hemesenorum civium Romanorum Maximiniana ab Aquinco milia passuum L\n
EDCS-09400609.translation: For Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the third time, emperor for the fifth time, consul, proconsul, father of the country, and for Emperor Gaius Julius Verus Maximus Augustus, most noble Caesar, prince of youth, son of our Augustus, victor in Germany, Dacia, and Sarmatia, the cohort of Hemesenian Roman citizens, the Maximiniana, [restored] 50 miles from Aquincum.
EDCS-09400609.summary: This inscription dedicates the restoration of 50 milestones to Emperors Maximinus Thrax and his son Caesar Maximinus Daza, likely from the period of Maximinus' third tribunician power (AD 237-238), by a cohort of Roman citizens.

EDCS-09401166.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Iunoni Minervae Volcano Augustis sacrum Lucius Septimius Flaccus legatus Augusti pro praetore\n
EDCS-09401166.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Juno, Minerva, Vulcan, and the Augusti. Lucius Septimius Flaccus, imperial legate with proconsular authority.
EDCS-09401166.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the Capitoline Triad and the emperors by Lucius Septimius Flaccus, a provincial governor, possibly related to a temple or a dedication of territory.

EDCS-09401169.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Claudio Tacito Pio Felici Invicto Augusto V M P \n
EDCS-09401169.translation: For Emperor Caesar Marcus Claudius Tacitus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, X miles.
EDCS-09401169.summary: This inscription marks the distance (X miles) for Emperor Tacitus, dating to his reign (AD 275-276).

EDCS-09401544.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Iulio Philippo Pio Felici Invicto Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate patri patriae consuli p<r=P>oconsuli et Marco Iulio Philippo nobilissimo Caesari principi iuventutis filio Augusti nostri et Marciae Otaciliae Severae Augustae coniugi Augusti nostri milia passuum LXXVIIII \n
EDCS-09401544.translation: For Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power, father of the country, consul, proconsul, and for Marcus Julius Philippus, most noble Caesar, prince of youth, son of our Augustus, and for Marcia Otacilia Severa Augusta, wife of our Augustus, 79 miles.
EDCS-09401544.summary: This inscription marks 79 miles for Emperor Philip the Arab, his son Caesar Philip II, and his wife Marcia Otacilia Severa, likely indicating a road construction or maintenance project.

EDCS-09401546.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno et Mercurio \n
EDCS-09401546.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus and Mercury.
EDCS-09401546.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the syncretic deity Jupiter Dolichenus and Mercury, likely of private origin.

EDCS-09401549.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Censorinius Maximus beneficiarius consularis accepta missione votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-09401549.translation: Censorinius Maximus, a beneficiary of the governor, having received his discharge, willingly paid his vow as was deserved.
EDCS-09401549.summary: This is a votive inscription by a military official (beneficiarius consularis) thanking Jupiter Optimus Maximus after completing his service.

EDCS-09401550.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Titus Flavius Iustus beneficiarius consularis legionis IIII Macrino et Celso consulibus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-09401550.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus. Titus Flavius Iustus, a beneficiary of the governor of the Legio IIII [Scutariorum or Flaviae Felicis], during the consulship of Macrinus and Celsus, willingly paid his vow as was deserved.
EDCS-09401550.summary: This votive inscription by a military official of Legio IIII thanks Jupiter Optimus Maximus during the consulship of Macrinus and Celsus (AD 245).

EDCS-09401551.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Iulius Salutaris beneficiarius consularis quod aras in hunc locum recollocaverit\n
EDCS-09401551.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus. Julius Salutaris, a beneficiary of the governor, because he relocated the altars to this place.
EDCS-09401551.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Julius Salutaris, a military official, for relocating altars.

EDCS-09700224.text_edition: \n Aesculapio et Hygiae Marcus Foviacius Verus iunior decurio kanabarum decurio municipii Aquinci IIvir quinquennalis flaminicius votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-09700224.translation: To Aesculapius and Hygieia, Marcus Foviacius Verus the younger, decurion of the traders, decurion of the municipium of Aquincum, duovir quinquennalis, priest, fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-09700224.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Marcus Foviacius Verus to the gods Aesculapius and Hygieia, likely in the municipium of Aquincum.

EDCS-09700225.text_edition: \n Silvano Silvestri Ulpius Nundinus discens regulatorum votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-09700225.translation: To Silvanus, Ulpius Nundinus, a trainee of the regulators, fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-09700225.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Ulpius Nundinus to the god Silvanus.

EDCS-09700230.text_edition: \n Aurelius Victor et Ponlius Messor votum solverunt libentes laeti merito\n
EDCS-09700230.translation: Aurelius Victor and Ponlius Messor fulfilled their vow willingly, joyfully, and deservedly.
EDCS-09700230.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Aurelius Victor and Ponlius Messor, likely to a deity.

EDCS-09700233.text_edition: \n V D SVPQVES Ulpius Nundinus beneficiarius consularis posuit Maximo II et Urbano consulibus SVP QVES\n
EDCS-09700233.translation: Ulpius Nundinus, a soldier acting as a deputy for a consular official, erected this, with Maxius and Urbanus as consuls.
EDCS-09700233.summary: This inscription records the erection of a monument by Ulpius Nundinus, a beneficiarius consularis, during the consulship of Maxius and Urbanus.

EDCS-09700235.translation: A centurion of the [legion] to its most faithful recruits.
EDCS-09700235.summary: This inscription is a dedication by a centurion to the loyal recruits of a legion, location unspecified.

EDCS-09700236.text_edition: \n legionis II adiutricis stipendiorum XX Secundus etius Epaphroditus CCC B\n
EDCS-09700236.translation: From the II Adiutrix Legion, Secundus Etius Epaphroditus [dedicates this] for 20 years of service.
EDCS-09700236.summary: This inscription seems to be a dedication by Secundus Etius Epaphroditus related to 20 years of service in the II Adiutrix Legion.

EDCS-09700241.summary: This is a simple dedication or statement of creation by a person named Resatus.

EDCS-09700242.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulius Candidianus miles legionis II adiutricis stipendiorum XXIIII qui vixit nnos XXXX Claudia Bonosa coniugi carissimo faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-09700242.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Iulius Candidianus, a soldier of the II Adiutrix Legion with 24 years of service, who lived 40 years, had this made for his dearest wife, Claudia Bonosa.
EDCS-09700242.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Iulius Candidianus, a soldier of the II Adiutrix Legion, set up by his wife Claudia Bonosa.

EDCS-09700243.text_edition: \n Dis M(anibus) Marco Ulpio Maxim militi legionis II adiutricis centuria Herenni nnorum XXX \n
EDCS-09700243.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Marcus Ulpius Maxim, a soldier of the II Adiutrix Legion, in the century of Herennius, 30 years old.
EDCS-09700243.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Marcus Ulpius Maxim, a soldier of the II Adiutrix Legion, who died at the age of 30.

EDCS-09700288.text_edition: \n Dis Magnis pro salute templensium Lucius Virius Luci filius Mercator sacerdos votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-09700288.translation: To the Great Gods, for the welfare of the temple members, Lucius Virius Mercator, son of Lucius, the priest, fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-09700288.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Lucius Virius Mercator, a priest, to the Great Gods for the welfare of the temple community.

EDCS-09700290.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelia Sura quae vixit annos XXXV menses X Aurelius Fortunio qui vixit annos XIIII menses XI Aureliae Candidae vivae Aurelia Lucilla libertis carissimis Marcus Aurelius Maximianus nutrici pientissimae faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-09700290.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Aurelia Sura, who lived 35 years and 10 months, and Aurelius Fortunio, who lived 14 years and 11 months. Aureliae Candidae, while alive, and to her dearest freedmen. Marcus Aurelius Maximianus had this made for his most devoted nurse.
EDCS-09700290.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aurelia Sura, Aurelius Fortunio, Aureliae Candidae, and a nurse, set up by Marcus Aurelius Maximianus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09700291.text_edition: \n Flavia Tattunis filia Usaiu Eravisca nnum LXXX hic sita est Quintus Flavius Titucus matri ob pietatem posuit\n
EDCS-09700291.translation: Flavia Tattunis, daughter of Usaiu, of the Eravisci tribe, who lived 80 years, is buried here. Quintus Flavius Titucus placed this for his mother out of devotion.
EDCS-09700291.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Flavia Tattunis, an immigrant of the Eravisci tribe, erected by her son Quintus Flavius Titucus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09700292.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelius Nigrinus miles legionis I adiutricis Severianae strator consularis Aurelio Victori fratri qui vi<c=G>(sit) sic nnos XXVIII merenti opto <s=R>(it) <t=I>ibi terra <l=I>evis Aurelio Modesto veterano legionis I adiutricis Severianae et Aureliae S<c=G>enuae matri vivus vivis parentibus suis posuit \n
EDCS-09700292.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Aelius Nigrinus, a soldier of the I Adiutrix Severiana legion, an attendant to a consular governor. To Aurelius Victor, his brother, who lived 28 years, deserving of this. May the earth lie lightly upon you. Aurelius Modestus, a veteran of the I Adiutrix Severiana legion, and Aurelia Senua, his mother, set this up for themselves while alive.
EDCS-09700292.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a soldier, Aelius Nigrinus, and his brother Aurelius Victor, set up by a veteran Aurelius Modestus and his mother Aurelia Senua, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09700293.text_edition: \n Marcus Aurelius Do veteranus ex beneficiario cons(ularis) --- patri sanctissimo Mo--- p(osuit)\n
EDCS-09700293.translation: Marcus Aurelius Do, veteran, formerly a clerk of a consular governor... to his most sacred father Mo--- placed this.
EDCS-09700293.summary: This is a funerary epitaph fragment for a veteran, Marcus Aurelius Do, who set up the monument for his deceased father, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09701113.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Iunoni Reginae Suruco \n
EDCS-09701113.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Juno Regina, and Surucus.
EDCS-09701113.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter, Juno, and a local deity named Surucus, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-09701114.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Aurelius Mercu...
EDCS-09701114.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter, possibly by Aurelius Mercu..., likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-09701117.text_edition: \n Aurelius Mucianus quondam beneficiarius consularis I stipendiorum XVIIII et Aureliae Firmillae coniugis et Aureli-- Domni\n
EDCS-09701117.translation: Aurelius Mucianus, formerly a clerk of a consular governor with 19 years of service, and to Aurelia Firmilla, his wife, and Aurelius Domnus...
EDCS-09701117.summary: This is a funerary epitaph fragment mentioning Aurelius Mucianus, his wife Aurelia Firmilla, and another Aurelius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09701120.summary: This inscription records the price of a burial plot, likely from a Roman cemetery.

EDCS-09701121.text_edition: \n L e annorum XX hic sit-- est Valerius Felicio \n
EDCS-09701121.translation: ...aged 20 years is buried here. Valerius Felicio...
EDCS-09701121.summary: This is a funerary epitaph fragment for a 20-year-old individual named Valerius Felicio, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09701122.text_edition: \n hic situs est Marcus Birrius Teretina Gla Genn\n
EDCS-09701122.translation: Marcus Birrius Teretina Gla Genn... is buried here.
EDCS-09701122.summary: This is a funerary epitaph fragment for Marcus Birrius Teretina Gla Genn..., likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09701123.text_edition: \n PAE Flavia Tertia matri viva fecit\n
EDCS-09701123.translation: Flavia Tertia made this for her mother while she was alive.
EDCS-09701123.summary: A funerary dedication by Flavia Tertia to her living mother, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09701126.translation: Ulpius Antos, soldier of the legion.
EDCS-09701126.summary: A fragmentary inscription identifying a soldier named Ulpius Antos, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09701128.text_edition: \n Alorix Bassi filius decurio Eraviscus nnorum XXXV hic situs est filii patri posuerunt\n
EDCS-09701128.translation: Alorix, son of Bassus, a cavalry decurion of the Eravisci, aged 35, is buried here; his sons erected this for their father.
EDCS-09701128.summary: A funerary epitaph for Alorix, an Eraviscan decurion, erected by his sons, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09701129.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Caio Iulio Vero Maximino Pio Felici Invicto Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate III imperatori V consuli proconsuli patri patriae et Caio Iulio Vero Maximo nobilissimo Caesari principi iuventutis Augusti nostri filio Germanicis Dacicis Sarmaticis maximis ab Aquinco milia passuum XXII Imperatori Caesari Marco Annio Floriano Pio Felici Invicto Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate patri patriae proconsuli ab Aquinco milia passuum XXII\n
EDCS-09701129.translation: To Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 3rd time, imperator for the 5th time, consul, father of the fatherland, and to Gaius Julius Verus Maximus, most noble Caesar, prince of youth, our Augustus, son of Maximinus, 22 miles from Aquincum. To Emperor Caesar Marcus Annius Florianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power, father of the fatherland, proconsul, 22 miles from Aquincum.
EDCS-09701129.summary: An inscription recording distances from Aquincum to the emperors Maximinus Thrax and his son, and possibly the short-lived Florianus, likely from the early 4th century AD.

EDCS-09701131.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Iulius Philippus Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate consul pater patriae proconsul et Marcia Otacilia Severa sanctissima Augusta coniunx Augusti nostri ab Aquinco milia passuum XXIII\n
EDCS-09701131.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power, consul, father of the fatherland, proconsul, and Marcia Otacilia Severa, most holy Augusta, wife of our Augustus, 23 miles from Aquincum.
EDCS-09701131.summary: An inscription recording a distance from Aquincum to Emperor Philip the Arab, his wife Otacilia Severa, dating to his reign (244-249 AD).

EDCS-09701132.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Iulius Philippus Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate consul pater patriae proconsul et Marcus Iulius Philippus nobilissimus Caesar princeps iuventutis et Marcia Otacilia Severa sanctissima Augusta coniunx Augusti nostri ab Aquinco milia passuum XXII\n
EDCS-09701132.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power, consul, father of the fatherland, proconsul, and Marcus Julius Philippus, most noble Caesar, prince of youth, and Marcia Otacilia Severa, most holy Augusta, wife of our Augustus, 22 miles from Aquincum.
EDCS-09701132.summary: An inscription recording a distance from Aquincum to Emperor Philip the Arab, his son, and his wife Otacilia Severa, dating to Philip the Younger's co-rule (247-249 AD).

EDCS-09701135.text_edition: \n centuria Antonini ES Crescens Propinquus centuria Cassi Potentis Propinquus PR\n
EDCS-09701135.translation: Centuria of Antoninus's ES Crescens Propinquus, centuria of Cassius Potens Propinquus, in the province of Rome.
EDCS-09701135.summary: A record of two centurions, Crescens Propinquus and Potens Propinquus, likely related to military or administrative divisions during the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09701141.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Aurelius Vellatus votum solvit libens m(erito)\n
EDCS-09701141.translation: Aurelius Vellatus gladly and deservedly paid his vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.
EDCS-09701141.summary: A votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Aurelius Vellatus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09701142.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico Aelius Sabinus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-09701142.translation: Aelius Sabinus gladly and deservedly paid his vow to Silvanus Domestus.
EDCS-09701142.summary: A votive inscription dedicated to Silvanus Domestus by Aelius Sabinus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09701145.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VIIII consul III pater patriae restituit ab Aquinco milia passuum XXXVIIII\n
EDCS-09701145.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 9th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the fatherland, restored this, 39 miles from Aquincum.
EDCS-09701145.summary: An inscription recording the restoration of a road or milestone by Emperor Severus Alexander, indicating a distance from Aquincum, dating to 222-235 AD.

EDCS-09701148.summary: This inscription is a simple funerary dedication to Atilius Atilianus.

EDCS-09701150.text_edition: \n Bozi Vellases filia annorum XXX hic sita est P<r>oc<u>lus et Calvisius Cassites fili titulum merito posuerunt\n
EDCS-09701150.translation: Bozi, daughter of Vellases, aged 30, is buried here. Procullus and Calvisius Cassites, her sons, worthily placed this monument.
EDCS-09701150.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Bozi, erected by her sons Procullus and Calvisius Cassites, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09701151.text_edition: \n Exounomarae Annamati filiae annorum XVII hic sita est Annamatus Casulonis filius Suollae Troucissae et sibi vivus <f=E>eci<t=I> \n
EDCS-09701151.translation: Exounomara, daughter of Annamatus, aged 17, is buried here. Annamatus, son of Casulo, made this for himself and for Suolla, daughter of Troucissus, while alive.
EDCS-09701151.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Exounomara, set up by her father Annamatus, who also provided for himself and Suolla, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09701153.text_edition: \n Marcus Ulpius Attianus votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-09701153.translation: Marcus Ulpius Attianus gladly, joyfully, and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-09701153.summary: This is a votive inscription recording Marcus Ulpius Attianus fulfilling a vow, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09701154.text_edition: \n Naius Radai filius annorum XL hic situs est Radaius pater et fili et Nata coniunx fecerunt\n
EDCS-09701154.translation: Naius, son of Radaius, aged 40, is buried here. Radaius his father, and his sons, and Nata his wife, made this.
EDCS-09701154.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Naius, son of Radaius, erected by his father, sons, and wife Nata, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09701155.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Antonio Gordiano Pio Felici Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate consuli patri patriae proconsuli ab Aquinco milia passuum LI\n
EDCS-09701155.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, holder of tribunician power, consul, father of the country, proconsul, 51 miles from Aquincum.
EDCS-09701155.summary: This inscription records a milestone or boundary marker, indicating the distance from Aquincum to Emperor Gordian III, dating to his reign (238-244 AD).

EDCS-09701156.text_edition: \n Valauna Nigrini filia annorum XXX Dervae Comonis filiae vivae Nigrinus Namtiboli filius sibi et coniugi et filiae titulum merito posuit\n
EDCS-09701156.translation: Valauna, daughter of Nigrinus, aged 30. Nigrinus, son of Namtibolus, worthily placed this monument for Dervae, daughter of Comon, while she was alive, for himself, his wife, and his daughter.
EDCS-09701156.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Valauna, erected by her father Nigrinus for her, his wife, and daughter, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09701157.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Publius Aelius Decoratus imaginifer votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-09701157.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus. Publius Aelius Decoratus, standard-bearer, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-09701157.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Publius Aelius Decoratus, an imaginifer, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09701162.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VIIII consul III pater patriae restituit ab Aquinco milia passuum X<L=I>IIII \n
EDCS-09701162.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, holder of tribunician power for the 9th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the country, restored [this road/structure]. 44 miles from Aquincum.
EDCS-09701162.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a road or structure by Emperor Severus Alexander, marking the distance from Aquincum, and dates to his reign (222-235 AD).

EDCS-09701169.text_edition: \n Bauso Boni filius nnorum L hic situs est Atta fratri optimo pietati sic titulum memoriae posuit\n
EDCS-09701169.translation: Bauso, son of Boni, aged 50, is buried here. Atta placed this monument in memory of his loving brother out of devotion.
EDCS-09701169.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Bauso, erected by his brother Atta, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09701170.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ulpio Revocato Trurso frater heres ex testamento posuit \n
EDCS-09701170.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Ulpius Revocatus, his brother and heir, set this up in accordance with his will.
EDCS-09701170.summary: A funerary inscription honoring Ulpius Revocatus, set up by his brother and heir.

EDCS-09800273.text_edition: \n Lucius Cornelius Latinianus legatus Augusti pro praetore votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-09800273.translation: Lucius Cornelius Latinianus, imperial legate with praetorian power, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-09800273.summary: An inscription recording a vow fulfilled by Lucius Cornelius Latinianus, an imperial legate, likely of the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09800274.text_edition: \n decurio coloniae Aquincensis duumviralis equo publico et Aurelia Vibianus adiutor officii consularis Rusticus et Rusticianus inpendiis suis fecerunt ad extruendum nominibus nte his lavacris utentur Aelius Verinus Flavius Opimianus Victor Terentius Vitalis erianus Septimius Valerianus us Septimius Aurelius ter et semel consulibus\n
EDCS-09800274.translation: Aurelia Vibiana, assistant to the consular governor, Rusticus, and Rusticianus, a decurion of the colony of Aquincum and holder of the duumviral office with public horse, made these at their own expense for the construction of these baths, which Aelius Verinus, Flavius Opimianus, Victor Terentius Vitalis, and Septimius Valerianus will use, with Septimius Aurelius and others as consuls for the first and a half time.
EDCS-09800274.summary: This inscription records the construction of baths in the colony of Aquincum, funded by local officials and dedicated to specific individuals, likely dating to the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09800275.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelio Stratonico militi legionis I adiutricis ARES P officii consularis stipendiorum XVII vixit annos XXXV Flavia Flavina coniugi carissimo arcam ex suo posuit\n
EDCS-09800275.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Flavia Flavina set up this tomb for her dearest husband, Aurelius Stratonicus, a soldier of the I Adiutrix legion, who served for 17 years, lived 35 years, and was an assistant to the consular governor.
EDCS-09800275.summary: A funerary inscription for Aurelius Stratonicus, a soldier of the I Adiutrix legion, set up by his wife, likely dating to the late 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09900343.text_edition: \n Calmenus Sindionis filius annorum XXV titulum posuerot sic filii heredes\n
EDCS-09900343.translation: Calmenus, son of Sindio, aged 25, set up the monument; his sons are his heirs.
EDCS-09900343.summary: An epitaph for Calmenus, who died at age 25, with his sons designated as heirs.

EDCS-09900364.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Lucio Septemio sic Severo Pertinaci Augusto patri patriae Arabico Adiabenico imperatori V consuli II tribunicia potestate II<I> pontifici maximo et Marco Aurelio Antonino Caesari cohors I Mmiliaria Aurelia Antoniniana nova Severiana constituta Pisone et Iuliano consulibus\n
EDCS-09900364.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Augustus, father of the country, Arabicus, Adiabenicus, consul for the fifth time, holding tribunician power for the third time, pontifex maximus, and to Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus. The First Cohort of Miliaria Aurelia Antoniniana Nova Severiana was established, with Pison and Julianus as consuls.
EDCS-09900364.summary: An inscription dedicating a military cohort, the I Mmiliaria Aurelia Antoniniana Nova Severiana, to Emperors Septimius Severus and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus (Caracalla), during their consulship with Pison and Julianus, likely around 200 CE.

EDCS-09900367.text_edition: \n Centuriae sacrum Exsuperatus |(centurio) cohortis Hemesenorum Gordianae Genii CV \n
EDCS-09900367.translation: Sacred to the century. Exsuperatus, centurion of the Gordian cohort of Hemesenorum, offering 105.
EDCS-09900367.summary: A dedication by Exsuperatus, a centurion of a military cohort, to the genius of his century, dating to the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09900368.text_edition: \n Septimius Constantinus optio spei et Iulius Iulianus beneficiarius legati Praesente et Extricato consulibus\n
EDCS-09900368.translation: Septimius Constantinus, staff officer, and Julius Julianus, aide-de-camp to the legate, during the consulship of Praesens and Extricatus.
EDCS-09900368.summary: An inscription mentioning two military officials, Septimius Constantinus and Julius Julianus, during a specific consulship, likely in the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09900369.summary: A fragmentary inscription containing only a personal name, Lucius Septimius Ganus, possibly an owner or dedicator.

EDCS-09900371.text_edition: \n beneficiarius consularis legionis II adiutricis pro salute sua et suorum votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-09900371.translation: An aide-de-camp to the consular governor of the II Adiutrix legion fulfilled his vow gladly and deservedly for his own safety and that of his family.
EDCS-09900371.summary: An inscription recording a vow fulfilled by an aide-de-camp of the II Adiutrix legion for the well-being of himself and his family, likely in the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09900372.translation: To S. AV, a beneficiarius consularis.
EDCS-09900372.summary: This inscription is a dedication to S. AV, a beneficiarius consularis, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09900386.text_edition: \n us Maqui et Pattuo custores armorum cohortis |(milliariae) novae Severianae votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-09900386.translation: Maquius and Pattuus, custodians of the arms of the new Severan cohort (milliaria), fulfilled their vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-09900386.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Maquius and Pattuus, custodians of arms for a Severan cohort, likely from the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09900390.text_edition: \n Genio turmae et Eponae Reginae Iulius Victor eques vexillarius cohortis |(milliariae) novae Severianae Gordianae Surorum sagittariorum in honorem turmae votum solvit libens merito Imperatore domino nostro Gordiano II et et Pompeiano consulibus Kalendis Iuniis\n
EDCS-09900390.translation: To the Genius of the troop and to Epona Regina, Iulius Victor, an equestrian standard-bearer of the new Severan Gordian cohort (milliaria) of archers from Syria, in honor of his troop, fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly, on the Kalends of June, during the consulship of our Emperor Lord Gordianus II and Pompeianus.
EDCS-09900390.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Iulius Victor, an equestrian standard-bearer, to the Genius of his troop and to Epona Regina, during the reign of Gordian III, dated to June 1, 239 CE.

EDCS-09900392.text_edition: \n Herculi Augusto Publius Aelius Aelianus praefectus legionis II adiutricis protector Gallieni Augusti nostri agens vices legati votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-09900392.translation: To Hercules Augustus, Publius Aelius Aelianus, prefect of the II Adiutrix legion, protector of our Emperor Gallienus Augustus, acting with the authority of a legate, fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-09900392.summary: This inscription is a votive dedication to Hercules Augustus by Publius Aelius Aelianus, prefect of the II Adiutrix legion, during the reign of Gallienus (253-268 CE).

EDCS-09900393.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Ulpius Victor votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-09900393.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Ulpius Victor fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-09900393.summary: This is a simple votive inscription to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Ulpius Victor, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09900394.text_edition: \n Invicto Optimo Maximo Patrio Iunoni Reginae pro salute sua suorumque omnium Aurelius Marcellus quod miles votavit veteranus solvit IImmpperatoribus ddominis nnostris Maximiano Augusto V et Maximiano nobilissimo Caesare II consulibus votum libens solvit\n
EDCS-09900394.translation: To the Unconquered, Best, Greatest, Patriotic, and to Juno Regina, for his own safety and that of all his kin, Aurelius Marcellus, who vowed as a soldier, fulfilled as a veteran, willingly and deservedly, during the consulship of our Emperors Lords Maximianus Augustus and Maximianus nobilissimus Caesar II.
EDCS-09900394.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by the veteran Aurelius Marcellus for the safety of himself and his family, during the second consulship of Maximian, dated to 298 CE or 303 CE.

EDCS-09900395.text_edition: \n Mercurio Augusto sacrum Lucius Atticius Atticinus et Caius Atticius Verecundus deccuriones\n
EDCS-09900395.translation: Sacred to Mercury Augustus: Lucius Atticius Atticinus and Caius Atticius Verecundus, decurions.
EDCS-09900395.summary: This is a dedication to Mercury Augustus by two decurions, Lucius Atticius Atticinus and Caius Atticius Verecundus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09900396.text_edition: \n ------ legionis piae] fidelis Philippianae Aurelius Avitianus Aurelius Py<rrh=HRR>us Aurelius Bitus Claudius Proculus Aurelius Iustus Aurelius Florus Domitius Lupianus Vipsanius Marinianus Iulius Severinus Septimius Atta Claudius Felicianus Aurelius Albanus Iulius Silvanus Aurelius Numerius Titianus Aurelius Carus Septimius Mucianus Dips Maximus Aurelius Florus Aelius Maximus Iulius Lernatus Aelius Crescens Iulius Nodia Claudius Valens Aurelius Trice Iulius Vitalis Septimius Ius Aurelius lianus Aurelius Antoninus Aurelius ius \n
EDCS-09900396.translation: ..., of the pious and faithful Philippian legion. Aurelius Avitianus, Aurelius Pyrrhus, Aurelius Bitus, Claudius Proculus, Aurelius Iustus, Aurelius Florus, Domitius Lupianus, Vipsanius Marinianus, Iulius Severinus, Septimius Atta, Claudius Felicianus, Aurelius Albanus, Iulius Silvanus, Aurelius Numerius, Titianus Aurelius Carus, Septimius Mucianus, Dips Maximus, Aurelius Florus, Aelius Maximus, Iulius Lernatus, Aelius Crescens, Iulius Nodia, Claudius Valens, Aurelius Trice, Iulius Vitalis, Septimius Ius, Aurelius Iulianus, Aurelius Antoninus, Aurelius Ius.
EDCS-09900396.summary: This inscription lists the names of soldiers belonging to the Philippian legion, likely dating to the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09900397.text_edition: \n et Ilus Aelius A Septimius Ca Septimius Prob Septimius Num Septimius Au Septimius Urs Septimius AtI Septimius Septimius Septimius Vi Au \n
EDCS-09900397.translation: ..., and Ilus, Aelius A..., Septimius Ca..., Septimius Prob..., Septimius Num..., Septimius Au..., Septimius Urs..., Septimius AtI..., Septimius..., Septimius..., Septimius Vi..., Au...
EDCS-09900397.summary: This is a fragmented inscription listing numerous names, possibly of soldiers or members of a collegium, with extensive lacunae.

EDCS-09900400.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publio Aelio Crispino quondam corniculario legionis II adiutricis piae fidelis \n
EDCS-09900400.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. To Publius Aelius Crispinus, formerly a cornicularius of the II Adiutrix Pia Fidelis legion.
EDCS-09900400.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Publius Aelius Crispinus, a former cornicularius of the II Adiutrix legion, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09900401.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelius Lupus vixit nnos VIIII menses XI dies VIII Aelius Ingenuus decurio filio pio faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-09900401.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aelius Lupus lived 9 years, 11 months, 8 days. Aelius Ingenuus, a decurion, arranged for this to be made for his dutiful son.
EDCS-09900401.summary: A tombstone recording the life span of a child and erected by his father, a decurion, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09900402.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Proculo signifero legionis II adiutricis piae fidelis et Iuliae Optatae eius vivae s veteranus legionis supra scriptae pater et Aelia M parentes filio karissimo et Aelio Procul vivi sibi et Proculo supra scripto faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-09900402.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Proculus, standard-bearer of the Second Adiutrix Legion Pia Fidelis, and for his wife Julia Optata, while she was alive. A veteran of the aforementioned legion, the father, and Aelia M, the parents, arranged for this to be made for their dearest son and for themselves, Proculus and Aelia M, while alive.
EDCS-09900402.summary: A funerary inscription for Proculus, a standard-bearer, and his wife Julia Optata, erected by his parents, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09900404.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aeliae Severinae Septimius Severinus eques matri vivae fecit\n
EDCS-09900404.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Septimius Severinus, an equestrian, made this for his mother Aelia Severina while she was alive.
EDCS-09900404.summary: A funerary inscription erected by Septimius Severinus, an equestrian, for his mother Aelia Severina, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09900405.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Aurelius Domitianus veteranus legionis II adiutricis domo Ancyra vivos sic sibi fecit adiutantibus nepotes sic suos sic filies sic filios sic Gregorio et Laurentio fratres sic\n
EDCS-09900405.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Marcus Aurelius Domitianus, a veteran of the Second Adiutrix Legion, from Ancyra, made this for himself while alive, with the assistance of his grandsons and sons, Gregorio and Laurentio, brothers.
EDCS-09900405.summary: A funerary inscription for Marcus Aurelius Domitianus, a veteran from Ancyra, who arranged for his own tomb while alive, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09900406.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sabatiae Senecae filiae suae I S ESI Dis Manibus Aurelia Maxima vixit nnos LVI coniugi entius vivus titulum posuit\n
EDCS-09900406.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Sabatia Seneca, his daughter. To the spirits of the departed. Aurelia Maxima lived 56 years; her husband placed this monument for her while alive.
EDCS-09900406.summary: A funerary inscription for Sabatia Seneca, whose daughter erected it, and for Aurelia Maxima, for whom her husband erected a monument while she was alive, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09900407.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelio Monnio collegium fabrum\n
EDCS-09900407.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Aurelius Monnus. The guild of fabric workers.
EDCS-09900407.summary: A funerary inscription honoring Aurelius Monnus, erected by the guild of fabric workers, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09900410.text_edition: \n Blastaiu Batei filius annorum LXX Comatumarus Saconis filius sibi et coniugi titulum posuit\n
EDCS-09900410.translation: For Blastaius, son of Bateius, 70 years old, and Comatumarus, son of Saconis, placed this monument for himself and his wife.
EDCS-09900410.summary: A funerary inscription for Blastaius and Comatumarus, who jointly erected it for themselves and their wives, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09900411.text_edition: \n Calio Blasi filius nnm XXXXV est defunctus\n
EDCS-09900411.translation: Calus, son of Blasus, 35 years old, has died.
EDCS-09900411.summary: A simple funerary epitaph recording the death and age of Calus, son of Blasus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09900412.text_edition: \n Claudio Trophimo nnorum LXXX hic situs est collegium fabrum et centonariorium posuit\n
EDCS-09900412.translation: For Claudius Trophimus, 80 years old, he lies here. The guild of fabric workers and the guild of centonarii placed this.
EDCS-09900412.summary: A funerary inscription for Claudius Trophimus, erected by two guilds, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09900414.text_edition: \n Comatumara Vani filia annorum XXVI hic sita est titulum posuit Mesio coniugi suae et socrui vivae Usiae\n
EDCS-09900414.translation: Comatumara, daughter of Vanius, 26 years old, lies here. She placed this monument for her husband Mesius and her mother-in-law Usia, while they were alive.
EDCS-09900414.summary: A funerary inscription for Comatumara, who erected a monument for her husband and mother-in-law while they were alive, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09900415.summary: This is a tombstone recording the death of Crispus at the age of 60.

EDCS-09900417.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Titus Flavius Felicio Augustalis coloniae Aquincensium vivos sic sibi et Flaviae Secundinae quondam conlibertae et uxori quae vixit annos LV et Tito Flavio Felicissimo quondam filio naturali qui vixit nnos XXIII et Tito Flavio Ingenuo filio legitimo et Tito Flavio Felici filio naturali et Flaviae Feliculae filiae naturali et Flaviae Felicissimae nepti vivis Titus Flavius Felicio supra scriptis et sibi faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-09900417.translation: To the departed spirits. Titus Flavius Felicio, Augustalis of the colony of Aquincense, provided this monument in his lifetime for himself and for Flavia Secundina, formerly his freedwoman and wife, who lived 55 years, and for Titus Flavius Felissimo, formerly his natural son, who lived 23 years, and for Titus Flavius Ingenuus, his legitimate son, and for Titus Flavius Felix, his natural son, and for Flavia Felicula, his natural daughter, and for Flavia Felicissima, his granddaughter. Titus Flavius Felicio, as stated above, arranged for this to be made for himself and them.
EDCS-09900417.summary: This is a tombstone dedicated by Titus Flavius Felicio to himself and various deceased family members and freedwoman, likely from the Roman colony of Aquincum.

EDCS-09900418.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flavius Superianus biarcho qui militavit Constantia vixsit sic nnos \n
EDCS-09900418.translation: To the departed spirits. Flavius Superianus, biarchus, who served in Constantia, lived [ ] years.
EDCS-09900418.summary: This is a tombstone for Flavius Superianus, a biarchus who served in Constantia, recording his age at death.

EDCS-09900419.text_edition: \n Iulius Rufus domo Dalmata veteranus legionis II adiutricis annorum LX collegium fabrum et centonariorum posuit\n
EDCS-09900419.translation: Julius Rufus, from Dalmatia, a veteran of the II Adjutrix Legion, aged 60, erected by the guild of fabric workers and centonarii.
EDCS-09900419.summary: This is a tombstone erected by the guild of fabric workers and centonarii for Julius Rufus, a 60-year-old veteran of the II Adjutrix Legion from Dalmatia.

EDCS-09900420.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Sperati filius nnrum XX\n
EDCS-09900420.translation: To the departed spirits. Lucius Speratius, aged 20.
EDCS-09900420.summary: This is a tombstone for Lucius Speratius, recording his age at death.

EDCS-09900422.text_edition: \n Bellicus amicus Masuia Casui filia annorum LXV titulum posuit Namio Atunis filius coniugi et sibi vivo\n
EDCS-09900422.translation: Bellicus, friend of Masuia daughter of Casus, aged 65, erected this monument in his lifetime for his husband Namio son of Atunis and for himself.
EDCS-09900422.summary: This tombstone, erected during the lifetime of Bellicus and Masuia, records their ages and commemorates her husband Namio and themselves.

EDCS-09900423.text_edition: \n Tatunnis filius vixit annos XXV Medulivia Tuionis filia vixit annos XXX\n
EDCS-09900423.translation: Tatunnis son of [ ] lived 25 years; Medulivia daughter of Tuionis lived 30 years.
EDCS-09900423.summary: This monument records the ages at death of two individuals, Tatunnis and Medulivia.

EDCS-09900424.text_edition: \n Mira Crescentis filia annorum XXX titulum posuit Marcus Attius Rufus veteranus legionis II adiutricis sibi et coniugi bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-09900424.translation: Mira daughter of Crescens, aged 30, erected this monument for Marcus Attius Rufus, a veteran of the II Adjutrix Legion, and for his deserving wife, during his lifetime.
EDCS-09900424.summary: This tombstone was erected by Mira for Marcus Attius Rufus, a veteran of the II Adjutrix Legion, and his wife, commemorating them during his lifetime.

EDCS-09900425.text_edition: \n X C P R D Mutsia Bonionis filia nnrum L\n
EDCS-09900425.translation: To the departed spirits. Mutia daughter of Bonio, aged 50.
EDCS-09900425.summary: This is a tombstone recording the death of Mutia at the age of 50.

EDCS-09900426.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Nonio Ianuario veterano legionis II adiutricis piae fidelis et Ae[liae ------\n
EDCS-09900426.translation: To the departed spirits. For Nonius Ianuarius, a veteran of the II Adjutrix Legion, pious and faithful, and for Aelia [ ].
EDCS-09900426.summary: This tombstone is dedicated to the departed spirits of Nonius Ianuarius, a veteran of the II Adjutrix Legion, and Aelia.

EDCS-09900427.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quintus Masculi filius annorum XX hic situs titulum possuit sic Masculus <f=E>ilio suo \n
EDCS-09900427.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Masculus set up this tombstone for his son Quintus, son of Masculus, aged 20.
EDCS-09900427.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Quintus, son of Masculus, set up by his father Masculus.

EDCS-09900428.text_edition: \n Retdimara Atalonis filia annorum LX Sacro Nertomari filius vivos fecit\n
EDCS-09900428.translation: Retdimara, daughter of Atalo, aged 60, and Sacro, son of Nertomarus, had this made while alive.
EDCS-09900428.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Retdimara and Sacro, who commissioned their tombstone while alive.

EDCS-09900429.text_edition: \n Ritimarus Rismari filius annorum XXXX titulus positus\n
EDCS-09900429.translation: Ritimarus, son of Rismarus, aged 40, had this tombstone set up.
EDCS-09900429.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Ritimarus, son of Rismarus, who had his tombstone erected.

EDCS-09900430.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus P Saco Vindelionis filius annorum LX\n
EDCS-09900430.translation: To the spirits of the departed. P. Saco, son of Vindelio, aged 60.
EDCS-09900430.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for P. Saco, son of Vindelio.

EDCS-09900431.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Septimia Solia <a>nnorum LX Septimius Senecio libertus patronae suae bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-09900431.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Septimia Solia, aged 60. Septimius Senecio, her freedman, set up this monument for his well-deserving patroness.
EDCS-09900431.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Septimia Solia, set up by her freedman Septimius Senecio.

EDCS-09900432.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Sisiu, daughter of Bageto.

EDCS-09900433.text_edition: \n Speratus filius annorum XVIII pater titulum posuit Marcellus sibi et coniugi vivus L CO\n
EDCS-09900433.translation: Speratus, aged 18, his father Marcellus set up this monument for himself and his wife while alive.
EDCS-09900433.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Speratus, and also for his father Marcellus and Marcellus' wife, commissioned by Marcellus while alive.

EDCS-09900434.text_edition: \n Tertio Noibionis filio nnorum XL Satimara Atresi filia marito caro et sibi viva faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-09900434.translation: Satimara, daughter of Atres, had this made for her husband Tertius, son of Noibio, aged 40, and for herself while alive.
EDCS-09900434.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Tertius and Satimara, commissioned by Satimara while alive.

EDCS-09900435.text_edition: \n Urso marito pientissimo et Tertio nepoti eius Tacattu uxor viva et sibi et eis faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-09900435.translation: Tacattu, his wife, had this made for her most devoted husband Urso and for their grandson Tertius while alive, and for herself.
EDCS-09900435.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Urso and his grandson Tertius, commissioned by Tacattu while alive.

EDCS-09900436.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Velideatus Teraniscionis filius annorum XLI dierum C et sibi Vironianius Atalonis filius \n
EDCS-09900436.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Velideatus, son of Teraniscio, aged 41 years and 100 days. Vironianius, son of Atalo, [also set this up] for himself.
EDCS-09900436.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Velideatus, son of Teraniscio, and also for Vironianius, son of Atalo.

EDCS-09900437.text_edition: \n Verodubena Cesonis filia annorum XXX titulum posuit Maius Cuci\n
EDCS-09900437.translation: Verodubena, daughter of Cesonius, aged 30, set up this monument.
EDCS-09900437.summary: A tombstone recording the act of Verodubena, daughter of Cesonius, who set up the monument for herself.

EDCS-09900439.translation: To CS, and his wife, and his heirs.
EDCS-09900439.summary: A fragmentary inscription, likely a dedication or epitaph, mentioning CS, his wife, and heirs.

EDCS-09900443.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Septimius Avvo et Septimii A<tt=II>a e<t=I> Avva filii eorum posuerun<t=I> Sep<t=I>imiae Paulinae coniugi carissimae cum parbulos sic <f=E>ilios sic eorum \n
EDCS-09900443.translation: To the Divine Shades. Septimius Avvo and his sons Septimii Atta and Avva, their children, dedicated this to Septimia Paulina, their dearest wife, with their small children, their children.
EDCS-09900443.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated by Septimius Avvo and his sons to Septimia Paulina, his wife, likely from the late Roman period.

EDCS-09900444.text_edition: \n miles cohortis eiusdem Septimia Theodora pientissimis vivus sibi faciendum curavit et Zosimenae coniugi nniano filio et nepoti et Diomedi nepoti nepoti et Omainae et \n
EDCS-09900444.translation: Septimia Theodora of the same cohort took care to have this made while alive for her most devoted husband, and for her son and grandson Zosimena, and for her grandson and grandson Diomedes, and for Omaina, and...
EDCS-09900444.summary: A funerary monument initiated by Septimia Theodora for her husband and other relatives, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-09900450.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico Iulius Crispinianus veteranus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-09900450.translation: Julius Crispinianus, a veteran, willingly paid his vow to Silvanus Domestiscus, as was deserved.
EDCS-09900450.summary: A votive inscription recording a veteran, Julius Crispinianus, fulfilling a vow to the deity Silvanus Domestiscus.

EDCS-09900451.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Septimio Dasio quondam militi legionis II adiutricis stipendiorum XIIII vixit annos XXXVI Aelia Sura mater eius et Aurelius Severus immunis legionis eiusdem heredes titulum bene merenti faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-09900451.translation: To the Divine Shades. Aelia Sura, his mother, and Aurelius Severus, immunis of the same legion, as heirs, took care to have this monument made for Septimius Dasus, formerly a soldier of the II Adiutrix legion, who served for 14 years and lived for 36 years, to the well-deserving.
EDCS-09900451.summary: A funerary epitaph for Septimius Dasus, a soldier of the II Adiutrix legion, erected by his mother and a fellow soldier, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-09900452.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Verienda Terti filia annorum XL hic sita est titulum posuit Valerinus sibi vivo et coniugi\n
EDCS-09900452.translation: To the Divine Shades. Verienda, daughter of Tertius, aged 40, lies here. Valerinus set up this monument for himself while alive, and for his wife.
EDCS-09900452.summary: A funerary epitaph for Verienda, daughter of Tertius, erected by Valerinus for himself and his wife.

EDCS-09900462.text_edition: \n ------ p]ro salute domini nostri Gordiani Augusti Cicerius Homuncio beneficiarius consularis legionis I adiutricis votum solvit libens merito Sabino II et Venusto consulibus\n
EDCS-09900462.translation: For the safety of our lord Gordian Augustus. Cicerius Homuncio, beneficiarius consularis of the I Adiutrix legion, willingly paid his vow as was deserved, under consuls Sabinus II and Venustus.
EDCS-09900462.summary: A votive inscription by Cicerius Homuncio, a soldier of the I Adiutrix legion, for the health of Emperor Gordian III, dated by the consuls Sabinus and Venustus.

EDCS-09900463.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Iunoni Reginae Publius Aelius Constans candidatus legionis II adiutricis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-09900463.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, to Juno Regina. Publius Aelius Constans, candidate of the II Adiutrix legion, willingly paid his vow as was deserved.
EDCS-09900463.summary: A votive inscription by Publius Aelius Constans, a soldier of the II Adiutrix legion, to Jupiter and Juno.

EDCS-09900464.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Iunoni Reginae Iulius Martinianus et Aurelius Dines et Memorina votum solverunt libentes merito\n
EDCS-09900464.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, to Juno Regina. Julius Martinianus and Aurelius Dines and Memorina willingly paid their vows as was deserved.
EDCS-09900464.summary: A votive inscription by Julius Martinianus, Aurelius Dines, and Memorina to Jupiter and Juno.

EDCS-09900465.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Iunoni Reginae Crispius Potens votum solvit Perpetuo et Corneliano consulibus\n
EDCS-09900465.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest and Juno Queen, Crispius Potens fulfilled his vow, Perpetuus and Cornelius being consuls.
EDCS-09900465.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter and Juno, with the vow fulfilled by Crispius Potens during the consulship of Perpetuus and Cornelius, likely in the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09900466.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Terrae Matri Iulius Rufus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-09900466.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Mother Earth, Julius Rufus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-09900466.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter and Terra Mater, with the vow fulfilled by Julius Rufus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09900467.text_edition: \n Libero Patri Paulinus votum libies sic solvit\n
EDCS-09900467.translation: To Father Liber, Paulinus gladly fulfilled his vow in this way.
EDCS-09900467.summary: This is a dedication to Liber Pater, with the vow fulfilled by Paulinus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09900468.text_edition: \n Nemesi Augustae Publius Nigrius Quintilianus beneficiarius consularis\n
EDCS-09900468.translation: To Nemesis Augusta, Publius Nigrius Quintilianus, a consular attendant, [dedicated this].
EDCS-09900468.summary: This is a dedication to Nemesis Augusta by Publius Nigrius Quintilianus, who served as a beneficiary of a consul, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09900469.text_edition: \n Silvano Iulius Rufus votum solvit libens merito Laeliano et Pastore consulibus\n
EDCS-09900469.translation: To Silvanus, Julius Rufus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow, Laelianus and Pastor being consuls.
EDCS-09900469.summary: This is a dedication to Silvanus, with the vow fulfilled by Julius Rufus during the consulship of Laelianus and Pastor, likely in the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09900470.summary: This is a dedication to Silvanus Domesticus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09900471.text_edition: \n Silvano Silvestri Crispius Potens votum solvit\n
EDCS-09900471.translation: To Silvanus of the Woods, Crispius Potens fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-09900471.summary: This is a dedication to Silvanus of the Woods by Crispius Potens, likely from the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09900473.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurus vixit nnos XXII Vitalis vixit nnos V bruti et neboti sic posuerunt Atrevius Cata parentes bene merentibus\n
EDCS-09900473.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed: Aurus lived 22 years, Vitalis lived 5 years. Brutus and Nepos set this up for their well-deserving parents, Atrevius Cata [being the father].
EDCS-09900473.summary: This is a tombstone for Aurus and Vitalis, set up by their parents Atrevius Cata, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09900474.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, possibly the name of a person or a place, with no further context.

EDCS-09900475.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caesernius Martialis miles legionis primae adiutricis signifer vixit annos XL Aelia ata coniugi carissimo et Caeserniae Provinciae filiae carissimae posuit\n
EDCS-09900475.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed: Caesernius Martialis, a standard-bearer soldier of the First Legion Adjutrix, lived 40 years. Aelia Ata placed this for her dearest husband and Caesernia Provincia, her dearest daughter.
EDCS-09900475.summary: This is a tombstone for Caesernius Martialis and his daughter Caesernia Provincia, dedicated by his wife Aelia Ata, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09900476.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Propinco sic annos XV et Aelius Attianus vixit annos XVII Aelius itus coniugi \n
EDCS-09900476.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Propinus lived 15 years and Aelius Attianus lived 17 years. Aelius...
EDCS-09900476.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the ages of two individuals, Propinus and Aelius Attianus.

EDCS-09900477.text_edition: \n ------ vi]xit annos V Sabinus vixit annos et Serotinus iunior vixit Lucius Septimius Serotinus domo nia Sabinilla parentes\n
EDCS-09900477.translation: ...lived 5 years. Sabinus lived ... years and Serotinus the younger lived. Lucius Septimius Serotinus from [town name], parents Sabinilla...
EDCS-09900477.summary: This is a funerary inscription, likely recording the ages of several individuals, with parents named Lucius Septimius Serotinus and Sabinilla.

EDCS-09900478.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus N D annos menses VII Inus us signifer bene merito posuit\n
EDCS-09900478.translation: To the spirits of the departed. [Name] lived ... years and 7 months. Inus erected this for the well-deserving standard-bearer.
EDCS-09900478.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to a standard-bearer by Inus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09900482.translation: Tiberius made this while alive for himself and...
EDCS-09900482.summary: This is a funerary inscription where Tiberius records that he made this monument for himself and others.

EDCS-09900484.text_edition: \n ci filiae Rusticae annorum XXXXV Iulius Honoratus coniugi pientissimae et Moderato filio annorum V posuit\n
EDCS-09900484.translation: ...for his daughter Rustica, aged 45. Iulius Honoratus placed this for his most dutiful wife and for his son Moderatus, aged 5.
EDCS-09900484.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Iulius Honoratus commemorating his wife and son.

EDCS-09900485.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelia Severa Aelio Saturnino coniugi pientissimo vixit annos LXXX et Aelio Exsuperato et Apuleio Macriniano filiis et Aureliae Nigrinae matri et FIMS et Atois et sibi memoriam posuit\n
EDCS-09900485.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aurelia Severa placed this memorial for her most dutiful husband Aelius Saturninus, who lived 80 years, and for her sons Aelius Exsuperatus and Apuleius Macrinianus, and for her mother Aurelia Nigrina, and for FIMS, and for Atois, and for herself.
EDCS-09900485.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Aurelia Severa, a comprehensive memorial for her family members.

EDCS-09900486.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelio V annos VII dom me \n
EDCS-09900486.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aurelius ... lived 7 years. [From] home...
EDCS-09900486.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription recording the age of Aurelius.

EDCS-09900488.text_edition: \n Surius Essimni filius Cattenas miles cohortis I Vindelicorum nnorum XXXX stipendiorum XX hic situs est Florus et Bassus CE\n
EDCS-09900488.translation: Surius, son of Essimnus, from Cattenas, a soldier of the 1st Cohort of the Vindelici, aged 40, with 20 years of service, is buried here. Florus and Bassus [erected this]...
EDCS-09900488.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Surius, a soldier in the 1st Cohort of the Vindelici, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09900489.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Claudius Celer veteranus alae Brittonum pro salute sua et suorum votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-09900489.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest. Claudius Celer, a veteran of the ala Brittonum, fulfilled his vow for his own health and that of his family, willingly, gladly, deservedly.
EDCS-09900489.summary: This is a votive inscription by Claudius Celer, a veteran of the ala Brittonum, dedicated to Jupiter for his and his family's well-being.

EDCS-09900494.translation: For Mansuetus, of the 1st Cohort (miliaria) of the Novaesiani, lived 6 years...
EDCS-09900494.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription recording the age of Mansuetus, a soldier from the 1st Cohort (miliaria) of the Novaesiani.

EDCS-09900495.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelia Dobnina quae vixsit sic annos viginti et duos\n
EDCS-09900495.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Aurelia Dobnina, who lived for twenty-two years.
EDCS-09900495.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aurelia Dobnina, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09900496.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Attio Germanioni liberto vixit nnos XL Apuleius Basileus posuit bene merenti\n
EDCS-09900496.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Attius Germanio, freedman, lived 40 years. Apuleius Basileus set this up for him, his deserving freedman.
EDCS-09900496.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by Apuleius Basileus for his freedman Attius Germanio, probably from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09900497.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aureliae Ingenuae coniugi pientissimae vixit nnos XXX Aurelius Crescentio posuit et Aurelio Crescentiano et Aureliae Ursae \n
EDCS-09900497.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Aurelia Ingenua, most devoted wife, who lived 30 years. Aurelius Crescentio set this up, and for Aurelius Crescentianus and Aurelia Ursa.
EDCS-09900497.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by Aurelius Crescentio for his wife Aurelia Ingenua and two other individuals, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09900498.translation: The pious one set this up.
EDCS-09900498.summary: A very brief inscription recording a pious act, possibly the dedication of a monument or offering, with an uncertain date.

EDCS-09900499.summary: A fragmentary inscription mentioning 'kin and family', likely part of a longer funerary or dedicatory text, date uncertain.

EDCS-09900501.text_edition: \n Deo Azizo pro salute domini nostri Alexandri Augusti et Iuliae Augustae Iulius manus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-09900501.translation: To the god Azizo, for the welfare of our lord Alexander Augustus and Julia Augusta, Iulius Manus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-09900501.summary: A votive inscription to the god Azizo, dedicated by Iulius Manus for the welfare of Emperor Alexander Severus and Julia Augusta, likely from the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09900502.summary: An inscription consisting of a single name, 'Cautes', possibly a deity or a person's name, date uncertain.

EDCS-09900504.text_edition: \n Dianae Augustae pro salute domini nostri Imperatoris Alexandri Augusti vetterani cohortis |(miliariae) Hemesenorum votum solverunt libentes merito\n
EDCS-09900504.translation: To the August Diana, for the welfare of our lord Emperor Alexander Augustus, the veterans of the first cohort (miliaria) of Hemesenorum gladly and deservedly fulfilled their vow.
EDCS-09900504.summary: A votive inscription by veterans of the Hemeseni cohort to Diana Augusta for the welfare of Emperor Alexander Severus, likely from the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09900505.text_edition: \n Dianae Titus Flavius Quintianus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-09900505.translation: To Diana, Titus Flavius Quintianus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-09900505.summary: A votive inscription to Diana by Titus Flavius Quintianus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09900506.text_edition: \n Deae Dianae Rat Fuscus pro se Prisciano et Valeriano et suis votum solvit libens merito Modesto et Probo consulibus\n
EDCS-09900506.translation: To the goddess Diana, Rat Fuscus for himself, Priscianus, Valerianus, and his family gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow, under the consulship of Modestus and Probus.
EDCS-09900506.summary: A votive inscription to Diana by Rat Fuscus and his family, dated to the consulship of Modestus and Probus (likely 228 or 230 CE).

EDCS-09900508.text_edition: \n Dis oimnibus Sabinus veteranus Sabininanus veteranus ex voto <p=B>osuit sic \n
EDCS-09900508.translation: Sabinus, a veteran from Sabiniana, set this up to all the gods in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-09900508.summary: This is a dedication to all the gods by a veteran, likely dating from the Imperial period.

EDCS-09900509.text_edition: \n Deo Dobrati Eutices sic servus dedit\n
EDCS-09900509.translation: Eutices, a slave, dedicated this to the god Dobraius.
EDCS-09900509.summary: This records a dedication to the god Dobraius by a slave, probably from the Imperial period.

EDCS-09900511.text_edition: \n Herculi Conservatori domus Heraclitianae Marcus Aurelius Heraclitus |(centurio) cohortis XI urbanae Severianae cum Aureliis Italla Thraca et Heraclito iuniore filiis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-09900511.translation: To Hercules the Protector of the Heraclitan household, Marcus Aurelius Heraclitus, centurion of the XI Urban Cohort Severiana, together with his children Aurelia Italia Thraca and Heraclitus the Younger, fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-09900511.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Hercules Conservator by Marcus Aurelius Heraclitus, a centurion, and his family, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09900514.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo votum solverunt libentes merito Publii Aelii Septimus et Decoratus decuriones municipii et arm civitatis Eraviscorum\n
EDCS-09900514.translation: Publius Aelius Septimus and Decoratus, decurions of the municipium and leading citizens of the Eravisci tribe, fulfilled their vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-09900514.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by two decurions of the Eravisci municipality, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09900515.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Aurelius Castus immunis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-09900515.translation: Aurelius Castus, exempt from normal duties, fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-09900515.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Aurelius Castus, who held a privileged status, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09900516.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Aurelius Damas veteranus Hemesenorum votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-09900516.translation: Aurelius Damas, a veteran of the Hemesenian cavalry, fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-09900516.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Aurelius Damas, a veteran, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09900517.text_edition: \n Pro salute domini nostri Severi Alexandri Augusti et Iuliae Mamaeae Augustae Iovi Optimo Maximo Conservatori domus Heraclitianae Marcus Aurelius Heraclitus |(centurio) statorum praetorianorum cum filiis suis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-09900517.translation: For the health of our lord Severus Alexander Augustus and Julia Mamaea Augusta, Marcus Aurelius Heraclitus, centurion of the praetorian guard stators, together with his sons, fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Conservator of the Heraclitan household willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-09900517.summary: This is a lengthy votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Conservator by Marcus Aurelius Heraclitus, a centurion, for the health of Emperor Severus Alexander and Julia Mamaea, likely dating to their reign (222-235 CE).

EDCS-09900519.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Aurelius Rufus |(centurio) pro se suisque omnibus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-09900519.translation: Aurelius Rufus, a centurion, fulfilled his vow for himself and all his family to Jupiter Optimus Maximus willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-09900519.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Aurelius Rufus, a centurion, on behalf of himself and his family, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09900520.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Marcus Aurelius Verissimus beneficiarius consularis legionis II adiutricis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-09900520.translation: Marcus Aurelius Verissimus, a beneficiary official of a consular governor of the II Adjutrix Legion, fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-09900520.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Marcus Aurelius Verissimus, a military official attached to a legion, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09900522.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Cornelius beneficiarius votum libens merito\n
EDCS-09900522.translation: Cornelius, a beneficiary official, fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-09900522.summary: This is a votive inscription to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Cornelius, a beneficiary official, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09900523.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Caius Flavius Cai filius Domitius Severus \n
EDCS-09900523.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, Gaius Flavius Domitius Severus, son of Gaius, [dedicated this].
EDCS-09900523.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter, likely by Gaius Flavius Domitius Severus, possibly in the Imperial period.

EDCS-09900524.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, Paterno [dedicated this].
EDCS-09900524.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter, likely by Paterno, possibly in the Imperial period.

EDCS-09900525.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Iunoni Reginae Libero Telluri Aurelius Barsamsus strator consularis sacravit\n
EDCS-09900525.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, to Juno Regina, and to Liber and Tellus, Aurelius Barsamsus, quaestor of a consul, consecrated [this].
EDCS-09900525.summary: This inscription records the consecration of a shrine to Jupiter, Juno, Liber, and Tellus by Aurelius Barsamsus, a former quaestor, likely in the Imperial period.

EDCS-09900527.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Culminali Aurelius Mammianus veteranus cum suis omnibus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-09900527.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, Guardian of the Threshold, Aurelius Mammianus, a veteran, with all his family, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-09900527.summary: This inscription records a veteran, Aurelius Mammianus, fulfilling a vow to Jupiter Culminalis for himself and his family, possibly in the Imperial period.

EDCS-09900529.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Heroni pro salute Imperatoris domini nostri Severi Alexandr[i] Augusti cultores collegii eius templum a solo extruxerunt curantibus sic Aurelio Mes<t=E>rio patre \n
EDCS-09900529.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, to the God Heron, the worshippers of his college, with Aurelius Mestrius as father overseeing, built this temple from the ground up for the health of our lord Severus Alexander Augustus.
EDCS-09900529.summary: This inscription records the construction of a temple to Jupiter and Heron by the members of a college for the health of Emperor Severus Alexander, under the supervision of Aurelius Mestrius, likely in the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09900530.text_edition: \n Libero Patri Aurelius Iustinianus armorum custos votum solvit libentes merito\n
EDCS-09900530.translation: To Liber the Father, Aurelius Iustinianus, keeper of arms, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-09900530.summary: This inscription records Aurelius Iustinianus, an armorum custos, fulfilling a vow to Liber Pater, likely in the Imperial period.

EDCS-09900531.text_edition: \n Libero Patri Aurelius Monimus veteranus pro salute sua et suorum votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-09900531.translation: To Liber the Father, Aurelius Monimus, a veteran, for the health of himself and his family, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-09900531.summary: This inscription records a veteran, Aurelius Monimus, fulfilling a vow to Liber Pater for his own and his family's well-being, likely in the Imperial period.

EDCS-09900532.text_edition: \n Libero Patri et Liberae Aurelius Maturus summus curator votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-09900532.translation: To Liber the Father and Libera, Aurelius Maturus, the chief curator, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-09900532.summary: This inscription records Aurelius Maturus, the chief curator, fulfilling a vow to Liber Pater and Libera, likely in the Imperial period.

EDCS-09900533.text_edition: \n Mercurio lucrorum potenti Aurelius Hermes negotians pro salute sua \n
EDCS-09900533.translation: To Mercury, powerful in gains, Aurelius Hermes, the merchant, for his own health [dedicated this].
EDCS-09900533.summary: This inscription records a merchant, Aurelius Hermes, dedicating something to Mercury for his health, likely in the Imperial period.

EDCS-09900534.text_edition: \n Deo Soli Invicto Mithrae Antonius Veranus pater pientissimus suo in loco feliciter posuit\n
EDCS-09900534.translation: To the Unconquered Sun God Mithras, Antonius Veranus, a most devoted father, happily placed [this] in his own place.
EDCS-09900534.summary: This inscription records Antonius Veranus, a devoted father, dedicating a monument to the Unconquered Sun God Mithras, likely in the Imperial period.

EDCS-09900535.text_edition: \n Deo Soli Invicto Mithrae Atta Vervici libert-- votum merito solvit\n
EDCS-09900535.translation: To the Unconquered Sun-god Mithras, Atta, freedman of Vervicus, has fulfilled his vow as was deserved.
EDCS-09900535.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled to the god Sol Invictus Mithras by Atta, a freedman, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09900537.translation: Sacred to the god Silvanus, by Aurelius Mari.
EDCS-09900537.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the god Silvanus by Aurelius Mari, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09900538.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico Saturnina votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-09900538.translation: To Silvanus Domesticus, Saturnina has willingly and deservedly fulfilled her vow.
EDCS-09900538.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled to Silvanus Domesticus by Saturnina, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09900539.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico sacrum Secundinus pro se et suis votum posuit\n
EDCS-09900539.translation: Secundinus has placed this offering to Silvanus Domesticus for himself and his family, with a vow.
EDCS-09900539.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to Silvanus Domesticus by Secundinus for his family, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09900540.text_edition: \n Deo Soli Invicto Antonius Veranus pater votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-09900540.translation: To the Unconquered Sun-god, Antonius Veranus the father has willingly, joyfully, and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-09900540.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled to the god Sol Invictus by Antonius Veranus, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09900541.text_edition: \n Soli Invicto Aurelius Arbas Aelia innina Soli Aelius Vitalianus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-09900541.translation: To the Unconquered Sun, Aurelius Arbas and Aelia Innina have, with their son Aelius Vitalianus, willingly, joyfully, and deservedly fulfilled their vow.
