EDCS-26600562.summary: This inscription records a soldier named Titus Julius Valens of the First Cohort (milliaria) Nova Severiana of Syrian archers fulfilling a vow for the health of the emperors.

EDCS-26600563.text_edition: \n Tito Flavio Titi filio Claudia Agrippiniensi equiti legionis II adiutricis misso honesta missione vixit annos Tito Flavio Senecae Primitivae et vivae Titus Flavius L Verus filius \n
EDCS-26600563.translation: To Titus Flavius, son of Titus, of the Claudian tribe, a cavalryman of the Second Adjutant Legion, honorably discharged. He lived [years?]. To Titus Flavius Verus, son of Titus Flavius Seneca and Primitiva, while alive.
EDCS-26600563.summary: This inscription records a cavalryman named Titus Flavius, who served in the Second Adjutant Legion and was honorably discharged, and also mentions his son, Titus Flavius Verus.

EDCS-26600564.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flavius Restitutus sibi vivus fecit Iustae annorum coniugi et bene merenti M D T M P\n
EDCS-26600564.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Flavius Restitutus made this for himself while alive, and for his wife Iusta, who lived X years, a well-deserving woman. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-26600564.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Flavius Restitutus for himself and his deceased wife Iusta, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600565.text_edition: \n Ava Magimari filia annorum XXV et Siliduna et Appinus filii heredes Sisi et Quintus et Medulius \n
EDCS-26600565.translation: Ava, daughter of Magimarus, aged 25, and her sons Siliduna and Appinus, and her heirs Sisi, Quintus, and Medulius.
EDCS-26600565.summary: This inscription lists individuals and their relationships, likely related to inheritance or a family memorial, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600566.text_edition: \n Retimarus vixit annos LX filii eius faciendum curaverunt Severus n posuit\n
EDCS-26600566.translation: Retimarus lived for 60 years. His sons arranged for this to be made; Severus placed it.
EDCS-26600566.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Retimarus and indicating that his sons commissioned the monument, with Severus responsible for its placement, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600588.text_edition: \n Valerius Proculus Savaria optio legionis II adiutricis stipendiorum VI nnorum XXV hic situs est Valeria Procula mater et Valerius Fuscus frater titulum memoriae posuerunt\n
EDCS-26600588.translation: Valerius Proculus, quartermaster of the II Adjutrix legion, served for VI years, aged 25, is buried here. His mother Valeria Procula and his brother Valerius Fuscus set up this memorial stone.
EDCS-26600588.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Valerius Proculus, a soldier of the II Adjutrix legion, commissioned by his mother and brother, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600591.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus in memoriam Aureli Minervini adulescentis nepotis aetate integra qui vixit nnos XVII fecit Aurelius Crispus decurio coloniae Equaestris sic eques Romanus avunculus\n
EDCS-26600591.translation: To the spirits of the underworld, in memory of Aurelius Minervinus, a young man in his prime, who lived 17 years. Aurelius Crispus, decurion of the colony Equaestris and a Roman knight, his uncle, had this made.
EDCS-26600591.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aurelius Minervinus, a young man, erected by his uncle Aurelius Crispus, a decurion, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600595.text_edition: \n Bonae memoriae coniugis carissimae Hoc iacet in lapide Aurelia Marcellina pientissima coniunx quem lapis ipse tegit rapta de luce serena huic aetas primis cum florebat in annis invida fatorum genesis mihi sustulit illam superante genetrice sua quae se cupiebat ob illam ante morti dari quam sibi flebilis casus acciderit filiusque permansit innocentius et quicumque legis nostros tu nosti dolores sic at et Elysias senior descendis ad auras Iulius Victorinus beneficiarius consularis faciendum curavit et pater Aurelius Eptala pientissim--\n
EDCS-26600595.translation: To the memory of my dearest wife. Here lies in this stone Aurelia Marcellina, most dutiful wife, whom this stone covers, snatched from the clear light while she was flourishing in her first years; envious fate took her from me before her mother wished to die for her, a tragic event. Her son remained innocent. And whoever reads this, you know our sorrows; may you too descend to the Elysian fields as an elder. Julius Victorinus, an administrative officer of a consular governor, arranged for this to be made, and his father Aurelius Eptala, most dutiful--
EDCS-26600595.summary: This is a poignant funerary epitaph lamenting the early death of Aurelia Marcellina, commissioned by her husband Julius Victorinus and potentially her father Aurelius Eptala, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600596.text_edition: \n Libero Patri et Liberae Aelius Nianus \n
EDCS-26600596.translation: To Liber Pater and Libera. Aelius Nianus.
EDCS-26600596.summary: This is a dedication to the deities Liber Pater and Libera by Aelius Nianus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600597.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caiae Valeriae Alpinae annorum VIII hic sita est pater et mater filiae bene merenti posuerunt et sibi vivis Aelius Ressatus explorator et Valeria Aelias mater\n
EDCS-26600597.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Caia Valeria Alpina, aged 8, is buried here. Her father and mother, for their well-deserving daughter, set this up, and for themselves while alive. Aelius Ressatus, an intelligence officer, and his wife Valeria Aelias, the mother.
EDCS-26600597.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for the young Caia Valeria Alpina, set up by her parents who also reserved space for themselves, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600598.text_edition: \n cohortis I Noricorum vixit annos XXXV et Marco AcIIvo vixit nnos nianus vivus sibi et patri bene m(erenti)\n
EDCS-26600598.translation: Of the I Noricorum cohort, he lived 35 years, and Marcus Acilius, he lived X years, while alive for himself and his well-deserving father--
EDCS-26600598.summary: This inscription, likely part of a funerary monument, records the lifespan of a soldier from the I Noricorum cohort and possibly another individual named Marcus Acilius, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600599.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelio Proculino militi qui vixit annos XXXV dies L Aurelius Proculus veteranus legionis II adiutricis natione Surus domo Hemesa cum Claudia Candida parentes filio karissimo posuerunt cum Aelia Iustina coniuge\n
EDCS-26600599.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Aurelius Proculinus, a soldier who lived 35 years and 50 days. Aurelius Proculus, a veteran of the II Adjutrix legion, from Hemsa in Syria, with Claudia Candida, his wife, his parents, set this up for their dearest son, along with Aelia Iustina, his wife.
EDCS-26600599.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aurelius Proculinus, a soldier, erected by his veteran father and mother, and including his wife, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600600.text_edition: \n veni MantiN domi sic Velauno coniugi V\n
EDCS-26600600.translation: I, Mantius, have come to this place; thus, Velaunus, to my wife.
EDCS-26600600.summary: An epitaph for Velaunus, the wife of Mantius, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-26600602.text_edition: \n Herculi sacrum Marcus Ulpius Gemellus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-26600602.translation: Marcus Ulpius Gemellus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Hercules.
EDCS-26600602.summary: A votive inscription dedicated to Hercules by Marcus Ulpius Gemellus, likely fulfilling a promise.

EDCS-26600603.text_edition: \n Herculi Augusti Marcus Domitius Secundinus decurio alae Brittonum votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-26600603.translation: Marcus Domitius Secundinus, a decurion of the Wing of the Britons, gladly, joyfully, and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Hercules Augustus.
EDCS-26600603.summary: A votive inscription to Hercules Augustus by Marcus Domitius Secundinus, a decurion in the ala Brittonum, likely from the Roman military.

EDCS-26600604.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Marcus Ulpius Marcellus beneficiarius consularis votum solvit libens merito legionis IIII Flaviae felicis\n
EDCS-26600604.translation: Marcus Ulpius Marcellus, a beneficiarius of a consular governor of Legio IIII Flavia Felix, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.
EDCS-26600604.summary: A votive inscription to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Marcus Ulpius Marcellus, a soldier associated with Legio IIII Flavia Felix, likely from the Roman military.

EDCS-26600605.text_edition: \n Virtuti et Honori Lucius Ulpius Marcellus legatus Augusti pro praetore nniae inferioris votum solvit\n
EDCS-26600605.translation: Lucius Ulpius Marcellus, imperial legate pro praetore of Lower Pannonia, fulfilled his vow to Virtue and Honor.
EDCS-26600605.summary: A votive inscription to Virtue and Honor by Lucius Ulpius Marcellus, a high-ranking imperial official (legate) in Pannonia Inferior.

EDCS-26600606.text_edition: \n Caesernia Firmilla viva fecit sibi et Marcio Lucidiano coniugi annorum XLIIII et Marcio Lucido filio annorum XL et Marcio Firminiano militi annorum XXVII et Ulpio SI A Lucio candidato legionis II adiutricis et Ulpiae Proclinae niri sic et Luciae et Marcio Iuliano annorum VI nepotibus\n
EDCS-26600606.translation: Caesernia Firmilla made this while alive for herself and for her husband Marcius Lucidianus, aged 42, and for her son Marcius Lucidus, aged 40, and for her son Marcius Firminianus, a soldier, aged 27, and for Ulpius Si Lucius, a candidate of Legio II Adjutrix, and for Ulpia Proclina, and for her grandchildren Lucius Marcius Iulianus, aged 6.
EDCS-26600606.summary: A funerary monument set up by Caesernia Firmilla for herself and various family members, including her husband, sons, and grandchildren, dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-26600607.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcio Restutiano patri defuncto annorum LXXV Septimiae Ursinae vivae parentibus pientissimis et Marcio Urso militi legionis III Italicae defuncto annorum XXV et Marciae Aquilinae defunctae annorum XXXV et Marcio Coto defuncto annorum X fratribus item Suconiugi suae optimae et Ael \n
EDCS-26600607.translation: To the spirits of the underworld: for Marcius Restutianus, my father, deceased at age 75, and for Septimia Ursina, alive, my most devoted parents, and for Marcius Urso, a soldier of Legio III Italica, deceased at age 25, and for Marcia Aquilina, deceased at age 35, and for Marcius Coto, deceased at age 10, my brothers; also for my most excellent wife Suconigis, and Aelia...
EDCS-26600607.summary: A complex funerary inscription for multiple deceased family members, including parents, siblings, and a wife, likely set up by a surviving family member, dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-26600608.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelius Ursus vixit annos VIIII posuit Aelius Ianuarius pater filio pientissimo\n
EDCS-26600608.translation: Aelius Ursus lived 9 years. Aelius Ianuarius, the father, erected this for his most devoted son.
EDCS-26600608.summary: A tombstone set up by Aelius Ianuarius for his young son, Aelius Ursus, dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-26600609.text_edition: \n AE annos XXV Ulpius Pirus pater vixit annos XL posuit Ulpia Ruca mater filiis dulcissimis heres eorum\n
EDCS-26600609.translation: Aelia lived 25 years. Ulpius Pirus, the father, lived 40 years. Ulpia Ruca, the mother, erected this for her most beloved sons, their heir.
EDCS-26600609.summary: A funerary monument erected by Ulpia Ruca for her sons, listing their ages and indicating she is their heir, dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-26600610.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Caenio Secundino annorum LXX et Lucio Caenio Flore[ntino ------\n
EDCS-26600610.translation: To the spirits of the underworld: for Lucius Caenius Secundinus, aged 70, and for Lucius Caenius Florentinus...
EDCS-26600610.summary: A fragmentary funerary inscription mentioning Lucius Caenius Secundinus and Lucius Caenius Florentinus, likely commemorating deceased family members, dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-26600612.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Septimius Probatus veteranus legionis II adiutricis vixit nnos XLV et Septimiae Boniatae coniugi eiusdem et Septimio Probiano et Probillae et Respecto et Proculino filiis Aurelius Servatus genero optimo filiae et nepotibus et Aureliae Respectae coniugi pientissimae memoriam posuit\n
EDCS-26600612.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Septimius Probatus, a veteran of the Second Adjutant Legion, lived 45 years, and Septimia Boniata, his wife, and their children Septimius Probianus, Probilla, Respectus, and Proculinus. Aurelius Servatus, an excellent son-in-law, with his daughters, grandchildren, and Aurelia Respecta, his most dutiful wife, erected this memorial.
EDCS-26600612.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by Aurelius Servatus for his deceased wife, her parents (including the veteran Septimius Probatus), and their children, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600613.text_edition: \n Fortunae Salutari cohortis I Alpinorum equitatae Publius Clodius Severus praefectus duobus Auggustis ddominis nnostris consulibus\n
EDCS-26600613.translation: To Fortuna Salutaris, of the First Cohort of Alpiners, cavalry wing. Publius Clodius Severus, prefect, during the consulship of our two Augusti Lords.
EDCS-26600613.summary: This is a dedication to the goddess Fortuna Salutaris by Publius Clodius Severus, prefect of the cavalry wing of the First Cohort of Alpiners, likely dating to the reign of one of the co-emperors named Augustus.

EDCS-26600614.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari divi Antonini filio divi Hadriani nepoti divi Traiani Parthici pronepoti divi Nervae abnepoti Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto tribunicia potestate XVII consuli III cohors I Alpinorum peditata\n
EDCS-26600614.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Antoninus, grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva, Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, holding tribunician power for the 17th time, consul for the 3rd time, [dedicated by] the First Cohort of Alpiners, infantry wing.
EDCS-26600614.summary: This is an inscription honoring Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, listing his imperial titles and ancestry, and likely dedicated by the infantry wing of the First Cohort of Alpiners, dating to his 17th tribunician year (around 177-178 AD).

EDCS-26600615.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Septimius Tatulo veteranus ex optione cohortis I Thracum Germanicae et Aurelia Verina coniux Lucius Aurelius qui et Septimius Constans filius miles cohortis supra scriptae Aurelius qui et Septimius Constantinus filius miles legionis I adiutricis Aurelius qui et Septimius Victorinus filius Aurelius qui et Septimius Verinus filius Aureli- qu- et Septimi- Aemili-) fili-) Mereccusae mater sic vixit annos LXX vivi sibi fecerunt\n
EDCS-26600615.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius Septimius Tatulo, a veteran ex-optio of the First Cohort of Thracian Germans, and his wife Aurelia Verina; their son Lucius Aurelius who is also Septimius Constans, a soldier of the above-mentioned cohort; Aurelius who is also Septimius Constantinus, a son, a soldier of the First Adjutant Legion; Aurelius who is also Septimius Victorinus, a son; Aurelius who is also Septimius Verinus, a son; and their mother Mereccusa, of the family of Aemilius, lived 70 years. They made [this] for themselves while alive.
EDCS-26600615.summary: This is a funerary monument for Lucius Septimius Tatulo and his wife Aurelia Verina, and their sons, including soldiers and a veteran, erected by themselves during their lifetime, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600616.text_edition: \n ri Quirina Sirmio Maximo quondam veterano ex signifero legionis II adiutricis et Cominiae Maximillae filiae eius et Aeliae Proculinae uxori eius E L Novllianus \n
EDCS-26600616.translation: To Quirina. To Maximus Sirmio, formerly a veteran ex-signifer of the Second Adjutant Legion, and to his daughter Cominia Maximilla, and to his wife Aelia Proculina. E. L. Novellianus [erected this].
EDCS-26600616.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Maximus Sirmio, a veteran, his daughter, and wife, erected by E. L. Novellianus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600617.text_edition: \n eres et Flaviae filiae Firminae et Flaviae Firmae filiis Flavius Firmus veteranus legionis II adiutricis pientissimis? ---\n
EDCS-26600617.translation: To his sons and to Flavia Firmina, and to her sons Flavia Firma. Flavius Firmus, a veteran of the Second Adjutant Legion, [erected this] for his most dutiful [sons]...
EDCS-26600617.summary: This is a funerary inscription likely set up by Flavius Firmus, a veteran of the Second Adjutant Legion, for his sons and for Flavia Firmina and her sons, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600618.text_edition: \n Oplus Laepocus Volsetis filius Aexilvas veteranus nnorum LIII stipendiorum XXIX hic situs est Lucius Petillius Cai filius heres posuit\n
EDCS-26600618.translation: Oplus Laepocus, son of Volsetis, Aexilvas, a veteran, aged 53, with 29 years of service, lies here. Lucius Petillius, son of Caius, his heir, erected [this].
EDCS-26600618.summary: This is a tombstone for Oplus Laepocus Aexilvas, a veteran of 53 years with 29 years of service, erected by his heir Lucius Petillius, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600619.text_edition: \n ------] Anaco pater et Atenatis pia mater Marciano et Salonino filiis p(osuerunt)\n
EDCS-26600619.translation: [...] Anacus the father and Atenatis the dutiful mother [erected this] for their sons Marcianus and Saloninus.
EDCS-26600619.summary: This is a funerary inscription by parents for their sons Marcianus and Saloninus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600620.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Tullius Fortunatus veteranus cohortis |Dquingenariae Maurorum domo Africa vixit annos LXX Septimiae Dubitatae coniugi et filis eorum Fabius Baritio veteranus cohortis eiusdem heres eius amico bene merenti titulum\n
EDCS-26600620.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Marcus Tullius Fortunatus, a veteran of a quinqegenary cohort of Moors, from Africa, lived 70 years. Septimia Dubitata, his wife, and their sons. Fabius Baritio, a veteran of the same cohort, his heir, [erected] this monument for a well-deserving friend.
EDCS-26600620.summary: This is a funerary monument for Marcus Tullius Fortunatus, his wife Septimia Dubitata, and their sons, with an additional dedication by the heir of a fellow veteran, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-26600621.text_edition: \n Deo Aeterno pro salute domini nostri Severi Alexandri Pii Felicis Augusti et Iuliae Mamaeae Augustae matris Augusti votum reddidit libens Cosmius praepositus stationis Spondill a synagoga Iudaeorum\n
EDCS-26600621.translation: To the Eternal God. For the safety of our lord Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, and Julia Mamaea Augusta, Mother of Augustus. Cosmius, prefect of the station Spondilla, from the synagogue of the Jews, fulfilled his vow willingly.
EDCS-26600621.summary: This is a votive inscription by Cosmius, a Jewish official, for the safety of Emperor Severus Alexander and his mother Julia Mamaea, dating to Severus Alexander's reign (222-235 AD).

EDCS-26600622.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Culminari Aurelius Her<c=O>ulanus eques cohortis |(miliariae) Hemesenorum \n
EDCS-26600622.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, of the Summit, Aurelius Herculanus, a cavalryman of the 1st Cohort of Hemesenorum, offered this.
EDCS-26600622.summary: This is a dedication to the god Jupiter, made by a soldier of the 1st Cohort of Hemesenorum.

EDCS-26600623.text_edition: \n Libero Patri sacrum Calventius beneficiarius consularis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-26600623.translation: To Liber Pater, Calventius, a subordinate of the consular governor, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-26600623.summary: This is a votive inscription to Liber Pater, dedicated by Calventius, a subordinate official.

EDCS-26600625.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Antonio Gordiano Pio Felici Invicto Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate III patri patriae consuli proconsuli cohors I |(miliaria) Hemesenorum Gordiana sagittariorum equitata civium Romanorum numini eius devotissima\n
EDCS-26600625.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the 3rd time, Father of the Fatherland, Consul, Proconsul, the 1st Cohort of Hemesenorum, called Gordiana, the mounted archers, Roman citizens, most devoted to his divine will, set this up.
EDCS-26600625.summary: This is an inscription dedicated to Emperor Gordian III by the 1st Cohort of Hemesenorum, likely commemorating his reign or a specific event.

EDCS-26600627.text_edition: \n Marcus Aurelius Deisan domo Hemesa ve<t=I>(eranus) <e=I>x s<ta>tori tribuni cohortis I |(miliariae) H<e=P>mesenorum vivus sibi <e=I><t> viv<en=M>tibus Aureliis Monimo stratori et Regi<l=I>iano li<b=D>rario l<e=I>gati Iegionis II adiutricis filiis posuit et Aurelio Abigeneo qui vixit nnos XVII menses X <d>(ies) XVII et Aurelio Prisciano m<il=R>(iti) legionis II adiutricis nepotibus \n
EDCS-26600627.translation: Marcus Aurelius Deisan, from Hemesa, a veteran, standing tax collector, of the 1st Cohort of Hemesenorum, of the mounted 1st Cohort, Roman citizens, lived and dedicated this in his lifetime for Aurelius Monimus, a staff officer, and Regilianus, a clerk of the legate of Legio II Adiutrix, his sons, and for Aurelius Abigeneus, who lived 17 years, 10 months, and 17 days, and for Aurelius Priscianus, a soldier of Legio II Adiutrix, his grandsons.
EDCS-26600627.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by Marcus Aurelius Deisan for his sons and grandsons, with himself also being honored.

EDCS-26600628.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valerius Pusintulus protector qui vixit annos XXXVIIII in quadragensimo fatis animam reddidit heredes bon<o=I> patri memoriam fecerunt Andronico et Probo consulibus \n
EDCS-26600628.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Valerius Pusintulus, a protector, who lived 39 years and gave his soul to fate in his fortieth year, his heirs erected this memorial to their good father. In the consulship of Andronicus and Probus.
EDCS-26600628.summary: This is a tombstone recording the death of Valerius Pusintulus and erected by his heirs in the consulship of Andronicus and Probus.

EDCS-26600629.text_edition: \n Hoc lapide tegitur Aurelia Pia piissima coniux ex Nicia cives sic Bythynica sic originis orta ter undenos annos non plus adoleverat aetas florentem EDIIC matura vita perempta vos testor\n
EDCS-26600629.translation: Aurelia Pia, a most dutiful wife, born of Nicia, a citizen of Bythynia, is covered by this stone; her age had not yet reached eleven years; I call you to witness that she was taken by a premature death in her prime.
EDCS-26600629.summary: This is an epitaph for Aurelia Pia, who died at a young age, emphasizing her virtue and origin.

EDCS-26600635.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Teutano pro salute Imperatorum Marci Aureli Antonini et Luci Aureli Commodi et ordinis Aquincensium Titus Flavius Macer et Publius Aelius Ianuarius IIviri municipii eiusdem posuerunt III Idus Iunias Orfito et Rufo consulibus\n
EDCS-26600635.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, the Teutonic, for the safety of the Emperors Marcus Aurelius Antoninus and Lucius Aurelius Commodus, and for the order of Aquincum, Titus Flavius Macer and Publius Aelius Ianuarius, the two duumvirs of the same municipality, placed this. The Ides of June, in the consulship of Orfitus and Rufus.
EDCS-26600635.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter by the duumvirs of Aquincum for the safety of Emperors Marcus Aurelius Antoninus and Lucius Aurelius Commodus.

EDCS-26600636.text_edition: \n Sirmio optio cohortis I Alpinorum equitatae centuria Maximi annorum XLVI stipendiorum XXVI hic situs est titulum posuit Flavius Rufinus eques bucinator cohortis eiusdem heres ex testamento <f=T>(ecit) \n
EDCS-26600636.translation: Maximus of the century of Maximi, aged 46, with 26 years of service, an optio of the 1st Cohort of Alpinorum, cavalry, lies here. Flavius Rufinus, a cavalryman and bugler of the same cohort, his heir by will, erected this monument.
EDCS-26600636.summary: This is a tombstone for Maximus, an optio in the 1st Cohort of Alpinorum, erected by his heir.

EDCS-27600001.text_edition: \n Lucius Valerius Luci filius Papias domo ia nnorum LV hic situs est Lucius Valerius Germanus et Lucius Valerius Firmus heredes ex testamento faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-27600001.translation: Lucius Valerius Papias, son of Lucius, from Iannor, aged 55, lies here. Lucius Valerius Germanus and Lucius Valerius Firmus, his heirs by will, saw to the erection of this monument.
EDCS-27600001.summary: This is a tombstone for Lucius Valerius Papias, erected by his heirs.

EDCS-27600002.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Marcus Claudius CAEN
EDCS-27600002.summary: This is a fragmentary epitaph, likely for Marcus Claudius CAEN.

EDCS-27600003.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute Impperatorum Luci Septimi Severi et Marci Aureli Antonini [et Publi Septimi] Getae Caesaris Auggustorum nnostrorum Marcus Aurelius Priscinus speculator legionis II adiutricis\n
EDCS-27600003.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, for the safety of our Emperors Lucius Septimius Severus, Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, and Caesar Augustus Publius Septimius Geta, Marcus Aurelius Priscinus, a bodyguard of the Second Adiutrix Legion, [dedicated this].
EDCS-27600003.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter, Juno, and other deities for the safety of the emperors, set up by a soldier of the Second Adiutrix Legion, dating to the Severan period.

EDCS-27600004.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Iunoni Reginae pro salute domini nostri Augusti totiusque domus eius ISER Maximinus RI \n
EDCS-27600004.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Juno Regina, for the safety of our lord Augustus and his whole household, ISER Maximinus [dedicated this].
EDCS-27600004.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter and Juno for the safety of the emperor and his family, made by an individual named Maximinus, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-27600006.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico sacrum Septimius Longinus cornicularius legionis II adiutricis piae fidelis Severianae\n
EDCS-27600006.translation: Sacred to Silvanus Domestius. Septimius Longinus, a clerk of the Second Adiutrix Legion, Pious, Faithful, Severian.
EDCS-27600006.summary: This is a dedication to the god Silvanus, made by Septimius Longinus, an official of the Second Adiutrix Legion, likely from the Severan period.

EDCS-27600007.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quinti Postumi Lepidini praefecti equitum\n
EDCS-27600007.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed, for Quintus Postumius Lepidinus, a cavalry prefect.
EDCS-27600007.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Quintus Postumius Lepidinus, a cavalry prefect, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-27600009.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Retonius Danuvius Augustalis coloniae Aquincensium vivus sibi fecit\n
EDCS-27600009.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Caius Retonius Danuvius, an Augustalis of the colony of Aquincum, made this for himself while alive.
EDCS-27600009.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by Caius Retonius Danuvius, a prominent citizen of Aquincum, for himself during his lifetime.

EDCS-27600010.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cai Valeri Callimorphi natione Graeca sic Caius Valerius Ael<i=T>anus fratribus et sibi memoriae causa tumulum fecit opto sit tibi terra levis \n
EDCS-27600010.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Caius Valerius Aelianus, of Greek origin, made this tomb for his brothers and himself as a memorial; may the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-27600010.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Caius Valerius Aelianus for himself and his brothers, likely from the Roman imperial period, with a wish for a peaceful afterlife.

EDCS-27600014.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Titus Ba filius Pollia Efeso A legionis adiutricis centuria Iuli iri annorum XXX stipendiorum XII hic situs est\n
EDCS-27600014.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Titus Ba, son of [?], of the Pollia tribe, from Ephesus, a soldier in the Second Adiutrix Legion, serving in the first century under the centurion Iulius Irius, aged 30, with 12 years of service, lies here.
EDCS-27600014.summary: This is a tombstone for Titus Ba, a soldier in the Second Adiutrix Legion, detailing his age, service, and commander, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-27700106.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publio Aelio Iustino militi legionis II adiutricis exacto ad pra<e=A>t<oriu=ICO>m militavit ann \n
EDCS-27700106.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Publius Aelius Iustinus, a soldier of the Second Adiutrix Legion, served for [number] years, having been detailed to the prefect.
EDCS-27700106.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Publius Aelius Iustinus, a soldier of the Second Adiutrix Legion, noting his service and detail, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-27700305.text_edition: \n Iulius Macrinus Aurelius Pit<h=L>on Artius Quintus Caecilius Osor Pos Itonu Aurelius Bienn Iulius Euodus Aurelia Thaunura Flavia Thiodora sic Vibiariu<s=T> Proculus Aurelius Quintus Aurelius Rusticus Titinius Sabinus libertus Tita Solva Pithon NOA Aurelius Primus Augustalis Avit L \n
EDCS-27700305.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Iulius Macrinus Aurelius Pithon, Artius Quintus, Caecilius Osor, Pos Itonu Aurelius Bienn, Iulius Euodus, Aurelia Thaunura, Flavia Thiodora, and Vibiarius Proculus, Aurelius Quintus, Aurelius Rusticus, Titinius Sabinus, freedman of Tita Solva Pithon, NOA Aurelius Primus, Augustalis, Avit L [set this up].
EDCS-27700305.summary: This complex funerary monument records the names of numerous individuals, including freedmen and an Augustalis, likely from the Roman imperial period in Aquincum.

EDCS-27800691.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelio Messio veterano <e>x praetorio annorum LX Flav<i>a Ingenua coniugi pientissimo posuit hic situs est\n
EDCS-27800691.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Aelius Messius, a veteran from the praetorian guard, aged 60. Flavia Ingenua placed this for her most devoted husband. He lies here.
EDCS-27800691.summary: This is a tombstone erected by Flavia Ingenua for her husband, Aelius Messius, a veteran of the praetorian guard, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-28000241.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo pro sa<l=I>ute Imperatoris Hadriani Augusti votum Silvanus Tescoris fil(ius) miles cohortis vico Cuetrone \n
EDCS-28000241.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, for the health of the Emperor Hadrian Augustus, Silvanus, son of Tescoris, a soldier of the cohort, from the village of Cuetrone, fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-28000241.summary: A soldier from the village of Cuetrone dedicates a vow to Jupiter for Emperor Hadrian's health.

EDCS-28000245.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valeria Aemilia usu stolae <u=E>xor nata trium liberorum equitum Romanorum mater vixit nnos XXXVII Valerius Timotheus tribunus cohortis III Batavorum coniugi pientissimae \n
EDCS-28000245.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Valeria Emilia, wife, mother of three children, and mother of Roman equestrians, lived for 37 years. Valerius Timotheus, tribune of the III Cohort of Batavians, [erected this] for his most devoted wife.
EDCS-28000245.summary: A Roman tribune dedicates a tombstone to his wife, a mother of three, who died at age 37.

EDCS-28000246.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tito Aelio Karo veterano cohortis III Batavorum domo municipio Batavorum nnorum ianus \n
EDCS-28000246.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Tito Aelius Karo, a veteran of the III Cohort of Batavians, from the town of the Batavians, [lived] 72 years.
EDCS-28000246.summary: A tombstone records the life of Tito Aelius Karo, a veteran of the III Cohort of Batavians, who lived to 72.

EDCS-28000247.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Lucius Antonius Honoratus bene<f=B>(iciarius) consularis votum solvit libens merito Imperatore Commodo III consule \n
EDCS-28000247.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, Lucius Antonius Honoratus, a beneficarius consularis, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow, during the consulship of Emperor Commodus (III).
EDCS-28000247.summary: Lucius Antonius Honoratus, a beneficiarius consularis, dedicates a vow to Jupiter during the consulship of Commodus.

EDCS-28000276.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription possibly containing names.

EDCS-28000279.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Vergissa Magimari filius nnorum LXXXI hic situs est Appius filius patri pientissimo titulum memoriae posuit\n
EDCS-28000279.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Vergissa, son of Magimarus, lived 81 years and is buried here. Appius, his son, erected this monument for his most devoted father.
EDCS-28000279.summary: Appius erected a tombstone for his father Vergissa, who lived to 81.

EDCS-28100223.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno Lucius Iulius Quirina Passenianus praefectus pro sua salute et Marci Iulii Catuli patris votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28100223.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, Dolichenus, Lucius Julius Passenianus from the tribe Quirina, prefect, fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly for his own health and that of Marcus Julius Catulus, his father.
EDCS-28100223.summary: Lucius Julius Passenianus, a prefect, dedicates a vow to Jupiter Dolichenus for himself and his father.

EDCS-28100228.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute Imperatoris Marci Aureli Antonini Pii Augusti et Genio cohortis |(miliariae) Hemesenorum Antoninianae Titus Claudius Procus beneficiarius consularis legionis II adiutricis piae <f=E>idelis Antoninianae Imperatore Antonino IIII et Caelio Balbino II consulibus \n
EDCS-28100228.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, and to the Genius of the I Hemesenorum Antoniniana Cohort (miliaria), for the health of the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus, Titus Claudius Procus, beneficiarius consularis of the II Adiutrix Pia Fidelis Antoniniana Legion, during the consulship of Emperor Antoninus (IIII) and Caelius Balbinus (II), fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-28100228.summary: Titus Claudius Procus, a soldier from the II Adiutrix Legion, dedicates a vow to Jupiter and the cohort's Genius for the Emperor's health during the consulship of Antoninus and Balbinus.

EDCS-28100234.text_edition: \n AMALOGVERO domo Betavos sic eq(ues) ala Hispanorum O IN COLS et IC decurio et NO SVO heredes poserunt\n
EDCS-28100234.translation: AMALOGVERVS, from the Betavi tribe, an equestrian of the Ala Hispanorum (O IN COLS?), and decurion (?), and in his own right, his heirs erected [this].
EDCS-28100234.summary: Heirs erected a monument for Amalogverus, an equestrian and decurion from the Betavi tribe serving in the Ala Hispanorum.

EDCS-28100235.text_edition: \n Nertus Dumnotali sic filius veteranus ala Hispanorum I sesquiplicarius Lingauster nnorum LX stipendiorum XXXVI hic situs est Valens frater heres titulum memoriae posuit F---\n
EDCS-28100235.translation: Nertus, son of Dumnotalis, a veteran of the Ala Hispanorum, a sesquiplicarius, 60 years old, with 36 years of service, is buried here. Valens, his brother and heir, erected this monument for him [...]
EDCS-28100235.summary: Valens erected a tombstone for his brother Nertus, a 60-year-old veteran of the Ala Hispanorum with 36 years of service.

EDCS-28100236.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo dis deabusque Septimius Bauleus eques capsarius votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28100236.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus and the gods and goddesses, Septimius Bauleus, an eques capsarius, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-28100236.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter and other deities by Septimius Bauleus, an eques (knight) who served as a capsarius (a type of soldier or official), likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-28100237.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Augusto Iulius Taurus miles sesquip(licarius) eques votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28100237.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Augustus, Iulius Taurus, a soldier (sesquip(licarius)), an eques, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-28100237.summary: This votive inscription records Iulius Taurus, a soldier and eques, fulfilling a vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Augustus, probably from the Imperial period.

EDCS-28100238.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Marcus Aurelius Priscus signifer cohortis I |(milliariae) novae Severianae Surorum sagittariorum votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28100238.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Marcus Aurelius Priscus, standard-bearer of the I (milliaria) Nova Severiana cohort of Syrian archers, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-28100238.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter by Marcus Aurelius Priscus, a standard-bearer in a specific auxiliary cohort of archers, likely from the Severan period given the cohort's name.

EDCS-28100250.text_edition: \n missicius alae II Aravacorum nnorum LX Tiberius Claudius Coslicim \n
EDCS-28100250.translation: Tiberius Claudius Coslicim, a missicius (discharged soldier) of the II Aravacorum alae (cavalry unit), aged sixty years.
EDCS-28100250.summary: This inscription records the age of Tiberius Claudius Coslicim, a discharged soldier from the II Aravacorum cavalry unit, likely from the Roman military.

EDCS-28500317.text_edition: \n Clausa iacet lapidi coniunx pia cara Sabina artibus edocta superabat sola maritum vox ei grata fuit pulsabat pollice cordas set sic <c=G>ito rapta silet ter denos duxerat annos heu male quinque minus set sic plus tres menses habebat bis semptemque dies vixit haec ipsa superstes spectata in populo hydraula grata regebat sis felix quicumque leges te numina servent et pia voce cane Aelia Sabina vale Titus Aelius Iustus hydraularius salariarius legionis II adiutricis coniugi faciendum curavit \n
EDCS-28500317.translation: Here lies buried in stone my chaste and dear wife Sabina, who surpassed her husband in her skills alone; her voice was pleasing, she struck the strings with her finger, but alas, snatched away so quickly, she is silent. She had been married for thirty years, and five years less, but she lived three months more, and seven days more than that. She herself, surviving and respected in public, led the pleasing water organ. May you be happy, whoever reads this; may the gods protect you and may you sing with a pious voice. Farewell, Aelia Sabina. Titus Aelius Iustus, hydraularius (organ player) and salariarius (paid official) of the II Adjutrix legion, had this made for his wife.
EDCS-28500317.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Aelia Sabina, a talented hydraularius (water organ player), who died at the age of 38 years, 5 months, and 14 days, commissioned by her husband Titus Aelius Iustus, a member of the II Adjutrix legion, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28500851.text_edition: \n Publius A T RAQOF FPVSE POAT TRAD\n
EDCS-28500851.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription that appears to record a Roman personal name, Publius, with incomplete filiation or tribal information.

EDCS-28500854.summary: This is a very brief and fragmentary inscription, possibly recording ownership or a place name, with abbreviations.

EDCS-28500858.summary: This inscription records the name Lucius Cannutius, likely representing a person's name for funerary or ownership purposes.

EDCS-28500859.summary: This inscription records the name Domitius Maximini, possibly indicating ownership or belonging, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-28500860.summary: This inscription records the name Quinti Volusi Narcissi, likely indicating ownership or belonging, probably from the Roman period.

EDCS-28500861.summary: This inscription records the name of Titus Fadius Verus, likely a funerary or ownership marker.

EDCS-28500975.summary: This inscription records the abstract concept of 'Equity', possibly on a building or monument related to justice or fairness.

EDCS-28500976.summary: This inscription records 'Faith to Constantine', likely a dedication or honorific to an emperor named Constantine.

EDCS-28600008.text_edition: \n Cornelius Flavus signifer legionis XIIII geminae\n
EDCS-28600008.translation: Cornelius Flavus, standard-bearer of the Legio XIIII Gemina.
EDCS-28600008.summary: This inscription records the name and rank of Cornelius Flavus, a standard-bearer in the Legio XIIII Gemina, likely from his tombstone.

EDCS-28600009.text_edition: \n Parthenopeni Flavius Cogitatus signifer legionis II adiutricis \n
EDCS-28600009.translation: Flavius Cogitatus, standard-bearer of the Legio II Adjutrix, to Parthenopenus.
EDCS-28600009.summary: This inscription records Flavius Cogitatus, a standard-bearer in the Legio II Adjutrix, dedicating it to Parthenopenus, likely a funerary monument.

EDCS-28600010.text_edition: \n Quintus Frendullus Rufus Vicetinus vixit nnos XXXXIIX militavit nnos XXIII armorum custos\n
EDCS-28600010.translation: Quintus Frendullus Rufus Vicetinus lived 49 years, served 23 years, and was a weapons keeper.
EDCS-28600010.summary: This inscription records the life and military service of Quintus Frendullus Rufus Vicetinus, an armorum custos, likely on his tombstone.

EDCS-28600011.text_edition: \n Iuli sic Atheni sic liberto et heredi Iuli Crescentis quondam |(centurionis) legionis X geminae qui post obitum eius vixit annum I menses tres Iulia Iulia coniugi carissimo faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-28600011.translation: Iulia Iulia had this made for her dearest husband, Julius Crescentis, former centurion of the Legio X Gemina, who after his death lived for 1 year and 3 months, as well as for Athenis, his freedman and heir.
EDCS-28600011.summary: This inscription records a wife's commission of a tomb for her husband, a former centurion of Legio X Gemina, and his freedman heir, likely from the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-28600012.text_edition: \n Hic quiescunt sic duas sic matres duas sic filias sic numero tres faciunt et advenas II parvolas sic qui suscitabit cuius condicio est Iulius Herculanus veteranus faciendum curavit in re sua\n
EDCS-28600012.translation: Here rest two mothers, three daughters, and two visiting little girls; Julius Herculanus, a veteran, had this funerary monument made at his own expense for those who would raise them.
EDCS-28600012.summary: This inscription records the burial of two mothers, three daughters, and two visiting children, commissioned by a veteran named Julius Herculanus for their upkeep.

EDCS-28600013.text_edition: \n Caio Iulio C(ai)? f(ilio) Ommentiaeo militi legionis I adiutricis stipendiorum XXII et Flaviae Gemellae coniugi eius Iulius Ampliatus libertus patronis bene merentibus et Iulius Maximus miles legionis II adiutricis\n
EDCS-28600013.translation: To Gaius Julius Ommentianus, son of Gaius, of the Voltinia tribe, soldier of the Legio I Adjutrix with 22 years of service, and to his wife Flavia Gemella; Julius Ampliatus, freedman, for his well-deserving patrons; and Julius Maximus, soldier of the Legio II Adjutrix.
EDCS-28600013.summary: This inscription records the dedication to a soldier of Legio I Adjutrix and his wife, with additional mention of a freedman and a soldier of Legio II Adjutrix, likely a shared funerary monument.

EDCS-28600014.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulio Tatuloni militi legionis II adiutricis beneficiario tribuni fuit in expeditione bello desideratus et qui vixit nnos XLV Septi<mi=IR>a Quintilla coniux pientissima cum Probilla HERV et Quintilliano filio eorum ob memoriamIS et OM IMP II \n
EDCS-28600014.translation: To the Divine Shades of Julius Tatulo, soldier of the Legio II Adjutrix, a beneficiary of the tribune, who was lost on campaign in war, and who lived 45 years. His most devoted wife, Septimia Quintilla, with their son Probilla HERV and Quintillianus, for memory, and Ompl and Imp II.
EDCS-28600014.summary: This inscription records the death and age of Julius Tatulo, a soldier of Legio II Adjutrix, commissioned by his wife and son, and mentions military service and imperial honors, likely a tombstone from the late 1st or early 2nd century AD.

EDCS-28600015.text_edition: \n Caius Iulius Vius Donatus veteranus legionis II adiutricis a<n=V>(norum) <L=E>X collegium fabrum et centonariorum posuit \n
EDCS-28600015.translation: Gaius Julius Vius Donatus, a veteran of the II Adjutrix Legion, after V years of service, placed this [monument] for the college of craftsmen and fullers.
EDCS-28600015.summary: This is a dedication by a veteran and a guild to the college of craftsmen and fullers, likely from the Roman military period.

EDCS-28600016.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulius Victorinus |(centurio) legionis IIII Flaviae Sphinc\n
EDCS-28600016.translation: To the departed spirits. Julius Victorinus, a centurion of the IV Flavia Felix Legion, [died].
EDCS-28600016.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Julius Victorinus, a centurion in the IV Flavia Felix Legion, likely dating to the Roman military period.

EDCS-28600017.text_edition: \n ------] SSCISSIANI militi legionis II adiutricis armorum custodi et duplario stipendiorum XIIII nnorum XXXII Porolissenisi provincia Dacia <L=I>(ucius) Novius Provincialis miles legionis eiusdem heres fecit \n
EDCS-28600017.translation: To the departed spirits. To [name illegible] Sscissianus, a soldier of the II Adjutrix Legion, keeper of the armoury and a duplarius with 14 years of service and 32 years of age, from Porolissum in the province of Dacia. Lucius Novius Provincialis, a soldier of the same legion, made this as heir.
EDCS-28600017.summary: This tombstone records the life of Sscissianus, a soldier and armoury keeper in the II Adjutrix Legion, with his heir erecting the monument, dating to the Roman military period.

EDCS-28600018.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Memoriae quondam Quinti Numitori Felicis |(centurionis) legionis I aduitricis piae fidelis vixit annis LX stipendiorum XL Aelia Sabina coniugi pientissimo faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-28600018.translation: To the departed spirits. In memory of Quintus Numitorius Felix, formerly a centurion of the I Adjutrix Legion, pious and faithful, who lived for 60 years with 40 years of service. Aelia Sabina had this made for her most pious husband.
EDCS-28600018.summary: This is a tombstone for Quintus Numitorius Felix, a centurion in the I Adjutrix Legion, erected by his wife, dating to the Roman military period.

EDCS-28600019.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Pacato Mucaris militi legionis II adiutricis stupendiorum sic X vixit annis XXXV Bato Neritani commilitoni obsequentissimo et fratri ex testamento fieri curavit ex HS nummum DCCC\n
EDCS-28600019.translation: To the departed spirits. For Pacatus Mucaris, a soldier of the II Adjutrix Legion, with 10 years of service, who lived for 35 years. Bato of Neritani had this made by his will for his most obedient fellow soldier and brother, for 800 sesterces.
EDCS-28600019.summary: This tombstone is for Pacatus Mucaris, a soldier of the II Adjutrix Legion, erected by his brother and fellow soldier according to his will, dating to the Roman military period.

EDCS-28600020.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Petilius Firmus veteranus ex beneficiario praefecti legionis II adiutricis piae fidelis A Gaius Petilius <Fi=TE>rminianus et Petilia \n
EDCS-28600020.translation: To the departed spirits. Gaius Petilius Firmus, a veteran and former deputy prefect of the II Adjutrix Legion, pious and faithful. Gaius Petilius Firminianus and Petilia [erected this].
EDCS-28600020.summary: This tombstone honors Gaius Petilius Firmus, a veteran and former deputy prefect of the II Adjutrix Legion, with his heirs erecting the monument, dating to the Roman military period.

EDCS-28600021.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Gaio Pollio Gai filio domo colonia Aelia Mursa Martiano veterano legionis II adiutricis ex custode armorum qui vixit annos LX CCaii Pollii Marinianus et Marcellina et Martina filii et heredes patri piissimo faciendum curaverunt optamus sit tibi terra levis\n
EDCS-28600021.translation: To the departed spirits. For Gaius Pollio Gaius son, from the colony Colonia Aelia Mursa, a veteran of the II Adjutrix Legion, who served as armoury keeper and lived for 60 years. Gaius Pollus Marinianus and Marcellina and Martina, his sons and heirs, had this made for their most pious father. We wish the earth may be light upon you.
EDCS-28600021.summary: This tombstone is for Gaius Pollio, a veteran and former armoury keeper of the II Adjutrix Legion, erected by his sons and heirs, dating to the Roman military period.

EDCS-28600022.text_edition: \n Pompulenio Adaucto Pompulenius Iunius |(centurio) legionis II adiutricis liberto merentissimo posuit\n
EDCS-28600022.translation: Pompulenius Iunius, a centurion of the II Adjutrix Legion, erected this for his deserving freedman, Pompulenius Adauctus.
EDCS-28600022.summary: This is a dedication by a centurion of the II Adjutrix Legion for his freedman, dating to the Roman military period.

EDCS-28600024.text_edition: \n vixit annos XLVI Q Samius Sirmi legionis II adiutricis coniugi bene merenti et sibi et Claudiae Tiberinae et Liciniae Valentinae neptibus vivus sibi fecit \n
EDCS-28600024.translation: He lived for 46 years. Q. Samius of Sirmium, [erected this] for his well-deserving wife and for himself and for Claudia Tiberina and Licinia Valentina, his granddaughters, during his lifetime.
EDCS-28600024.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for a woman who lived 46 years, erected by her husband, Q. Samius, for himself and his granddaughters, dating to the Roman military period.

EDCS-28600025.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Septimia Valeria viva sibi et Septimiae Martinae filiae defunctae annorum XV item Caio Te Spectato veterano coniugi fecit\n
EDCS-28600025.translation: To the departed spirits. Septimia Valeria, while alive, made this for herself and for her deceased daughter Septimia Martina, aged 15, and also for her husband Gaius Te Spectatus, a veteran.
EDCS-28600025.summary: This tombstone is a self-funded monument by Septimia Valeria for herself, her deceased daughter, and her veteran husband, dating to the Roman military period.

EDCS-28600026.text_edition: \n Publio Tarrutenio Stellatina Proculo Taurinis evocato legionis II adiutricis stipendiorum XLVI annorum LXXVII hic situs est Quintus Cornelius Felix cornicularius praefecti legionis eiusdem heres ex testamento faciendum curavit et immunes et discentes\n
EDCS-28600026.translation: To Publius Tarrutenius Proculus of the Stellatina tribe, recalled into service ('evocatus') of the II Adjutrix Legion, with 46 years of service and aged 77, lies here. Quintus Cornelius Felix, chief clerk ('cornicularius') to the prefect of the same legion, his heir by will, saw to its construction, with exemption and (provision for) learners.
EDCS-28600026.summary: A funerary monument for Publius Tarrutenius Proculus, a recalled veteran of the II Adjutrix Legion, dating from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-28600027.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tito Torio Gemelliano quondam OC--- fabrum legionis II adiutricis et Toriae Geminae matri eius Aurelia Serena coniux et Toria Gemellina filia heredes vivi fecerunt Tit--- Au |(milia)\n
EDCS-28600027.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Tito Torio Gemellianus, formerly a craftsman ('faber') of the II Adjutrix Legion, and his mother, Toria Gemina. Aurelia Serena, his wife, and Toria Gemellina, his daughter, living heirs, had this made. Tit--- Au...
EDCS-28600027.summary: A tombstone for Tito Torio Gemellianus, a craftsman of the II Adjutrix Legion, and his mother, erected by his wife and daughter, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-28600028.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Valerius Luci Aniensi Crescens Vercellis miles legionis II adiutricis piae fidelis centuria Comini Paulini annorum XXXX stipendiorum XVIIII testamento fieri iussit heres faciendum curavit hic situs est\n
EDCS-28600028.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Lucius Valerius Crescens of the Aniensis tribe, son of Lucius, from Vercelli, a soldier of the II Adjutrix Legion, pious and faithful, of the century of Cominius Paulinus, aged 40, with 19 years of service, ordered this to be made in his will. His heir saw to its construction. Lies here.
EDCS-28600028.summary: A tombstone for Lucius Valerius Crescens, a soldier of the II Adjutrix Legion, who served for 19 years and died at age 40, erected as per his will, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-28600029.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Publio Valerio Heliodoro t<r=H>(ibuno legionis II adiutricis Lucius Publius Valerius Chilo filius eius \n
EDCS-28600029.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Lucius Publius Valerius Heliodorus, tribune of the II Adjutrix Legion. His son, Lucius Publius Valerius Chilo.
EDCS-28600029.summary: A funerary dedication for Lucius Publius Valerius Heliodorus, a tribune of the II Adjutrix Legion, made by his son, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-28600030.text_edition: \n Caio Valerio Cai filio Claudia Secundo Aemona veterano legionis II adiutricis annorum LX hic situs est collegium fabrum et centonariorum posuit\n
EDCS-28600030.translation: To Gaius Valerius, son of Gaius, from Aemona, a veteran of the II Adjutrix Legion, aged 60, lies here. The guild of craftsmen and 'centonarii' (firefighters/tent-makers) erected this.
EDCS-28600030.summary: A tombstone for Gaius Valerius, a veteran of the II Adjutrix Legion, erected by his guild of craftsmen and 'centonarii', likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-28600031.text_edition: \n Quinto Vatinio Adiutori quondam centurioni legionis II adiutricis honesta missione <m=V>(isso) Aelius Diodotus clarissimus vir heres <e=P>ius fecit \n
EDCS-28600031.translation: For Quintus Vatinis Adiutor, formerly a centurion of the II Adjutrix Legion, honourably discharged. Aelius Diodotus, a most distinguished man, his heir, built this piously.
EDCS-28600031.summary: A funerary monument for Quintus Vatinis Adiutor, an honourably discharged centurion of the II Adjutrix Legion, erected by his heir, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-28600032.text_edition: \n Hic sita sum matrona genus nomenque Veturia Fortunati coniux de patre Veturio nata ter novenos misera et nupta bis octo per annos unicuba uniiuga quae post sex partus uno superstite obii Titus Iulius Fortunatus |(centurio) legionis II adiutricis piae fidelis coniugi incomparabili et insigni in se pietate\n
EDCS-28600032.translation: I lie here, a matron. My name is Veturia, wife of Fortunatus, daughter of Veturius. Having been married thrice nine years (27), and a wife twice eight years (16) in one marriage, who after six childbirths, with one survivor, died. Titus Iulius Fortunatus, centurion of the II Adjutrix Legion, pious and faithful, to his incomparable and distinguished wife, remarkable for her devotion to him.
EDCS-28600032.summary: A tombstone for Veturia, wife of a centurion of the II Adjutrix Legion, detailing her marital status, children, and length of marriage, erected by her husband, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-28600033.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Ulpio Iustino militi TER legionis II VLL\n
EDCS-28600033.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Marcus Ulpius Iustinus, soldier of the II Legion [Adiutrix?], [V]L[L?]...
EDCS-28600033.summary: A partial funerary dedication for Marcus Ulpius Iustinus, a soldier of the II Legion, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-28600035.text_edition: \n miles legionis II adiutricis faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-28600035.summary: A fragment indicating that a soldier of the II Adjutrix Legion arranged for the construction of a monument, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-28600036.text_edition: \n Francus ego cives sic Romanus miles in armis egregia virtute tuli bello mea dextera semper\n
EDCS-28600036.translation: I, Francus, a Roman citizen, am a soldier in arms. I bore great bravery in war with my right hand always.
EDCS-28600036.summary: A personal inscription of a soldier named Francus, likely from the Roman imperial period, boasting of his military prowess.

EDCS-28600039.text_edition: \n Caius Cornelius Corinthus ob Augustalitatem splendidissimo sic templum sic IIiterum peperit\n
EDCS-28600039.translation: Gaius Cornelius Corinthus, because of his Augustales priesthood, provided this temple a second time with his splendid resources.
EDCS-28600039.summary: This inscription records a private individual, Gaius Cornelius Corinthus, who funded the rebuilding or restoration of a temple related to his prestigious Augustales office, likely in the Imperial period.

EDCS-28600040.text_edition: \n Caius Iulius Severus ob honorem magisterii collegii fabrum silanum pecunia sua fecit Muciano et Fa<b=D>iano consulibus \n
EDCS-28600040.translation: Gaius Julius Severus, on account of the magistracy of the guild of the Silanian craftsmen, paid for this from his own funds, with Mucianus and Fabianus as consuls.
EDCS-28600040.summary: This is a dedication by Gaius Julius Severus for the office of magistracy in the guild of Silanian craftsmen, funded by himself and dated to the consulship of Mucianus and Fabianus, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28600042.text_edition: \n OMV Caesernius Barba qui vixit annos X et Publiis et Clementiano et Quinto Publio X et Faventinae itemque Aureliae neptiae eius Caesernia a heres et coniux marito incomparabili et filiis \n
EDCS-28600042.translation: Omvius Caesernius Barba, who lived for 10 years, and to Publius, Clementianus, Quintus Publius, and Faventina, and also to his granddaughter Caesernia, his heir and wife, to her incomparable husband and sons.
EDCS-28600042.summary: This text appears to be a dedication on a tombstone for Omvius Caesernius Barba, who died at age 10, and mentions his family members including his heir and wife, Caesernia, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28600043.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cai Iuli Filetionis domo Africa medico qui vixit nnos XXXV Caius Iulius Filetus et Iulia Euthenia parentes filio karissimo faciendum curaverunt et Iulio Athenodoro fratri eius qui vixit nnos XXXV Euthenia IS ad quem sepultura collegium centonariorum Xdecem milia CCC contulit\n
EDCS-28600043.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed, for Caius Julius Filetion, from Africa, a doctor who lived 35 years. Caius Julius Filetus and Iulia Euthenia, his parents, arranged for this to be made for their dearest son, and for his brother, Julius Athenodorus, who lived 35 years. Euthenia, for whom the guild of carpet-makers granted 10,300 sesterces for burial.
EDCS-28600043.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Caius Julius Filetion, a doctor, and his brother Julius Athenodorus, both deceased at 35 years old, with their parents commissioning the monument and a guild contributing to the burial costs, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28600045.text_edition: \n CVM DECIAN MISTORI AEDI MAGISTRATVI Lucius Atticius Patrinus heres faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-28600045.translation: Lucius Atticius Patrinus, the heir, arranged for this to be made in honor of Decius Mistor, during his magistracy of the aedileship.
EDCS-28600045.summary: This inscription records Lucius Atticius Patrinus as the heir who commissioned a monument for Decius Mistor during his term as an aedile, suggesting a public or semi-public office, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28600046.text_edition: \n Marco Ulpio Marci fi<l=I>io Triclili--- nnorum LXX et Aelius Catulliinus nnorum XVIII hic situs Aelia Catta coniugi pientissimo et \n
EDCS-28600046.translation: For Marcus Ulpius Triclilius, son of Marcus, aged 70 years, and Aelius Catullinus, aged 18 years, who lies here. Aelia Catta to her most devoted husband and [to their children?].
EDCS-28600046.summary: This is a funerary inscription likely for Marcus Ulpius Triclilius and Aelius Catullinus, with Aelia Catta dedicating the monument to her husband, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28600047.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publius Albucius Publi filius Valentinus \n
EDCS-28600047.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed, for Publius Albucius Valentinus, son of Publius.
EDCS-28600047.summary: This is a simple funerary dedication to Publius Albucius Valentinus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28600049.text_edition: \n Flavius Philaenius Aureli- C NE coniugi karissim Aurelius Candidus IN VP N \n
EDCS-28600049.translation: For Flavius Philaenius, beloved husband of Aurelia. Aurelius Candidus [erected this?].
EDCS-28600049.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Flavius Philaenius by his wife Aurelia, with Aurelius Candidus possibly being the dedicator or intermediary, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28600050.text_edition: \n Claudianus Flavius A Aurelius Firmanus Valerius Luc Poblicius Optianus Flavius Quirinus Aurelius Maximus Aurelius Festi Aurelius Firminianus Aurelius Prima Flavius Victorinus Claudius cu Aurelius Valentinus Septimius Valentinus Septimius Maximianus Aurelius Montanus Aurelius Saturninus Aurelius Iustus Iulius Sabinianus QVE Aurelius Mercurialis Ulpius Salinator Antonius Marinianus [A]ur(elius) Maximus ex vo(to) pos(uerunt)? honori eorum posuerunt\n
EDCS-28600050.translation: Claudianus, Flavius Aurelius Firmanus, Valerius Lucian, Publicius Optianus, Flavius Quirinus, Aurelius Maximus, Aurelius Festus, Aurelius Firminianus, Aurelius Primus, Flavius Victorinus, Claudius, Aurelius Valentinus, Septimius Valentinus, Septimius Maximianus, Aurelius Montanus, Aurelius Saturninus, Aurelius Justus, Iulius Sabinianus, and Aurelius Mercurialis, Ulpius Salinator, Antonius Marinianus, [Aurelius] Maximus, have set this up from a vow in their honor.
EDCS-28600050.summary: This inscription records a group of individuals, many with Aurelian names, who collectively erected something, possibly a monument or shrine, as a fulfillment of a vow, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28600051.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Baebius Valens nnorum LX hic situs est Iulia Casta coniugi pientissimo et sibi viva titulum memoriae posuit\n
EDCS-28600051.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed, for Baebius Valens, aged 60 years, who lies here. Iulia Casta placed this title of memory for her most devoted husband and for herself while alive.
EDCS-28600051.summary: This funerary inscription records Iulia Casta dedicating a memorial for her husband, Baebius Valens, and also for herself during her lifetime, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28600052.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Battae Vibi filiae nnorum et Ressorae nnorum Memoris filiae hic sitae sunt et Sisiunae neptiae sic anniculae fecit Verbugia filia eius et sibi viva\n
EDCS-28600052.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Batta, daughter of Vibius, aged [unknown], and Ressora, daughter of Memoria, aged [unknown], lie here, and Sisiuna, granddaughter, a mere child. Verbugia, her daughter, made this for them and for herself while alive.
EDCS-28600052.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial place of Batta, Ressora, and Sisiuna, with the monument erected by Verbugia, who intended it for herself as well.

EDCS-28600053.text_edition: \n Brogimara Dallonis filia nnorum XXV et Iantuna filia annorum II hic sitae sunt Magio coniugi et filiae titulum memoriae posuit\n
EDCS-28600053.translation: Brogimara, daughter of Dallon, aged 25, and Iantuna, daughter, aged 2, lie here. Magius placed this monument in memory of his wife and daughter.
EDCS-28600053.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Brogimara and Iantuna, with the monument erected by Magius in memory of his wife and daughter.

EDCS-28600054.summary: This fragmentary inscription simply records the name and status of Servius Cornelius, a freedman.

EDCS-28600055.summary: This inscription records that Herennia Mar erected this monument during her lifetime.

EDCS-28600056.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulio Valentino vixit nnos XXX Iulia Sissa \n
EDCS-28600056.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Iulius Valentinus lived 30 years. Iulia Sissa [made this].
EDCS-28600056.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Iulius Valentinus, who lived 30 years, with the monument likely erected by Iulia Sissa.

EDCS-28600057.text_edition: \n Iulia Utta Epponis filia Florina natione Anartia annorum LXXXV hic sita est Cobromara Regilli filia matri titulum memoriae posuit\n
EDCS-28600057.translation: Iulia Utta Florina, daughter of Eppon, of the Anartian nation, aged 85, lies here. Cobromara, daughter of Regillus, placed this monument in memory of her mother.
EDCS-28600057.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Iulia Utta Florina, an Anartian woman aged 85, with the monument erected by her daughter Cobromara.

EDCS-28600058.text_edition: \n Aulus Lucanius Auli filius Lepidus nnorum VIII hic situs est Lucanius Capito et Kania Utsia parentes posuerunt\n
EDCS-28600058.translation: Aulus Lucanius Lepidus, son of Aulus, aged 8, lies here. Lucanius Capito and Kania Utsia, his parents, erected this.
EDCS-28600058.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Aulus Lucanius Lepidus, aged 8, with the monument erected by his parents.

EDCS-28600059.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lupo vixit annis III Alexandenr bene merenti\n
EDCS-28600059.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Lupos lived 3 years. For Alexander, who deserved well.
EDCS-28600059.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated to Lupos, who lived 3 years, and likely also commemorates Alexander, who deserved well.

EDCS-28600060.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marciae Meroes sic Fabia Secundina mammulae bene merenti\n
EDCS-28600060.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Marcia Meroes, who was called Fabia Secundina, a cherished little mother.
EDCS-28600060.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated to Marcia Meroes, also known as Fabia Secundina, described as a beloved 'little mother'.

EDCS-28600061.text_edition: \n Matsiu Tuionis filius nnorum XL hic situs est\n
EDCS-28600061.translation: Matsius, son of Tuion, aged 40, lies here.
EDCS-28600061.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Matsius, son of Tuion, aged 40.

EDCS-28600062.text_edition: \n liae vixit Rufinae filiae vixit annum I Mansuetae coniugi et Saturninae filiae vivis fecerunt\n
EDCS-28600062.translation: To Ilia, who lived for Rufina, his daughter, who lived for one year. Mansueta and Saturnina made this for them while they were alive.
EDCS-28600062.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Ilia and Rufina, set up by Mansueta and Saturnina.

EDCS-28600063.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Quintus Venn, husband.
EDCS-28600063.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Quintus Venn, set up by his wife, dated by the mention of his name.

EDCS-28600064.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Septimius Archelaus quondam \n
EDCS-28600064.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Lucius Septimius Archelaus, formerly.
EDCS-28600064.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Septimius Archelaus, likely set up by his family or heir.

EDCS-28600065.text_edition: \n Aulus Vettius Auli libertus Crescens nnorum L hic situs est Vettia Auli liberta Nome patrono et coniugi suo pientissimo viva titulum memoriae posuit Lucius Vipstanus Luci libertus Senecio amicus faciundum curavit\n
EDCS-28600065.translation: Aulus Vettius Crescens, freedman of Aulus, aged 50, lies here. Vettia Nome, freedwoman of Aulus, set up this tombstone for her patron and most devoted husband while alive. Lucius Vipstanus Senecio, freedman of Lucius, saw to its construction.
EDCS-28600065.summary: This tombstone was erected by Vettia Nome for her patron and husband, Aulus Vettius Crescens, with construction overseen by Lucius Vipstanus Senecio, likely dating to the 1st-2nd century AD.

EDCS-28600067.text_edition: \n M GAVALI Aelia Higia sic <p=E>ientissima coniux et filiae suae annorum XXX Laleae matri <p=R>iaentissimae ac <f=L>iliae suae annorum X \n
EDCS-28600067.translation: To Aelia Higia, a most devoted wife, and to her daughter, aged 30. To Laleia, mother, most devoted, and to her daughter, aged 10.
EDCS-28600067.summary: This funerary inscription honors Aelia Higia, her daughter, and Laleia and her daughter, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28600069.text_edition: \n Caio Lucilio Marciano quondam cornicu<l=I>(ario) legionis II adiutricis Caius Lucilius Marcianus iunior MAI COLEN V F EXS IN RVERES PANN INFE CI \n
EDCS-28600069.translation: To Gaius Lucilius Marcianus, formerly a horn player of the II Adjutrix Legion. Gaius Lucilius Marcianus the Younger [set up this monument]...
EDCS-28600069.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Lucilius Marcianus, a horn player of the II Adjutrix Legion, set up by his son, Gaius Lucilius Marcianus the Younger, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28600071.text_edition: \n IFRCE Vrosius et Aur(elia) Philumene parentes filiae carissimae faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-28600071.translation: Ifrce. Urosius and Aurelia Philumene, parents, arranged for this to be made for their dearest daughter.
EDCS-28600071.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a daughter, set up by her parents Urosius and Aurelia Philumene, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28600076.text_edition: \n MA Aurelius Iu et heres ob incomparabilem eius dum supervixit in se pietatem\n
EDCS-28600076.translation: To M. Aurelius Iu and his heir, for his incomparable piety towards him while he lived.
EDCS-28600076.summary: This dedication is to M. Aurelius Iu and his heir, expressing gratitude for piety, likely a personal dedication from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28600077.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Iunoni Reginae Minervae ceteris Dis Deabusque omnibus possessores vici Vindoniani ex voto pusuerunt quorum nomina sunt Aurelius Aepictetianus sacerdos Aurelius Vettianus equo publico Iulius Victorinus decurio Aurelius Candidianus dec(urio) Aurelius Ianuarius veteranus Aurelius Trofimus C Aurelius Androtanus -- Aurelius Maximus Antonius Quirinus III Idus Octobres Imperatore domino nostro Alexandro Severo Augusto III et Cassio Dione II consulibus item Marcus Aurelius Aepictetianus decurio coloniae Aquincensium sacerdotalis aram donavit in honorem vicanorum vici Vindoniani quae ara consecrata est in possessione Aureli Vettiani equitis Romani permissu eiusdem precario petentibus vicanis Vindoniani\n
EDCS-28600077.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Juno Regina, Minerva, and all the other Gods and Goddesses. The residents of the Vindoniana district set this up in fulfillment of a vow. Their names are: Aurelius Aepictetianus, priest; Aurelius Vettianus, equestrian of the public office; Iulius Victorinus, decurion; Aurelius Candidianus, decurion; Aurelius Ianuarius, veteran; Aurelius Trofimus; C. Aurelius Androtanus; Aurelius Maximus Antonius Quirinus. On the Ides of October, during the consulship of our lord Emperor Alexander Severus Augustus, the third time, and Cassius Dio, the second time. Also, Marcus Aurelius Aepictetianus, decurion of the colony of Aquincum, holding the priesthood, dedicated this altar in honor of the residents of the Vindoniana district, which altar was consecrated in the possession of Aurelius Vettianus, Roman equestrian, with his permission, at the request of the Vindoniana residents.
EDCS-28600077.summary: This is a dedication of a public altar by residents of the Vindoniana district of Aquincum to Jupiter, Juno, Minerva, and other gods, dated to October 13, 250 AD, during the consulship of Alexander Severus and Cassius Dio.

EDCS-28600078.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Canius Otiorix m<i=E>les legionis II adiutricis qui defunctus est in Parthia titulum fecit Canius Speratus filius suus sibi vivo et coniugi vivae \n
EDCS-28600078.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Canius Otiorix, soldier of the II Adjutrix Legion, who died in Parthia. Canius Speratus, his son, made this monument for himself while alive and for his wife while alive.
EDCS-28600078.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Canius Otiorix, a soldier of the II Adjutrix Legion who died in Parthia, set up by his son, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28600079.text_edition: \n Marbu XX titulum faciendum curavit V et coniugi \n
EDCS-28600079.translation: Marbu saw to the making of this monument for himself and his wife.
EDCS-28600079.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Marbu for himself and his spouse, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28600080.text_edition: \n et Marciae Otaciliae Severae sanctissimae Augustae coniugi Augusti nostri \n
EDCS-28600080.translation: and for Marcia Otacilia Severa, the most holy Augusta, wife of our Augustus.
EDCS-28600080.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Marcia Otacilia Severa, wife of the Emperor Philip the Arab, likely from the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-28600081.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Marcus Ulpius Pusinnio |(centurio) exploratorum votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28600081.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, Marcus Ulpius Pusinnio, a centurion of scouts, fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-28600081.summary: This is a votive inscription from Marcus Ulpius Pusinnio, a centurion of scouts, dedicating a fulfillment of a vow to Jupiter, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28600083.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Aurelius Firminus veteranus solvit votum libens merito\n
EDCS-28600083.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, Aurelius Firminus, a veteran, fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-28600083.summary: This is a votive inscription from Aurelius Firminus, a veteran, dedicating a fulfillment of a vow to Jupiter, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28600085.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico Aelia Clita mater votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28600085.translation: To Silvanus Domesticius, Aelia Clita, a mother, fulfilled her vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-28600085.summary: This is a votive inscription from Aelia Clita, a mother, dedicating a fulfillment of a vow to Silvanus Domesticius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28600087.text_edition: \n co o votum otatus est pro salute sua et Aureliaes sic Valentinaes! et Aurelio Alexandro et Aurel \n
EDCS-28600087.translation: … fulfilled his vow for his own health and that of Aurelia Valentina and Aurelius Alexander and Aurelia.
EDCS-28600087.summary: This fragment of a votive inscription records the fulfillment of a vow for the health of several individuals, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28600089.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelio Victorino nnorum XXX stipendiorum XIII duplicario alae I Thracum veteranae et Aelio Liciniano annorum XII filiis pientissimis Aelia Flavina infelicissima mater et sibi viva posuit\n
EDCS-28600089.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed, for Aelius Victorinus, aged 30, with 13 years of service, a duplicarius of the I Thracum Veterana ala, and for Aelius Licinianus, aged 12, his most devoted sons, Aelia Flavina, his most unhappy mother, erected this monument for herself while alive.
EDCS-28600089.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Aelia Flavina for her deceased sons, Aelius Victorinus and Aelius Licinianus, and herself, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28600090.text_edition: \n Aurelio Maximo veterano <l=I>egionis II adiutricis patri et Aeliae Primae matri et Aeliae Ressillae aviae Aurelia Ingenua filia parentibus karissimis titulum de suo faciendum curavit Aurelius Valens miles legionis II adiutricis socris benignissimis \n
EDCS-28600090.translation: Aurelia Ingenua, daughter, had this monument made at her own expense for her most beloved parents, Aurelius Maximus, a veteran of the Legio II Adjutrix, and Aelia Prima, mother, and Aelia Ressilla, grandmother; Aurelius Valens, a soldier of the Legio II Adjutrix, for his most benevolent father-in-law.
EDCS-28600090.summary: This funerary monument was commissioned by Aurelia Ingenua for her parents and grandmother, and by Aurelius Valens for his father-in-law, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28600091.text_edition: \n Titus Flavius Bonio Quirina Andautonia eques alae Frontonianae turma Ingenui annorum XXXIIII stipendiorum XVI hic situs est Campanus S eques alae eiundem sic heres titulum memoriae posuit\n
EDCS-28600091.translation: Titus Flavius Bonio Quirinus Andautonia, a cavalryman of the Frontoniana ala, troop of Ingenuus, aged 34, with 16 years of service, lies here; Campanus S., a cavalryman of the same ala, his heir, erected this monument in memory.
EDCS-28600091.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Titus Flavius Bonio Quirinus Andautonia, a cavalryman, erected by his heir, Campanus S., likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28600093.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tito Flavio Titi filio Quirina Severo Gogaeno annorum LVIII decurio coloniae Sirmi quaestori IIviro praefecto fabrum hic situs est Flavia Severina filia patri pientissimo posuit\n
EDCS-28600093.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed, for Titus Flavius Severus Gogaenus, son of Titus, of the Quirina tribe, aged 58, a decurion of the colony of Sirmium, quaestor, IIvir, prefect of the engineers, lies here; Flavia Severina, his daughter, erected this monument for her most devoted father.
EDCS-28600093.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Titus Flavius Severus Gogaenus, a prominent official in the colony of Sirmium, erected by his daughter, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28600095.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus DI Rosionis XXXXXF et Atresso filio eius XX Surus cum fratribus parentibus simis posuit\n
EDCS-28600095.translation: To the departed spirits. Surus, along with his brothers and devoted parents, erected this monument to his father, Rosio, son of XXXXX, and his brother, Atresso.
EDCS-28600095.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by Surus and his brothers to his father and brother, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28600097.text_edition: \n Comatum ara Usionis filia annorum XXXVI de proprio titulum posuit hic est sita\n
EDCS-28600097.translation: Comata, daughter of Usio, aged 36, erected this tombstone at her own expense; she is buried here.
EDCS-28600097.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by Comata for herself, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28600098.text_edition: \n Saetibolus annorum XIII et Quintus annorum VII hic situs est sic Magissaes fecit parens titulum posuit memoriae\n
EDCS-28600098.translation: Saetibolus, aged 13, and Quintus, aged 7, are buried here. Magissaes made this and erected the tombstone in memory.
EDCS-28600098.summary: A funerary epitaph commemorating two young children, Saetibolus and Quintus, erected by Magissaes, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28600102.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Hadriani filius divi Traiani Parthici nepos divi Nervae pronepos Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Pius pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate \n
