EDCS-28600383.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to Silvanus Domesticius by Aurelius Phylargus for himself and his family, likely in Aquincensium.

EDCS-28600384.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico |(sacrum) Cornelius Genetivos sic decurio ex singularibus votum libens merito II Silanis consulibus\n
EDCS-28600384.translation: Sacred to Silvanus Domesticius, Cornelius Genetivos, decurion of the Singulares, paid his vow willingly and deservedly, under the consulship of the two Silani.
EDCS-28600384.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to Silvanus Domesticius by Cornelius Genetivos, a decurion of the Singulares, during the consulship of the Silani (likely AD 194).

EDCS-28600385.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico Gaius Secundinius Sabinianus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28600385.translation: Gaius Secundinius Sabinianus willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Silvanus Domesticus.
EDCS-28600385.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to Silvanus Domesticus by Gaius Secundinius Sabinianus.

EDCS-28600387.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico sacrum Lucius Seranus <E=F>rotius IIIIIIvir aedilis decurio co<l=I>(oniae) Aquincensium votum solvit libens merito \n
EDCS-28600387.translation: Lucius Seranus, a sextumvir, aedile, and decurion of Colonia Aquincensium, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Silvanus Domesticus.
EDCS-28600387.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to Silvanus Domesticus by Lucius Seranus, an official in Colonia Aquincensium.

EDCS-28600388.text_edition: \n Silvano Herbario Septimia Constantina votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28600388.translation: Septimia Constantina willingly and deservedly fulfilled her vow to Silvanus Herbarus.
EDCS-28600388.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to Silvanus Herbarus by Septimia Constantina.

EDCS-28600389.text_edition: \n Silvano Silvestro sic Publius Aelius Capito votum solvit libens merito Imperatore Antonino Augusto II et Advento consulibus\n
EDCS-28600389.translation: Publius Aelius Capito willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Silvanus Silvestris, under the consulship of Emperor Antoninus Augustus II and Adventus.
EDCS-28600389.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to Silvanus Silvestris by Publius Aelius Capito, dated to the consulship of Antoninus Augustus II and Adventus.

EDCS-28600390.text_edition: \n Silvano Silvestri sacrum Corinthus nummularius votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28600390.translation: Sacred to Silvanus Silvestris; Corinthus, a money-changer, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-28600390.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to Silvanus Silvestris by Corinthus, a money-changer.

EDCS-28600391.text_edition: \n Silvano Silvestri sacrum Tiberius Iulius Masculus princeps legionis II adiutricis piae fidelis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28600391.translation: Tiberius Iulius Masculus, chief of the Second Adjutrix Legion, pious and faithful, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Silvanus Silvestris.
EDCS-28600391.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to Silvanus Silvestris by Tiberius Iulius Masculus, a military officer.

EDCS-28600392.text_edition: \n Silvano Silvestri pro inpedimentis |(centuriae) Iuli Alexandri Licinius Candidus dono dedit\n
EDCS-28600392.translation: Licinius Candidus gave this gift to Silvanus Silvestris for the impedimenta (supplies or equipment) of the century of Iulius Alexander.
EDCS-28600392.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Silvanus Silvestris for the supplies of a military century by Licinius Candidus.

EDCS-28600393.text_edition: \n Silvano Silvestri Septimius Firmanus candidatus domini nostri pro salute sua votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28600393.translation: Septimius Firmanus, a candidate, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Silvanus Silvestris for his own health.
EDCS-28600393.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to Silvanus Silvestris for the health of Septimius Firmanus, a candidate.

EDCS-28600394.text_edition: \n Silvano Silvestro sic Valerius Maximianus optio votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28600394.translation: Valerius Maximianus, an optio, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Silvanus Silvestris.
EDCS-28600394.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to Silvanus Silvestris by Valerius Maximianus, a military optio.

EDCS-28600395.text_edition: \n Volcano sacrum veterani et cives Romani consistentes ad legionem II adiutricem curam agentibus Valerio Respecto et Utedio Maximino ma<g=C>(istris) \n
EDCS-28600395.translation: Sacred to Vulcan; the veterans and Roman citizens residing at the Second Adjutrix Legion, with Valerius Respectus and Utedius Maximino serving as magistrates.
EDCS-28600395.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Vulcan by veterans and Roman citizens associated with the Second Adjutrix Legion.

EDCS-28600396.text_edition: \n Libero et Liberae pro salute Augusti et aeternitatis sic imperii in honorem Marci Minici Martialis e(quo) publico Marcus Minicius Hermaiscus locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-28600396.translation: To Liber and Libera, for the health of Augustus and the eternal reign, in honor of Marcus Minicius Martialis, a public place was granted from public funds by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-28600396.summary: This inscription records the granting of a public space by decree of local officials to honor Marcus Minicius Martialis, dedicated to the gods Liber and Libera for the well-being of the emperor.

EDCS-28600397.text_edition: \n Fortunae Conservatrici et Bonae Deae Iunoni Aelia Theodora votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28600397.translation: Aelia Theodora willingly and deservedly fulfilled her vow to Fortuna Conservatrix and Bona Dea Iuno.
EDCS-28600397.summary: This inscription records a personal votive offering by Aelia Theodora to the goddesses Fortuna Conservatrix and Bona Dea Iuno.

EDCS-28600400.text_edition: \n ] Marcus Aurelius Licinius adiutor officii consularis cum suis votum solvit libens merito Imperatore domino nostro Alexandro Augusto III et Dione consulibus\n
EDCS-28600400.translation: Marcus Aurelius Licinius, assistant to the consular office, with his dependents, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow, during the reign of our lord Alexander Augustus III and with Dion as consul.
EDCS-28600400.summary: This inscription records a personal vow fulfilled by Marcus Aurelius Licinius, an administrative assistant, during the consulship of Alexander and Dion.

EDCS-28600403.text_edition: \n sacrum Sextus Pomius curator veteranorum legionis XIII <g=C>(eminae) \n
EDCS-28600403.translation: Sacred to the veterans of the 13th Gemina Legion, Sextus Pomius, curator.
EDCS-28600403.summary: This is a sacred dedication to the veterans of the 13th Gemina Legion, commemorating Sextus Pomius as curator.

EDCS-28600404.text_edition: \n Sedes A Atilia Cai filia Prisca pro se suisque votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28600404.translation: Aetia Prisca, daughter of Caius, fulfilled her vow willingly and deservedly for herself and her dependents.
EDCS-28600404.summary: This inscription records a personal vow fulfilled by Aetia Prisca for herself and her family.

EDCS-28600411.text_edition: \n ] pro salute ddominorum nnostrorum Maximiano Iovio Invict<o=I> Augusto et Maximino nobilissimo Caesari Iulius Valerianus et Aurelius Maximus dduumvviri coloniae Aquincensium \n
EDCS-28600411.translation: For the health of our lords Maximianus Iovius Invictus Augustus and Maximinus Nobilissimus Caesar, Iulius Valerianus and Aurelius Maximus, duoviri of the colony of Aquincensium.
EDCS-28600411.summary: This inscription records a dedication for the health of the emperors Maximianus and Maximinus by the duoviri of the colony of Aquincensium.

EDCS-28600412.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Aurelio Fideli ex praepositis qui vixit nnos LX coniugi maerentissimo sic Valeria Decorata faciendum <c=Q>uravit \n
EDCS-28600412.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Valeria Decorata had this made for Marcus Aurelius Fidelis, formerly prefect, who lived 60 years, her most grieving husband.
EDCS-28600412.summary: This funerary inscription records the memorial erected by Valeria Decorata for her deceased husband, Marcus Aurelius Fidelis, a former prefect.

EDCS-28600413.text_edition: \n Schola speculatorum legionum I et II adiutricium piarum fidelium Severianarum refecta per eosdem quorum nomina infra scripta sunt dedicante Flavio Aeliano legato Augusti pro praetore Kalendis Octobribus Modesto et Probo consulibus Aelius Optatus Aelius Candidus Macrius Sabinianus Iulius Secundianus Iulius Emeritus Cassius Fuscinus Iulius Probinus Aelius Urbanus Publius Martinus Valerius Festus Iulius Alexander Flavius Emeritus Valerius Clementinus Iulius Priscanus Ver Verissimus Iulius Ingenuus Iulius Celerinus Iunius Adiutor Aurelius Lupus Flavius Celsinus curante Aurelio Pertinace frumentario\n
EDCS-28600413.translation: The barracks of the speculatores of the 1st and 2nd Adjutrix Pious Faithful Severian Legions, repaired by the same individuals whose names are written below, dedicated by Flavius Aelianus, legate of the emperor with praetorian power, on the Kalends of October, with Modestus and Probus as consuls. Aelius Optatus, Aelius Candidus, Macrius Sabinianus, Iulius Secundianus, Iulius Emeritus, Cassius Fuscinus, Iulius Probinus, Aelius Urbanus, Publius Martinus, Valerius Festus, Iulius Alexander, Flavius Emeritus, Valerius Clementinus, Iulius Priscanus, Ver Verissimus, Iulius Ingenuus, Iulius Celerinus, Iunius Adiutor, Aurelius Lupus, Flavius Celsinus, under the supervision of Aurelius Pertinax, the grain official.
EDCS-28600413.summary: This inscription details the repair and dedication of the barracks for the speculatores of two legions, listing numerous individuals involved and mentioning the governor Flavius Aelianus and the grain official Aurelius Pertinax, dating it to the consulship of Modestus and Probus.

EDCS-28600414.text_edition: \n Thermas maiores legionis II adiutricis Claudianae magno tempore intermissas et dest<i=L>tutas retracatis porticibus aditibusque prius refecit exhiberi inde incepisse militibus iussit pridie Kalendas Iulias praeside provinciae egregio viro Clementio Silvino et cura Aureli Frontini praefecti legionis eiusdem Paterno II et Mariniano consulibus \n
EDCS-28600414.translation: The main baths of the 2nd Adjutrix Claudiana Legion, which had been long interrupted and neglected, with their porticoes and entrances repaired, were ordered by him to be opened for the soldiers on the day before the Kalends of July, under the presidency of the excellent provincial governor Clementius Silvinus, and by the care of Aurelius Frontinus, prefect of the same legion, with Paternus II and Marinianus as consuls.
EDCS-28600414.summary: This inscription records the repair and reopening of the main baths for the 2nd Adjutrix Claudiana Legion, ordered by the prefect Aurelius Frontinus under the governor Clementius Silvinus, during the consulship of Paternus II and Marinianus.

EDCS-28600415.text_edition: \n Excubitorium ad tutelam signorum et imaginum sacrarum Publius Turranius Firminus veteranus ex corniculario legionis II adiutricis Antoninianae pecunia sua a solo restituit Sabino II et Anullino consulibus\n
EDCS-28600415.translation: The guardhouse for the protection of sacred standards and images, Publius Turranius Firminus, a veteran formerly of the cornicularii of the 2nd Adjutrix Antoniniana Legion, restored from the ground up at his own expense, during the consulship of Sabinus II and Anullinus.
EDCS-28600415.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a guardhouse by Publius Turranius Firminus, a veteran of the 2nd Adjutrix Antoniniana Legion, funded by himself, during the consulship of Sabinus II and Anullinus.

EDCS-28600416.text_edition: \n P Publio Aelio Iusto nnorum III Aeliae Verinae nnum VII Publius Aelius Provincialis veteranus legionis II adiutricis avos sic et Publius Aelius Provincialis sevir coloniae Aquincensis et Aelia Concordia parentes filiis dulcissimis fecerunt\n
EDCS-28600416.translation: To Publius Aelius Justus, aged 3 years, and Aelia Verina, aged 7 years. Publius Aelius Provincialis, a veteran of the II Adjutrix Legion, and Publius Aelius Provincialis, a *sevir* of the colony of Aquincum, and Aelia Concordia, parents, made this for their dearest children.
EDCS-28600416.summary: A funerary monument erected by parents for their young children, one of whom was the son of a veteran and *sevir* of Aquincum.

EDCS-28600417.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publius Aelius Claudia Lusimacus sic Th<e=F>ssalonicia miles legionis II adiutricis annorum XXX stipendiorum XIII centuria Ulpi Etrusci ETVS BITVS M \n
EDCS-28600417.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Publius Aelius Claudia Lusimacus, a soldier of the II Adjutrix Legion, aged 30 years, with 13 years of service, in the century of Ulpius Etruscus.
EDCS-28600417.summary: A funerary inscription for Publius Aelius Claudia Lusimacus, a soldier of the II Adjutrix Legion, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28600418.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus memoriae Publi Aeli Martialis quondam veterani ex custode armorum legionis II adiutricis patris et Flaviae Agathes sic matris Aelius Aelianus praefectus legionis supra scriptae protector Augusti parentibus carissimis regressus ad Lares patrios faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-28600418.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld and in memory of Publius Aelius Martialis, formerly a guard of the arms of the II Adjutrix Legion, and of Flavia Agatha, his mother. Aelius Aelianus, prefect of the aforementioned legion and *protector Augusti*, had this made for his most cherished parents upon his return to the ancestral hearth.
EDCS-28600418.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by Aelius Aelianus, a prefect and *protector Augusti* of the II Adjutrix Legion, for his deceased parents, Publius Aelius Martialis and Flavia Agatha.

EDCS-28600419.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publius Aelius Publi filius Maecia Mestrius Pelagonia optio legionis II adiutricis centuria Attei Dextri annorum XXXVIIII stipendiorum XVIIII hic situs est optiones legionis eiusdem faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-28600419.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Publius Aelius, son of Publius, of the Maecia tribe, Mestrius Pelagonia, an *optio* of the II Adjutrix Legion, in the century of Atteius Dexter, aged 39 years, with 19 years of service, lies here. The *optiones* of the same legion had this made.
EDCS-28600419.summary: A funerary inscription for Publius Aelius, an *optio* in the II Adjutrix Legion, erected by his colleagues and dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28600421.text_edition: \n Memoriae quondam Marci Aureli Augustalis coloniae Aquinci vixit nnos XLI MMarci Aureli Augendus et Amandus liberti et heredes ex voluntate testamenti curantibus Decimio Dionisio Augustale coloniae supra scriptae et Aurelio Papia veterano legionis II adiutricis faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-28600421.translation: In memory of Marcus Aurelius Augustalis of the colony of Aquincum, who lived 41 years. His freedmen and heirs, Marcus Aurelius Augendus and Amandus, by his will, had this made, under the care of Decimius Dionysius, an Augustalis of the aforementioned colony, and Aurelius Papia, a veteran of the II Adjutrix Legion.
EDCS-28600421.summary: A funerary monument for Marcus Aurelius Augustalis of Aquincum, erected by his freedmen and heirs and overseen by local officials.

EDCS-28600422.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelio Antonino signifero legionis II adiutricis stipendiorum XVIII qui vixit annos XLV Septimia Lupa coniux et Aurelia Antonia filia curante Aurelium Aristomenetem libertum eorum faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-28600422.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Aurelius Antoninus, a standard-bearer (*signifer*) of the II Adjutrix Legion, with 18 years of service, who lived 45 years. Septimia Lupa, his wife, and Aurelia Antonia, his daughter, had this made, under the care of their freedman Aurelius Aristomenes.
EDCS-28600422.summary: A funerary monument for Aurelius Antoninus, a standard-bearer of the II Adjutrix Legion, erected by his wife and daughter.

EDCS-28600423.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Aurelius Florentinus benefciarius consularis qui vixit annis XXXIII Maesia Canpanil<l=I>a coniugi carissimo faciendum curavit \n
EDCS-28600423.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Maesia Canpanilla had this made for her most devoted husband, Marcus Aurelius Florentinus, a *beneficiarius consularis*, who lived 33 years.
EDCS-28600423.summary: A funerary monument dedicated by Maesia Canpanilla to her husband, Marcus Aurelius Florentinus, a *beneficiarius consularis*.

EDCS-28600424.text_edition: \n Marcus Aurelius Maximus veteranus cohortis equitatae ex corn<i=V>culario sibi et Aeliae Vconiugi pien<t=I>issimae vivis et Aeliae Maximaeae \n
EDCS-28600424.translation: Marcus Aurelius Maximus, veteran of an equestrian cohort, formerly a *cornicularius*, for himself and for his most devoted wife, Aelia Vco, and Aelia Maximaea, while alive.
EDCS-28600424.summary: A funerary monument erected for himself and his wife by Marcus Aurelius Maximus, a veteran and former *cornicularius*, and possibly their daughter.

EDCS-28600425.text_edition: \n ------] Hermiae medico legionis IIII Flaviae marito pientissimo Aurelia Mama uxor infelicissima \n
EDCS-28600425.translation: [In memory] of Hermia, physician of the IV Flavia Legion, for her most devoted husband.
EDCS-28600425.summary: A funerary dedication by a wife for her husband, who was a physician in the IV Flavia Legion.

EDCS-28600426.text_edition: \n Memoriae quondam Marci Aureli Melliti librarii legati legionis II adiutricis vixit nnos XVIII stipendiorum IIII Marcus Aurelius Martialis veteranus ex signifero legionis supra scriptae filio dulcissimo faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-28600426.translation: In memory of Marcus Aurelius Mellitus, scribe of the legate of the II Adjutrix Legion, who lived 18 years, with 4 years of service. Marcus Aurelius Martialis, a veteran and former standard-bearer of the aforementioned legion, had this made for his dearest son.
EDCS-28600426.summary: A funerary monument for Marcus Aurelius Mellitus, a scribe and son of a veteran standard-bearer of the II Adjutrix Legion.

EDCS-28600427.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Dis Manibus Aureliae Bonosae coniugi pientissimae quae vixit annis XXXV Valerius Valerianus |(centurio) legionis bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-28600427.translation: To the divine shades. Valerius Valerianus, a centurion of the legion, placed this for his most devoted wife Aurelia Bonosa, who lived 35 years, as she deserved.
EDCS-28600427.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aurelia Bonosa, set up by her husband Valerius Valerianus, a centurion, and likely dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-28600428.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus memoriae quondam Aureliae Martiae domo Aquinco vixit nnos XLI dies IIII Marcus Ulpius Dasianus veteranus legionis II adiutricis coniugi karissimae faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-28600428.translation: To the divine shades. For the memory of Aurelia Marcia, formerly of Aquincum, who lived 41 years and 4 days. Marcus Ulpius Dasianus, a veteran of the II Adiutrix legion, had this made for his dearest wife.
EDCS-28600428.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aurelia Marcia, set up by her husband Marcus Ulpius Dasianus, a veteran of the II Adiutrix legion, and likely dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-28600429.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelia Valentina quae vixit nnos X menses V dies V G Lucidus legionis II adiutricis MIVA VAR SEX A vivus coniugi benignissimae\n
EDCS-28600429.translation: To the divine shades. Aurelia Valentina, who lived 10 years, 5 months, and 5 days. G. Lucidus of the II Adiutrix legion had this made for his most benevolent wife while still alive.
EDCS-28600429.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aurelia Valentina, set up by her husband G. Lucidus, a soldier of the II Adiutrix legion, and likely dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-28600430.text_edition: \n Aureliae Victorinae quae vixit nnis XVII mensibus VI diebus X Aurelius Viator miles cohortis |(milliariae) Maurorum coniugi karissimae\n
EDCS-28600430.translation: For Aurelia Victorina, who lived 17 years, 6 months, and 10 days. Aurelius Viator, a soldier of the Maurorum milliary cohort, for his dearest wife.
EDCS-28600430.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aurelia Victorina, set up by her husband Aurelius Viator, a soldier, and likely dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-28600431.text_edition: \n Lucio Cassio Pudenti quondam militi legionis II adiutricis adiutori officii corniculariorum consularis stipendiorum XIII qui vixit nnos XXXII Lucius Cassius Regulianus veteranus legionis II adiutricis ex beneficiario consularis pater infelicissimus filio piissimo faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-28600431.translation: Lucius Cassius Regulianus, a veteran of the II Adiutrix legion, a former subordinate of the cornicularii of the consular staff, who served 13 years and lived 32 years, had this made for his most dutiful son, Lucius Cassius Pudentus, formerly a soldier of the II Adiutrix legion, a former subordinate of the consular staff.
EDCS-28600431.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Cassius Pudentus, set up by his father Lucius Cassius Regulianus, a veteran of the II Adiutrix legion, and likely dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-28600432.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tito Claudio Efficaci quondam militi legionis II sic piae fidelis beneficiario consularis domo Luceria Ap<ul=I>a qui vixit nnos XXXVII Calpurnia Hermione coniu<g=C>i bene merentisisimo fecit \n
EDCS-28600432.translation: To the divine shades. Calpurnia Hermione made this for her most deserving husband, Titus Claudius Efficax, formerly a soldier of the II legion, pious and loyal, a subordinate of the consular staff, from Luceria Apulia, who lived 37 years.
EDCS-28600432.summary: A funerary epitaph for Titus Claudius Efficax, set up by his wife Calpurnia Hermione, and likely dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-28600433.text_edition: \n Claudius veteranus ex cohorte |(milliaria) Maurorum vixit annos LX Claudius Vitalianus miles cohortis novae filius et Flavia Marcella coniux posuit sic Aelius Iucundianus Aelius Valerianus milites\n
EDCS-28600433.translation: Claudius, a veteran from the Maurorum milliary cohort, lived 60 years. Claudius Vitalianus, a soldier of the new cohort, and Flavia Marcella, his wife, placed this. Also Aelius Iucundianus and Aelius Valerianus, soldiers.
EDCS-28600433.summary: A funerary epitaph for Claudius, a veteran, set up by his son Claudius Vitalianus and his wife Flavia Marcella, along with other soldiers, likely dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-28600434.text_edition: \n Marcus Cocceius Moesicus annorum XV hic situs est Marcus Cocceius Matumari filius Florus princeps Cocceia Oxidubna Quintionis filia coniux filio et sibi vivi posuerunt\n
EDCS-28600434.translation: Marcus Cocceius Moesicus, aged 15, lies here. Marcus Cocceius Matumari, his son, Florus, the princeps, and Cocceia Oxidubna, daughter of Quintio, his wife, placed this for their son and for themselves while alive.
EDCS-28600434.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Cocceius Moesicus, aged 15, set up by his father, son, and wife, and likely dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-28701116.translation: The First Ulpian Cohort of Pannonians, Antoniniana.
EDCS-28701116.summary: This inscription records the name of a Roman auxiliary cohort, the Cohors I Ulpia Pannoniorum, dating to the period of the Antonine emperors.

EDCS-28701119.translation: The Seventh Cohort of Breuci, Antoniniana.
EDCS-28701119.summary: This inscription records the name of a Roman auxiliary cohort, the Cohors VII Breucorum, dating to the period of the Antonine emperors.

EDCS-28701120.translation: The Seventh Cohort of Breuci, Antoniniana.
EDCS-28701120.summary: This inscription records the name of the Seventh Cohort of Breuci, with the honorific title 'Antoniniana', likely dating to the Antonine period.

EDCS-28701121.translation: The Seventh Cohort of Breuci, Severiana.
EDCS-28701121.summary: This inscription records the name of the Seventh Cohort of Breuci, with the honorific title 'Severiana', likely dating to the Severan period.

EDCS-28701134.translation: The Seventh Cohort of Breuci, Antoniniana.
EDCS-28701134.summary: This inscription records the name of the Seventh Cohort of Breuci, with the honorific title 'Antoniniana', likely dating to the Antonine period.

EDCS-28701135.translation: The Seventh Cohort of Breuci, Antoniniana.
EDCS-28701135.summary: This inscription records the name of the Seventh Cohort of Breuci, with the honorific title 'Antoniniana', likely dating to the Antonine period.

EDCS-28701210.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribuniciae potestatis VII pater patriae restituit milia passuum XV\n
EDCS-28701210.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 7th time, father of the fatherland, restored fifteen miles.
EDCS-28701210.summary: This inscription records the restoration of fifteen miles of road by Emperor Alexander Severus, dated by his seventh tribunician power, indicating a repair or construction project.

EDCS-28701212.text_edition: \n Impperatores Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Arabicus Adiabenicus maximus et Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Auggusti et Publius Septimius Geta nobilissimus Caesar [] [] coloniae splendidissimae curante Lucio Baebio Caeciliano legato Augustorum pro praetore\n
EDCS-28701212.translation: The Emperors Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Arabicus Adiabenicus Maximus and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus and Publius Septimius Geta Nobilissimus Caesar, [through the agency of] ... of the most splendid colony, under the supervision of Lucius Baebius Caecilianus, legate of the Augusti, propraetor.
EDCS-28701212.summary: This inscription records a dedication or work overseen by the legate Lucius Baebius Caecilianus on behalf of Emperors Septimius Severus, Caracalla, and Geta, suggesting a provincial administrative act.

EDCS-28701213.text_edition: \n legionis II adiutricis piae fidelis Antoninianae curam agente Pontio Pontiano legato Augusti pro praetore ab Aquinco milia passuum III\n
EDCS-28701213.translation: Of the Second Legion Adiutrix Pia Fidelis Antoniniana, under the supervision of Pontius Pontianus, legate of the Augustus, propraetor, three miles from Aquincum.
EDCS-28701213.summary: This inscription records a milestone erected by the Second Legion Adiutrix, supervised by its legate Pontius Pontianus, indicating a road marker three miles from Aquincum.

EDCS-28701215.text_edition: \n Domino nostro Imperatori Liciniano Licinio Pio Felici Invicto Augusto ab Aquinco milia passuum V\n
EDCS-28701215.translation: To our Lord Emperor Licinius Licinius Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, five miles from Aquincum.
EDCS-28701215.summary: This inscription records a milestone dedicated to Emperor Licinius, marking a distance of five miles from Aquincum, likely from the period of his reign.

EDCS-28701216.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus [Alexander Pius] Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribuniciae potestatis VIIII consul III pater patriae restituit ab Aquinco milia passuum VI\n
EDCS-28701216.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus [Alexander Pius] Felix Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 9th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the fatherland, restored six miles from Aquincum.
EDCS-28701216.summary: This inscription records the restoration of six miles of road by Emperor Alexander Severus, dated by his ninth tribunician power and third consulship, indicating a road maintenance project.

EDCS-28701217.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Caio Iulio Vero Maximino Invicto Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate patri patriae consuli proconsuli et Caio Iulio Vero Maximo nobilissimo Caesari ab Aquinco milia passuum VI Dominis nnostris Flavio Valerio Constantio et Galerio Valerio Maximiano nobilissimis Caesaribus DDominis nnostris Con[stantino ------\n
EDCS-28701217.translation: To Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Invictus Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power, father of the fatherland, consul, proconsul, and to Gaius Julius Verus Maximus Nobilissimus Caesar, six miles from Aquincum. To our Lords Flavius Valerius Constantius and Galerius Valerius Maximianus Nobilissimi Caesares, to our Lords Con[stantine ------
EDCS-28701217.summary: This inscription records a milestone indicating six miles from Aquincum, dedicated to Emperors Maximinus Thrax and his son Maximus Caesar, and later updated or referring to Constantinus and other Augusti, indicating a long period of use and revision.

EDCS-28701218.text_edition: \n Imperator FIRIO M Maximianus F C milia passuum \n
EDCS-28701218.summary: This inscription, likely a milestone, records a distance associated with Emperor FIRIO M Maximianus, possibly dating to the late 3rd century CE.

EDCS-28701219.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari [Marco Aurelio] Antonino [Pio Felici] Invicto Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate V consuli IIII proconsuli ab Aquinco milia passum VIII\n
EDCS-28701219.translation: To Emperor Caesar [Marcus Aurelio] Antoninus [Pio Felici] Invicto Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power 5, consul 4, proconsul. 8 miles from Aquincum.
EDCS-28701219.summary: This milestone inscription honors Emperor Caracalla and indicates a distance of 8 miles from Aquincum, likely dating to his reign (198-217 CE).

EDCS-28701221.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus A[lexander] Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribuniciae potestatis VIIII consul III pater patriae restituit ab Aquinco milia passuum VIII\n
EDCS-28701221.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus A[lexander] Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power 9, consul 3, father of the fatherland, restored. 8 miles from Aquincum.
EDCS-28701221.summary: This milestone inscription indicates that Emperor Severus Alexander restored a section of road, covering 8 miles from Aquincum, and dates to his reign (222-235 CE).

EDCS-28701223.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Iulio Philippo Pio Felici Augusto pontifici maximo tribuniciae potestatis consuli patri patriae proconsuli et Marciae Otaciliae Severae sanctissimae Augustae coniugi Augusti nostri ab Aquinco milia passuum VIII\n
EDCS-28701223.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Iulius Philippus Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power, consul, father of the fatherland, proconsul, and Marcia Otacilia Severa, his most holy Augusta, wife of our Augustus. 8 miles from Aquincum.
EDCS-28701223.summary: This milestone inscription honors Emperor Philip the Arab and his wife Marcia Otacilia Severa, marking a distance of 8 miles from Aquincum, likely dating to 244-249 CE.

EDCS-28701224.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Iulio Philippo Augusto pontifici maximo tribuniciae potestatis consuli patri patriae proconsuli et Marco Iulio Philippo nobilissimo Caesari et Marciae Otaciliae Severae sanctissimae coniugi Augusti nostri ab Aquinco milia passuum VIII\n
EDCS-28701224.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Iulius Philippus Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power, consul, father of the fatherland, proconsul, and Marcus Iulius Philippus, most noble Caesar, and Marcia Otacilia Severa, his most holy wife of our Augustus. 8 miles from Aquincum.
EDCS-28701224.summary: This milestone inscription honors Emperor Philip the Arab, his son Philip II, and his wife Marcia Otacilia Severa, marking a distance of 8 miles from Aquincum, likely dating to 247-249 CE.

EDCS-28701227.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VIIII consul III pater patriae restituit ab Aquinco milia passuum VIIII\n
EDCS-28701227.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power 9, consul 3, father of the fatherland, restored. 9 miles from Aquincum.
EDCS-28701227.summary: This milestone inscription indicates that Emperor Severus Alexander restored a section of road, covering 9 miles from Aquincum, and dates to his reign (222-235 CE).

EDCS-28701229.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus ponti<f=I>(ex) maximus tribunicia potestas sic VIIII consul III pater patriae restituit ab Aquinco milia passuum XXXIIII \n
EDCS-28701229.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power 9, consul 3, father of the fatherland, restored. 34 miles from Aquincum.
EDCS-28701229.summary: This milestone inscription indicates that Emperor Severus Alexander restored a section of road, covering 34 miles from Aquincum, and dates to his reign (222-235 CE).

EDCS-28701234.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Caio Messio Quinto Traiano Decio Pio Felici Invicto Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate patri patriae consuli proconsuli ab Aquinco milia passuum XXXVI\n
EDCS-28701234.translation: To Emperor Caesar Gaius Messius Quintus Traianus Decius Pius Felix Invicto Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power, father of the fatherland, consul, proconsul. 36 miles from Aquincum.
EDCS-28701234.summary: This milestone inscription honors Emperor Decius and marks a distance of 36 miles from Aquincum, likely dating to his reign (249-251 CE).

EDCS-28701238.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus [Opellius] Seve<r=P>us Macrinus Pius Augustus consul proconsul tribunicia potestate [et Opellius] Diadumenianus nobilissimus Caesar vias et pontes vetustate corruptas restituerunt cura agente Aelio Tricciano legato Augusti pro <p=R>raetore ab Aquinco milia passuum XLVI \n
EDCS-28701238.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus [Opellius] Severus Macrinus Pius Augustus, consul, proconsul, with tribunician power, and Opellius Diadumenianus, most noble Caesar, restored roads and bridges corrupted by age, under the administration of Aelius Triccianus, legate of the emperor for praetorian authority. 46 miles from Aquincum.
EDCS-28701238.summary: This milestone inscription records the restoration of roads and bridges by Emperors Macrinus and Diadumenianus, administered by legate Aelius Triccianus, marking 46 miles from Aquincum, likely dating to 217-218 CE.

EDCS-28701260.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VIIII consul III pater patriae restituit ab Aquinco milia passuum LXXIII\n
EDCS-28701260.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power 9, consul 3, father of the fatherland, restored. 73 miles from Aquincum.
EDCS-28701260.summary: This milestone inscription indicates that Emperor Severus Alexander restored a section of road, covering 73 miles from Aquincum, and dates to his reign (222-235 CE).

EDCS-28701261.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Gaius Iulius Verus Maxi[minus] Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate II imperator IIII consul proconsul pater patriae et Gaius Iulius Verus Maximus nobilissimus Caesar filio sic Augusti nostri Dacici Germanici Sarmatici maximis sic ab Aquinco milia passuum XCVII\n
EDCS-28701261.translation: Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the second time, emperor for the fourth time, consul, proconsul, father of the country, and Gaius Julius Verus Maximus, most noble Caesar, son of our emperor, conqueror of Dacia, Germany, and Sarmatia, from Aquincum, 97 miles.
EDCS-28701261.summary: This inscription records the mileage from Aquincum, likely related to military or administrative infrastructure, honoring Emperors Maximinus Thrax and his son, Maximus Caesar, dating to their joint rule.

EDCS-28701262.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Lucio Septimio Sevevero Pio Pert<i=E>naci Augusto Arabico Adiabenico Parthico maximo divi Marci Aureli filio divi Commodi fratri divi Antonini Pii nepoti divi Hadriani pronepoti divi Traiani Parthici abnepoti divi Nervae adnepoti imperatori XI tribunicia potestate VII consuli II proconsuli Imperatori Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pert<i=E>naci Augusti filio divi Antonini nepoti divi Antonini Pii pronepoti divi Hadriani abnepoti divi Traiani Parthici et divi Nervae adnepoti Publio Septimio Getae Caesa[re] Augusti? filio curante Lucio Baebio Caeciliano legato Auggustorum pro praetore ab Aquinco \n
EDCS-28701262.translation: To Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, Arabicus, Adiabenicus, Parthicus Maximus, son of the deified Marcus Aurelius, brother of the deified Commodus, grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva, emperor for the eleventh time, with tribunician power for the seventh time, consul for the second time, proconsul, and to Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, son of Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, grandson of the deified Antoninus, great-grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, and great-great-great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva, and to Publius Septimius Geta Caesar, son of Augustus, with Lucius Baebius Caecilianus, legate of the emperors, pro praetor, in charge, from Aquincum.
EDCS-28701262.summary: This lengthy dedication honors several emperors, including Septimius Severus, Caracalla, and Geta, and records the administrator Lucius Baebius Caecilianus from Aquincum, likely marking a road or administrative boundary during the reign of Severus and his sons.

EDCS-28701265.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Caio Iulio Vero Maximino Pio Felici Augusto G D S tribunicia potestate consul procons(uli) et Caio Iulio Vero Maximo \n
EDCS-28701265.translation: To Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus, with tribunician power, consul, proconsul, and to Gaius Julius Verus Maximus.
EDCS-28701265.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperors Maximinus Thrax and his son, Maximus Caesar, likely honoring a monument or structure.

EDCS-28701270.summary: This is a very brief inscription, possibly a fragment, indicating ownership or dedication related to Emperor Hadrian.

EDCS-28701281.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Caius Iulius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate bis imperator III consul proconsul pater patriae et Caius Iulius Verus Maximus nobilissimus Caesar filius Augusti nostri Dacici Germanici Sarmatici imperatores maximi ab Aquinco milia passuum CLX\n
EDCS-28701281.translation: Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the second time, emperor for the third time, consul, proconsul, father of the country, and Gaius Julius Verus Maximus, most noble Caesar, son of our emperor, conqueror of Dacia, Germany, and Sarmatia, emperors, from Aquincum, 160 miles.
EDCS-28701281.summary: This inscription records the mileage from Aquincum, honoring Emperors Maximinus Thrax and his son, Maximus Caesar, dating to their joint rule.

EDCS-28701283.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VIII consul III pater patriae restituit ab Aquinco milia passuum VIII\n
EDCS-28701283.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the eighth time, consul for the third time, father of the country, restored, from Aquincum, 8 miles.
EDCS-28701283.summary: This inscription records that Emperor Severus Alexander restored a road or structure, indicating its distance from Aquincum and dating it to his eighth tribunician power and third consulship.

EDCS-28701284.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Caio Iulio [Vero Ma][ximino] Invicto Pio Felici Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate patri patriae consuli procons(uli) et Imperatori Caio Iul[io] [Vero Maxi]mo no[bilissimo] Caesari Pio Felici Augusto ab Aquinco milia passuum CII\n
EDCS-28701284.translation: To Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus, unconquered, Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power, father of the country, consul, proconsul, and to Emperor Gaius Julius Verus Maximus, most noble Caesar, Pius Felix Augustus, from Aquincum, 102 miles.
EDCS-28701284.summary: This inscription marks the mileage from Aquincum and is dedicated to Emperors Maximinus Thrax and his son, Maximus Caesar.

EDCS-28701285.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari [Caio Iulio Ve][ro Maximi][no] Pio Felici Augusto Germanico maximo Dacico maximo Sarmatico maximo pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate patri patriae consuli proconsuli et Imperatori Caio Iulio Vero Maximo Germanico maximo Dacico maximo Sarmatico maximo nobilissimo Caesari Pio Felici Augusto ab Aquinco milia passuum VIII\n
EDCS-28701285.translation: To Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus, Pius Felix Augustus, conqueror of Germany, conqueror of Dacia, conqueror of Sarmatia, chief priest, with tribunician power, father of the country, consul, proconsul, and to Emperor Gaius Julius Verus Maximus, conqueror of Germany, conqueror of Dacia, conqueror of Sarmatia, most noble Caesar, Pius Felix Augustus, from Aquincum, 8 miles.
EDCS-28701285.summary: This inscription records a distance of 8 miles from Aquincum and is dedicated to Emperors Maximinus Thrax and his son, Maximus Caesar, highlighting their military titles.

EDCS-28701286.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar [] [] [] Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus pater patriae ab Aquinco milia passuum CII\n
EDCS-28701286.translation: Emperor Caesar [name missing] Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, father of the country, from Aquincum, 102 miles.
EDCS-28701286.summary: This inscription records a distance of 102 miles from Aquincum and mentions an emperor (likely Maximinus Thrax, given context) but has significant lacunae.

EDCS-28701287.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Caio Iulio][Iulio Maximino Invicto Pio Felici Augusto pontifici maximo tribuniciae potestatis patri patriae proconsuli legio II adiutrix pia fidelis <et=LI> [] ab Aquinco milia passuum III \n
EDCS-28701287.translation: To Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Maximinus, unconquered, Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power, father of the country, proconsul, and the Legio II Adiutrix Pia Fidelis, from Aquincum, 3 miles.
EDCS-28701287.summary: This inscription marks a distance of 3 miles from Aquincum and is dedicated to Emperor Maximinus Thrax and the loyal Legio II Adiutrix, likely commemorating a road or construction project.

EDCS-28701289.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus tribunicia potestate XV consul III et Imperator Caesar Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus tribunicia potestate consul II divi Augusti Antonini filii divi Hadriani nepotes divi Traiani Parthici pronepotes divi Nervae abnepotes ab Aquinco milia passuum \n
EDCS-28701289.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, holding tribunician power for the 15th time, consul for the 3rd time, and Emperor Caesar Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus, holding tribunician power for the 1st time, consul for the 2nd time, sons of the deified Augustus Antoninus, grandsons of the deified Hadrian, great-grandsons of the deified Trajan Parthicus, and great-great-grandsons of the deified Nerva, from Aquincum, 32 miles.
EDCS-28701289.summary: This is a milestone inscription from Aquincum, dating to the joint reign of Marcus Aurelius and Lucius Verus, recording distances.

EDCS-28701290.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Lucio Septimio Severo Pio Pertinaci Augusto Arabico Adiabenico Parthico maximo divi Marci filio divi Commodi fratri divi Antonini Pii nepoti divi Hadriani pronepoti divi Traiani Parthici abnepoti divi Nervae adnepoti imperatori XI tribunicia potestate VI consuli II proconsuli et Imperatori Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertinacis Augusti nostri filio divi Antonini nepoti divi Antonini Pii pronepoti divi Hadriani abnepoti divi Traiani Parthici et divi Nervae adnepoti et [] curante Tiberio Claudio Claudiano legato Auggustorum pro praetore ab Aquinco milia passuum \n
EDCS-28701290.translation: To Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, son of the deified Marcus, brother of the deified Commodus, grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, and great-great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva, Emperor for the 11th time, holding tribunician power for the 6th time, consul for the 2nd time, proconsul, and to Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, son of our Emperor Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, grandson of the deified Antoninus, great-grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, and great-great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus and the deified Nerva, under the administration of Tiberius Claudius Claudianus, legate of the Augusti pro praetore, from Aquincum, 32 miles.
EDCS-28701290.summary: This is a milestone inscription from Aquincum, dating to the co-rule of Septimius Severus and Caracalla, with the governor Tiberius Claudius Claudianus overseeing the administration.

EDCS-28701291.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Caio Messio Quinto Traiano Decio Pio Felici Invicto Augusto pontifici maximo tribuniciae potestatis bis consuli bis designato ter et Quinto Herennio Etrusco Messio Decio nobilissimo Caesari consuli designato et Caio Valenti Hostiliano Messio Quinto nobilissimo [Caesari ------\n
EDCS-28701291.translation: To Emperor Caesar Gaius Messius Quintus Traianus Decius Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 2nd time, consul for the 2nd time, and consul designate for the 3rd time, and to Quintus Herennius Etruscus Messius Decius, most noble Caesar, consul designate, and to Gaius Valens Hostilianus Messius Quintus, most noble Caesar...
EDCS-28701291.summary: This is a dedication or milestone inscription, beginning with the titles of Emperor Decius and continuing with those of his sons, Herennius Etruscus and Hostilianus.

EDCS-28701292.text_edition: \n ------ Flavio] Aeliano clarissimo viro legato Augusti pro praetore ab Aquinco milia passuum III\n
EDCS-28701292.translation: ...Flavius Aelianus, most illustrious man, legate of the emperor pro praetore, from Aquincum, 3 miles.
EDCS-28701292.summary: This is a fragment of a milestone inscription from Aquincum, mentioning the governor Flavius Aelianus and recording a distance of 3 miles.

EDCS-28800761.text_edition: \n ------ in pa]ce qui VICxit sic ---us ADRIqui vicxit sic \n
EDCS-28800761.translation: ...in peace who lived thus... ADRI who lived thus...
EDCS-28800761.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, possibly funerary, recording the lifespan of an individual or individuals.

EDCS-28800762.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription, possibly dedicatory or honorary, to an unknown deity or person.

EDCS-28900084.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maximianus ER Augustus Pius Felix pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate consul pater patriae milia passuum XXXII\n
EDCS-28900084.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maximianus Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power, consul, father of the fatherland, 32 miles.
EDCS-28900084.summary: This is a milestone inscription, dating to the co-rule of Maximian and Diocletian, recording a distance of 32 miles.

EDCS-28900085.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Valerio Maximiano Pio Felici Augusto pontifici maximo \n
EDCS-28900085.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maximian Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus...
EDCS-28900085.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely a milestone or dedication, honoring Emperor Maximian.

EDCS-28900086.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Iulio Philippo Pio Felici invicto Augusto Parthico maximo pontifici maximo tribuniciae potestatis patri patriae proconsuli a Brigetione milia passuum XXXIII\n
EDCS-28900086.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus Parthicus Maximus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power, father of the fatherland, proconsul, from Brigetio, 33 miles.
EDCS-28900086.summary: This is a milestone inscription from Brigetio, dating to the reign of Philip the Arab, recording a distance of 33 miles.

EDCS-28900089.text_edition: \n PRINIIANI Parthicus ET SE REN VSVS \n
EDCS-28900089.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, possibly part of a list of victories or titles, mentioning 'Parthicus' and possibly 'Serenus'.

EDCS-29000454.text_edition: \n ------] Tiberio Claudioo MARI F annorum L hic situs est\n
EDCS-29000454.translation: Tiberius Claudius, son of Marius, aged 50, is buried here.
EDCS-29000454.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Tiberius Claudius, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29000530.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Antonio Gordiano Pio Felici Invicto Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate III patri patriae consuli proconsuli cohors I Noricorum Gordiana equitata devota numini maiestatique eius\n
EDCS-29000530.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, Tribune for the 3rd time, Father of the Fatherland, Consul, Proconsul. The 1st Cohort of Noricans, the Gordian, mounted, devoted to his divinity and majesty.
EDCS-29000530.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Gordian III by the first cohort of Norican cavalry, dating to his reign (238-244 AD).

EDCS-29000533.text_edition: \n Aurelius Valerianus vixit annos LXX Maximinae nepoti et Saturnae coniugi Firmus et Firmanus titulum filii parentibus \n
EDCS-29000533.translation: Aurelius Valerianus lived 70 years. Firmus and Firmanus, sons, erected this monument to their parents, Maximina his granddaughter and Satura his wife.
EDCS-29000533.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Aurelius Valerianus, erected by his sons, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29000534.text_edition: \n Pollitius Laelianus pro se et filiis suis votum solvit libens merito Serviano III et Varo consulibus\n
EDCS-29000534.translation: Pollitius Laelianus fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly for himself and his sons. Consuls: Servianus and Varus.
EDCS-29000534.summary: This is a dedication recording the fulfillment of a vow by Pollitius Laelianus, with the consuls indicating a date of 236 AD.

EDCS-29000535.text_edition: \n Iunoni Reginae sacrum pro salute Aberiorum Vigoris et Vigoriani Lucani et Lucaniani Aberius Acutus pater et Claudia Matrona mater votum solverunt libentes merito\n
EDCS-29000535.translation: To Juno Regina, sacred for the health of Aberius Vigoris and Vigorianus Lucanus and Lucanianus. Aberius Acutus, father, and Claudia Matrona, mother, fulfilled their vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-29000535.summary: This is a dedication to Juno Regina for the health of a family, likely made by the parents Aberius Acutus and Claudia Matrona, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29000538.translation: Brother LI, and also Aurelius [...] Iul[ius?]
EDCS-29000538.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription, likely funerary or dedicatory, mentioning individuals named LI and Aurelius, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29000539.translation: To the spirits of the underworld, Claudius Maximus, aged [...]
EDCS-29000539.summary: This is a funerary inscription fragment dedicated to the Manes of Claudius Maximus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29000546.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sexti sic Iuventio Primitivo domo Aquileia annorum XXVII Ulpia Epicharis fratri faciendum curavit ob merita eius faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-29000546.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Ulpia Epicharis had this made for her brother Sextus Iuventius Primitivus of Aquileia, aged 27, because of his merits.
EDCS-29000546.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Sextus Iuventius Primitivus, commissioned by his sister Ulpia Epicharis, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29000548.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Barsemis Abbei decurio alae firmae Kata<f=P>ractariae ex numero Hosro<en>ruorum magister cohortis |(miliariae) Hemesenorum domo Carris et Aurelia Iulia coniux <ei=PL>us votum solverunt libentes merito Aurelia Thicimim et Aurelia Salia <e=F>t filias sic Barsimia Titia ? nepotes cum suprascriptis posuerunt \n
EDCS-29000548.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. Barsemis, decurion of the first wing of the Katafractarii, from the unit of the Hosroenoi, commander of a milliary cohort of the Hemesenoi, from Carrhae, and his wife Aurelia Iulia fulfilled their vow willingly and deservedly. Aurelia Thicimim and Aurelia Salia, daughters, with the aforementioned, placed this.
EDCS-29000548.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by a military officer Barsemis and his family, likely dating to the 3rd century AD given the military units.

EDCS-29000549.text_edition: \n Deo Soli Augusto limus stationarius publici votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29000549.translation: To the divine Sol Augustus. Limus, a public stationarius, fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-29000549.summary: This is a dedication to the god Sol Augustus by a public servant named Limus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29000550.summary: This is a dedication to the god Mithras, likely from a Roman military context given the deity's association with soldiers, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29000551.text_edition: \n Soli Invicto Deo Marcus Ulpius Petuernus\n
EDCS-29000551.translation: To Sol Invictus Deo, Marcus Ulpius Petuernus.
EDCS-29000551.summary: This is a dedication to the unconquered sun god Sol, made by Marcus Ulpius Petuernus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29000555.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelio Gaiano veterano legionis I adiutricis vixit nnos LXX et Aureliae Valentinae vixit annos Aureliae Gaianae et Aurelio Ausiano stratore legati legionis II adiutricis vixit nnos XL stipendiorum XXII qui est desideratus bello Dacico Aurelia marito dulcissimo\n
EDCS-29000555.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed, to Aurelius Gaianus, veteran of the I Adiutrix Legion, who lived 70 years, and to Aurelia Valentina, who lived [age missing] years; to Aurelia Gaiana and Aurelius Ausianus, stride-bearers of the legate of the II Adiutrix Legion, who lived 40 years and 22 years of service, who was lost in the Dacian War; Aurelia [dedicates this] to her most sweet husband.
EDCS-29000555.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating Aurelius Gaianus and Aurelia Valentina, with a specific mention of Aurelius Ausianus who died in the Dacian Wars, likely dating to the late 1st or early 2nd century CE.

EDCS-29000557.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Claudiae Monimosae Monimi quondam Aglavi veterani filiae quae vixit nnos L Aelianus Genuilis veteranus legionis II adiutricis coniugi rarissimae\n
EDCS-29000557.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed, to Claudia Monimosa, daughter of Monimus, formerly of Aglavi, who lived 50 years; and to Aelianus Genuilis, veteran of the II Adiutrix Legion, to his most rare wife.
EDCS-29000557.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Claudia Monimosa, daughter of Monimus, and her husband Aelianus Genuilis, a veteran of the II Adiutrix Legion, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29000558.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lissinia Galla vixit annis XXI Calpurnius Irenaeus tribunus coniugi rarissimae bene merenti posuit\n
EDCS-29000558.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed, Lissinia Galla, who lived 21 years. Calpurnius Irenaeus, tribune, placed [this] for his most rare wife, well-deserving.
EDCS-29000558.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lissinia Galla, who died at 21, dedicated by her husband Calpurnius Irenaeus, a military tribune, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29000561.text_edition: \n Asulus Blatumari filius vivos sic sibi et Deivae Namiorigis filiae annorum XXX coniugi Blatumaro filio annorum VI hic siti sunt\n
EDCS-29000561.translation: Asulus, son of Blatumarus, himself alive, for himself and for Deiva, daughter of Namiorigis, aged 30, his wife, and for Blatumarus, son of [Asulus?], aged 6, are buried here.
EDCS-29000561.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Asulus and his family, including his wife Deiva and son Blatumarus, likely a self-dedication made during his lifetime, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29000562.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Nemoratta Sperati filia annorum X titulus positus hic situs\n
EDCS-29000562.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed, Nemoratta, daughter of Speratus, aged 10. The inscription was placed here; she is buried here.
EDCS-29000562.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Nemoratta, daughter of Speratus, who died at age 10, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29000563.text_edition: \n esia Tutui filia annorum XXX hic sita est\n
EDCS-29000563.translation: Aesia, daughter of Tutui, aged 30, is buried here.
EDCS-29000563.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aesia, daughter of Tutui, who died at age 30, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29000565.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus titulum posuit Florina Ni<g=C>rino virginuo suo qui vixit annos L et mensis sic septem \n
EDCS-29000565.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed, Florina placed this monument for Nigrinus, her virgin husband, who lived 50 years and seven months.
EDCS-29000565.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Nigrinus, a virgin husband, placed by his wife Florina, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29000575.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Suadilla Comonis liberta annorum LXXX titulum posu--- Vibius Moget SIFLVI \n
EDCS-29000575.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed, Suadilla, freedwoman of Comon, aged 80. Vibius Moget placed the monument... [illegible].
EDCS-29000575.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Suadilla, a freedwoman, who died at 80, with a partial dedication by Vibius Moget, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29000581.text_edition: \n Iunoni Reginae Aurelius Respectus veteranus legionis II adiutricis piae fidelis ex voto posuit aram dono dedit Agricola et Clementino consulibus\n
EDCS-29000581.translation: To Juno Regina, Aurelius Respectus, a veteran of the Second Adjutrix Legion, pious and faithful, set up this altar as a gift according to his vow, during the consulship of Agricola and Clementinus.
EDCS-29000581.summary: This inscription records a veteran's dedication of an altar to Juno Regina, dating to the consulship of Agricola and Clementinus.

EDCS-29200188.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno et Deo Paterno Comageno Marcus Aurelius Apollinaris decurio municipii Murselensium votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29200188.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus and the god Paternus Comagenus, Marcus Aurelius Apollinaris, a decurion of the municipality of Murselenses, willingly paid his vow as deserved.
EDCS-29200188.summary: This inscription records a decurion from Murselenses fulfilling a vow to Jupiter Dolichenus and the god Paternus Comagenus.

EDCS-29200189.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno et Deo Paterno Comageno Marcus Aurelius Apollinaris decurio municipii Murselensium votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29200189.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus and the god Paternus Comagenus, Marcus Aurelius Apollinaris, a decurion of the municipality of Murselenses, willingly paid his vow as deserved.
EDCS-29200189.summary: This inscription records a decurion from Murselenses fulfilling a vow to Jupiter Dolichenus and the god Paternus Comagenus.

EDCS-29200191.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno EA dono dedit\n
EDCS-29200191.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus, EA gave this as a gift.
EDCS-29200191.summary: This inscription records a gift given to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus by EA.

EDCS-29300065.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute et reditu Imperatoris Marci Aureli Antonini Augusti et Iuliae Augustae matris castrorum et senatus Aelius Surus decurio coloniae Bassianensium et IIvir votum merito solvit\n
EDCS-29300065.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, for the safety and return of the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus and Julia Augusta, mother of the camps and senate, Aelius Surus, decurion and former duovir of the colony of Bassianenses, willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-29300065.summary: This inscription records Aelius Surus, a decurion and former duovir of Bassianenses, fulfilling a vow for the well-being and return of Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus and Julia Augusta.

EDCS-29300066.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Paterno Aelius Messius duplicarius votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29300066.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Paternus, Aelius Messius, a duplicarius, willingly paid his vow as deserved.
EDCS-29300066.summary: This inscription records Aelius Messius, a duplicarius, fulfilling a vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Paternus.

EDCS-29300068.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Arubiano posuerunt Gemellinus et Vitalis libientes voto suo\n
EDCS-29300068.translation: Gemellinus and Vitalis willingly dedicated this to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Arubianus according to their vow.
EDCS-29300068.summary: This inscription records Gemellinus and Vitalis dedicating something to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Arubianus.

EDCS-29300069.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Iunoni Reginae Marcus Antonius Proculinus decurio coloniae Bassianae votum posuit libens merito\n
EDCS-29300069.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Juno Regina, Marcus Antonius Proculinus, a decurion of the colony of Bassiana, willingly set up this vow.
EDCS-29300069.summary: This inscription records Marcus Antonius Proculinus, a decurion of Bassiana, fulfilling a vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Juno Regina.

EDCS-29300070.text_edition: \n Silvano sacrum Ulpi Neratius et Nigrinus decuriones coloniae et Sabinus et Quintio posuerunt\n
EDCS-29300070.translation: Sacred to Silvanus: Ulpius Neratius and Nigrinus, decurions of the colony, and Sabinus and Quintius, set this up.
EDCS-29300070.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a shrine to Silvanus by decurions of the colony and others.

EDCS-29300073.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelio Ianuarino qui vixit nnos X Aurelius Ianuarius tribunus legionis II adiutricis et Aurelia Valentina filio carissimo et sibi vivi posuerunt\n
EDCS-29300073.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead: For Aurelius Ianuarinus, who lived 10 years, Aurelius Ianuarius, tribune of the Second Adjutrix Legion, and Aurelia Valentina, for their dearest son and for themselves, set this up while living.
EDCS-29300073.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Aurelius Ianuarinus, set up by his father, a legionary tribune, and his mother, commemorating their son and themselves.

EDCS-29300074.text_edition: \n Ene vixit nnos XXV Zosimus bene merenti\n
EDCS-29300074.translation: Zosimus lived 25 years; for the well-deserving one.
EDCS-29300074.summary: This is a tombstone recording the lifespan of Zosimus, likely a freedman.

EDCS-29300075.translation: Dasmenus and his heirs set this up for their son.
EDCS-29300075.summary: This monument was erected by Dasmenus and his heirs to commemorate a son.

EDCS-29300078.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Lucius Didius Herculanus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29300078.translation: Lucius Didius Herculanus gladly and deservedly paid his vow to Jupiter Best and Greatest.
EDCS-29300078.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Lucius Didius Herculanus.

EDCS-29300081.text_edition: \n Silvano Bellatori sacrum Lucius Marcellus decurio coloniae IIvir quinquennalis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29300081.translation: Lucius Marcellus, decurion, former duovir quinquennalis of the colony, gladly and deservedly paid his vow to Silvanus Bellator.
EDCS-29300081.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Silvanus Bellator by Lucius Marcellus, an official from a colony.

EDCS-29300083.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus HAE Ance vixit annos XXVI Iovius Tusculanus praefectus equitum alae It<u=Y>raeorum uxori optimae castissimae obsequentissima<e=F> piissimae \n
EDCS-29300083.translation: To the departed spirits. HAE Ance lived 26 years. Iovius Tusculanus, prefect of cavalry of the Ala I Auréorum, for his most excellent, most chaste, most obedient, most devoted wife.
EDCS-29300083.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Ance, wife of Iovius Tusculanus, a cavalry prefect, likely dating to the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-29300085.text_edition: \n Titus Cominius Titi filius Voltinia Severus Vienna (centurio) legionis II adiutricis donis donatus ab imperatore Caesare Augusto bello Dacico torquibus armillis phaleris corona vallari vixit nnos XXXXV Titus Caesernius Macedo procurator Augusti heres ex testamento posuit\n
EDCS-29300085.translation: Titus Cominius Severus, son of Titus, of the Voltinia tribe, centurion of the Legio II Adjutrix, honored with gifts of torcs, armlets, phalerae, and a civic crown by the emperor Caesar Augustus in the Dacian War, lived 45 years. Titus Caesernius Macedo, procurator of Augustus, erected this as heir by will.
EDCS-29300085.summary: This funerary inscription honors Titus Cominius Severus, a centurion, who lived 45 years and received military honors, erected by his heir Titus Caesernius Macedo, an imperial procurator.

EDCS-29300086.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Simplicius vix(it) nnos V \n
EDCS-29300086.translation: To the departed spirits. Aurelius Simplicius lived 5 years.
EDCS-29300086.summary: This is a tombstone for a child named Aurelius Simplicius who died at the age of five.

EDCS-29300087.text_edition: \n Genio ordinis ---ius Titi filius Quirina vius AR\n
EDCS-29300087.translation: To the genius of the order... son of Titus, of the Quirina tribe... AR.
EDCS-29300087.summary: This fragmented inscription likely records a dedication to the 'genius' of a collegium or council by a member.

EDCS-29300096.text_edition: \n A \xcf\x89 in pace quiescit Macarius diaconus curante Gerontia coniuge\n
EDCS-29300096.translation: Macarius, the deacon, rests here in peace, with Gerontia his wife overseeing.
EDCS-29300096.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Macarius, a deacon, whose wife Gerontia is mentioned as arranging the burial.

EDCS-29300098.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-29300098.text_edition: \n Aurelia Urbic<a=I> Flavio Martiniano cumpare sic suo in pacae sic carissimo e<t=I> dulcissimae filiae mater piissima doliens sic <f=T>ratri et filiae maemoriam sic posuit \n
EDCS-29300098.translation: Aurelia Urbica, a most devoted mother, grieving, set this up in memory of her dear husband Flavius Martinianus and her sweetest daughter, as well as her brother and daughter.
EDCS-29300098.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Aurelia Urbica in memory of her husband, daughter, and brother.

EDCS-29300100.text_edition: \n iae in pace quae vixit annos VIIII menses quattuor V Iovinus \n
EDCS-29300100.translation: May you rest in peace. Iovinus]
EDCS-29300100.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of an unnamed individual and the dedicant Iovinus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29400005.text_edition: \n ciano ani annorum XII et Flavio Apollinari vixit annos VIII et Flavius Marcellus suis et sibi vivus posuit\n
EDCS-29400005.translation: Flavius Marcellus dedicated this for his family: for Flavianus, who lived 12 years, and for Flavius Apollinaris, who lived 8 years, and for himself, while alive.
EDCS-29400005.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by Flavius Marcellus for his family members, indicating their ages at death, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29400006.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Septimius Valens custos armorum cohortis II votum posuit libens merito\n
EDCS-29400006.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, Septimius Valens, keeper of the arms of the II Cohort, willingly fulfilled his vow, as deserved.
EDCS-29400006.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicating a structure or offering to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Septimius Valens, an official in the II Cohort, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29400008.text_edition: \n Marco Domitio Marci filio Viminacio legionis IIII Flaviae firmae stipendiorum XVII qui vixit annos ndio \n
EDCS-29400008.translation: To Marcus Domitius, son of Marcus, from Viminacium, of the IV Flavia Felix legion, with 17 years of service, who lived [---] days.
EDCS-29400008.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Marcus Domitius, a soldier of the IV Flavia Felix legion, recording his service years and partial lifespan, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29400009.summary: This inscription is too fragmentary to determine its content or purpose, but likely records a name and possibly a dedication or funerary information.

EDCS-29400012.text_edition: \n NI votum solvit libens merito Maximo et Paterno consulibus\n
EDCS-29400012.translation: [---] willingly fulfilled his vow, as deserved, under the consulship of Maximo and Paternus.
EDCS-29400012.summary: This is a votive inscription recording the fulfillment of a vow during the consulship of Maximo and Paternus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29400013.text_edition: \n Deo sancto Herculi Titus Flavius Macrianus praefectus cohortis I Hispanorum eqquitum tribunus cohortis II Augustae Dacorum piae fidelis |(miliariae) eqquitum votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29400013.translation: To the holy god Hercules, Titus Flavius Macrianus, prefect of the I Cohort of Spanish Equites and tribune of the II Augustan Dacian Pia Fidelis Cohort (miliaria) Equites, willingly fulfilled his vow, as deserved.
EDCS-29400013.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Hercules by Titus Flavius Macrianus, a high-ranking military officer, detailing his commands, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29400016.text_edition: \n Dis deabusque Publius Marius Saturninus ex voto posuit libens merito\n
EDCS-29400016.translation: To the gods and goddesses, Publius Marius Saturninus dedicated this in fulfillment of his vow, willingly and as deserved.
EDCS-29400016.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to the gods and goddesses by Publius Marius Saturninus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29400017.text_edition: \n Fortunae Casuali Aelius Balbinus tesserarius votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29400017.translation: To Fortuna Casualis, Aelius Balbinus, a tesserarius, willingly fulfilled his vow, as deserved.
EDCS-29400017.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Fortuna Casualis by Aelius Balbinus, a military official, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29400019.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, Gaius I[---]
EDCS-29400019.summary: This is a votive inscription beginning with a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Gaius I[---], likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29400021.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Aurelius Diomedes qui vixit annos menses VIIII dies Marcus Aurelius Achilleus veteranus ex custode armorum legionis II adiutricis vivos sic <f=E>ecit <f=E>ilio karissimo \n
EDCS-29400021.translation: To the Divine Shades. Marcus Aurelius Diomedes, who lived 9 months and 7 days. Marcus Aurelius Achilleus, a veteran and former armour keeper of the II Adjutrix Legion, made this while alive for his dearest son.
EDCS-29400021.summary: A funerary inscription recording the death of Marcus Aurelius Diomedes and erected by his father, a veteran.

EDCS-29400022.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aureliae Sabi<n=M>ae quondam coniugi Aureli Abellonis veterani quae <vi=N>xit nnos XXX Aurelii Gratus et Grata filii heredes matri bene merenti fecerunt curante Aurelio Sabino tutore puerorum supra scriptorum hoc monumentum heredem non sequetur \n
EDCS-29400022.translation: To the Divine Shades. Aurelia Sabina, once the wife of Aurelius Abellon, a veteran, who lived 30 years. Aurelii Gratus and Grata, sons and heirs, made this monument for their well-deserving mother, with Aurelius Sabinus, guardian of the aforementioned children, overseeing it. This monument will not pass to the heir.
EDCS-29400022.summary: A funerary inscription honoring Aurelia Sabina, erected by her sons and heirs, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-29400024.summary: A fragmentary inscription mentioning a magistrate named Aureli, likely from an official context.

EDCS-29400028.summary: A simple inscription with two names, possibly funerary or commemorative.

EDCS-29400032.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest. Aurelius Zenobius.
EDCS-29400032.summary: A dedication to Jupiter made by Aurelius Zenobius.

EDCS-29400033.text_edition: \n Danuvio sacrum Vetulenus Apronianus legatus legionis II adiutricis piae fidelis \n
EDCS-29400033.translation: Sacred to the Danube. Vetulenus Apronianus, legate of the II Adjutrix Legion, dutiful and loyal.
EDCS-29400033.summary: A dedication to the river god Danuvius by Vetulenus Apronianus, a legate of the II Adjutrix Legion, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-29400036.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus domo sic aeternae et perpetuae securitati permittente patrono Alfius Vitalis libertus eius vivus sibi fecit\n
EDCS-29400036.translation: To the Divine Shades, for eternal and perpetual security, with the permission of his patron, Alfius Vitalis, his freedman, made this for himself while alive.
EDCS-29400036.summary: A funerary inscription where a freedman, Alfius Vitalis, dedicated a monument for himself with his patron's permission.

EDCS-29400037.text_edition: \n Bonae Deae et Panthaeo sic Dianae Silvanabus Gaius Iulius Valens praefectus legionis adiutricis piae fidelis ex voto\n
EDCS-29400037.translation: To the Good Goddess, and to Panthaeos, and to Diana, and to the Silvani. Gaius Iulius Valens, prefect of the Adjutrix Legion, dutiful and loyal, in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-29400037.summary: A votive inscription dedicated to multiple deities by Gaius Iulius Valens, prefect of the Adjutrix Legion.

EDCS-29400038.text_edition: \n Dis et Genio provinciae Pannoniae Caius Valerius Pudens legatus Augusti pro praetore\n
EDCS-29400038.translation: To the Divine Shades and the Genius of the province of Pannonia. Caius Valerius Pudens, imperial legate with proconsular power.
EDCS-29400038.summary: A dedication to the deities of Pannonia by Caius Valerius Pudens, the imperial legate, likely from the late 1st or early 2nd century CE.

EDCS-29400039.text_edition: \n Fortunae Augustae sacrum Caius Valerius Valentinus IIvir et Caius Iulius Victorinus et Publius Petronius Clemens aedilicii deccuriones municipii Aquincensium aram ex voto posuerunt III Idus Semptembres Falcone et Claro consulibus\n
EDCS-29400039.translation: To Fortuna Augusta. Caius Valerius Valentinus, duovir, and Caius Iulius Victorinus and Publius Petronius Clemens, officials of the aedilician rank, council members of the municipality of Aquincensium, placed this altar in fulfillment of a vow on the 11th day before the Ides of September in the consulship of Falcon and Clarus.
EDCS-29400039.summary: A votive altar dedicated to Fortuna Augusta by local magistrates and council members of Aquincensium, dated to 132 CE.

EDCS-29400040.text_edition: \n Fortunae huius loci Caius Valerius Pudens legatus Augusti pro praetore\n
EDCS-29400040.translation: To the Fortuna of this place, Caius Valerius Pudens, imperial legate with praetorian power.
EDCS-29400040.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the local Fortuna by an imperial legate, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-29400041.translation: To the Genius of the century, Aurelius Victor.
EDCS-29400041.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the genius of a centuria by Aurelius Victor, likely a soldier.

EDCS-29400042.text_edition: \n Genio |(centuriae) Claudi Tricci sacrum Aelius Sabinus signifer ex voto posuit libens merito\n
EDCS-29400042.translation: Sacred to the Genius of the century: Aelius Sabinus, standard-bearer, set this up in fulfillment of a vow, willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-29400042.summary: This inscription records a military dedication to the genius of a centuria by a standard-bearer, Aelius Sabinus.

EDCS-29400044.text_edition: \n Iunoni Caelesti Quintus Caecilius Rufinus Crepereianus consularis legatus Auggustorum pro praetore votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29400044.translation: To Juno Caelestis, Quintus Caecilius Rufinus Crepereianus, consular legate of the emperors, fulfilled his vow, willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-29400044.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled to Juno Caelestis by Quintus Caecilius Rufinus Crepereianus, a consular legate, probably in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-29400045.text_edition: \n Iunoni Reginae Cornelia Iulia pro se et suis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29400045.translation: To Juno Regina, Cornelia Iulia fulfilled her vow for herself and her family, willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-29400045.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled to Juno Regina by Cornelia Iulia for herself and her family.

EDCS-29400047.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Quintus Caecilius Rufinus Crepereianus consularis legatus Auggustorum pro praetore votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29400047.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Quintus Caecilius Rufinus Crepereianus, consular legate of the emperors, fulfilled his vow, willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-29400047.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Quintus Caecilius Rufinus Crepereianus, a consular legate, probably in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-29400048.text_edition: \n I Iovi Optimo Maximo Caius Iulius Rogatus miles legionis III Augustae veteranus legionis II adiutricis ex voto aram posuit libens Gentiano et Basso consulibus\n
EDCS-29400048.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Caius Iulius Rogatus, a soldier of the III Augusta legion, a veteran of the II Adjutrix legion, set up this altar in fulfillment of a vow, willingly and deservedly, in the consulship of Gentianus and Bassus.
EDCS-29400048.summary: This inscription records a military man's dedication of an altar to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, dated to the consulship of Gentianus and Bassus (likely 211 CE or later).

EDCS-29400049.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Lucretius Priscus veteranus legionis II adiutricis votum solvit libens\n
EDCS-29400049.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Lucretius Priscus, a veteran of the II Adjutrix legion, fulfilled his vow, willingly.
EDCS-29400049.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Lucretius Priscus, a veteran of the II Adjutrix legion.

EDCS-29400050.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Caius Iulius Geminus Capellianus legatus Augusti pro praetore \n
