EDCS-29400050.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Caius Iulius Geminus Capellianus, imperial legate with praetorian power.
EDCS-29400050.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Caius Iulius Geminus Capellianus, an imperial legate, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-29400052.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Dis Deabusque omnibus Titus Clementius Silvius vir egregius agens vicess praesidis solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-29400052.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest and to all the Gods and Goddesses, Titus Clementius Silvius, an outstanding man serving as deputy governor, fulfilled his vow, willingly, joyfully, and deservedly.
EDCS-29400052.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter and all the gods by Titus Clementius Silvius, a high-ranking imperial official, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-29400053.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Custodi Dis Deabusque Quintus Fabius Commodus tribunus legionis II adiutricis\n
EDCS-29400053.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, the Guardian, and to the gods and goddesses, Quintus Fabius Commodus, tribune of the Second Legion Adjutrix.
EDCS-29400053.summary: A dedication to Jupiter and other deities by a military tribune, likely from the 2nd century CE or later.

EDCS-29400054.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Iunoni Reginae Iulius Quintus veteranus ex beneficiario consularis votum solvit libens merito Arriano et Papo cons(ulibus)\n
EDCS-29400054.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest and to Juno Regina, Julius Quintus, a veteran and formerly a *beneficiarius consularis*, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow, with Arrianus and Papus as consuls.
EDCS-29400054.summary: A votive offering to Jupiter and Juno by a veteran, dated by the consuls Arrianus and Papus (likely 137 CE).

EDCS-29400055.text_edition: \n H OIV PIS Iovi Optimo Maximo Iunoni Reginae sacrum Septimius Iulianus miles pequarius sic legionis II adiutricis piae fidelis et Aelius Hilarus pro salute sua et suorum votum solverunt libentes merito Pio et Proculo consulibus\n
EDCS-29400055.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Best and Greatest, Juno Regina. Septimius Julianus, a soldier (*miles*) and pig-rearer (*pecquarius*) of the Second Legion Adjutrix, pious and faithful, and Aelius Hilarus, for their health and that of their families, willingly and deservedly fulfilled their vow, with Pius and Proculus as consuls.
EDCS-29400055.summary: A dedication by two soldiers of the Second Legion Adjutrix for their health, dated by the consuls Pius and Proculus (likely 196 CE).

EDCS-29400056.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Iunoni Reginae sacrum Marcus Ulpius Emeritus et Tiberius Claudius Exuperatus beneficiarii consulares legionis II adiutricis agentes curam legionis et coloniae Aquincensium votum libentes merito solverunt Fausstino et Rufino consulibus\n
EDCS-29400056.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Best and Greatest, Juno Regina. Marcus Ulpius Emeritus and Tiberius Claudius Exuperatus, *beneficiarii consulares* of the Second Legion Adjutrix, responsible for the legion and the colony of Aquincum, willingly and deservedly fulfilled their vow, with Faustinus and Rufinus as consuls.
EDCS-29400056.summary: A votive offering to Jupiter and Juno by two high-ranking soldiers of the Second Legion Adjutrix, dated by the consuls Faustinus and Rufinus (likely 197 CE).

EDCS-29400057.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Iunoni Neptuno Marti Lucius Valerius Italus optimo collegio negotiantium donum dedit\n
EDCS-29400057.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Juno, Neptune, and Mars, Lucius Valerius Italus gave this offering to the most excellent college of merchants.
EDCS-29400057.summary: A dedication by Lucius Valerius Italus to several gods on behalf of a merchants' association, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-29400061.text_edition: \n Minervae Victrici Caius Valerius Pudens legatus Augusti pro praetore\n
EDCS-29400061.translation: To Minerva Victrix, Gaius Valerius Pudens, imperial legate with praetorian authority.
EDCS-29400061.summary: A dedication to Minerva Victrix by Gaius Valerius Pudens, a high-ranking imperial governor, probably dating to the late 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-29400062.text_edition: \n Adonio Septimia Marcella et Septimius Silvanus et Septimius Marcianus pro filiis ex voto posuit sic\n
EDCS-29400062.translation: To Adonis, Septimia Marcella and Septimius Silvanus and Septimius Marcianus set this up according to their vow for their sons.
EDCS-29400062.summary: A votive dedication to Adonis by a family, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-29400063.text_edition: \n Balti D<e=I>ae Divinae et D<e=I>ae S<y=U>riae templum fecit Flavius \n
EDCS-29400063.translation: Flavius made a temple to the divine Balti and the divine Syria.
EDCS-29400063.summary: The construction of a temple to two goddesses, Balti and Syria, by Flavius, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-29400065.text_edition: \n Herculi Auggustorum Aurelius Firminus praefectus legionis II adiutricis ex protectore votum solvit libens merito Impperatoribus ddominis nnostris Diocletiano III et Maximiano Auggustis conssulibus\n
EDCS-29400065.translation: To Hercules Augustus, Aurelius Firminus, prefect of the Second Legion Adjutrix, formerly a *protector*, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow, during the consulship of our lords Emperors Diocletian III and Maximian Augusti.
EDCS-29400065.summary: A votive offering to Hercules Augustus by Aurelius Firminus, prefect of the Second Legion Adjutrix, dated to the joint consulship of Diocletian and Maximian (likely 293 CE).

EDCS-29400066.text_edition: \n Iunoni Reginae Publius Aelius Maximinus tabularius civitatis Eraviscorum votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29400066.translation: To Juno Regina, Publius Aelius Maximinus, a registrar (*tabularius*) of the Eravisci tribe, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-29400066.summary: A votive offering to Juno Regina by a local official of the Eravisci tribe, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-29400067.text_edition: \n Iunoni Reginae Kapito votum solvit libens merito Apro et Maximo consulibus\n
EDCS-29400067.translation: To Juno Regina, Kapito willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow, with Apro and Maximus as consuls.
EDCS-29400067.summary: A votive inscription to Juno Regina, fulfilled by Kapito during the consulship of Apro and Maximus.

EDCS-29400068.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Marcus Aurelius Eptacentus strator legati legionis II adiutricis piae <f=E>(idelis) Severianae ex voto votum solvit libens merito \n
EDCS-29400068.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Marcus Aurelius Eptacentus, adjutant to the legate of Legio II Adjutrix Pia Fidelis Severiana, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-29400068.summary: A votive inscription to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, offered by Marcus Aurelius Eptacentus, an adjutant of Legio II Adjutrix, likely in the Severian period.

EDCS-29400069.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Marcus? Aurelius anus s \n
EDCS-29400069.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Marcus? Aurelius anus s
EDCS-29400069.summary: An incomplete inscription dedicating something to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, possibly by Marcus Aurelius.

EDCS-29400070.text_edition: \n consul---) Iovi Optimo Maximo Caius Genialius Martialis OMIVA dono dedit\n
EDCS-29400070.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Caius Genialius Martialis, OMIVA, gave this offering.
EDCS-29400070.summary: An inscription recording a gift to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Caius Genialius Martialis, mentioning an abbreviation OMIVA.

EDCS-29400071.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Titus Flavius Fabia Romanus Roma beneficiarius procuratoris Augusti votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29400071.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Titus Flavius Fabia Romanus, Roma, beneficiary of the emperor's procurator, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-29400071.summary: A votive inscription to Jupiter Optimus Maximus offered by Titus Flavius Fabia Romanus, who served as a beneficiary of a procurator Augusti.

EDCS-29400072.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Teutano pro salute adque incolumitate Imperatoris Caesaris Aureliani Pii Felicis Invicti Augusti totiusque domus divinae eius et civitatis Eraviscorum Titus Flavius Titanus augur et Marcus Aurelius o consulibus\n
EDCS-29400072.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, for the safety and well-being of our lord Emperor Caesar Aurelianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, and of his entire divine household, and of the city of the Eravisci, Titus Flavius Titanus, augur, and Marcus Aurelius, consuls.
EDCS-29400072.summary: An inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus for the safety of Emperor Aurelian and the city of the Eravisci, dating to the consulship of Titus Flavius Titanus and Marcus Aurelius.

EDCS-29400073.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Iunoni Reginae Aurelius Victorinus strato<r=T> consularis votum solvit libens merito \n
EDCS-29400073.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Juno Regina, Aurelius Victorinus, consularis strator, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-29400073.summary: A votive inscription to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Juno Regina, fulfilled by Aurelius Victorinus, a former high-ranking official (strator consularis).

EDCS-29500102.text_edition: \n Pro salute domini nostri Imperatoris Marci Aureli Antonini Augusti Aurelii Florus et Mercator IIviri quinquennales coloniae Aquincensium tempulum Nemesis vetustate conlapsum restituerunt Messalla et Sabino consulibus VIII Kalendas Iulias Aurelio Suro salariario\n
EDCS-29500102.translation: For the safety of our lord Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, Aurelii Florus and Mercator, quinquennial duoviri of the colony of Aquincensium, restored the small temple of Nemesis, collapsed by age, on the 8th day before the Kalends of July, with Aurelius Sura, the salaried official, Messalla and Sabinus as consuls.
EDCS-29500102.summary: An inscription recording the restoration of a temple of Nemesis by two quinquennial duoviri of Aquincensium for the safety of Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, dated to 8 days before July 1st, during the consulship of Messalla and Sabinus.

EDCS-29500103.text_edition: \n Deae Dianae Nemesi Augustae honoribus et favoribus Gaio Iulio Victorin<o=I> equo publico aedilicio IIvirali et Tito Flavio Luciano quaestoricio IIvirali pontificibus quinquennalibus coloniae Aquincensium Pupilius Hyliatianus sic antesstis sic numini eius deae posuit V Kalendas Iulias Aemiliano et Basso consulibus \n
EDCS-29500103.translation: To the August Goddess Nemesis, for honors and favor, Pupilius Hyliatianus, who held the public horse, aedilician, and IIviral offices, and Titus Flavius Lucianus, quaestorian and IIviral, pontiffs with quinquennial authority of the colony of Aquincensium, set up these statues to her divinity, 5 days before the Kalends of July, with Aemilianus and Bassus as consuls.
EDCS-29500103.summary: An inscription recording the dedication of statues to the Goddess Nemesis by high-ranking magistrates of Aquincensium, including former pontiffs, dated to 5 days before July 1st, during the consulship of Aemilianus and Bassus.

EDCS-29500104.text_edition: \n Nemesi Omnipotenti Augustae Marcus Ulpius Zosimus XII Kalendas Septembres Rustico II et Aquilino\n
EDCS-29500104.translation: To the Almighty August Nemesis, Marcus Ulpius Zosimus, on the 12th day before the Kalends of September, under Rusticus II and Aquilinus.
EDCS-29500104.summary: An inscription dedicating something to the Almighty August Nemesis by Marcus Ulpius Zosimus, dated to 12 days before September 1st, during the consulship of Rusticus II and Aquilinus.

EDCS-29500105.summary: This is a dedication to the goddess Nemesis by Tetedius Agathimerus.

EDCS-29500106.text_edition: \n Genio loci Marcus Aurelius Surus Iunior Imperatore domino nostro Severo \n
EDCS-29500106.translation: To the Genius of the place, Marcus Aurelius Surus Iunior, under our lord Emperor Severus.
EDCS-29500106.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the Genius Loci by Marcus Aurelius Surus Iunior during the reign of Emperor Severus.

EDCS-29500108.text_edition: \n coloniae Aquincensium Popilius Hyliatianus sic V Kalendas Iulias --- co(n)s(ulibus)\n
EDCS-29500108.translation: To the colony of Aquincum, Popilius Hyliatianus, thus, five days before the Kalends of July, when [---] were consuls.
EDCS-29500108.summary: This inscription records an official act by Popilius Hyliatianus concerning the colony of Aquincum, dated by consulship.

EDCS-29500110.text_edition: \n LR Lucius Fabi nus P IIvir \n
EDCS-29500110.summary: This brief inscription records Lucius Fabinus as a duovir, likely a local magistrate.

EDCS-29500116.text_edition: \n Deo Silvano votum libiens solvi ego Legitimus\n
EDCS-29500116.translation: To the god Silvanus, I Legitimus gladly paid my vow.
EDCS-29500116.summary: This is a votive offering to the god Silvanus by Legitimus.

EDCS-29500117.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico Mansuetus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29500117.translation: To Silvanus Domestico, Mansuetus gladly paid his vow, as was deserved.
EDCS-29500117.summary: This is a votive offering to Silvanus Domestico by Mansuetus.

EDCS-29500118.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico Titius Faustinus beneficiarius consularis\n
EDCS-29500118.translation: To Silvanus Domestico, Titius Faustinus, aide of the consular governor.
EDCS-29500118.summary: This inscription records Titius Faustinus, an aide to a consular governor, dedicating something to Silvanus Domestico.

EDCS-29500119.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico Valerius Calboforus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29500119.translation: To Silvanus Domestico, Valerius Calboforus gladly paid his vow, as was deserved.
EDCS-29500119.summary: This is a votive offering to Silvanus Domestico by Valerius Calboforus.

EDCS-29500120.text_edition: \n Silvano Silvestri Augusto Gaius Iulius Proculinus quaestionarius legionis I adiutricis et Marci Aurelii Vibianus et Lucidus milites legionis II adiutricis \n
EDCS-29500120.translation: To Silvanus Silvestris Augustus, Gaius Iulius Proculinus, investigator of the First Adjutant Legion, and Marcus Aurelius Vibianus and Lucidus, soldiers of the Second Adjutant Legion.
EDCS-29500120.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Silvanus Silvestris Augustus made by military personnel from two legions.

EDCS-29500121.text_edition: \n Silvano et Silvanis sacrum Flavia Secundina votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29500121.translation: Sacred to Silvanus and the Silvanes, Flavia Secundina gladly paid her vow, as was deserved.
EDCS-29500121.summary: This is a votive offering to the god Silvanus and the female Silvanes by Flavia Secundina.

EDCS-29500122.text_edition: \n Deo Cautopati Marcus Antonius Victorinus decurio coloniae Aquincensium aedilis\n
EDCS-29500122.translation: To the god Cautopates, Marcus Antonius Victorinus, decurion of the colony of Aquincum, aedile.
EDCS-29500122.summary: This is a dedication to the god Cautopates by Marcus Antonius Victorinus, a decurion and aedile of Aquincum.

EDCS-29500123.text_edition: \n Fonti perenni Marcus Antonius Victorinus decurio coloniae Aquincensium aedilis\n
EDCS-29500123.translation: To the perennial spring, Marcus Antonius Victorinus, decurion of the colony of Aquincum, aedile.
EDCS-29500123.summary: This records a dedication to a perennial spring by Marcus Antonius Victorinus, a decurion and aedile of Aquincum.

EDCS-29500124.text_edition: \n Deo Cauti Marcus Antonius Victorinus decurio coloniae Aquincensium aedilis\n
EDCS-29500124.translation: To the god Cautus, Marcus Antonius Victorinus, decurion of the colony of Aquincum, aedile.
EDCS-29500124.summary: This is a dedication to the god Cautus by Marcus Antonius Victorinus, a decurion and aedile of Aquincum.

EDCS-29500125.translation: [...] decurion of the colony of Aquincum, aedile.
EDCS-29500125.summary: This fragment records the titles 'decurion of the colony of Aquincum' and 'aedile'.

EDCS-29500126.text_edition: \n Invicto Deo Soli Mithrae Claudius Patasio votum solvit libens merito Pedone et Bradua consulibus VIII Idus Iunias\n
EDCS-29500126.translation: To Sol Mithras the Invincible, Claudius Patasio fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly, in the consulship of Pedo and Bradua, on the eighth day before the Ides of June.
EDCS-29500126.summary: This is a votive inscription to Sol Mithras the Invincible by Claudius Patasio, dated by consuls to the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-29500127.text_edition: \n Soli Invicto Mithrae sacrum Gaius Flavius Avitus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29500127.translation: Sacred to Sol Mithras the Invincible, Gaius Flavius Avitus fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-29500127.summary: This is a votive inscription to Sol Mithras the Invincible by Gaius Flavius Avitus.

EDCS-29500128.text_edition: \n Terrae Matri Aurelius Droles miles legionis II adiutricis piae fidelis Severianae votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29500128.translation: To the Mother Earth, Aurelius Droles, a soldier of the II Adiutrix Pia Fidelis Severiana legion, fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-29500128.summary: This is a votive inscription to the Mother Earth by Aurelius Droles, a soldier of the II Adiutrix legion.

EDCS-29500129.text_edition: \n Urbi Romae Lucius Cassius Marcellinus legatus Augusti pro praetore consul designatus\n
EDCS-29500129.translation: To the city of Rome, Lucius Cassius Marcellinus, imperial legate with proconsular power, consul-designate.
EDCS-29500129.summary: This records titles of Lucius Cassius Marcellinus, including imperial legate and consul-designate, likely in the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-29500130.text_edition: \n ] Caius Iulius Septimius Castinus consul designatus legatus Aug[ggustorum] pro praetore Pannoniae inferioris legatus <leg>(ionis) I Minerviae ex praecepto dominorum nnnostrorum dux vexillationum IIII Germanicarum VIII Augustae XVIII Primigeniae I Minerviae XXX Ulpiae adversus defectores et rebelles proconsul Cretae et Cyrenarum iuridicus per Apuliam Calabriam Lucaniam Bruttios curator viae Salariae curator Aeclanensium praetor tutelarius tribunus plebis quaestor tribunus militum legionis I adiutricis item V Macedonicae\n
EDCS-29500130.translation: [...] Caius Iulius Septimius Castinus, consul-designate, imperial legate with proconsular power over Lower Pannonia, legate of the I Minerva legion by order of our lords, commander of four vexillations of the IV Germanica, VIII Augusta, XVIII Primigenia, I Minerva, XXX Ulpia, against defectors and rebels, proconsul of Crete and Cyrene, iuridicus through Apulia, Calabria, Lucania, Bruttium, curator of the Salarian Way, curator of the people of Aeclanum, praetor tutelarius, tribune of the plebs, quaestor, military tribune of the I Adiutrix and V Macedonica legions.
EDCS-29500130.summary: This extensive inscription lists the numerous prestigious offices held by Caius Iulius Septimius Castinus, dating to the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-29500131.text_edition: \n Caius Iulius Septimius Castinus consul designatus legatus Augggustorum pro praetore Pannoniae inferioris legatus legionis I Minerviae ex praecepto dominorum nnnostrorum dux vexillationum IIII Germanicarum VIII Augustae XVIII Primigeniae I Minerviae XXX Ulpiae adversus defectores et rebelles proconsul Cretae et Cyrenarum iuridicus per Apuliam Calabriam Lucaniam Bruttios curator viae Salariae curator Ae<c=T>lanensium praetor tutelarius tribunus plebis quaestor tribunus militum legionis I adiutricis item V Macedonicae \n
EDCS-29500131.translation: Caius Iulius Septimius Castinus, consul-designate, imperial legate with proconsular power over Lower Pannonia, legate of the I Minerva legion by order of our lords, commander of four vexillations of the IV Germanica, VIII Augusta, XVIII Primigenia, I Minerva, XXX Ulpia, against defectors and rebels, proconsul of Crete and Cyrene, iuridicus through Apulia, Calabria, Lucania, Bruttium, curator of the Salarian Way, curator of the people of Aeclanum, praetor tutelarius, tribune of the plebs, quaestor, military tribune of the I Adiutrix and V Macedonica legions.
EDCS-29500131.summary: This extensive inscription lists the numerous prestigious offices held by Caius Iulius Septimius Castinus, dating to the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-29500132.text_edition: \n ] Caius Iulius Septimius Castinus consul designatus legatus Augggustorum pro praetore Pannoniae inferioris legatus legionis I Minerviae ex praecepto dominorum nnnostrorum dux vexillationum IIII Germanicarum VIII Augustae XXVIII Primigeniae I Minerviae XXX Ulpiae adversus defectores et rebelles proconsul Cretae et Cyrenarum iuridicus per Apuliam Calabriam Lucaniam Bruttios curator viae Salariae curator Ae<c=T>lanensium praetor tutelarius tribunus plebis quaestor tribunus militum legionis I adiutricis item V Macedonicae \n
EDCS-29500132.translation: Gaius Julius Septimius Castinus, consul-elect, imperial legate with proconsular authority of Pannonia Inferior, legate of the I Minerva legion, by order of our lords the emperors, commander of detachments of the IV Germanica, VIII Augusta, XXVIII Primigenia, I Minerva, XXX Ulpia, against defectors and rebels; proconsul of Crete and Cyrene; legal official for Apulia, Calabria, Lucania, and the Bruttii; curator of the Via Salaria; curator of the Aethelans; praetor; tutor; quaestor; military tribune of the I Adjutrix legion; also of the V Macedonica.
EDCS-29500132.summary: This inscription is a career summary of Gaius Julius Septimius Castinus, detailing his military and administrative appointments.

EDCS-29500133.text_edition: \n Aurelius Maximus veteranus legionis II adiutricis piae fidelis Antoninianae \n
EDCS-29500133.translation: Aurelius Maximus, a veteran of the II Adjututrix Pia Fidelis Antoniniana legion.
EDCS-29500133.summary: This is a brief epitaph or dedication by Aurelius Maximus, a veteran of a specific Roman legion.

EDCS-29500134.text_edition: \n Claudius Pompeius Faustus decurio coloniae Aquincensium aedilicius IIviralis praefectus collegii fabrum votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29500134.translation: Claudius Pompeius Faustus, decurion of the colony of Aquincum, formerly aedile and duumvir, commander of the guild of craftsmen, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-29500134.summary: This inscription records Claudius Pompeius Faustus fulfilling a vow, listing his civic and guild offices in Aquincum.

EDCS-29500135.text_edition: \n Caius Iulius Mar signifer Lucius Septimius Vitalis tesserarius Lucius Septimius Ianuarius custos armorum Maximo et Aeliano consulibus votum solverunt libentes merito\n
EDCS-29500135.translation: Gaius Julius Mar, standard-bearer; Lucius Septimius Vitalis, signaller; Lucius Septimius Ianuarius, armourer; under the consulship of Maximo and Aelianus, they willingly and deservedly fulfilled their vow.
EDCS-29500135.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by three soldiers (a standard-bearer, signaller, and armourer) during the consulship of Maximo and Aelianus.

EDCS-29500136.translation: Statilius Proculus, in memory of his century.
EDCS-29500136.summary: This is a memorial dedication by Statilius Proculus, likely related to his service in a Roman army century.

EDCS-29500138.text_edition: \n Iustus tribunus militum legionis X geminae votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29500138.translation: Iustus, military tribune of the X Gemina legion, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-29500138.summary: This inscription records Iustus, a military tribune, fulfilling a vow to the X Gemina legion.

EDCS-29500139.text_edition: \n us speculator legionis II adiutricis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-29500139.translation: A speculator of the II Adjutrix legion, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-29500139.summary: This inscription records an unnamed speculator from the II Adjutrix legion fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-29500144.translation: Under the consulship of Pius and Proculus.
EDCS-29500144.summary: This is a dating formula, noting the consuls Pius and Proculus, likely the start of a larger inscription.

EDCS-29500145.text_edition: \n Modesto et Probo consulibus Aprotect--- votum solvit libens merito domino nostro Probo Augusto III et Paterno iterum consulibus\n
EDCS-29500145.translation: Under the consulship of Modestus and Probus, Aprotectus fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly to our lord Probus Augustus for the third time and Paternus for the second time.
EDCS-29500145.summary: This inscription records Aprotectus fulfilling a vow during the consulship of Modestus and Probus, and the third consulship of Emperor Probus.

EDCS-29500146.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus balneum a solo territorio legionis II adiutricis piae fidelis Severianae fecit curante Flavio Marciano consulari\n
EDCS-29500146.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus built the bath from the ground up for the territory of the II Adjutrix Pia Fidelis Severiana legion, under the supervision of Flavius Marcianus, a consular official.
EDCS-29500146.summary: This inscription records Emperor Severus Alexander funding the construction of a bathhouse for the II Adjutrix legion, overseen by Flavius Marcianus.

EDCS-29500147.text_edition: \n Imperatori Antonino Augusto Suetrius Sabinus legatus Augusti pro praetore provinciae nnoniae inferioris devotissimus numini eius\n
EDCS-29500147.translation: To Emperor Antoninus Augustus, Suetrius Sabinus, imperial legate with praetorian authority of the province of Lower Pannonia, most devoted to his divine majesty.
EDCS-29500147.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Emperor Antoninus Augustus by Suetrius Sabinus, the imperial legate of Lower Pannonia, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-29500148.summary: This inscription appears to record the name of Aurelius Gentilis Valerius, likely marking a grave or a property owner.

EDCS-29500149.summary: This inscription records the name of Aurelius Julianus, likely marking a grave or a property owner.

EDCS-29500150.summary: This inscription records the name of Aurelius Septimus, likely marking a grave or a property owner.

EDCS-29500152.summary: This inscription marks a place belonging to Bonathus, possibly a property marker or grave.

EDCS-29500154.summary: This inscription records the name of Julius Felix, likely marking a grave or a property owner.

EDCS-29500155.translation: Of Valerius Julianus and Aelius Quintus.
EDCS-29500155.summary: This inscription records the names of Valerius Julianus and Aelius Quintus, likely marking a shared grave or property.

EDCS-29500156.summary: This inscription records the name of Gaius Valerius Servinus, likely marking a grave or a property owner.

EDCS-29500157.text_edition: \n Locus Valeri karcerarii sic legionis II ad(iutricis)\n
EDCS-29500157.translation: Place of Valerius the jailer of the Second Adjutrix Legion.
EDCS-29500157.summary: This inscription marks a place belonging to Valerius, who served as a jailer for the Second Adjutrix Legion.

EDCS-29500161.summary: This inscription records the name of Severinus, identified as a veteran, likely marking his grave or a monument.

EDCS-29500174.text_edition: \n Caius Titius Cai filius Sergia Antonius Peculiaris decurio coloniae Septimiae Aquinci item decurio municipii Singiduni ornamentum forus sic rei publicae donum dedit\n
EDCS-29500174.translation: Gaius Titius Antonius Peculiaris, son of Gaius, of the Sergia tribe, a decurion of the colony of Aquincum and also a decurion of the municipium of Singidunum, gave this ornament for public display to the state.
EDCS-29500174.summary: This inscription records an ornamental public gift made by a decurion of Aquincum and Singidunum, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-29500178.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publius Aelius Claudia Lucus Thessalonica veteranus legionis II adiutricis et Publius Aelius Lycus veteranus legionis eiusdem <h=A>eres amico bene merenti fecit \n
EDCS-29500178.translation: To the departed spirits. Publius Aelius Lycus, a veteran of the same legion, and Publius Aelius Lucus of Thessalonica, a veteran of the II Adjutrix legion, erected this for their well-deserving friend.
EDCS-29500178.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates a well-deserving friend, erected by two veterans of the II Adjutrix legion, probably from the late 1st to the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-29500179.text_edition: \n D(is) M(anibus) Albio Aemilio Maximo Conconnibito Pollioni annorum L avunculo et Albiae Ingenuae coniugi et Albio Aemiliano et Ingenuo filiis et Maximae filiae eius et Naeviae Ianuariae sorori dulci vivis posuit Sextus Naevius Tertius tutor\n
EDCS-29500179.translation: To the departed spirits. Sextus Naevius Tertius, as guardian, erected this for his uncle Albius Aemilius Maximus Conconnibitus Pollio, aged 50, for his wife Albia Ingenua, for his sons Albius Aemilianus and Ingenuus, for his daughter Maxima, and for his sister Naevia Ianuaria, his sweet sister.
EDCS-29500179.summary: This funerary inscription records the erection of a tomb by a guardian for his deceased relatives, including his uncle, wife, children, and sister, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-29500180.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Annia Iuliane Anni Callimachi primi pili filia vixit annos XXV Aurelius Philippus |(centurio) coniugi suae pientissimae fecit memoriam sic\n
EDCS-29500180.translation: To the departed spirits. Annia Iuliana, daughter of Annius Callimachus, former primipilus, lived 25 years. Aurelius Philippus, a centurion, made this memorial for his most devoted wife.
EDCS-29500180.summary: This funerary inscription records the erection of a memorial by a centurion for his wife, Annia Iuliana, who died at age 25, probably dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-29500181.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelio Aulupori veterano legionis II adiutricis Aurelius Vitalis \n
EDCS-29500181.translation: To the departed spirits. Aurelius Vitalis [erected this] for Aurelius Aulupor, a veteran of the II Adjutrix legion.
EDCS-29500181.summary: This funerary inscription records the erection of a tomb for Aurelius Aulupor, a veteran of the II Adjutrix legion, by Aurelius Vitalis, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-29500182.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Aurelio Flavo militi legionis II adiutricis duplario et interpetri sic Germanorum officii consularis et Marco Aurelio annorum XIII filio communi Aurelia Quaeta marito et filio dulcissimis scribi in memoriam iussit\n
EDCS-29500182.translation: To the departed spirits. Aurelia Quaeta ordered this to be inscribed in memory of her husband Marcus Aurelius Flavus, a soldier and interpreter in the II Adjutrix legion, a duplarius, and of the Germans of the consular office, and of their common son Marcus Aurelius, aged 13, to her most beloved husband and son.
EDCS-29500182.summary: This funerary inscription records the erection of a memorial by a wife and mother for her husband, a soldier and interpreter in the II Adjutrix legion, and their young son, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-29500183.text_edition: \n Aurelio Messio equiti legionis II adiutricis stependiorum sic X vixit annis XXX Aurelius Mesuquas contibernalis sic et heres eiusdem fecit\n
EDCS-29500183.translation: For Aurelius Messius, a cavalryman in the II Adjutrix legion, who served for 10 periods and lived 30 years. Aurelius Mesuquas, his tentmate and heir, erected this.
EDCS-29500183.summary: This funerary inscription records the erection of a memorial by a tentmate and heir for Aurelius Messius, a cavalryman in the II Adjutrix legion who served 10 terms and lived 30 years, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-29500184.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Aurelius Titus qui vixit annos LXX et coniugi eius Aureliae Maternae quae vixit annos L item filiorum eorum Marco Aurelio Titiano qui militavit in cohorte I Ast<u=O>rum qui vixit annos XXX item Aurelio Surae qui vixit annos XX item Aurelio Valenti qui vixit annos XII posuit titulum parentibus suis Marcus Aurelius Clemens |(centurio) legionis VI Ferratae qui est probatus in legione II adiutrice a Cornelio Plotiano legato \n
EDCS-29500184.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcus Aurelius Clemens, a centurion of the VI Ferrata legion, who was approved in the II Adjutrix legion by the legate Cornelius Plotianus, erected this monument for his parents Marcus Aurelius Titus, who lived 70 years, and Aurelia Materna, who lived 50 years, and for their sons Marcus Aurelius Titianus, who served in the I Cohort of Asturians and lived 30 years, Aurelius Surae, who lived 20 years, and Aurelius Valens, who lived 12 years.
EDCS-29500184.summary: This funerary inscription records the erection of a monument by a centurion for his parents and three brothers, with details about their lives and military service, likely dating to the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-29500186.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Claudio Agesonio Theodoro qui vixit nnos IIII menses III dies XX Agesonius Kalandinus |(centurio) protector et Caerellia Theodora filio carissimo faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-29500186.translation: To the departed spirits. Agesonius Kalandinus, a centurion protector, and Caerellia Theodora arranged for this to be made for their dearest son Claudius Agesonius Theodorus, who lived 4 years, 3 months, and 20 days.
EDCS-29500186.summary: This funerary inscription records the erection of a tomb for a young child, Claudius Agesonius Theodorus, by his parents, one of whom was a centurion protector, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-29500187.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tiberio Claudio Constanti militi legionis II adiutricis et Claudio Firmino et Claudiae Firmae Claudius Tacitus posuit patrono matri fratri pientissimis et sibi vivus\n
EDCS-29500187.translation: To the departed spirits. Claudius Tacitus erected this for his most devoted patron Tiberius Claudius Constans, a soldier in the II Adjutrix legion, and for Claudius Firminus and Claudia Firma, his mother and brother, and for himself while living.
EDCS-29500187.summary: This funerary inscription records the erection of a tomb by Claudius Tacitus for his patron, his mother, his brother, and himself, with the patron being a soldier in the II Adjutrix legion, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-29601408.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tiberius Claudius Dasius veteranus legionis II adiutricis hic situs est vixit annos Claudia Irene liberta nomine collegii Cereris patrono pientissimo et sibi faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-29601408.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Tiberius Claudius Dasius, a veteran of the Second Adiutrix Legion, rests here. He lived [X] years. Claudia Irene, his freedwoman, with the college of Ceres, had this made for her most devoted patron and for herself.
EDCS-29601408.summary: A tombstone for Tiberius Claudius Dasius, a veteran of the Second Adiutrix Legion, erected by his freedwoman and the college of Ceres, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29601410.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publio Sittio Publi filio Papiria Theveste ex Africa Mustiolo veterano legionis II adiutricis qui vixit annos LXXXX Publii Sittii Donatus Mustiolus et Faustina heredes per Sittium Doretus sic patrem fecerunt\n
EDCS-29601410.translation: To the Divine Spirits. For Publius Sittius Mustiolus, a veteran of the Second Adiutrix Legion, son of Publius, from Theveste in Africa, who lived 90 years. Publii Sittii Donatus Mustiolus and Faustina, his heirs, had this made for their father through Sittius Doretus.
EDCS-29601410.summary: A tombstone for Publius Sittius Mustiolus, a veteran of the Second Adiutrix Legion, erected by his heirs, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29601411.text_edition: \n Marcus Valerius Aniensi Fidus Foro Iuliensium miles legionis X geminae centuria Vibiani stipendiorum VIIII heres fecit\n
EDCS-29601411.translation: Marcus Valerius Fidus of Forum Iulii, a soldier in the Tenth Gemina Legion, in the century of Vibianus, with 9 years of service, erected this as heir.
EDCS-29601411.summary: A funerary inscription for Marcus Valerius Fidus, a soldier of the Tenth Gemina Legion, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29601412.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Valerio Maximiano armorum custodi legionis IIII Flaviae stipendiorum IX vixit annis XXX Aurelius Fronto tubicen legionis eiusdem heres eius posuit\n
EDCS-29601412.translation: To the Divine Spirits. For Gaius Valerius Maximianus, armor keeper of the Fourth Flavia Felix Legion, with 9 years of service. He lived 30 years. Aurelius Fronto, a trumpeter of the same legion, his heir, placed this.
EDCS-29601412.summary: A tombstone for Gaius Valerius Maximianus, an armor keeper in the Fourth Flavia Felix Legion, erected by his heir, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29601413.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valerius Valenti tesserario legionis qui vixit \n
EDCS-29601413.translation: To the Divine Spirits. For Valerius Valenti, a tesserarius (guard commander) of the legion, who lived [X] years.
EDCS-29601413.summary: A tombstone fragment for Valerius Valenti, a tesserarius of a legion, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29601414.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus et memoriae Valeri Veri quondam militis legionis librarii consularis Valerius Iulianus decurio coloniae Aquincensis filio carissimo\n
EDCS-29601414.translation: To the Divine Spirits and memory of Valerius Verus, formerly a soldier and scribe of a consular legion. Valerius Iulianus, decurion of the colony of Aquincum, for his dearest son.
EDCS-29601414.summary: A funerary inscription for Valerius Verus, a former soldier and scribe, set up by his son, a decurion in Aquincum, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29601415.text_edition: \n Octaviae Revoccae coniugi sanctissimae quae vixit nnos XXV Marcus Ulpius Natalis veteranus ex beneficiario consularis et Marci Ulpi Octavianus et Sabinianus filii piissimi\n
EDCS-29601415.translation: To Octavia Revocca, my most holy wife, who lived 25 years. Marcus Ulpius Natalis, a veteran, formerly a beneficiary of a consul, and his sons Marcus Ulpius Octavianus and Sabinianus, my most devoted sons.
EDCS-29601415.summary: A funerary monument for Octavia Revocca, wife of a veteran, erected by her husband and sons, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29601416.text_edition: \n S Valeriae Ursulae filiae nnorum XVI et Valerio Constanti coniu<g=C>i nnorum LXX et Valerio Martino filio nnorum XXII hic siti sunt \n
EDCS-29601416.translation: Are buried here: Valeria Ursula, daughter, 16 years old, and Valerius Constans, husband, 70 years old, and Valerius Martinus, son, 22 years old.
EDCS-29601416.summary: A tombstone recording the burial of three family members: Valeria Ursula, Valerius Constans, and Valerius Martinus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29601417.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Vigilius Primullus miles legionis II adiutricis stipendiorum XVII Iulia Victorina coniugi carissimo faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-29601417.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Vigilius Primullus, soldier of the Second Adiutrix Legion, with 17 years of service. Iulia Victorina, his dearest wife, had this made.
EDCS-29601417.summary: A tombstone for Vigilius Primullus, a soldier of the Second Adiutrix Legion, erected by his wife, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29601419.text_edition: \n Marcus TIA GE Aurelius Fortunat Favius Favil Favius Til tus Aurelius Vatis sic Naevius AG Victor et Firmus Decumus et Aeliano consulibus iusserunt? fieri\n
EDCS-29601419.translation: Marcus TIA GE Aurelius Fortunat Favius Favil Favius Til tus Aurelius Vatis sic Naevius AG Victor and Firmus Decumus and Aelianus ordered this to be made under the consuls?
EDCS-29601419.summary: An incomplete inscription possibly recording the erection of a monument or building, mentioning several individuals and possibly consuls, its precise nature is unclear due to damage and illegibility.

EDCS-29601421.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelio Eufimiano sic PACMIO sepulcri iura CAVII et Uriacio sic filio sic vivus sic et Tigris coniugi <k=H>arissimo et filio faciendum curavit \n
EDCS-29601421.translation: To the departed spirits of Aurelius Eufimianus, of the family of Pacmius, and of Cavius and Uriatius, his son. He took care to have the rights of the sepulchre made for himself while alive, and for Tigris, his dearest wife, and son.
EDCS-29601421.summary: This funerary inscription, likely from the 2nd-3rd century CE, records the commission of a tomb by Aurelius Eufimianus for himself, his wife Tigris, and his son, possibly related to the Pacmii and Cavii families.

EDCS-29601422.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iuliae Laetillae Marcus Aurelius Thaegenes decurio municipii Bregetionensium sic coniugi carissimae faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-29601422.translation: To the departed spirits of Iulia Laetilla. Marcus Aurelius Thaegenes, a decurion of the municipality of Bregetion, took care to have this made for his dearest wife.
EDCS-29601422.summary: This funerary inscription, likely from Bregetion (modern-day Hungary), records a tomb erected by Marcus Aurelius Thaegenes, a local decurion, for his beloved wife Iulia Laetilla.

EDCS-29601423.text_edition: \n Dis Titi Flavi Crispini Manibus decurionis municipii Brigetionis equo publico et Ulpiae Gemellinae matris eius Titus Flavius Eutyches libertus et heres\n
EDCS-29601423.translation: To the departed spirits of Titus Flavius Crispinus, decurion of the municipality of Brigetio with public horse, and of Ulpia Gemellina, his mother. Titus Flavius Eutyches, freedman and heir.
EDCS-29601423.summary: This inscription records the monument for Titus Flavius Crispinus, a decurion of Brigetio, and his mother Ulpia Gemellina, set up by his freedman and heir Titus Flavius Eutyches.

EDCS-29601424.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Floria Matrona quae vixit annis XL diebus numero V Marcus Petilius Clementinus decurio Aquinci quaestor coniugi <f=E>aciendum curavit \n
EDCS-29601424.translation: To the departed spirits of Floria Matrona, who lived 40 years and 5 days. Marcus Petilius Clementinus, quaestor and decurion of Aquincum, took care to have this made for his wife.
EDCS-29601424.summary: This funerary inscription from Aquincum records the monument for Floria Matrona, erected by her husband Marcus Petilius Clementinus, who held the offices of quaestor and decurion.

EDCS-29601425.text_edition: \n Septimio Victorino @(obito) decurioni coloniae Aquincensium et Potentinae coniugi eius et Tenacio militi legionis filio \n
EDCS-29601425.translation: To the departed spirits of Septimius Victorinus, who died as decurion of the colony of Aquincum, and of Potentina, his wife, and of Tenacius, a soldier in the legion, his son.
EDCS-29601425.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Septimius Victorinus, a decurion of Aquincum, and honors his wife Potentina and son Tenacius, a legionary soldier.

EDCS-29601426.text_edition: \n Ael Dom et Magiis et Emerito filiis? --- Caius Magius veteranus legionis \n
EDCS-29601426.translation: To Aelius, Domitius, and Magius, and Emeritus, sons? Caius Magius, a veteran of the legion.
EDCS-29601426.summary: This fragmentary inscription honors several individuals, possibly sons named Aelius, Domitius, Magius, and Emeritus, and identifies Caius Magius as a legionary veteran.

EDCS-29601427.text_edition: \n Cai- Iul-? E IE Aelius Iustus et Sossia Herais Aelio Victori filio carissimo posuerunt vixit annos VI posuerunt memoriam\n
EDCS-29601427.translation: Caius Iulius? Aelius Iustus and Sossia Herais placed this memorial for Aelius Victor, their dearest son, who lived for 6 years.
EDCS-29601427.summary: This memorial inscription records that Aelius Iustus and Sossia Herais erected a monument for their son Aelius Victor, who died at the age of six.

EDCS-29601428.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Antistiae Antisti filiae Firmae annorum XXV Servenius Urio coniugi pientissimae posuit DIOST TAECIMMR MQVI M ERSE\n
EDCS-29601428.translation: To the departed spirits of Antistia Firma, daughter of Antistius, aged 25. Servenius Urio placed this for his most pious wife. [--- illegible ---]
EDCS-29601428.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Antistia Firma, aged 25, and its erection by her husband Servenius Urio, followed by illegible text.

EDCS-29601429.text_edition: \n Lucius Aurelius Fortunatus natus natione Ca<p=R>padox nnorum LXXX hic situs collegium cultorum posuit \n
EDCS-29601429.translation: Lucius Aurelius Fortunatus, born of Cappadocian stock, aged 80. The guild of worshippers placed him here.
EDCS-29601429.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Lucius Aurelius Fortunatus, an 80-year-old Cappadocian, and states that he was buried by his collegium (guild).

EDCS-29601431.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aureliae Victorinae quae vixit annos XVIII menses II dies Aurelia Quinta filiae suae dulcissimae memoriam f(ecit)\n
EDCS-29601431.translation: To the departed spirits of Aurelia Victorina, who lived 18 years and 2 months. Aurelia Quinta made this memorial for her sweetest daughter.
EDCS-29601431.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Aurelia Victorina, aged 18 years and 2 months, and its erection by her mother Aurelia Quinta.

EDCS-29601432.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Euhelpistus libertus ob merita memoriae haec meruit\n
EDCS-29601432.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Euhelpistus, a freedman, earned this for his meritorious memory.
EDCS-29601432.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording that Euhelpistus, a freedman, erected this monument for himself due to his good deeds and memory.

EDCS-29601433.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flavia Carula quae vixit nnos VIIII menses II dies XXVII parentes vivi faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-29601433.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Flavia Carula, who lived 9 years, 2 months, and 27 days. Her parents, while alive, arranged for this to be made.
EDCS-29601433.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Flavia Carula, who died at a young age, with her parents commissioning the monument.

EDCS-29601434.text_edition: \n Marco Furio Pollia Rufo canaliclario et Memmiae Servandae coniugi eius Statius Constans frater et heres eius faciendum curavit cives Agrippinenses Transalpini \n
EDCS-29601434.translation: To Marcus Furius Rufus, a pipe layer, and his wife Memmia Servanda. Statius Constans, his brother and heir, and citizens of Agrippinenses Transalpini, arranged for this to be made.
EDCS-29601434.summary: This is a funerary inscription commissioned by Statius Constans for his brother Marcus Furius Rufus and his wife Memmia Servanda, with the mention of their origin as citizens of Agrippinenses Transalpini.

EDCS-29601435.text_edition: \n no avunculo et Geniali genero item aetatis Iuliae Quintinae sorori et Secundinae nepoti emae pientissimae\n
EDCS-29601435.translation: to [my] uncle and Genialis, son-in-law, and also to Julia Quintina, my sister, and to Secundina, my most devoted granddaughter.
EDCS-29601435.summary: This inscription appears to be a fragment of a longer funerary dedication, listing relatives including an uncle, son-in-law, sister, and granddaughter.

EDCS-29601436.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulio Alexandro et Iuliae Crispinae nnorum V<I=L> Iulius Crispus fratri et Aelia Maximina filiae posuerunt \n
EDCS-29601436.translation: To the Divine Spirits. For Julius Alexander and Julia, [aged] 5 years. Julius Crispus, for his brother, and Aelia Maximina, for her daughter, placed this.
EDCS-29601436.summary: This funerary inscription records a monument erected by Julius Crispus for his brother and Aelia Maximina for her daughter, both of whom died young.

EDCS-29601437.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Iulius Euritus domo Alexandria nnorum XXX hic situs est fecerunt Iulii Crispinus et Lynx conliberti et fratres\n
EDCS-29601437.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Caius Julius Euritus, from Alexandria, aged 30 years, is buried here. His freedmen and brothers, Julius Crispinus and Lynx, arranged for this to be made.
EDCS-29601437.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Caius Julius Euritus, a 30-year-old man from Alexandria, with the monument erected by his freedmen who were also like brothers.

EDCS-29601438.summary: This is a very brief funerary inscription recording the burial of Macrinia Alexandria.

EDCS-29601440.summary: This is a fragmented inscription that appears to be part of a funerary monument, recording the name Quintilius Valens and the beginning of his lifespan.

EDCS-29601441.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Septimio Colono Attusonio mulioni qui vixit annos amma coniugi carissimo ex suo fecit\n
EDCS-29601441.translation: To the Divine Spirits. For Septimius Colonus, a fuller, who lived for years. Amma made this for her dearest husband from her own resources.
EDCS-29601441.summary: This is a funerary inscription where Amma commissioned a monument for her husband Septimius Colonus, a fuller, using her own money.

EDCS-29601444.text_edition: \n CLARA fratri heres institutus et sibi vivus posuit\n
EDCS-29601444.translation: Clara, instituted as heir, erected this for her brother and for herself while alive.
EDCS-29601444.summary: This inscription records that Clara erected a monument for her brother and also for herself during her lifetime, having been appointed as his heir.

EDCS-29601445.text_edition: \n Volusius L Iuli M vixit X tice \n
EDCS-29601445.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Volusius, likely from the early Imperial period.

EDCS-29601448.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription where Cocceia is identified as the maker or dedicator of a monument, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29601449.translation: to the most dutiful freedmen of Gaia.
EDCS-29601449.summary: This text likely records a dedication to freedmen, possibly from a funerary monument or a public dedication, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29601453.text_edition: \n legionis II adiutricis militibus legionis eiusdem civibusque Eraviscis \n
EDCS-29601453.translation: to the soldiers of the II Adjutrix legion and the citizens of Eravisci.
EDCS-29601453.summary: This is likely a dedication from a military monument or a public work, honoring soldiers of the II Adjutrix legion and the Eravisci citizens, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-29601457.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus tribunicia potestate XV consul III et Imperator Caesar Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus tribunicia potestate consul II divi Antonini filii divi Hadriani nepotes divi Traiani Parthici pronepotes divi Nervae abnepotes Sirmi milia passuum III\n
EDCS-29601457.translation: 3 Roman miles from Sirmium, from Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, holding tribunician power for the 15th time, consul for the 3rd time, and Emperor Caesar Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus, holding tribunician power for the 1st time, consul for the 2nd time, sons of the deified Antoninus, grandsons of the deified Hadrian, great-grandsons of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-grandsons of the deified Nerva.
EDCS-29601457.summary: This milestone inscription from 172 AD records the distance from Sirmium and names Emperors Marcus Aurelius and Lucius Verus, reflecting shared rule and imperial titles.

EDCS-29601458.text_edition: \n mperatori Caesari Lucio Septimio Severo Pio Pertinaci Augusto Arabico Adiabenico Parthico maximo divi Marci filio divi Commodi fratri divi Antonini Pii nepoti divi <Ha=AH>driani pronepoti divi Traiani Parthici abnepoti divi Nervae abnepoti patri patriae imperatori XI tribunicia potestate VI consuli II proconsuli et Imperatori Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto Luci Septimi Severi Pertinacis Augusti nostri filio divi Antonini nepoti divi Antonini Pii pronepoti divi Hadriani abnepoti [] divi Traiani Parthici et divi Nervae abnepoti et Lucio [Septimio Getae Caesari] Augusti nostri filio curante Tiberio Claudio Claudiano legato Auggustorum pro praetore a Sirmio milia passuum III \n
EDCS-29601458.translation: 3 Roman miles from Sirmium, for Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus, son of the deified Marcus, brother of the deified Commodus, grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva, father of the fatherland, emperor for the 11th time, holding tribunician power for the 6th time, consul for the 2nd time, proconsul, and Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, son of Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Augustus our emperor, grandson of the deified Antoninus, great-grandson of the deified Antoninus Pius, great-great-grandson of the deified Hadrian, [---] great-great-great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus and the deified Nerva, and Lucius [Septimius Geta Caesar], son of our emperor Augustus, under the supervision of Tiberius Claudius Claudianus, legate of the emperors, pro praetor.
EDCS-29601458.summary: This milestone inscription from between 202 and 209 AD records the distance from Sirmium and honors Emperors Septimius Severus and his son Caracalla, along with Geta, under the administration of the legate Claudianus.

EDCS-29601460.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Iulio Philippo Pio Felici Invicto Augusto pontifici maximo Parthico maximo tribunicia potestate consul patri patriae proconsuli et Marco Iulio Philippo nobilissimo Caesari et Marciae Otaciliae Severae sanctissimae Augustae coniugi Augusti nostri matri castrorum et exercitus legio II adiutrix pia fidelis Philippiana devota numini maiestati[que eorum ------\n
EDCS-29601460.translation: for Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, chief priest, greatest Parthian, holding tribunician power, consul, father of the fatherland, proconsul, and Marcus Julius Philippus the most noble Caesar, and Marcia Otacilia Severa, the most holy Augusta, wife of our Augustus, mother of the camp and army; the II Adjutrix legion, loyal and faithful, devoted to Philippus, devoted to their divine majesty [---].
EDCS-29601460.summary: This inscription from the reign of Philip the Arab (244-249 AD) records dedications to the imperial family and the loyalty of the II Adjutrix legion.

EDCS-29601462.text_edition: \n L maximus tribuniciae potestatis VIIII consul III pater patriae restituit ab Aquinco milia passuum VIII\n
EDCS-29601462.translation: [---] maximus, holding tribunician power for the 9th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the fatherland, restored [it], 8 Roman miles from Aquincum.
EDCS-29601462.summary: This milestone inscription, likely from the reign of Emperor Gallienus (circa 260s AD), records the restoration of a road and the distance from Aquincum.

EDCS-29601463.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Antonio Gordiano Invicto Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate V consuli II patri patriae proconsuli ab Aquinco milia passuum VIII\n
EDCS-29601463.translation: 8 Roman miles from Aquincum, for Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Invictus Augustus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 5th time, consul for the 2nd time, father of the fatherland, proconsul.
EDCS-29601463.summary: This milestone inscription from 244 AD records the distance from Aquincum and dedicates the road segment to Emperor Gordian III.

EDCS-29601467.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurellius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribuniciae potestatis VIIII consul III pater patriae restituit ab Aquinco milia passuum X\n
EDCS-29601467.translation: 10 Roman miles from Aquincum, from Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurellius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 9th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the fatherland, restored [it].
EDCS-29601467.summary: This milestone inscription from 230 AD records the distance from Aquincum and indicates the restoration of the road by Emperor Severus Alexander.

EDCS-29601469.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VIIII consul III pater patriae restituit ab Aquinco milia passuum XV\n
EDCS-29601469.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 9th time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the fatherland, restored 15 miles of road from Aquincum.
EDCS-29601469.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a road by Emperor Alexander Severus, dating to his 9th tribunician power and 3rd consulship.

EDCS-29601470.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto tribunicia potestate XV consul III Imperatori Caesari Lucio Aurelio Vero Augusto tribunicia potestate consul II divi Antonini filii divi Hadriani nepotes divi Traiani Parthici pronepotes divi Nervae abnepotes ab Aquinco milia passuum \n
EDCS-29601470.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, with tribunician power for the 15th time, consul for the 3rd time, and to Emperor Caesar Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus, with tribunician power for the 1st time, consul for the 2nd time, sons of the deified Antoninus, grandsons of the deified Hadrian, great-grandsons of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-grandsons of the deified Nerva, [restored] 15 miles of road from Aquincum.
EDCS-29601470.summary: This inscription commemorates the restoration of a road by Emperors Marcus Aurelius and Lucius Verus, dating to Marcus Aurelius's 15th tribunician power and 3rd consulship.

EDCS-29601471.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VIIII consul III vias restituit ab Aquinco milia passuum XL\n
EDCS-29601471.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 9th time, consul for the 3rd time, restored roads for 40 miles from Aquincum.
EDCS-29601471.summary: This inscription records the restoration of roads by Emperor Alexander Severus, dating to his 9th tribunician power and 3rd consulship.

EDCS-29601473.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto tribunicia potestate XV consuli III et Imperatori Caesari Lucio Aurelio Vero Augusto tribunicia potestate consul II divi Antonini filii divi Hadriani nepotes divi Traiani Parthici pronepotes divi Nervae abnepotes ab Aquinco I\n
EDCS-29601473.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, with tribunician power for the 15th time, consul for the 3rd time, and to Emperor Caesar Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus, with tribunician power for the 1st time, consul for the 2nd time, sons of the deified Antoninus, grandsons of the deified Hadrian, great-grandsons of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-grandsons of the deified Nerva, [restored] 1 mile of road from Aquincum.
EDCS-29601473.summary: This inscription commemorates the restoration of a road by Emperors Marcus Aurelius and Lucius Verus, dating to Marcus Aurelius's 15th tribunician power and 3rd consulship.

EDCS-29601474.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Caio Iulio Vero Maximino Pio Felici Invicto Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate III imperatori V consuli proconsuli patri patriae et Caio Iulio Vero Maximo nobilissimo Caesari principi iuventutis Augusti nostri filio Germanicis Dacicis Sarmaticis maximis cohors I Thracum Germanica Maximiniana ab Aquinco milia passuum LV\n
EDCS-29601474.translation: To Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 3rd time, emperor for the 5th time, consul, proconsul, father of the fatherland, and to Gaius Julius Verus Maximus, most noble Caesar, prince of youth, son of our Augustus, [restored] 55 miles of road from Aquincum, by the Thracian German Cohort I Maximiniana, having received German, Dacian, Sarmatian honours.
EDCS-29601474.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a road by Emperor Maximinus Thrax and his son Caesar Maximus, dedicated by the Cohort I Thracum Germanica Maximiniana, likely during Maximinus Thrax's 3rd tribunician power and 5th acclamation as emperor.

EDCS-29601475.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Iulio Philippo Pio Felici Invicto Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate consul patri patriae consuli et Marco Iulio Philippo nobilissimo Caesari nostr filio Augusti nostri et Marciae Otaciliae Severae sanctissimae Augustae nostrae coniugi Augusti nostri matri castrorum exercitus ab Aquinco milia passuum LV\n
EDCS-29601475.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power, consul, father of the fatherland, and to Marcus Julius Philippus, our most noble Caesar, son of our Augustus, and to Marcia Otacilia Severa, our most holy Augusta, wife of our Augustus, mother of the camp and army, [restored] 55 miles of road from Aquincum.
EDCS-29601475.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a road by Emperor Philip the Arab, his son Caesar Philip, and Empress Otacilia Severa.

EDCS-29601476.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Gaio Messio Quinto Traiano Decio Pio Felici Invicto Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate patri patriae consuli proconsuli ab Aquinco milia passuum LV\n
EDCS-29601476.translation: To Emperor Caesar Gaius Messius Quintus Traianus Decius Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power, father of the fatherland, consul, proconsul, [restored] 55 miles of road from Aquincum.
EDCS-29601476.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a road by Emperor Decius, likely during his reign as emperor and consul.

EDCS-29601479.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Opellio Severo Macrino Pio Augusto consuli proconsuli tribunicia potestate et Ope<l=I>lio Diadumeniano nobilissimo Caesari vias et pontes vetustate conruptas restituerunt curam agente Aelio Tricciano legato Auggustorum pro praetore ab Aquinco milia passuum LXVI \n
EDCS-29601479.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Opellius Severus Macrinus Pius Augustus, consul, proconsul, with tribunician power, and to Opellius Diadumenianus, most noble Caesar, they restored roads and bridges broken by age, under the supervision of Aelius Ticianus, legate of the emperors pro praetore, [restored] 66 miles of road from Aquincum.
EDCS-29601479.summary: This inscription records the restoration of roads and bridges by Emperors Macrinus and Diadumenianus, overseen by legate Aelius Ticianus, dating to Macrinus's consulship and tribunician power.

EDCS-29601480.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Caius Iulius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus tribunicia potestate pater patriae consul designatus ab Aquinco milia passuum LVI\n
EDCS-29601480.translation: Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus, with tribunician power, father of the fatherland, consul designate, [restored] 56 miles of road from Aquincum.
EDCS-29601480.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a road by Emperor Maximinus Thrax, dating to his consulship designation and tribunician power.

EDCS-29900064.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus ulatrio contra scriptori Confluentium vixit nnos XLV Septimina innocentissimo charissimo sic coniu<g=C>i \n
EDCS-29900064.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Ulatius, a copyist, lived 45 years for the people of Confluentes. Septimina, to her most innocent and dearest husband.
EDCS-29900064.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Ulatius, a copyist, and is dedicated by his wife Septimina.

EDCS-29900066.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Herennio Tromentina Valenti Salona evocato legionis XI Claudiae |(centurioni) legionis eiusdem |(centurioni) legionis I adiutricis |(centurioni) legionis II adiutricis |(centurioni) legionis XV Apollinaris |(centurioni) legionis iterum I adiutricis |(centurioni) legionis IIII Flaviae cohortis V hastato posteriori stipendiorum LV vixit annis LXXV Marcus Herennius Helius libertus et heres patrono bene merenti faciundum curavit hoc monumentum heredem non sequetur\n
EDCS-29900066.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Marcus Herennius Valens of the Tromentina tribe, evocate of the eleventh Claudian legion (centurion of the same legion, centurion of the first Adjutrix legion, centurion of the second Adjutrix legion, centurion of the fifteenth Apollinaris legion, centurion of the first Adjutrix legion again, centurion of the fourth Flavian legion, a senior hastatus in the fifth cohort), lived for 75 years. Marcus Herennius Helius, freedman and heir, had this monument made for his well-deserving patron; it will not pass to an heir.
EDCS-29900066.summary: This funerary inscription records the long military career and death of Marcus Herennius Valens, a centurion in several legions, and the monument erected by his freedman and heir.

EDCS-29900068.text_edition: \n Dianae et Silvano Silvestri Dis praesidibus venationis Marcus Aurelius Pompeius sacerdotalis\n
EDCS-29900068.translation: To Diana and Silvanus Silvestris, presiding deities of the hunt, Marcus Aurelius Pompeius, priest.
EDCS-29900068.summary: This dedication honors the gods Diana and Silvanus by Marcus Aurelius Pompeius, who identifies himself as a priest of the hunt.

EDCS-29900121.text_edition: \n Marco V et Claudiae neptiae ni\n
EDCS-29900121.translation: To Marcus V and Claudia, his granddaughter.
EDCS-29900121.summary: This fragment is a dedication to Marcus V and his granddaughter Claudia.

EDCS-29900570.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus tribunicia potestate XV consul III et Imperator Caesar Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus tribunicia potestate consul II divi Antonini filii divi Hadriani nepotes divi Traiani Parthici pronepotes divi Nervae abnepotes ab Aq(uinco) milia? passuum? \n
EDCS-29900570.translation: From Aquincum, the distance [is X] miles, by Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, with tribunician power for the 15th time, consul for the 3rd time, and Emperor Caesar Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus, with tribunician power for the 1st time, consul for the 2nd time, sons of the deified Antoninus, grandsons of the deified Hadrian, great-grandsons of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-grandsons of the deified Nerva.
EDCS-29900570.summary: This milestone inscription, dating to the joint reign of Marcus Aurelius and Lucius Verus (mid-2nd century CE), indicates a distance of X miles from Aquincum.

EDCS-29900571.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix \n
EDCS-29900571.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix.
EDCS-29900571.summary: This fragment names Emperor Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander, likely part of a larger inscription dedicating a monument or recording an event during his reign (222-235 CE).

EDCS-29900572.text_edition: \n DDominorum nnostrorum Flavio Valerio Severo et Galerio Valerio Maximino nobilissimo Caesaribus ab Aquinco milia passuum VII s coniugi NOE PTPE\n
EDCS-29900572.translation: From Aquincum, 7 miles, to our Lords Flavius Valerius Severus and Galerius Valerius Maximian, most noble Caesars, [along with] the spouse NOE PTPE.
EDCS-29900572.summary: This milestone inscription, dating to the Tetrarchy (late 3rd/early 4th century CE), indicates a distance of 7 miles from Aquincum and mentions the Caesars Severus and Maximian.

EDCS-29900586.summary: This inscription likely refers to the 7th Cohort of Breuci, designated as 'Gordiana', indicating a unit that received this honor during the reign of Gordian III (238-244 CE).

EDCS-29900625.summary: This inscription refers to the 7th Cohort of Breuci, designated as 'Severiana', likely indicating a unit that received this honor during the reign of one of the Severan emperors (early 3rd century CE).

EDCS-29900649.summary: This is a fragment of Latin hexameter verse, possibly from a literary work or a metrical inscription.

EDCS-29900736.translation: Of Decius (or Decimus) / of the Emperor.
EDCS-29900736.summary: This inscription is a very fragmented title or dedication, possibly relating to an emperor or a figure named Decius/Decimus.

EDCS-29900738.text_edition: \n Grate qui fotues sic grae<c=G>am ancillam Lupi optionis legionis secundes sic quae est in domo vestra et malo tuo est per illam at sic nil perduces sic parentes tuos quottidie \n
EDCS-29900738.translation: Grate, who supports the family, Gratia, slave of Lupus, optio of the second legion, she who is in your house and is to your detriment, through her may you lose nothing, just as you would have lost your parents every day.
EDCS-29900738.summary: This is a curse tablet, likely from the Roman period, intended to invoke misfortune upon someone through magical means.

EDCS-29900739.summary: This is a short dedication to a master named Bromius, possibly from a slave or freedman.

EDCS-30100524.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Invictus Pius Felix Augustus, supreme pontiff, holder of tribunician power, father of the country, proconsul: 33 miles.
EDCS-30100524.summary: This is an milestone inscription commemorating a road of 33 miles, dedicated to Emperor Maximinus Thrax, likely in the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-30100698.summary: This is a simple funerary inscription recording the erection of a monument by Marcus P. Silvanus.

EDCS-30100699.text_edition: \n Invicto Deo sacrum Caius Iulius Ingenuus scriba civitatis Aquincensium\n
EDCS-30100699.translation: Sacred to the Invictus God. Gaius Julius Ingenuus, scribe of the citizenry of Aquincensium.
EDCS-30100699.summary: This is a religious dedication to the god 'Invictus' by Gaius Julius Ingenuus, a scribe in the Roman city of Aquincensium, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-30100700.text_edition: \n Invicto Deo sacrum pro salute Gai Iuli Victorini decurionis coloniae Aquincensium quinquennalis Gaius Iulius Primus libertus votum solvit libens merito Saturnino et Gallo consulibus X Kalendas Maias\n
EDCS-30100700.translation: Sacred to the Invictus God. Gaius Julius Primus, freedman, fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly for the health of Gaius Julius Victorinus, decurion of the colony of Aquincensium, quinquennalis, under the consulship of Saturninus and Gallus, ten days before the Kalends of May.
EDCS-30100700.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to the god 'Invictus' for the health of Gaius Julius Victorinus, a local official in Aquincensium, dating to the consular year given.

EDCS-30100701.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tito Aurelio Numerio militi medico legionis XXII Primigeniae piae fidelis et Gaio Iulio Mercatori militi legionis eiiusdem\n
EDCS-30100701.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed: for Titus Aurelius Numerius, soldier, physician of the XXII Primigenia legion, pious and loyal, and for Gaius Julius Mercator, soldier of the same legion.
EDCS-30100701.summary: This is a funerary inscription for two soldiers, a physician and another soldier from the XXII Primigenia legion.

EDCS-30100702.text_edition: \n Caius Castricius Cai Offentina Victor Como miles legionis II adiutricis centuria Marci Turbonis nnorum XXXVIII stipendiorum XIIII hic situs est Lucius Lucilius frater et heres posuit pro pietate\n
EDCS-30100702.translation: Caius Castricius Victor of the tribe Offentina, from Como, soldier of the II Adjutrix legion, under the centuria of Marcus Turbon, aged 38, with 14 years of service, lies here. Lucius Lucilius, his brother and heir, erected this out of filial piety.
EDCS-30100702.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Caius Castricius Victor, a soldier in the II Adjutrix legion, with his brother and heir erecting the monument, dating to the period when the II Adjutrix legion was active.

EDCS-30100703.text_edition: \n Titus Flavius Titi Quirina Magnus --- |(centurio) leg(ionis) --- |(centurio) legionis XII Fulminatae |(centurio) legionis |(centurio) legionis |(centurio) legionis III Gallicae |(centurio) legionis VIIcurator pontium viae Fulviae Fl(avius) --- Antipater et Flavius Modestus liberti et heredes ex HS XV semis milibus faciend(um) cur(averunt)\n
EDCS-30100703.translation: [Centurion] of the XII Fulminata legion, [Centurion] of the III Gallica legion, [Centurion] of the VII legion. Flavius [...] Antipater and Flavius Modestus, freedmen and heirs, had this built for 15,500 sesterces.
EDCS-30100703.summary: This inscription details the careers of multiple centurions and records the construction of a public work, possibly bridges on the Via Fulvia, funded by their freedmen heirs.

EDCS-30100704.text_edition: \n Reginus Troucetissae filius domo Trever sic nnrum XXX sesquiplicarius alae Aurianae I stipendiorum V hic situs est Receptus sesquiplicarius alae Asturum II frater idem heres posuit\n
EDCS-30100704.translation: Reginus, son of Troucetissa, from Trever, aged 30, sesquiplicarius of the ala Auriana I, with 5 years of service, lies here. Receptus, sesquiplicarius of the ala Asturum II, his brother and also heir, erected this.
EDCS-30100704.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Reginus, a soldier of auxiliary cavalry (sesquiplicarius), with his brother and heir Receptus erecting the monument.

EDCS-30100707.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publi Aeli Provincialis nnorum LXXX veterani legionis II adiutricis ex signiferi sibi et Pompeiae Valentinae quondam uxori vivus fecit\n
EDCS-30100707.translation: To the divine shades. Publius Aelius Provincialis, aged eighty, a veteran of the Second Adiutrix Legion, from the rank of standard-bearer, made this for himself and for Pompeia Valentina, his former wife, while he was still alive.
EDCS-30100707.summary: A funerary monument set up by a veteran for himself and his deceased wife, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30100708.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tito Flavio Constantino nnorum V et Flaviae Exsuperatae nnorum XX et Flaviae Constantinae nnorum II filiis Titus tas et \n
EDCS-30100708.translation: To the divine shades. For Titus Flavius Constantinus, aged five, and Flavia Exsuperata, aged twenty, and Flavia Constantina, aged two, children of Titus. [---]
EDCS-30100708.summary: A funerary epitaph for three children, likely set up by their father Titus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30100894.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valeria Aemilia usu stolae exornata trium liberorum equitum Romanorum mater vixit nnos XXXVII Valerius Timotheus tribunus cohortis III Batavorum coniugi pientissimae\n
EDCS-30100894.translation: To the divine shades. Valeria Aemilia, adorned with the use of a stola, mother of three children, wife of a Roman knight, lived thirty-seven years. Valerius Timotheus, tribune of the Third Cohort of Batavians, for his most devoted wife.
EDCS-30100894.summary: A funerary epitaph for Valeria Aemilia, wife of a Roman knight and mother, set up by her husband, a military tribune, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30100896.text_edition: \n Quinto Herennio Etrusco Messio Decio nobilissimo Caesari nostr<o=I> legio II adiutrix ter p<i=P>a ter fidelis Constaans Traiananana Deciana devota numini maiestati<q=O>ue eius \n
EDCS-30100896.translation: To Quintus Herennius Etruscus Messius Decius, our most noble Caesar, the Second Adiutrix Legion, thrice pious, thrice faithful, devoted to the divine spirit and majesty of Constans Trajanus Decianus.
EDCS-30100896.summary: An inscription recording the devotion of the Second Adiutrix Legion to Caesar Quintus Herennius Etruscus Messius Decius, likely dating to the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-30100897.translation: Love to a lover; if you love, a pledge.
EDCS-30100897.summary: A brief, enigmatic inscription expressing sentiments about love, likely a personal inscription from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30100899.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Teutano Conservatori et Iunoni Reginae IA SALMA LIA \n
EDCS-30100899.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Teutanus, the Preserver, and to Juno Regina. [---] [---] [---]
EDCS-30100899.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Teutanus and Juno Regina, found in the territory of the Eravisci, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30100901.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Teutano et dis deabusque omnibus pro salute ddominorum nnostrorum Imperatoris Caesaris Cai Vibi Treboniani Galli Pii Felicis Invicti Augusti pontificis maximi tribuniciae potestatis patris patriae etCai Va[lentis Host]iliani Messi [Qui]nti Pii Felicis Augusti et incolumitate civitatis E[raviscorum] Marci Aurelii Maturus flamen et Valens IIviri coloniae Aquincensium dedicaverunt diem III Idus Iunias divis Deccis consulibus\n
EDCS-30100901.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Teutanus and all the gods and goddesses, for the safety of our lords the Emperor Caesar Gaius Vibius Trebonianus Gallus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power, father of the fatherland, and of Gaius Valerius Hostilianus Messius Quintus Pius Felix Augustus, and for the well-being of the community of the Eravisci. Marcus Aurelius Maturus, flamen, and Valens, duumvirs of the colony of Aquincum, dedicated this on the third day before the Ides of June, with the consuls Deccius (and others).
EDCS-30100901.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Teutanus and Roman Emperors by local magistrates of Aquincum for the well-being of the empire and the community, dating to the reign of Trebonianus Gallus and Hostilian (251 CE).

EDCS-30100904.text_edition: \n IISISIO consulibus Iovi Optimo Maximo Teutano pro salute adque incolumitate domini nostri Imperatoris Domiti Aureliani Pii Felicis Invicti Augusti perpetui Imperatoris totiusque domus eius divinae et civitatis Eraviscorum Marcii Aurelii Domitianus augur quaestor et Castor flamen aedilicii duumviri coloniae Aquincensium dedicaverunt die pridie Iduum Decembrium Im<p=T>(eratore) Aureliano Augusto et Basso II consulibus \n
EDCS-30100904.translation: With IISISIO and [---] consuls, to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Teutanus, for the safety and well-being of our lord Emperor Domitius Aurelianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, perpetual emperor, and his entire divine household, and the community of the Eravisci. Marcus Aurelius Domitianus, augur, quaestor, and Castor, flamen, formerly aedile, duumvirs of the colony of Aquincum, dedicated this on the day before the Ides of December, with Emperor Aurelian Augustus and Bassus as consuls.
EDCS-30100904.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Teutanus by local officials of Aquincum for the safety of Emperor Aurelian and the community, dating to 271 CE.

EDCS-30100906.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Teutano pro salute Imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli I IV Iovi Optimo Maximo Teutano pro salute adque incolumitate ddominorum nnostrorum Marci Aureli Carini et Marci Aureli Numeriani Auggustorum totiusque domus divinae eorum et civitatis Eraviscorum Marcus Aurelius Polideuces aedilicius duumviralis vir egregius flamen et Marcus Aurelius Clemens aedilicius duumviralis equo publico quinquennales coloniae Aquincensium dedicaverunt III Idum Iunias Impperatoribus ddominis nnostris Carino II et Numeriano Auggustis consulibus\n
EDCS-30100906.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Teutanus, for the safety and well-being of our lords Marcus Aurelius Carinus and Marcus Aurelius Numerianus Augusti, and their entire divine household, and the community of the Eravisci.
