Marcus Aurelius Polideuces, formerly aedile, vir egregius, flamen, and Marcus Aurelius Clemens, formerly aedile, holding the public horse, quinquennales of the colony of Aquincum, dedicated this on the third day of the Ides of June, with our lords Carinus II and Numerian Augusti as consuls.
EDCS-30100906.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Teutanus by local officials of Aquincum for the safety of Emperors Carinus and Numerian and the community, dating to 284 CE.

EDCS-30100907.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Publius Aelius Valens cum suis omnibus votum solvit libens merito Iovi Optimo Maximo Teutano pro salute adque incolumitate ddominorum nnostrorum Marci Valeri Diocletiani Pii Felicis Invicti Augusti pontificis maximi tribunicia potestate II consulis III <p=R>(atris) patriae proconsulis et Marci Aureli Valeriani Maximiani Pii Felicis Invicti Augusti pontificis maximi tribunicia potestate patris patriae proconsulis totiusque domus divinae eorum finibus Eraviscorum Aelius Exuperatus eques Romanus et Aelius Ulpianus IIvviri coloniae Aquincensium dedicaverunt Maximo II et Aquilino consulibus III Idus Iunias \n
EDCS-30100907.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus. Publius Aelius Valens with all his household fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Teutanus, for the safety and well-being of our lords Marcus Valerius Diocletianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power II, consul III, father of the fatherland, proconsul, and Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maximianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power, father of the fatherland, proconsul, and their entire divine household, within the territory of the Eravisci. Aelius Exuperatus, Roman knight, and Aelius Ulpianus, duumvirs of the colony of Aquincum, dedicated this on the third day before the Ides of June, with Maximus II and Aquilinus as consuls.
EDCS-30100907.summary: A votive offering to Jupiter Teutanus by local officials of Aquincum for the safety of Emperors Diocletian and Maximian, dating to 286 CE.

EDCS-30100908.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Conservatori --- trib(unus) mil(itum) legionis II adiutricis piae fidelis AVIII EIIL IS Augusti IV I IIIII aram fecit LEG E VIII ILIIT IVIO Iovi Optimo Maximo Teutano Iunoni Reginae Minervae Sanctae ceterisque diis sic deabusque immortali<b=R>us pro salute Iovi Optimo Maximo Teutano Iunoni Reginae Minervae Sanctae ceterisque diis sic deabusque immortali<b=R>us pro salute adque incolumitate ddominorum nnostrorum Diocletiani e<t=I> Maximiani Invictissimorum Auggustorum totiusque domus divinae eorum et civitatis Eraviscorum Publius Licinius Domitius et Marcus Iulius Marcellus duumviri coloniae Aquincensium dedicaverunt die III Iduum Iuniarum domino nostro Maximiano Augusto II et Ianuariano conssulibus \n
EDCS-30100908.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, the Protector. A tribune of the II Adjutrix Legion, pious and faithful, (and other officers) erected this altar. To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, Teutanes, to Juno Regina, to Minerva the Holy, and to all other immortal gods and goddesses, for the health and well-being of our lords Diocletian and Maximian, the Invincible Augusti, and of their entire divine house, and of the city of the Eravisci, Publius Licinius Domitius and Marcus Iulius Marcellus, duumvirs of the colony of Aquincense, dedicated this on the third day before the Ides of June, during the consulship of our lord Maximian Augustus II and Ianuarianus.
EDCS-30100908.summary: This is a dedication of an altar to Jupiter, Juno, and Minerva by two duumvirs of Aquincense for the health of the emperors Diocletian and Maximian, dated to June 11, 293 AD.

EDCS-30100909.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Teutano Iunoni Regina<e=F> Minervae Sancta<e=F> c<e=F>t<e=F>risque diis sic d<e=F>abusque immo<rt=N>alibus pro salute adque incolumitate ddominorum nnostrorum Cai Aureli Valeriani Diocletiani et Aureli Valeri Maximiani Pii Felicis Invictorum Auggustorum totius<q=O>ue domus divinae eorum et civitatis Eraviscorum Aurelius Valens et Septimius Florus dduumviri coloniae Aquincensium dedicaverunt die III Iduum Iunia<s=R> ddominis nnostris Diocletiano et Maximiano Augustis conssulibus \n
EDCS-30100909.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, Teutanes, to Juno Regina, to Minerva the Holy, and to all other immortal gods and goddesses, for the health and well-being of our lords Caius Aurelius Valerius Diocletian and Aurelius Valerius Maximian, the Pious, the Fortunate, the Invincible Augusti, and of their entire divine house, and of the city of the Eravisci, Aurelius Valens and Septimius Florus, duumvirs of the colony of Aquincense, dedicated this on the third day before the Ides of June, during the consulship of our lords Diocletian and Maximian Augusti.
EDCS-30100909.summary: This is a dedication of an altar by two duumvirs of Aquincense to Jupiter, Juno, and Minerva for the health of the emperors Diocletian and Maximian, dated to June 11, 293 AD.

EDCS-30100910.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Teutano Publius Aelius us augur princeps civitatis Eraviscorum votum solvit libens merito pro se et suis\n
EDCS-30100910.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, Teutanes. Publius Aelius, augur and princeps civitatis of the Eravisci, paid his vow willingly and deservedly for himself and his family.
EDCS-30100910.summary: This is a votive dedication to Jupiter Teutanes by Publius Aelius, an augur and leader of the citizenry of the Eravisci, for himself and his family.

EDCS-30100912.text_edition: \n Titus Karinius Iuliacensis domo Arelate ex provincia Narbonensi beneficiarius consularis legionis II adiutricis piae fidelis stationes habui argentariarum Pannonicarum et Delmaticarum! procuratoris Aciminci Camponae aram feci translata statione X Kalendas Iunias Pedone et Bradua consulibus\n
EDCS-30100912.translation: Titus Karinius Iuliacensis, from Arelate in the province of Narbonensis, a beneficiary of a consular officer of the II Adjutrix Legion, pious and faithful, who had stations of the Pannonian and Dalmatian treasury procurators, erected this altar, having been transferred from his station on the tenth day before the Kalends of June, during the consulship of Pedo and Bradua.
EDCS-30100912.summary: This is a dedication of an altar by Titus Karinius, a soldier of the II Adjutrix Legion, to an unknown deity, dated to May 23, 193 AD.

EDCS-30100913.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Aurelius Valens signifer alae primae Thracum veteranae votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-30100913.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest. Aurelius Valens, standard-bearer of the First Thracian Wing, paid his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-30100913.summary: This is a votive dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Aurelius Valens, a standard-bearer of the First Thracian Wing.

EDCS-30100914.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno Marcus Aurelius Lipor veteranus alae I Thracum Severianae ex statore et Aurelius Valens filius eius votum solverunt libentes merito Fusco II et Dextro consulibus\n
EDCS-30100914.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, Dolichenus. Marcus Aurelius Lipor, a veteran of the First Thracian Severian Wing, formerly a cavalryman, and Aurelius Valens, his son, paid their vows willingly and deservedly, during the consulship of Fuscus II and Dexter.
EDCS-30100914.summary: This is a votive dedication to Jupiter Dolichenus by Marcus Aurelius Lipor, a veteran of the First Thracian Wing, and his son Aurelius Valens, dated to 185 AD.

EDCS-30100915.text_edition: \n Pro salute Impperatorum Cae[ssarum] ala I Thracum cui praeest Publius Cassius Publi filius Fabia Felix domo Roma praefectus equitum\n
EDCS-30100915.translation: For the health of the Emperors Caesars. The First Thracian Wing, commanded by Publius Cassius Felix, son of Publius, of the Fabia tribe, from Rome, cavalry prefect.
EDCS-30100915.summary: This inscription is a prayer for the health of the Emperors Caesars, sponsored by Publius Cassius Felix, prefect of the First Thracian Wing.

EDCS-30100916.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute Impperatorum ala I Thracum cui praeest Publius Cassius Publi filius Fabia Felix domo Roma praefectus equitum\n
EDCS-30100916.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, for the health of the Emperors. The First Thracian Wing, commanded by Publius Cassius Felix, son of Publius, of the Fabia tribe, from Rome, cavalry prefect.
EDCS-30100916.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus for the health of the Emperors, by Publius Cassius Felix, prefect of the First Thracian Wing.

EDCS-30100917.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo ala I Thracum veterana cui praeest Aelius Publianus primus pilus legionis II adiutricis\n
EDCS-30100917.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest. The First Thracian Veteran Wing, commanded by Aelius Publianus, first centurion of the II Adjutrix Legion.
EDCS-30100917.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Aelius Publianus, a first centurion of the II Adjutrix Legion commanding the First Thracian Veteran Wing.

EDCS-30100918.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute Severi Imperatoris ala I Thracum cui praeest Marcus Gongius Paternus Nestorianus domo Sufibus ex Africa praefectus equitum \n
EDCS-30100918.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, for the health of Emperor Severus. The First Thracian Wing, commanded by Marcus Gongius Paternus Nestorianus, from Sufes in Africa, cavalry prefect.
EDCS-30100918.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus for the health of Emperor Severus, by Marcus Gongius Paternus Nestorianus, prefect of the First Thracian Wing, likely dating to his reign (193-211 AD).

EDCS-30100919.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute Imperatorum [et] Ca[esaris] ala I Thracum veterana cui praeest Marcus Gongius Paternus Nestorianus praefectus equitum\n
EDCS-30100919.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, for the safety of the Emperors and Caesar, the First Thracian Veteran Cavalry Wing, commanded by Marcus Gongius Paternus Nestorianus, prefect of cavalry.
EDCS-30100919.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter by the First Thracian Veteran Cavalry Wing for the safety of the emperors, commanded by Marcus Gongius Paternus Nestorianus.

EDCS-30100920.text_edition: \n Dis deabusque Campestribus pro salute Impperatorum Claudius Claudianus consularis curante Gongio Nestoriano <p=I>raefecto equitum \n
EDCS-30100920.translation: To the Campestrian Gods and Goddesses, for the safety of the Emperors, Claudius Claudianus, former governor, with Gongius Nestoriano, prefect of cavalry, overseeing.
EDCS-30100920.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the Campestrian deities for the safety of the emperors, made by Claudius Claudianus under the supervision of Gongius Nestoriano.

EDCS-30100921.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Septimius Fabinus eques alae I Thracum veteranae votum solvit libens merito instante Marco Aelio Apolaneus sic colono\n
EDCS-30100921.translation: Septimius Fabinus, an equestrian of the First Thracian Veteran Cavalry Wing, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Jupiter, Best and Greatest, with the colonist Marco Aelius Apolaneus acting as intermediary.
EDCS-30100921.summary: This inscription records a votive offering by Septimius Fabinus, an equestrian of the First Thracian Veteran Cavalry Wing, to Jupiter.

EDCS-30100922.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo ala I Thracum veterana cui praeest Titus Vennonius Titi filius Stellatina Avitus domo Augusta Taurinorum ex Italia votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-30100922.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, the First Thracian Veteran Cavalry Wing, commanded by Titus Vennonius Avitus, son of Titus, of the Stellatina tribe, from Augusta Taurinorum in Italy, willingly and deservedly fulfilled its vow.
EDCS-30100922.summary: This inscription records a votive offering by the First Thracian Veteran Cavalry Wing, commanded by Titus Vennonius Avitus, to Jupiter.

EDCS-30100923.text_edition: \n Ala I Thracum veterana cui praeest Titus Vennonius Titi filius Stellatina Avitus domo Augusta Taurinorum ex Italia votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-30100923.translation: The First Thracian Veteran Cavalry Wing, commanded by Titus Vennonius Avitus, son of Titus, of the Stellatina tribe, from Augusta Taurinorum in Italy, willingly and deservedly fulfilled its vow.
EDCS-30100923.summary: This inscription records a votive offering by the First Thracian Veteran Cavalry Wing, commanded by Titus Vennonius Avitus, to Jupiter (though Jupiter is implied by context of similar inscriptions).

EDCS-30100925.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo ala I Thracum veterana cui praeest Titus Vennonius Av<i=E>tus praefectus equitum \n
EDCS-30100925.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, the First Thracian Veteran Cavalry Wing, commanded by Titus Vennonius Avitus, prefect of cavalry.
EDCS-30100925.summary: This inscription records the command of Titus Vennonius Avitus as prefect of cavalry over the First Thracian Veteran Cavalry Wing, dedicated to Jupiter.

EDCS-30100926.text_edition: \n Genio cohortis III Batavorum miliariae equitatae cui praeest Marcus Flavius Miles praefectus\n
EDCS-30100926.translation: To the Genius of the Third Cohort of Batavians, a thousand-strong, mounted unit, commanded by Marcus Flavius Miles, prefect.
EDCS-30100926.summary: This inscription records the command of Marcus Flavius Miles as prefect over the Third Cohort of Batavians, dedicated to its Genius.

EDCS-30100927.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo cohors III Batavorum milliaria equitata cui praeest Marcus Flavius Miles praefectus\n
EDCS-30100927.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, the Third Cohort of Batavians, a thousand-strong, mounted unit, commanded by Marcus Flavius Miles, prefect.
EDCS-30100927.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter by the Third Cohort of Batavians, commanded by Marcus Flavius Miles.

EDCS-30100928.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Flavius Iuncus praefectus equitum votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-30100928.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, Flavius Iuncus, prefect of cavalry, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-30100928.summary: This inscription records a votive offering by Flavius Iuncus, prefect of cavalry, to Jupiter.

EDCS-30100929.text_edition: \n pro salute Impperatorum Auggustorum nnostrorum Tiberius Claudius Claudianus consularis templum a solo fecit\n
EDCS-30100929.translation: For the safety of our Emperors Augusti, Tiberius Claudius Claudianus, former governor, built this temple from the ground up.
EDCS-30100929.summary: This inscription records the construction of a temple from its foundations by Tiberius Claudius Claudianus for the safety of the emperors.

EDCS-30100930.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Publius Aelius Perpetuus decurio municipii Aquincensium votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-30100930.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, Publius Aelius Perpetuus, a councilor of the Aquincensian municipality, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-30100930.summary: A votive inscription from Aquincensium, dated to the Roman Imperial period, recording a vow fulfilled by a local councilor to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.

EDCS-30100931.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Marcus Foviacius Verus Iunior decurio canabarum decurio municipii Aquincensium augur votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-30100931.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, Marcus Foviacius Verus the Younger, councilor of the canabae, councilor of the Aquincensian municipality, and augur, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-30100931.summary: A votive inscription from Aquincensium, dated to the Roman Imperial period, where a local councilor and augur fulfills a vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.

EDCS-30100932.text_edition: \n Caius Iulius Cai filius Fabia Maximus centurio cohortis VI Thracum veteranae hic situs est Caius Iulius Maximus Iulia Maximilla Iulius Valens Iulius Galata filii et heredes faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-30100932.translation: Gaius Julius Maximus, son of Gaius, of the Fabia tribe, centurion of the VI Veteran Cohort of Thracians, lies here. Gaius Julius Maximus, Iulia Maximilla, and Julius Valens and Julius Galata, his sons and heirs, arranged for this to be made.
EDCS-30100932.summary: A funerary inscription, likely from the Roman Imperial period, commemorating a centurion and the family members who arranged for his tomb.

EDCS-30100933.text_edition: \n Publio Caetronio Ingenuino annorum X et Valeriae Saturninae annorum XXX hic siti Publius Caetronius Ingenuus filio et uxori et sibi vivus fecit\n
EDCS-30100933.translation: For Publius Caetronius Ingenuus, aged 10, and Valeria Saturnina, aged 30, here lies. Publius Caetronius Ingenuus made this tomb for his son and wife and himself while alive.
EDCS-30100933.summary: A funerary inscription from the Roman Imperial period, recording a man's self-funded tomb for himself, his son, and his wife.

EDCS-30100935.summary: A fragmentary inscription, likely from the Roman Imperial period, that records a dedication to the god Liber.

EDCS-30100938.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Teutano Marcus Ulpius Valentinus et Marcus Ulpius Verus IIviri municipii Aquincensium iure dicundo posuerunt III Idus Iunias Mamertino et Rufo consulibus\n
EDCS-30100938.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, and Teutanus, Marcus Ulpius Valentinus and Marcus Ulpius Verus, the two duovirs of the Aquincensian municipality responsible for administering justice, erected this on the 11th day before the Kalends of June, during the consulship of Mamertinus and Rufus.
EDCS-30100938.summary: A public dedication from Aquincensium, dated to 181 AD, by two duovirs to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Teutanus, recording the consuls of the year.

EDCS-30100958.text_edition: \n Signis cohortis I Alpinorum peditatae Antoninianae Allius Exsuperatus praefectus dedit dedicavit\n
EDCS-30100958.translation: Allius Exsuperatus, the prefect, gave and dedicated this to the standards of the I Alpinorum Cohort, infantry, Antoniniana.
EDCS-30100958.summary: A dedication from the Roman Imperial period by a prefect to the standards of an infantry cohort, likely honoring military success or a specific event.

EDCS-30200443.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Aelius Celsinus miles legionis II adiutricis quaestionarius votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-30200443.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, Aelius Celsinus, a soldier of the II Adjutrix Legion, a quaestionarius, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-30200443.summary: A votive inscription from the Roman Imperial period, likely from a soldier of the II Adjutrix Legion, fulfilling a vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.

EDCS-30200445.summary: A simple dedication, likely from the Roman Imperial period, from Aelius Martinus to the god Hercules Augustus.

EDCS-30200446.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Titus Hortensius Frequens decurio co<l=I>(oniae) Mursensium equo publico votum solvit libens merito Asclepiades fecit \n
EDCS-30200446.translation: To Jupiter, Best and Greatest, Titus Hortensius Frequens, a councilor of Colonia Mursensium, supported by public funds, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow. Asclepiades made it.
EDCS-30200446.summary: A votive inscription from Colonia Mursensium, dated to the Roman Imperial period, recording a vow fulfilled by a local councilor and made by an artisan.

EDCS-30200447.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo sacrum Marcus Paenius Salutaris votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-30200447.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Best and Greatest. Marcus Paenius Salutaris willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-30200447.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Marcus Paenius Salutaris.

EDCS-30200448.text_edition: \n M dedit Idibus Iuliis Pompeiano et Avito consulibus\n
EDCS-30200448.translation: M. gave this on the Ides of July, when Pompeianus and Avitus were consuls.
EDCS-30200448.summary: This is a fragmentary dedication recording a gift made on a specific date during the consulship of Pompeianus and Avitus.

EDCS-30200449.text_edition: \n Iuliae Cai libertae Primillae nnum XXV hic sita est Caius Iulius Successus collibertae bene merenti de sua pecunia\n
EDCS-30200449.translation: Julia Primilla, freedwoman of Caius, lived 25 years. Caius Julius Successus placed her here, his fellow freedwoman, well-deserving, at his own expense.
EDCS-30200449.summary: This epitaph commemorates Julia Primilla, a freedwoman, who lived 25 years, and was buried by her fellow freedman, Caius Julius Successus.

EDCS-30200451.text_edition: \n Herculi Augusto sacrum Aurelius Gratianus qu<i=T> et Sab<i=T>nianus votum solvit libens merito \n
EDCS-30200451.translation: Sacred to Hercules Augustus. Aurelius Gratianus, also known as Sabinianus, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-30200451.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Hercules Augustus by Aurelius Gratianus, also called Sabinianus.

EDCS-30200452.text_edition: \n Tiberius Claudius aedi filius A Tunger eques alae \n
EDCS-30200452.translation: Tiberius Claudius, son of Aedius, from Tungri, a cavalryman of the [ala...].
EDCS-30200452.summary: This is a fragmentary military inscription identifying Tiberius Claudius, son of Aedius, a cavalryman from Tungri.

EDCS-30200453.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Iunoni Reginae Aurelius Mucianus miles legionis secundae adiutricis piae fidelis Severianae pro se et suis omnibus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-30200453.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, and Juno Regina. Aurelius Mucianus, a soldier of the Second Adjutrix Legion, Pious, Faithful, Severian, fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly for himself and all his family.
EDCS-30200453.summary: This votive inscription is dedicated to Jupiter and Juno by Aurelius Mucianus, a soldier of the Legio II Adjutrix, for himself and his family.

EDCS-30200454.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus ituti domo hic situs est moru Aeli- Seustus heres Flavius Dragilis ex praeposito Castri<p>s Constantiis qui vixit annis LXXIIII mensibus quinque diebus XV Aurelia Marciola compar carissima viva fecit haec sic titulo sic bene merento sic suo\n
EDCS-30200454.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. Itutus is buried here, having departed from home. His heir Flavius Dragilis, formerly Prefect of the Castra Constantiens, who lived 74 years, five months, and fifteen days. Aurelia Marciola, his dearest wife, made this for him while alive, as is fitting for one deserving and for herself.
EDCS-30200454.summary: This is a tombstone for Itutus, set up by his wife Aurelia Marciola and his heir Flavius Dragilis, a former military prefect, detailing his lifespan.

EDCS-30200456.summary: This is a simple epitaph recording the age of Namia Musaionis.

EDCS-30300816.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo vicus Res posuit decreto decurionum Blandius Victorinus\n
EDCS-30300816.translation: The district of Res dedicated this to Jupiter Best and Greatest by decree of the decurions, by Blandius Victorinus.
EDCS-30300816.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by the vicus of Res, with Blandius Victorinus mentioned as the responsible party acting on the decree of the decurions.

EDCS-30300817.text_edition: \n Caio Marcio Collina Pollioni Amastria veterano legionis II adiutricis nnorum LX collegium veteranorum posuit\n
EDCS-30300817.translation: The association of veterans dedicated this to Gaius Marcius Collina Pollio, a veteran of the II Adjutrix Legion, aged 60.
EDCS-30300817.summary: This inscription commemorates Gaius Marcius Collina Pollio, a 60-year-old veteran of the Legio II Adjutrix, and was erected by his veteran colleagues.

EDCS-30300818.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Pompeiae Sexti filiae Quartinae matri piissimae Publius Nonius Firmus fecit\n
EDCS-30300818.translation: To the divine shades. Publius Nonius Firmus made this for his most devoted mother, Pompeia Quartina, daughter of Sextus.
EDCS-30300818.summary: A funerary monument set up by Publius Nonius Firmus for his mother, Pompeia Quartina.

EDCS-30600047.translation: Set up by S, a beneficiarius consularis, for his dearest.
EDCS-30600047.summary: A funerary monument erected by a beneficiarius consularis for a beloved person, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-30600048.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Maximina annos vixit XXVII Gorgonius memoriam posuit compari suae carissimae de provincia Dalmatia defuncta penus colonia Sirmi Manibus posita memoria\n
EDCS-30600048.translation: To the divine shades. Maximina lived 27 years. Gorgonius set up this memorial for his dearest wife, who died from the province of Dalmatia, her household provisions placed in the colony of Sirmium.
EDCS-30600048.summary: A funerary monument erected by Gorgonius for his wife Maximina, who died at the age of 27, with specific details about her origin and possibly her burial provisions.

EDCS-30600049.text_edition: \n ------ paren]tes posiuerunt filiae Aureliae Florentinae quae vixit nnos V dies L?XVIII \n
EDCS-30600049.translation: Her parents set this up for their daughter Aurelia Florentina, who lived 5 years and 68 days.
EDCS-30600049.summary: A funerary monument erected by parents for their daughter Aurelia Florentina, who died at a young age.

EDCS-30600051.text_edition: \n posuerunt matronae merenti Rufillae annis LXX \n
EDCS-30600051.translation: The matrons set this up for their deserving Rufilla, aged 70.
EDCS-30600051.summary: A funerary monument erected by a group of matrons for Rufilla, who lived to be 70 years old.

EDCS-30600053.text_edition: \n AX in pace innocenti Htissimnae FLiae posuit \n
EDCS-30600053.translation: In peace for the most innocent, daughter, who deserved it. Set up.
EDCS-30600053.summary: A funerary monument for a deceased daughter described as innocent and deserving.

EDCS-30600055.summary: A simple funerary marker indicating the resting place of Garius.

EDCS-30600062.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo salutari et Genio domus eius Lucius Serenius Bassus |(centurio) legionis II adiutricis gravissima infirmitat<e=I> liberatus votum solvit libens merito \n
EDCS-30600062.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, bringer of health, and to the genius of his house, Lucius Serenius Bassus, a centurion of the II Adjutrix legion, freed from a most serious illness, gladly paid his vow as was deserved.
EDCS-30600062.summary: A votive inscription by Lucius Serenius Bassus, a centurion of the II Adjutrix legion, to Jupiter and the genius of his household, fulfilling a vow after recovering from illness, likely from the late 1st to 3rd century CE.

EDCS-30600063.text_edition: \n Marti sacrum Titus Claudius Martinus aquilifer legionis II adiutricis\n
EDCS-30600063.translation: Sacred to Mars. Titus Claudius Martinus, aquilifer of the II Adjutrix legion.
EDCS-30600063.summary: A dedication to Mars by Titus Claudius Martinus, an aquilifer (standard-bearer) of the II Adjutrix legion.

EDCS-30600064.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico Aurelius Acutianus ex voto\n
EDCS-30600064.translation: To Silvanus Domesticius, by vow. Aurelius Acutianus.
EDCS-30600064.summary: A votive inscription to Silvanus Domesticius by Aurelius Acutianus.

EDCS-30600067.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flavius Firmus miles IIISSICL PA \n
EDCS-30600067.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Flavius Firmus, soldier of the XXXIII Legion, and...?
EDCS-30600067.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Flavius Firmus, a soldier of the XXXIII Legion, possibly with additional, incomplete information.

EDCS-30600068.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valerio Maximino militi legionis stipendiorum XXIII qui vixit annos et Valeriae filiae eius qui vixit annos et Valerio Maximinoqui vixit annos VIII Valeria Maximina parentibus et filiis M et Valerio ano et Valerio filiis vivis et Ursino alumno sibi viva faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-30600068.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Valerius Maximius, soldier of the legion, with 23 years of service, who lived... years, and his daughter Valeria, who lived... years, and Valerius Maximius, who lived 8 years. Valeria Maximina had this made for her parents and children M and Valerius, and sons Valerius and Ursus, living, and for herself, living.
EDCS-30600068.summary: This funerary inscription records the dedication of a monument by Valeria Maximina for her deceased family members, including her husband Valerius Maximius, their children, and an adopted son, Ursus, indicating a late Roman period context.

EDCS-30600070.summary: This very short inscription likely records the equipment belonging to someone named Valentinus, possibly from a tomb or a military context.

EDCS-30600073.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Caius Iulius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate consul designatus pater patriae proconsul restituit a Poetovione milia passuum CXXXVII\n
EDCS-30600073.translation: Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus, Pius, Felix, Augustus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power, consul designate, father of the country, proconsul, restored [this road] 137 Roman miles from Poetovio.
EDCS-30600073.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a road over 137 miles from Poetovio by Emperor Maximinus Thrax, likely dating to his reign (235-238 AD).

EDCS-31001225.text_edition: \n Annum novum faustum <f=E>e<l>icem iucundum Sextus I P \n
EDCS-31001225.translation: A new year, happy, fortunate, pleasant. Sextus I. P. [had this made/dedicated].
EDCS-31001225.summary: This inscription is a New Year's greeting or dedication, possibly for a New Year's gift or shrine, by Sextus I. P.

EDCS-31500071.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute sua Iulius Co votum solvit merito\n
EDCS-31500071.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, for his own safety, Iulius Co fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-31500071.summary: This is a votive inscription by Iulius Co dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus for his well-being.

EDCS-31500072.text_edition: \n ctor Aurelius Vitalis Claudius Marcellinus Flavius Caesianus Aurelius Dignus Aurelius Disala Aurelius Uxello Aurelius Severus Aurelius Aurelius Septimius Iulius Aurelius Aurelius Aelius Aurelius \n
EDCS-31500072.translation: [Names of individuals including] Aurelius Vitalis, Claudius Marcellinus, Flavius Caesianus, Aurelius Dignus, Aurelius Disala, Aurelius Uxello, Aurelius Severus, Aurelius, Aurelius Septimius, Iulius Aurelius, Aurelius, Aelius Aurelius.
EDCS-31500072.summary: This inscription lists numerous names, likely comprising a group or collegium, possibly associated with a dedication or honorific text, with the context unclear due to fragmentation.

EDCS-31500074.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Lucius Marcius Avitus beneficiarius procuratoris votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-31500074.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Lucius Marcius Avitus, aide of the procurator, fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-31500074.summary: This votive inscription records Lucius Marcius Avitus, an aide to a procurator, dedicating a vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.

EDCS-31500076.text_edition: \n (ovi) Optimo Maximo et Iunioni Reginae sacrum Iulius Valerius veteranus legionis II aduitricis votum pro suis libens merito Marco Antonino III et Lucio Vero II consulibus\n
EDCS-31500076.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Juno Regina. Iulius Valerius, a veteran of the II Adjutrix Legion, fulfilled his vow for his family willingly and deservedly, in the consulship of Marcus Antoninus II and Lucius Verus II.
EDCS-31500076.summary: This inscription is a religious dedication by Iulius Valerius, a veteran of the II Adjutrix Legion, to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Juno Regina, dated to 167 AD.

EDCS-31500077.text_edition: \n Silvano pro salute Cai Pomini -- Decsiani sic pr legionis II adiutricis ex voto equisiones eius posuerunt\n
EDCS-31500077.translation: To Silvanus, for the safety of Caius Pominius -- Decesianus, a soldier of the II Adjutrix Legion, his fellow equites posuerunt [dedicated this] according to his vow.
EDCS-31500077.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Silvanus by the fellow equites (cavalrymen) of Caius Pominius Decesianus, a soldier in the II Adjutrix Legion, for his safety.

EDCS-31500078.text_edition: \n pro salute Cassi Pii Marcellini laticlavii legionis II adiutricis Aurelius Achilleus heres eiius votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-31500078.translation: For the health of Cassius Pius Marcellinus, a broad-striped senator of the II Adiutrix Legion, his heir Aurelius Achilleus willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-31500078.summary: A private vow recorded by an heir for the health of a high-ranking senator of the II Adiutrix Legion, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-31500079.text_edition: \n Lucius Caecilius Luci filius Quirina Verus Admedera sic miles legionis II adiutricis stipendiorum VIII annorum \n
EDCS-31500079.translation: Lucius Caecilius Verus, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, a soldier of the II Adiutrix Legion, after 8 years of service.
EDCS-31500079.summary: An epitaph for Lucius Caecilius Verus, a soldier of the II Adiutrix Legion, who died after 8 years of service, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-31500080.text_edition: \n Iulia Domitia Iulia Iulina soror Iulius Iulianus veteranus legionis II ex co vivus annorum LXXX opto sit tibi terra levis\n
EDCS-31500080.translation: Iulia Domitia, and her sister Iulia Iulina, and Iulius Iulianus, a veteran of the II Legion, from here, aged 80, ordered this: may the earth lie lightly on you.
EDCS-31500080.summary: A joint epitaph for Iulia Domitia, her sister Iulia Iulina, and a veteran of the II Legion named Iulius Iulianus, who died at the age of 80, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-31500081.text_edition: \n domo Mursa vixit annos XXXVI stipendiorum XVIII Aurelia Priscilla co<n=I>iux eius Prisca et Probilla filias sic et heredes posuerunt qui defunctus est Per<i=E>nth<i=O> et ossua sic eiius in hunc sic locum sic sunt \n
EDCS-31500081.translation: From Mursa, he lived 36 years, 18 years of service. His wife Aurelia Priscilla and his daughters Prisca and Probilla, his heirs, set this up. He died in Perinthos, and his remains are in this place.
EDCS-31500081.summary: An epitaph for a man from Mursa who served 18 years and lived to 36, set up by his wife and daughters, with his remains interred in this location, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-31500083.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tiberius Claudius Tiberi filius \n
EDCS-31500083.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Tiberius Claudius, son of Tiberius.
EDCS-31500083.summary: A funerary dedication to the 'Dis Manibus' (Spirits of the Underworld) for Tiberius Claudius, son of Tiberius, likely from the 1st to 3rd century CE.

EDCS-31500084.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Cassia lies here.
EDCS-31500084.summary: A simple funerary inscription dedicated to the 'Dis Manibus' (Spirits of the Underworld) for a woman named Cassia, likely from the 1st to 3rd century CE.

EDCS-31500085.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus memoriae Apro Atiutori sic Vomera Procula mater viva fecit qui vixit annis XXIII Perseus libertus patrono rarissimo <e=F>t dignissimo faciendum curavit \n
EDCS-31500085.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld, for the memory of Apro Atiutor Vomera. His mother Procula, while alive, had this made. His freedman Perseus arranged for it to be made for his most rare and most worthy patron, who lived 23 years.
EDCS-31500085.summary: A funerary monument for Apro Atiutor Vomera, paid for by his mother and arranged by his freedman, praising him as a rare and worthy patron, likely from the 1st to 3rd century CE.

EDCS-31500086.text_edition: \n Scorilo Ressati libertus domo Dacus sic annorum IXXXX sic hic situs est item Annamae sic coniugi vivae et Mattoni filiae vivae titulum posuerunt Quintus et Anculata filii patri pientissimo bene merenti\n
EDCS-31500086.translation: Scorilo, freedman of Ressatus, from Dacia, aged 90, lies here. Also, his children Quintus and Anculata placed this monument for Annamae, his wife, while alive, and for Matton, his daughter, while alive, and for their most devoted and deserving father.
EDCS-31500086.summary: An epitaph for Scorilo, his wife Annamae, and daughter Matton, set up by his sons Quintus and Anculata, recording his age at death and their devotion, likely from the 1st to 3rd century CE.

EDCS-31500088.text_edition: \n Secundus et Primitiva liberti eius bene merenti fecerunt\n
EDCS-31500088.translation: His freedmen Secundus and Primitiva made this for him, who deserved well.
EDCS-31500088.summary: A funerary dedication set up by freedmen for their deserving former master, likely from the 1st to 3rd century CE.

EDCS-32300110.summary: A fragmentary inscription mentioning the name Marcianus, possibly an epitaph or part of a longer dedication, date uncertain due to fragmentation.

EDCS-32300111.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Marcus Antonius Sotericus pro se et suis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-32300111.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Marcus Antonius Sotericus, for himself and his family, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-32300111.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Marcus Antonius Sotericus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32300112.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Aurelius Lucidus beneficiarius votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-32300112.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Aurelius Lucidus, a *beneficiarius*, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-32300112.summary: This votive inscription to Jupiter Optimus Maximus was made by Aurelius Lucidus, a military official, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32300113.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute Impperatorum Auggustorum dominorum nnostrorum Marci Aureli Antonini Pii Tiberius Claudius Quintus duplarius legionis II adiutricis piae fidelis pro se et suis votum solvit libens merito \n
EDCS-32300113.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, for the safety of our Emperors Augusti, our Lords Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius, Tiberius Claudius Quintus, *duplarius* of the II Adiutrix legion, loyal and faithful, for himself and his family, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-32300113.summary: This inscription is a votive offering to Jupiter Optimus Maximus made by Tiberius Claudius Quintus, a soldier of the II Adiutrix legion, for the welfare of the emperors, likely dating to the reign of Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius.

EDCS-32300114.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Aurelius Maximus votum libens s(olvit)\n
EDCS-32300114.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Aurelius Maximus willingly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-32300114.summary: This is a brief votive inscription to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Aurelius Maximus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32300115.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Gaius Iulius Faventinus cornicularius legati legionis I adiutricis pro salute sua suorumque omnium votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-32300115.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Gaius Iulius Faventinus, a *cornicularius* of the legate of the I Adiutrix legion, for his own safety and that of all his family, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-32300115.summary: This votive inscription to Jupiter Optimus Maximus was made by Gaius Iulius Faventinus, a military officer of the I Adiutrix legion, for his health and that of his family, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32300116.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Marcus Ulpius Optatus decurio municipii Aquincensium IIvir\n
EDCS-32300116.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Marcus Ulpius Optatus, *decurio* of the municipality of Aquincensium, *duumvir*.
EDCS-32300116.summary: This inscription records Marcus Ulpius Optatus, a local magistrate (*decurio* and *duumvir*) of Aquincensium, likely dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, and dates to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32300117.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Capitolino Claudius Valens miles legionis II adiutricis piae fidelis Antoninianae pro et suis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-32300117.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Capitolinus, Claudius Valens, soldier of the II Adiutrix legion, loyal and faithful Antoninianae, for himself and his family, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-32300117.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Capitolinus by Claudius Valens, a soldier of the II Adiutrix legion, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32300118.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Fidei veterani IS legionis II adiutricis votum libentes\n
EDCS-32300118.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest and to Fides, the veterans of the [II?] Adiutrix legion willingly [fulfilled their vows].
EDCS-32300118.summary: This inscription is a votive offering to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Fides (Faith) by veterans of the II Adiutrix legion, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32300119.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico sacrum ex vis<u=O> posuit ] \n
EDCS-32300119.translation: Sacred to Silvanus Domest[icus], he placed [this] according to a vision.
EDCS-32300119.summary: This is a dedication to Silvanus Domestiscus, likely placed by an individual who received a vision, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32300120.text_edition: \n Soli Invcito Deo pro salute Corneli Fructi Caius Cornelius Maximus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-32300120.translation: To Sol Invictus Deus, for the health of Cornelius Fructus, Caius Cornelius Maximus willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-32300120.summary: This votive inscription to Sol Invictus Deus was made by Caius Cornelius Maximus for the health of Cornelius Fructus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32300121.text_edition: \n Invicto Deo sacrum Caius Iulius Ingenuus scriba civitatis Aquincensium\n
EDCS-32300121.translation: Sacred to the Unconquered God. Gaius Julius Ingenuus, scribe of the community of Aquincensium.
EDCS-32300121.summary: This is a dedication to the Unconquered God by Gaius Julius Ingenuus, a scribe of the community of Aquincensium.

EDCS-32300122.text_edition: \n Invicto Deo sacrum Caius LIulius In<g=C>enuus scriba civitatis Aquincensium \n
EDCS-32300122.translation: Sacred to the Unconquered God. Gaius Julius Ingenuus, scribe of the community of Aquincensium.
EDCS-32300122.summary: This is a dedication to the Unconquered God by Gaius Julius Ingenuus, a scribe of the community of Aquincensium, with a correction in the name Iulius.

EDCS-32300123.translation: The keeper of arms gave this gift to the college.
EDCS-32300123.summary: This records a gift to a college by an 'armorum custos' (keeper of arms).

EDCS-32300125.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publio Aelio Valerio quondam speculatori legionis II adiutricis et Publio Aelio Valerino Aelia Alexandria patrono et filio faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-32300125.translation: Publius Aelius Valerius, formerly a scout of the II Adiutrix legion, and Publius Aelius Valerius, father and son, was commissioned to be made by Aelia Alexandria.
EDCS-32300125.summary: This is a funerary inscription commissioned by Aelia Alexandria for Publius Aelius Valerius, a scout of the II Adiutrix legion, and Publius Aelius Valerius, identified as patron and son.

EDCS-32300127.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelio Vibiano veterano legionis per Aurelium Bavilam filium et libertum \n
EDCS-32300127.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aurelius Vibianus, a veteran of the legion, was honored by Aurelius Bavila, his son and freedman.
EDCS-32300127.summary: This is a funerary inscription honoring Aurelius Vibianus, a veteran of a legion, set up by his son and freedman, Aurelius Bavila.

EDCS-32300128.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Calpurnio Decciano natione Italo militi legionis II adiutricis qui vixit nnos XXIIII menses V dies XVII Calpurnius Eutropus fratri pientissimo\n
EDCS-32300128.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Marcus Calpurnius Deccianus, of Italian origin, a soldier of the II Adiutrix legion, who lived 24 years, 5 months, and 17 days. Calpurnius Eutropus, to his most devoted brother.
EDCS-32300128.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Calpurnius Deccianus, a 24-year-old soldier of the II Adiutrix legion, set up by his brother Calpurnius Eutropus.

EDCS-32300129.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Carisio Luci filio Pom<pt=EN>ina Vero Arretio armorum custodi legionis \n
EDCS-32300129.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Lucius Carisius Verus, son of Lucius, from the tribe Pomptina, from Arretium, keeper of arms of the legion.
EDCS-32300129.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Carisius Verus, a soldier from Arretium who served as a keeper of arms in a legion.

EDCS-32300130.text_edition: \n Lucius Fulvius Secundus miles legionis II adiutricis centuria Publili Valentis stipendiorum XII hic situs est heredes titulum ex testamento posuerunt Marcus Traviius <e=F>t Ciltus \n
EDCS-32300130.translation: Lucius Fulvius Secundus, soldier of the II Adiutrix legion, in the century of Publilius Valens, with 12 years of service, is buried here. His heirs, Marcus Traviius and Ciltus, erected this tombstone according to his will.
EDCS-32300130.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of Lucius Fulvius Secundus, a soldier of the II Adiutrix legion with 12 years of service, whose heirs erected the monument according to his will.

EDCS-32300131.text_edition: \n Marcus Licinius Ianuarius cornicularius miles legionis II adiutricis piae fidelis nnorum XXX stipendiorum \n
EDCS-32300131.translation: Marcus Licinius Ianuarius, cornicularius, soldier of the II Adiutrix legion, pious and faithful, aged 30 years, with [specified] years of service.
EDCS-32300131.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Licinius Ianuarius, a cornicularius (an officer's assistant) in the II Adiutrix legion, who died at the age of 30.

EDCS-32300132.text_edition: \n Caius Nundinius Oufentina Tertullus Mediolano stipendiorum XIII\n
EDCS-32300132.translation: Gaius Nundinius Tertullus, from Oufentina tribe, from Mediolanum, with 13 years of service.
EDCS-32300132.summary: This inscription records Gaius Nundinius Tertullus, from Mediolanum, who served for 13 years, likely a funerary monument or a record of service.

EDCS-32300133.text_edition: \n Marcus Valerius Marci Aniensis Marcellus Vercellis miles legionis II adiutricis centuria Postumi Valentis annorum XL stipendiorum XVIII hic situs <e=H>(st) \n
EDCS-32300133.translation: Marcus Valerius Marcellus of the Aniensis tribe, from Vercelli, a soldier of the II Adiutrix legion, from the century of Postumius Valentis, aged 40, with 18 years of service, is buried here.
EDCS-32300133.summary: This is a tombstone for Marcus Valerius Marcellus, a soldier of the II Adiutrix legion, likely dating to the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-32300134.text_edition: \n C EI Caio Valerio Cai filio C ex armorum custode Tuscaniae C et Valeri-- Valerian etno filiis suis \n
EDCS-32300134.translation: To Gaius Valerius, son of Gaius, from the Tuscania tribe, guardian of weapons, and Valerius [--] for his sons Valerian and Etno.
EDCS-32300134.summary: This dedication is for the sons Valerian and Etno, commissioned by their father Gaius Valerius, who was a guardian of weapons, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32300135.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Ulpius veteranus legionis II adiutricis piae fidelis qui vixit nnos LXXsibi et Aureliae Iuliae ADconiugi eius faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-32300135.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Marcus Ulpius, a veteran of the II Adiutrix pia fidelis legion, who lived 70 years, arranged for this to be made for himself and for Aurelia Julia, his wife.
EDCS-32300135.summary: This tombstone records the death of Marcus Ulpius, a veteran of the II Adiutrix legion, and was made for himself and his wife, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32300136.text_edition: \n ucus Atae filius vivus sibi titulum posuit et coniugi Lucitae Kapitonis filia annorum XXXX \n
EDCS-32300136.translation: Ucus, son of Ata, while alive, placed this monument for himself and for his wife Lucita, daughter of Kapiton, aged 40.
EDCS-32300136.summary: This is a tombstone set up by Ucus for himself and his wife Lucita, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32300137.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus nnorum XXXXV hic situs est Attius Respectus patri pientissimo et Attiae Privatae matri suae vivae posuit titulum memoriae\n
EDCS-32300137.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Attius Respectus placed this monument in memory of his most devoted father and his mother Attia Privata, while he was alive, aged 45.
EDCS-32300137.summary: This tombstone was erected by Attius Respectus for his parents, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32300138.text_edition: \n Flavia Aiulo nnorum XX hic sita est Gallio sorori pientissimae et Avitus consobrinae pientissimae \n
EDCS-32300138.translation: Flavia Aiulo, aged 20, is buried here, for Gallio, her most devoted sister, and for Avitus, her most devoted cousin.
EDCS-32300138.summary: This tombstone records the burial of Flavia Aiulo, and also commemorates her sister Gallio and cousin Avitus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32300139.text_edition: \n Flor annorum XXX hic sita est Licinius Similis coniugi pientissimae et sibi vivus posuit\n
EDCS-32300139.translation: Flor, aged 30, is buried here. Licinius Similis placed this for his most devoted wife and for himself while alive.
EDCS-32300139.summary: This tombstone records the burial of Flor, and was set up by her husband Licinius Similis for her and himself, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32300140.text_edition: \n Marcia mulieris lib(erta) Aethale nnorum XLV hic sita est Lucius Marcius Felix coniunx et conlibertus \n
EDCS-32300140.translation: Marcia Aethale, freedwoman of Marcia, aged 45, is buried here. Lucius Marcius Felix, husband and freedman.
EDCS-32300140.summary: This tombstone records the burial of Marcia Aethale, a freedwoman, and is attributed to her husband Lucius Marcius Felix, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32300141.text_edition: \n Lucius Ocusius Domitianus vixit annos L et Ocusio Candido libertus sic eius vixit annos XL hic siti sunt Ocusia Filu \n
EDCS-32300141.translation: Lucius Ocusius Domitianus lived 50 years, and Ocusius Candidus, his freedman, lived 40 years. Ocusia Filu are buried here.
EDCS-32300141.summary: This tombstone records the burial of Lucius Ocusius Domitianus and his freedman Ocusius Candidus, and possibly Ocusia Filu, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32300142.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sallustius Sabinus et Iulia Iunilia parentes Sabiniano filio anniculo et mensium novem dierum? \n
EDCS-32300142.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Sallustius Sabinus and Iulia Iunilia, parents, for Sabinianus, their infant son, of nine months and nine days.
EDCS-32300142.summary: This tombstone records the death of an infant son, Sabinianus, and was set up by his parents Sallustius Sabinus and Iulia Iunilia, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32300143.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Septimiae Procellae qui sic vixit annos LX et Septimio Exuperato coniugi Pilius et Septimia entina gener et filia de suo fecerunt parentibus pientissimis pro pietate posuerunt\n
EDCS-32300143.translation: To the Divine Spirits of Septimia Procela, who lived for sixty years, and to her husband Septimius Exuperatus, Pilius and Septimia Entina, son-in-law and daughter, made this from their own resources, setting it up for their most devoted parents in recognition of their piety.
EDCS-32300143.summary: This is a tombstone set up by a son-in-law and daughter for their deceased parents, Septimia Procela and Septimius Exuperatus.

EDCS-32300144.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Opellius Severus Macrinus Pius Augustus, consul, proconsul, holder of tribunician power, and Ope[llius Diad]umenianus, most noble Caesar, restored roads and bridges corrupted by age, under the supervision of [Aelius Tric]c[ianus, legate of Augustus, propraetor], from Aquincum, 2 miles.
EDCS-32300144.summary: This inscription records the restoration of roads and bridges by Emperors Macrinus and Diadumenianus, with the work supervised by legate Aelius Triccianus, dating to their period of joint rule.

EDCS-32300145.translation: To Emperor Caesar [Caius] Iulius Verus Maximinus, Invictus, Pius, Felix Augustus, pontiff, father of the fatherland, proconsul, the legion II Adjutrix Pia Fidelis [restored the road] from Aquincum, 2 miles, [in a Maximinia]n manner.
EDCS-32300145.summary: This dedication is made to Emperor Maximinus Thrax by the legion II Adjutrix Pia Fidelis, recording the repair of a 2-mile stretch of road from Aquincum, likely during Maximinus's reign.

EDCS-32300146.translation: To Emperor Caesar [Caius] Iulius Verus Maximinus, Invictus, Pius, Felix Augustus, pontiff, father of the fatherland, consul, proconsul, and [Caius Iulius Verus] Maximus, most noble Caesar, the legion II Adjutrix Pia Fidelis [restored the road] from Aquincum, 2 miles, [in a Maximinia]n manner.
EDCS-32300146.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a 2-mile road section from Aquincum by the legion II Adjutrix Pia Fidelis, dedicated to Emperor Maximinus Thrax and his son, the Caesar Maximinus the Younger.

EDCS-32300147.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Iulius Philippus, Pius, Felix Maximus, Parthicus, greatest, holder of tribunician power, consul, father of the fatherland, proconsul, and Marcus Iulius Philippus, most noble Caesar, and Marcia Otacilia Severa, most holy Augusta, wife of our Augustus, and mother of the camp, the legion II Adjutrix Pia Fidelis Philippiana, devoted to their divinity and majesty, [restored the road] from Aquincum, 2 miles.
EDCS-32300147.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a 2-mile road segment from Aquincum by the legion II Adjutrix Pia Fidelis, dedicated to Emperor Philip the Arab, his son Caesar Philip, and Empress Otacilia Severa.

EDCS-32300487.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Marti Victoriae Augustae et Laribus Militaribus Caius Marius Cai filius Galeria Etruscus Gallianus tribunus laticlavius legionis II adiutricis quaestor designatus ex voto\n
EDCS-32300487.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Mars, Victoria Augusta, and the Military Lares, Caius Marius Galerianus, son of Caius, of the Galerian tribe, Etruscan, tribune with laticlavian rank of the legion II Adiutrix, designated quaestor, made this vow.
EDCS-32300487.summary: This is a votive inscription by Caius Marius Galerianus, a high-ranking officer in the legion II Adiutrix, dedicated to various gods and household spirits.

EDCS-32300488.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico Aurelius Mucatra beneficiarius tribuni pro salute sua et suorum votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-32300488.translation: Aurelius Mucatra, a beneficiarius tribune, fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly to Silvanus Domestus for his own health and that of his family.
EDCS-32300488.summary: This is a votive inscription by Aurelius Mucatra, a soldier serving as a beneficiarius tribune, dedicated to Silvanus Domestus for his well-being and that of his family.

EDCS-32300492.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tito Flavio Suro quaestionario legionis II adiutricis nnorum \n
EDCS-32300492.translation: To the Divine Spirits of Titus Flavius Surus, an official of the legion II Adiutrix, [who lived for] ... years.
EDCS-32300492.summary: This is a tombstone for Titus Flavius Surus, an official of the legion II Adiutrix, recording his death.

EDCS-32300493.text_edition: \n Aeliae Aeternae vixit nnos III et Aeliae Domnulae et Aelio vio Aelius Tatianus --- leg(ionis) II adiutricis \n
EDCS-32300493.translation: Aelius Tatianus ... of the legion II Adiutrix, set this up for Aelia Aeterna, who lived for 3 years, and Aelia Domnula, and Aelius Vi[o]us.
EDCS-32300493.summary: This is a tombstone set up by Aelius Tatianus, a soldier in the legion II Adiutrix, for his family members, including his young daughter Aelia Aeterna.

EDCS-32300503.text_edition: \n ] Pro salute domini nostri Aurelius Aelianus duplarius\n
EDCS-32300503.translation: ... For the safety of our lord, Aurelius Aelianus, duplarius.
EDCS-32300503.summary: This is a dedication or offering made by Aurelius Aelianus, a soldier of duplarius rank, for the well-being of his lord.

EDCS-32300504.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio patri patriae consuli IIII legio II adiutrix pia fidelis\n
EDCS-32300504.translation: To Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the fatherland, consul for the fourth time, by the Second Adiutrix Legion, loyal and faithful.
EDCS-32300504.summary: This inscription records an honorific dedication to Emperor Antoninus Pius, likely set up by the Second Adiutrix Legion.

EDCS-32300505.text_edition: \n sinus --- fil(ius) mil<i=E>tavit Miseni annis LVIII Mo posuit titulum memoriae \n
EDCS-32300505.translation: Sinus, son of ---, served in Misenum for 58 years. He set up this stone to his memory.
EDCS-32300505.summary: This is a memorial inscription for Sinus, a veteran who served 58 years in the Misenum fleet.

EDCS-32300511.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico LVI Lucius Antius votum solvit\n
EDCS-32300511.translation: To Silvanus Domesticius, aged 56, Lucius Antius willingly paid his vow.
EDCS-32300511.summary: This inscription records Lucius Antius fulfilling a vow to the god Silvanus Domesticius.

EDCS-32300512.text_edition: \n Aurelius Filander veteranus legionis II adiutricis piae fidelis Invicto Deo votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-32300512.translation: Aurelius Filander, a veteran of the Second Adiutrix Legion, loyal and faithful, willingly and deservedly paid his vow to the unconquered god.
EDCS-32300512.summary: This is a votive inscription by Aurelius Filander, a veteran of the Second Adiutrix Legion, to an unnamed unconquered deity.

EDCS-32300513.text_edition: \n Silvano Augusto Dom<e=F>stico sacrum Primus votum solvit libens merito \n
EDCS-32300513.translation: Sacred to Silvanus Augustus Domesticius. Primus willingly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-32300513.summary: This inscription records Primus fulfilling a vow to the god Silvanus Augustus Domesticius.

EDCS-32300519.text_edition: \n Pro salute domini nostri Augusti collegium Victorianorum cocinatorium collationibus suis fecerunt instante Caio Iulio Pacato magistro fani collegii suprascripti curatoribus Lucio Domitio Domitiano decurione decuriae IIII magistro fani IIII et Tiberio nno Felicianus sic decurione decuriae II magistro fani et Aurelio Nigrino decurione decuriae V et Tito Septimio Ingenuus sic decurione decuriae VI Maximo iterum et Aeliano consulibus Cnaeus Marcius Mansuetus patronus Vibius Lucius decurio\n
EDCS-32300519.translation: For the health of our lord Augustus, the association of Victorian victualers, through their contributions, made this, under the supervision of Gaius Julius Pacatus, master of the shrine of the aforementioned association, with curators Lucius Domitius Domitianus, decurion of the fourth turma and master of the shrine, and Tiberius --- Felicianus, decurion of the second turma and master of the shrine, and Aurelius Nigrinus, decurion of the fifth turma, and Titus Septimius Ingenuus, decurion of the sixth turma, with Maximo again, under the consuls Aelianus and Cn. Marcius Mansuetus, patron, Vibius Lucius, decurion.
EDCS-32300519.summary: This complex inscription records the construction of something by a guild of victualers for the emperor's health, listing numerous officials and patrons during the consulship of Aelianus and Marcius Mansuetus.

EDCS-32300520.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Aurelius Florianus beneficiarius consularis pro salute sua et suorum votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-32300520.translation: Aurelius Florianus, a consular attendant, willingly and deservedly paid his vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus for his own health and that of his family.
EDCS-32300520.summary: This is a votive inscription by Aurelius Florianus, a soldier on the consular staff, to Jupiter for his and his family's well-being.

EDCS-32300521.text_edition: \n Votum solvit libens merito Dito sic Patri et Proserpinae Aemilius Hermes pro filiis suis\n
EDCS-32300521.translation: Aemilius Hermes willingly and deservedly paid his vow to Father Dis and Proserpina for his sons.
EDCS-32300521.summary: This inscription records Aemilius Hermes fulfilling a vow to Pluto and Persephone for the sake of his sons.

EDCS-32300522.text_edition: \n Silvano Augusto Marcus Ulpius Sabinus commentariensis consularis legionis I adiutricis piae fidelis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-32300522.translation: To Silvanus Augustus, Marcus Ulpius Sabinus, clerk of the consul of the First Adiutrix Legion, loyal and faithful, willingly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-32300522.summary: This inscription records Marcus Ulpius Sabinus, an administrative clerk for the First Adiutrix Legion, fulfilling a vow to Silvanus Augustus.

EDCS-32300523.text_edition: \n signifer legionis II adiutricis votum solv--- liben--- merito Pisone et Iuliano consulibus\n
EDCS-32300523.translation: The standard-bearer of the Second Adiutrix Legion willingly paid his vow ... during the consulship of Piso and Iulianus.
EDCS-32300523.summary: This fragmented inscription records a standard-bearer of the Second Adiutrix Legion fulfilling a vow during the consulship of Piso and Iulianus.

EDCS-32300525.text_edition: \n Libero Patri Felix votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-32300525.translation: Felix gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Father Liber.
EDCS-32300525.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Liber Pater by Felix.

EDCS-32300526.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico Claudia Secundina votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-32300526.translation: Claudia Secundina gladly and deservedly fulfilled her vow to Silvanus Domesticius.
EDCS-32300526.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Silvanus Domesticius by Claudia Secundina.

EDCS-32300531.text_edition: \n Nemesi Primus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-32300531.translation: Primus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Nemesis.
EDCS-32300531.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Nemesis by Primus.

EDCS-32300532.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Claudius Secundus miles legionis II adiutricis beneficiarius tribuni laticlavii nnorum XXXX stipendiorum XX et Secundinus filius hic siti sunt Aelia Catta coniugi pientissimo et filio et sibi viva posuit\n
EDCS-32300532.translation: Claudius Secundus, a soldier of the II Adjutrix legion, a beneficiarius of a tribune with a broad stripe, aged 40, with 20 years of service, and Secundinus his son, are buried here. Aelia Catta, his wife, set this up for her most devoted husband, son, and herself while alive.
EDCS-32300532.summary: This is a tombstone for Claudius Secundus, a soldier, and his son Secundinus, set up by his wife Aelia Catta, likely from the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-32300534.text_edition: \n Tit-- Flavi-- Va domo GI al V D annorum L \n
EDCS-32300534.translation: Ti(tus) Flavius Va[l(erius?)], from Gi[gida?], aged 50.
EDCS-32300534.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription recording the name and age of an individual, likely a tombstone or dedication.

EDCS-32300537.text_edition: \n Laribus militaribus Caius Iulius Pisibanus Maximus Aemilius Papus tribunus laticlavius votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-32300537.translation: Caius Julius Pisibanus Maximus and Aemilius Papus, tribunes with broad stripes, gladly and deservedly fulfilled their vow to the military Lares.
EDCS-32300537.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to the military Lares by two tribunes.

EDCS-32300538.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Hevodus sic annum Felix pater posuit\n
EDCS-32300538.translation: Hevodus is buried here. His father Felix set this up.
EDCS-32300538.summary: This is a tombstone for Hevodus, set up by his father Felix.

EDCS-32300539.text_edition: \n Lucilia Restituta annorum Lucius Tlullius Primus vivus fecit\n
EDCS-32300539.translation: Lucius Tlullius Primus made this while alive for Lucilia Restituta, aged [unknown].
EDCS-32300539.summary: This is a funerary monument or dedication made by Lucius Tlullius Primus for Lucilia Restituta.

EDCS-32300542.text_edition: \n Silvano Silvestri Lucius Naevius Campanus praefectus kastrorum legionis IIII Flaviae pro salute sua et suorum votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-32300542.translation: Lucius Naevius Campanus, prefect of the camp of the IIII Flavia Felix legion, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow for his own safety and that of his family.
EDCS-32300542.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Silvanus Silvestris by Lucius Naevius Campanus, a military officer, for his welfare and that of his family.

EDCS-32300544.text_edition: \n veteranus legionis II adiutricis s hic situs est us Victor heredes ex testamento faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-32300544.translation: [Name of deceased], a veteran of the II Adjutrix legion, is buried here. His heirs, in accordance with his will, saw to its construction.
EDCS-32300544.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a veteran of the II Adjutrix legion, whose construction was overseen by his heirs as per his will.

EDCS-32300546.translation: Victor, a soldier of the... Legion.
EDCS-32300546.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription recording the name of a soldier, Victor, and likely his legion.

EDCS-32300548.text_edition: \n cius Euphrosinus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-32300548.translation: Cius Euphrosinus willingly fulfilled his vow, as was fitting.
EDCS-32300548.summary: This is a votive inscription where Cius Euphrosinus fulfilled a vow, likely to a deity.

EDCS-32300549.text_edition: \n Libero Patri et Liberae Augustae Aelius Karus beneficiarius legati pro salute sua et suorum votum solvit libens merito Agricola et Clementino consulibus\n
EDCS-32300549.translation: Aelius Karus, a staff officer of the legate, fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly for his own health and that of his family, when Agricola and Clementinus were consuls.
EDCS-32300549.summary: This is a votive inscription by Aelius Karus, a beneficiarius legati, dedicating a vow for his family's health during the consulship of Agricola and Clementinus.

EDCS-32300550.text_edition: \n Claudius Sextinus beneficiarius legati templum a solo relevavit Modesto et Probo consulibus\n
EDCS-32300550.translation: Claudius Sextinus, a staff officer of the legate, rebuilt the temple from the ground up, when Modestus and Probus were consuls.
EDCS-32300550.summary: This inscription records Claudius Sextinus, a beneficiarius legati, rebuilding a temple during the consulship of Modestus and Probus.

EDCS-32300554.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sextus Mentosilius miles Satrius \n
EDCS-32300554.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Sextus Mentosilius, a soldier of the Satrian [Tribe?].
EDCS-32300554.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Sextus Mentosilius, a soldier, dedicated to the Divine Spirits.

EDCS-32300556.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tito Magio Titi filio Tromentina Clementi Statiellis veterano legionis X geminae piae fidelis Publius Atilius Super heres fecit\n
EDCS-32300556.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Publius Atilius Super, the heir, made this for Titus Magius, son of Titus, of the Tromentina tribe, Clement, a veteran of the tenth legion Gemina Pia Fidelis.
EDCS-32300556.summary: This funerary inscription records Publius Atilius Super erecting a monument for Titus Magius, a veteran of the tenth legion, to the Divine Spirits.

EDCS-32300557.text_edition: \n Memoriae Cai Iuli Magni |(centurionis) legionis X Fretensis qui vixit annos XXXVII Publia Surilla coniugi carissimo faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-32300557.translation: For the memory of Caius Julius Magnus, centurion of the tenth legion Fretensis, who lived 37 years. Publia Surilla arranged for this to be made for her dearest husband.
EDCS-32300557.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Caius Julius Magnus, a centurion of the tenth legion, by his wife Publia Surilla.

EDCS-32300558.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Piae Celerinae annorum LXXV viva sibi fecit\n
EDCS-32300558.translation: To the Divine Spirits. For Pias Celerina, aged 75. She made this for herself while alive.
EDCS-32300558.summary: This is a funerary monument dedicated to the Divine Spirits, made by Pias Celerina for herself during her lifetime.

EDCS-32300559.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus quieti aeternae Publi Aeli Respectiani ex collegio centonariorum Publi Aeli quondam Domiti veterani liberti qui vixit annos XXX Aelias sic Fortunatat et Ingenua conlibertae collocaverunt optamus sit tibi terra levis\n
EDCS-32300559.translation: To the Divine Spirits, for eternal rest. Aelias Fortunata and Ingenua, freedwomen of Publius Aelius Respectianus, formerly a freedman of Domitius, a veteran, who lived 30 years, have placed this; we wish the earth may lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-32300559.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Publius Aelius Respectianus, a veteran and freedman, established by his freedwomen colleagues from the guild of tent-makers.

EDCS-32300560.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sextus Pompeius Carpus medicus domo Antiochiae sic S<y=V>riae sic sibi et liberto Pompeio Marcello vivus fecit \n
EDCS-32300560.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Sextus Pompeius Carpus, physician, from Antioch, and Syria. He made this for himself and his freedman Pompeius Marcellus while alive.
EDCS-32300560.summary: This funerary inscription records Sextus Pompeius Carpus, a physician from Antioch, dedicating a monument to himself and his freedman while alive.

EDCS-32300561.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Bithiniae Severae qui sic vixit nnos LX- Claudii Ursus et Maxima et Maximina filii matri pientissimae faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-32300561.translation: To the spirits of the departed, for Bithinia Severa, who lived 60 years. Claudius Ursus, Maxima, and Maximina, her children, arranged for this monument to be made for their most devoted mother.
EDCS-32300561.summary: A funerary epitaph for Bithinia Severa, set up by her children, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32300564.text_edition: \n Marco Egnatio Marci Galeria M Morico Lugduno militi legionis II adiutricis centuria Simini Rufi stipendiorum XXIII annorum XLV hic situs est\n
EDCS-32300564.translation: Marcus Egnatius, son of Marcus, of the Galerian tribe, from Lugdunum, a soldier in the II Adiutrix legion, in the century of Siminius Rufus, with 23 years of service and aged 45 years, is buried here.
EDCS-32300564.summary: An epitaph for Marcus Egnatius, a soldier of the II Adiutrix legion, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32300565.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publio Aelio Victorino beneficiario praefecti quondam legionis II adiutricis vixit annos XVIIII menses VIIII dies XIIII et Aureliae Noricae coniugi eius vixit annos XVIII Publius Aelius Dubitatianus consobrino et bruti faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-32300565.translation: To the spirits of the departed, for Publius Aelius Victorinus, a deputy of a former prefect of the II Adiutrix legion, who lived 19 years, 9 months, and 14 days, and for Aurelia Norica, his wife, who lived 18 years. Publius Aelius Dubitatianus arranged for this monument to be made for his cousin and his [---].
EDCS-32300565.summary: A funerary epitaph for Publius Aelius Victorinus and his wife Aurelia Norica, set up by a relative, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32300567.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Claudiae Tiberi filiae Iustinae nnorum XXX hic sitae Marcus Ulpius Quirina Sirmio Primus coniugi pientissimae et sibi vivos sic fecit\n
EDCS-32300567.translation: To the spirits of the departed, for Claudia Iustina, daughter of Tiberius, aged 30 years, buried here. Marcus Ulpius Sirmio Primus of the Quirina tribe arranged for this monument to be made for his most devoted wife and for himself while alive.
EDCS-32300567.summary: A funerary epitaph for Claudia Iustina, set up by her husband Marcus Ulpius Sirmio Primus for himself and his wife, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32300568.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelio Silvano |(centurioni) legionis II adiutricis domo Syria Palaestina colonia Aelia Capitolina stipendiorum LXI quui vixit annos LXXXVI Aelia Silvana filia et heres patri carissimo per Aelium Filoquirium monumentum faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-32300568.translation: To the spirits of the departed, for Aelius Silvanus, a centurion in the II Adiutrix legion, from the colony of Aelia Capitolina in Syria Palaestina, with 61 years of service, who lived 86 years. Aelia Silvana, his daughter and heir, arranged for this monument to be made for her dearest father through Aelius Filoquirus.
EDCS-32300568.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aelius Silvanus, a centurion of the II Adiutrix legion, erected by his daughter and heir, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32300569.text_edition: \n Severiano et Severae Hypni filiis et Iuventiae Corinthillae Coracinae filiis eius et Corinthiae Euaristi filiae Hypnus et Syr \n
EDCS-32300569.translation: For Severianus and Severa, children of Hypnus, and for Iuventia Corinthilla, their children, and for Corinthia, daughter of Euaristos. Hypnus and Syr [arranged for this monument to be made].
EDCS-32300569.summary: A dedication to several individuals, likely a funerary monument, erected by Hypnus and Syr, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32300570.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulio Tertio quondam stratori consularis stipendiorum XXV vixit nnos XL et Iulio Mauro filio vixit nnos IIII et Furniae Vernaculae Aurelia Felicula marito pientissimo viva sibi et suis faciendum curavit opto sit tibi terra levis\n
EDCS-32300570.translation: To the spirits of the departed, for Iulius Tertius, a former standard-bearer of a consular governor, with 25 years of service, who lived 40 years, and for Iulius Maurus, his son, who lived 4 years, and for Furnia Vernacula. Aurelia Felicula arranged for this monument to be made for her most devoted husband while alive, and for herself and her family. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-32300570.summary: A funerary epitaph for Iulius Tertius, his son Iulius Maurus, and Furnia Vernacula, erected by Aurelia Felicula, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32300571.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Septimio Vettio Papiriano |(centurioni) legionis I adiutricis domo Samisatis sic qui annos LXXIIII Vettius Varenus filius et heres eius faciendum curavit per Vettium Epictetum libertum suum\n
EDCS-32300571.translation: To the spirits of the departed, for Septimius Vettius Papirianus, a centurion in the I Adiutrix legion, from Samisata, who lived 74 years. Vettius Varenus, his son and heir, arranged for this monument to be made through his freedman Vettius Epictetus.
EDCS-32300571.summary: A funerary epitaph for Septimius Vettius Papirianus, a centurion of the I Adiutrix legion, erected by his son and heir, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32300572.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aureliae Aureliae quae vixit menses VIIII Aurelius Deipas mensor et Aelia Cas<hi=IIL>a parentes filiae dulcissimae faciendum curaverunt \n
EDCS-32300572.translation: To the spirits of the departed, for Aurelia [---], who lived 9 months. Aurelius Deipas, a surveyor, and Aelia Cas[---], her parents, arranged for this monument to be made for their dearest daughter.
EDCS-32300572.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aurelia, a young child, set up by her parents, a surveyor and his wife, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32300573.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Aurelio Dionisio praefecto legionis II adiutricis qui vixit nnos XXXVI menses III dies XXVII Aurelia Asclepiodota coniugi dulcissimo et Aurelia Dionisia filia faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-32300573.translation: To the spirits of the departed, for Marcus Aurelius Dionysius, prefect of the II Adiutrix legion, who lived 36 years, 3 months, and 27 days. Aurelia Asclepiodota, his wife, and Aurelia Dionisia, his daughter, arranged for this monument to be made for their dearest husband and father.
EDCS-32300573.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Aurelius Dionysius, prefect of the II Adiutrix legion, erected by his wife and daughter, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32300574.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iuliae quae et Flaviae Secundinae Flavi Secundi veterani legionis filiae Caius Valerius Seranus decurio coloniae Aquincensium equo publico matri incomparabili\n
EDCS-32300574.translation: To the Divine Manes. To Julia, also called Flavia Secundina, daughter of Flavius Secundus, a veteran of the legion. Gaius Valerius Seranus, decurion of the colony of Aquincum, holding the public equestrian rank, [erected this] to his incomparable mother.
EDCS-32300574.summary: A funerary epitaph for Julia Flavia Secundina, daughter of a legionary veteran, set up by Gaius Valerius Seranus, a decurion, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-32300575.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flaviae Paulae domo Mursa uxori annis XVII mensibus XI diebus XIIII Flavius Maximus beneficiarius consularis maritus coniugi rarissimae faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-32300575.translation: To the Divine Manes. Flavius Maximus, a beneficiarius consularis, had this made for his extremely rare wife, Flavia Paula, from Mursa, who lived 17 years, 11 months, and 14 days.
EDCS-32300575.summary: A funerary epitaph for Flavia Paula, wife of Flavius Maximus, a beneficiarius consularis, dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-32300577.text_edition: \n Iulio Candidiano veterano ex beneficiario consularis qui vixit annos LII et Iuliis Decorato qui vixit annos I et Decciano Candido Potentino Candidiano Aurelia Decorata coniu<g=C>i et filiis viva fecit \n
EDCS-32300577.translation: Aurelia Decorata erected this while alive for her husband Iulius Candidianus, a veteran and former beneficiarius consularis, who lived 52 years, and for her sons Iulius Decoratus, who lived 1 year, and Deccianus Candidus Potentinus Candidianus.
EDCS-32300577.summary: A funerary epitaph erected by Aurelia Decorata for her husband, a veteran beneficiarius consularis, and their children, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-32300578.text_edition: \n miles cohortis Ulpia Noviomagi Batavorum annorumVIII hic sit- miles cohortis eiusdem heres sibi P V titulum memoriae posuit\n
EDCS-32300578.translation: A soldier of the cohort Ulpia of Noviomagus Batavorum, aged 8, lies here. A soldier of the same cohort, his heir, placed this monument to his memory.
EDCS-32300578.summary: A tombstone for an 8-year-old soldier of the cohort Ulpia of Noviomagus Batavorum, erected by his heir, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-32300579.text_edition: \n Eburo Milionis filius miles in classe annorum LII hic situs est\n
EDCS-32300579.translation: Eburos, son of Milio, a soldier in the fleet, aged 52, lies here.
EDCS-32300579.summary: A tombstone for Eburos, son of Milio, a sailor aged 52, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-32300581.text_edition: \n Iulius Martialis hic situs est collegium fabrum et centonariorum faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-32300581.translation: Iulius Martialis lies here. The guild of blacksmiths and wool-workers had this made.
