EDCS-32300581.summary: A tombstone for Iulius Martialis, for whom the guild of blacksmiths and wool-workers arranged burial, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-32300582.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Gaio Caereio Sabino veterano legionis II adiutricis collegium fabrum et centonariorum ponendum curavit\n
EDCS-32300582.translation: To the Divine Manes. The guild of blacksmiths and wool-workers arranged for the burial of Gaius Caereius Sabinus, a veteran of Legio II Adjutrix.
EDCS-32300582.summary: A funerary epitaph for Gaius Caereius Sabinus, a veteran of Legio II Adjutrix, arranged by the guild of blacksmiths and wool-workers, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-32300583.text_edition: \n Caio Secconio Paterno domo Nemes sic nnorum LXX collegium fabrum et centonariorum posuit\n
EDCS-32300583.translation: The guild of blacksmiths and wool-workers erected [this] for Gaius Secconius Paternus, from Nemes, aged 70.
EDCS-32300583.summary: An epitaph for Gaius Secconius Paternus, aged 70, from Nemes, erected by the guild of blacksmiths and wool-workers, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-32300584.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelius Quintus cornicen domo Cirpi probatus annorum XX militavit \n
EDCS-32300584.translation: To the Divine Manes. Aelius Quintus, a cornicen from Cirpi, approved [for service], aged 20, served.
EDCS-32300584.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aelius Quintus, a cornicen (horn-blower) aged 20, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-32300585.text_edition: \n Marco Lucillio sic Germano signifero legionis II adiutricis piae fidelis M(arcus) Ulpius \n
EDCS-32300585.translation: To Marcus Lucillius Germanus, standard-bearer of Legio II Adjutrix, pious and faithful. Marcus Ulpius [erected this].
EDCS-32300585.summary: An epitaph honoring Marcus Lucillius Germanus, a standard-bearer of Legio II Adjutrix, erected by Marcus Ulpius, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-32300586.text_edition: \n Titus Rusticius Quintus annorum LX hic situs est collegium fabrum et centonariorum posuit\n
EDCS-32300586.translation: Titus Rusticius Quintus, aged sixty, is buried here. The guild of builders and carpet-makers erected this.
EDCS-32300586.summary: A tombstone for Titus Rusticius Quintus, set up by the guild of builders and carpet-makers, likely from the Imperial Roman period.

EDCS-32300587.text_edition: \n Caesernius Zosimus annorum LXXX natione Cilix hic situs est collegium fabrum et centonariorum posuit\n
EDCS-32300587.translation: Caesernius Zosimus, aged eighty, a Cilician by birth, is buried here. The guild of builders and carpet-makers erected this.
EDCS-32300587.summary: A tombstone for Caesernius Zosimus, an eighty-year-old Cilician, erected by the guild of builders and carpet-makers, likely from the Imperial Roman period.

EDCS-32300590.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus s Cordi nnum scolastico in FR Licinius Licander beneficiarius legati faciendu<m=N> curavit --- cum? Surilla matre titulu<m=N> <i=V>nscri<b=D>endum curaverunt \n
EDCS-32300590.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Licinius Licander, a junior officer (beneficiarius) of the legate, saw to the making of this for Cordius, a schools official. His mother Surilla and he had it inscribed.
EDCS-32300590.summary: A tomb inscription for Cordius, a schools official, commissioned by his mother and himself, with his mother Surilla also mentioned, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32300640.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld for Publius Aelius [---].
EDCS-32300640.summary: A fragmentary tomb inscription dedicated to the underworld spirits for an individual named Publius Aelius, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32300663.translation: Lucius Aurelius Sequens, a veteran from Aniensis (the district), from Caesaraugusta, formerly of the II Adjutrix Legion, has been donated.
EDCS-32300663.summary: A tomb inscription for Lucius Aurelius Sequens, a veteran of the II Adjutrix Legion from Caesaraugusta, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32300666.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Aurelio Sereno veterano ex |(centurione) cohortis I Lusitanorum domo Bassianis vixit nnos LVI hoc sepulcrum fecit Claudia Maximilla coniugi pientissimo et sibi posuit\n
EDCS-32300666.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Claudia Maximilla erected this tomb for her most devoted husband, Marcus Aurelius Serenus, a veteran of the I Cohort of Lusitanians, from Bassianis, who lived fifty-six years, and for herself.
EDCS-32300666.summary: A tomb inscription recording the erection of a sepulchre by Claudia Maximilla for her husband Marcus Aurelius Serenus, a veteran, and for herself, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32300688.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Teutano pro salute Impperatorum Severi et Antonini et Getae Caesaris et incolumitate Eraviscorum Lucius Aurelius Silvanus et Publius Proculeius Proculinus IIviri coloniae Aquincensium\n
EDCS-32300688.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, the Teutonic deity, for the safety of the Emperors Severus and Antoninus and Caesar Geta, and for the well-being of the Eravisci. Lucius Aurelius Silvanus and Publius Proculeius Proculinus, the two chief magistrates of the colony of Aquincum, [made this offering].
EDCS-32300688.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Teutonicus by two magistrates of Aquincum for the safety of Emperors Severus, Antoninus, and Geta, dating to their reigns (c. 209-211 CE).

EDCS-32400042.text_edition: \n PA et Iulio Dio patri Paga Iuliae Homena nte sororibus Iul(ius) Rogatus miles legionis interprex sic Sarmatarum ex officio consularis sibi parentibus et mus filius eius \n
EDCS-32400042.translation: For Paga and for Julius Dio, father, and for his sisters Paga and Homena. Julius Rogatus, a soldier of the [?] legion, an interpreter from the consular staff, [made this] for himself, his parents, and his son Mus.
EDCS-32400042.summary: A dedication by Julius Rogatus, a soldier and interpreter, for himself, his parents, and his son, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32500193.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Memoriae Petroniae Valeriae quondam in flore aetatis constitutae quae vixit nnos XIII menses XI dies XV et Petronio Caesiano qui vixit nnos IIII menses V dies VIIII et Petronio Caeserio qui vixit nnos V menses V dies V et Petroniae Nonnosae quae vixit nnos II menses III dies VIII Petronius Censorinus et Aurelia Maximina parentes filiis carissimis faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-32500193.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Petronius Censorinus and Aurelia Maximina, parents, had this made for their dearest children: Petronia Valeria, who lived thirteen years, eleven months, and fifteen days; Petronius Caesianus, who lived four years, five months, and nine days; Petronius Caeserius, who lived five years, five months, and five days; and Petronia Nonnosa, who lived two years, three months, and eight days.
EDCS-32500193.summary: A tomb inscription recording the death of four children, erected by their parents Petronius Censorinus and Aurelia Maximina, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32500203.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Titus Claudius Titi filius Tutor DOS annorum vivus fecit sibi et coniugi pientissimae Veturiae Paulinae annorum LXX hic s<i=E>(ti) sunt \n
EDCS-32500203.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Titus Claudius Tutor, son of Titus, aged [?], made this tomb while alive for himself and for his most devoted wife Veturia Paulina, aged seventy. They are buried here.
EDCS-32500203.summary: A tomb inscription for Titus Claudius Tutor and his wife Veturia Paulina, erected by Tutor during his lifetime, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32500206.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tiberio Claudio Vindiciano veterano ex duplicario \n
EDCS-32500206.translation: To the Divine Spirits of Tiberius Claudius Vindiciano, a veteran and former duplicarius.
EDCS-32500206.summary: This is a funerary inscription honoring Tiberius Claudius Vindiciano, a veteran, dating likely from the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-32700087.text_edition: \n Valerius Crescens et Decoratus filius hic siti sunt Britta coniugi et filio posuit\n
EDCS-32700087.translation: Valerius Crescens and his son Decoratus lie here. Britta placed this for her wife and son.
EDCS-32700087.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Britta for her deceased wife and son, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-32701054.summary: This is a dedication to the god Hercules by Flavius Diogenes, probably from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-32701055.text_edition: \n Nemesi Augustae Caius Larininus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-32701055.translation: To Nemesis Augusta, Caius Larininus gladly fulfilled his vow, as was deserved.
EDCS-32701055.summary: This is a votive inscription to the goddess Nemesis Augusta by Caius Larininus, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-32701056.text_edition: \n Venanti I M I et INME pro sal[ute ------\n
EDCS-32701056.translation: To Venantius, [I M I] and [I N M E] for the health [of...]
EDCS-32701056.summary: This is a fragmented votive inscription dedicated to Venantius, possibly for his health, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-32701057.text_edition: \n Asclepio et Hygiae Iulius Magnus DVI\n
EDCS-32701057.translation: To Asclepius and Hygieia, Iulius Magnus [DVI].
EDCS-32701057.summary: This is a dedication to the gods Asclepius and Hygieia by Iulius Magnus, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-32701063.text_edition: \n Cogitatus decurio alae Pannoniorum votum posuit libens animo\n
EDCS-32701063.translation: Cogitatus, decurion of the Pannonian ala, set up this vow with a willing heart.
EDCS-32701063.summary: This is a votive inscription by Cogitatus, a decurion in a cavalry unit from Pannonia, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-32701064.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Aelius Candidus miles cohortis I Thracum votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-32701064.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Aelius Candidus, a soldier of the I Thracum cohort, gladly fulfilled his vow, as was deserved.
EDCS-32701064.summary: This is a votive inscription to Jupiter by Aelius Candidus, a soldier in the I Thracum cohort, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-32701065.summary: This is a dedication to the sun god Sol Invictus, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-32701067.text_edition: \n Lucius Fidiclanius Luci filius Fresianus domo Iader decessit annorum XX \n
EDCS-32701067.translation: Lucius Fidiclanius Fresianus, son of Lucius, from Iader, died at the age of 20.
EDCS-32701067.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the death of Lucius Fidiclanius Fresianus, a young man from Iader, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-32701071.text_edition: \n Diis sic Patriis Conservatoribus Lucius Flavius Aper vir perfectissimus praeses votum libens merito posuit\n
EDCS-32701071.translation: To the Fatherly Gods Protectors, Lucius Flavius Aper, a most excellent man and governor, willingly and deservedly set up this vow.
EDCS-32701071.summary: This is a dedication to protective father gods by Lucius Flavius Aper, a governor, likely dating to the 3rd century CE or later when the title 'vir perfectissimus' became common for provincial governors.

EDCS-32701072.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Antonius Securus duplicarius legionis II adiutricis piae fidelis Gordianae votum solvit merito Imperatore domino nostro Gordiano Augusto et Pompeiano consulibus\n
EDCS-32701072.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Antonius Securus, a duplicarius of the II Adjutrix legion, pious and faithful, Gordian, fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly, under our Emperor Gordian Augustus and Pompeianus as consuls.
EDCS-32701072.summary: This is a votive inscription to Jupiter by Antonius Securus, a soldier of the II Adjutrix legion, dated to the consulship of Gordian and Pompeianus, placing it in 240 CE.

EDCS-32701073.text_edition: \n Silvano Silvestri Aurelius Claudianus advokatus sic ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-32701073.translation: To Silvanus of the Forest, Aurelius Claudianus, advocate, set this up according to his vow.
EDCS-32701073.summary: This is a votive inscription to Silvanus, the god of forests, by Aurelius Claudianus, an advocate.

EDCS-32701074.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelio Bito cornicini legionis II adiutricis stipendiorum XVIII bello desiderato vixsit sic nnos XXXVIII menses III et Vitalino filio vixit nnos IIII menses XI dies XVIII et Bitillae filiae et Dalmatio filio et Quintililiano filio posuit Aurelia Quintilila coniugi et filibus suis memoriam\n
EDCS-32701074.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed, Aurelius Bito, a cornicen of the II Adjutrix legion, who served 18 years and died in the desired war, lived 38 years, 3 months, and Vitalinus, his son, who lived 4 years, 11 months, and 18 days, and his daughter Bitilla, and his son Dalmatinus, and his son Quintilianus, Aurelia Quintilila, his wife, set up this memorial to her husband and her sons.
EDCS-32701074.summary: This is a funerary monument for Aurelius Bito, a soldier of the II Adjutrix legion, his wife Aurelia Quintilila, and their children, commemorating their lives and Bito's service.

EDCS-32701075.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Bitus cornis sic legionis II adiutricis filio carissimo Aurelio Vitalini sic qui vixsit sic annos IIII mensses XI dies XVIII pietatem filio suo fecit\n
EDCS-32701075.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed, Aurelius Bitus, a cornicen of the II Adjutrix legion, made this tribute of affection for his dearest son Aurelius Vitalinus, who lived 4 years, 11 months, and 18 days.
EDCS-32701075.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Aurelius Bitus to his young son Aurelius Vitalinus, who died at the age of four.

EDCS-32701076.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Hieraxs sic veteranus legionis II adiu<t=I>ricis piae fidelis <A=N>n<t=I>oninanae Vettiae Fortunatae coniugi carissimae pientissimae faci<e=F>ndum curavit \n
EDCS-32701076.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed, Aurelius Hierax, a veteran of the II Adjutrix legion, pious and faithful, Annoninana, arranged for the erection of this monument for his most beloved and most devoted wife, Vettia Fortunata.
EDCS-32701076.summary: This is a funerary monument commissioned by Aurelius Hierax, a veteran of the II Adjutrix legion, for his wife Vettia Fortunata.

EDCS-32701077.text_edition: \n Tiberius Tiberi filius Claudius Satto Camboduno veteranus legionis X geminae hic situs est annorum LX Ulpia Ursula coniugi pientissimo titulum posuit m(emoriae)\n
EDCS-32701077.translation: Tiberius Claudius Satto Cambodunus, a veteran of the X Gemina legion, who lived 60 years, is buried here; Ulpia Ursula erected this tombstone to her most devoted husband, for remembrance.
EDCS-32701077.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Tiberius Claudius Satto Cambodunus, a veteran of the X Gemina legion, erected by his wife Ulpia Ursula.

EDCS-32701078.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Manilius Rogatus veteranus et Aelia Dubitata Zebuca filia nnorum X parentes vivi fecerunt\n
EDCS-32701078.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed, Manilius Rogatus, a veteran, and Aelia Dubitata, his daughter, aged 10, their parents made this while alive.
EDCS-32701078.summary: This is a funerary monument created during the lifetime of the parents for their daughter Aelia Dubitata, who died at the age of 10.

EDCS-32701079.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ulpiae Optatae Marcus Ulpius Optatus veteranus legionis II adiutricis et Ulpia Marcia et Nasidia Severa parentes filiae pientissimae quae vixit anno I mensibus VII diebus XXII\n
EDCS-32701079.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed, for Ulpia Optata, Marcus Ulpius Optatus, a veteran of the II Adjutrix legion, and Ulpia Marcia and Nasidia Severa, parents, to their most devoted daughter, who lived 1 year, 7 months, and 22 days.
EDCS-32701079.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Ulpia Optata, a young child, set up by her parents, including a veteran of the II Adjutrix legion.

EDCS-32701080.text_edition: \n Lucio Septimio Fusco schol<a=E>stico qui vixit nnis XVIII mensibus VIIII diebus XIIII Lucius Septimius Fuscinus decurio municipii Mogetianae et Iulia Maximilla parentes filio karissimo memoriae dulcissimae \n
EDCS-32701080.translation: For Lucius Septimius Fuscus, a scribe, who lived 18 years, 9 months, and 14 days, Lucius Septimius Fuscinus, a decurion of the municipality of Mogetiana, and Iulia Maximilla, parents, set this up for their dearest son, for sweet remembrance.
EDCS-32701080.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Septimius Fuscus, a scribe who died young, set up by his parents, one of whom was a decurion in the municipality of Mogetiana.

EDCS-32701081.text_edition: \n vixit nnos LXX Claudii Ursus Maxima et Maximina filii patri carissimo et Bithinia Severa coniugi pientissimo faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-32701081.translation: Claudius Ursus, Maxima and Maximina, his children, had this made for their dearest father and for Bithinia Severa, his most devoted wife. He lived 70 years.
EDCS-32701081.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a man named Claudius Ursus, erected by his children and wife, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32900124.translation: To the Divine Manes of Septimia Marina, his wife.
EDCS-32900124.summary: This is a brief funerary dedication to Septimia Marina, likely set up by her husband, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34100083.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Statia liberta Ingenui annorum XXXV\n
EDCS-34100083.translation: To the Divine Manes. Statia, freedwoman of Ingenuus, aged 35 years.
EDCS-34100083.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Statia, a freedwoman, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34100084.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius nus stipendiorum XXIII qui vixit annos menses VII dies XI deifunctus expeditione Germanica Lauriaco morte sua Ulpia Firmilla coniugi bene merenti et Aurelius Firmanus pateri reli<c=Q>uias corpoiris sibi allatas per Festum nepotem suum condiderunt \n
EDCS-34100084.translation: To the Divine Manes. Ulpia Firmilla, his devoted wife, and Aurelius Firmanus, his father, dedicated this funerary monument for Aurelius Nus, who served 23 years and lived 7 months and 11 days, having died during the German campaign at Lauriacum, and for himself, having had his remains brought back by his grandson Festus.
EDCS-34100084.summary: This is a complex funerary epitaph for Aurelius Nus, who died in military service, erected by his wife and father, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34100085.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publius Aelius Auscultus Publius Aelius Victor Publio Aelio Victorino sacerdotali patrono pientissimo posuerunt\n
EDCS-34100085.translation: Publius Aelius Auscultus and Publius Aelius Victor dedicated this to Publius Aelius Victorinus, their most devoted priest and patron.
EDCS-34100085.summary: This is a dedication to Publius Aelius Victorinus, likely a patron or priest, erected by his clients Publius Aelius Auscultus and Publius Aelius Victor, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34100086.translation: Limarus, son of Cucalo, aged 16 years.
EDCS-34100086.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a young man named Limarus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34100087.text_edition: \n Magia Sicionis filia nnorum LII Semusscuttus Demnonis filius sibi vivus et coniugi titulum memoriae posuit\n
EDCS-34100087.translation: Semusscuttus, son of Demno, and Magia, daughter of Sicio, aged 52 years, erected this monument for themselves while still alive, and for their spouse.
EDCS-34100087.summary: This is a joint funerary monument erected by Semusscuttus and Magia for themselves and their spouses during their lifetime, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34100088.text_edition: \n Masia Cauronis annorum XXX Epmagurix Appi posuit sibi et coniugi carissimae\n
EDCS-34100088.translation: Epmagurix, son of Appius, erected this for Masia, daughter of Cauron, aged 30 years, and for himself and his dearest wife.
EDCS-34100088.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by Epmagurix for his wife Masia and for himself and his wife, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34100089.translation: For Camio, son of Masacon, to the Divine Manes.
EDCS-34100089.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Camio, son of Masacon, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34100090.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Appius posuit filio Somovito annorum decem\n
EDCS-34100090.translation: To the Divine Manes. Appius erected this for his son Somovitus, aged ten years.
EDCS-34100090.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a child named Somovitus, erected by his father Appius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-34100091.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Bonia qui vixit nnos IL coniugi carissimae \n
EDCS-34100091.translation: To the spirits of the departed, for Bonia, who lived 49 years, his dearest wife.
EDCS-34100091.summary: A funerary epitaph for Bonia, a wife, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-35500629.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-35500629.text_edition: \n legato Imperatoris Caesaris Augusti C provinciae Ante diem VII Idus Septembres Euphrata et Romano consulibus ex gregale Priscino Prisci filio Prisco ex Pannonia inferiore Iatumentianis et Prisco filio eius et Aprili filio eius descriptum et recognitum ex tabula aerea quae fixa est Romae in muro post templum divi Augusti ad Minervam Luci Pulli Marcionis Cai Publici Luperci Marci Iuni Pii Tiberi Claudi Iuliani Tiberi Claudi Cassandri Luci Pulli Benigni Tiberi Iuli Dativi\n
EDCS-35500629.translation: By decree of the Emperor Caesar Augustus, legate of the province, on the seventh day before the Ides of September, when Euphrates and Romanus were consuls, from the ranks, from Priscus, son of Priscus, from Lower Pannonia, to Priscinus, formerly a soldier from Iatumenta, and to his son Priscus, and to his son Aprilis. This document has been copied and verified from a bronze tablet which is fixed at Rome on the wall behind the Temple of the Divine Augustus, near the Temple of Minerva, belonging to Lucius Pullius Marcion, Caius Publicius Lupercus, Marcus Junius Pius, Tiberius Claudius Julianus, Tiberius Claudius Cassander, Lucius Pullius Benignus, Tiberius Julius Dativus.
EDCS-35500629.summary: This inscription is a legal record of a land grant or military discharge, issued under imperial authority and involving soldiers from Lower Pannonia, dating to the consulship of Euphrates and Romanus (likely late 1st century AD).

EDCS-38000299.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Publius Aelius Cesorinus decurio coloniae Bassianensium ex voto posuit\n
EDCS-38000299.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Publius Aelius Cesorinus, a decurion of the colony of Bassianenses, set this up in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-38000299.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by a decurion of the colony of Bassianenses, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-38000305.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quinto Nercio Quirina Victori Sirmio militi legionis II adiutricis centuria Iu Proculi annorum XXX stipendiorum Tetrini MAT \n
EDCS-38000305.translation: To the spirits of the departed, for Quintus Nercinus Quirinus Victor, a soldier of the II Adjutrix Legion, from the century of Proculus, 30 years old, with X years of service.
EDCS-38000305.summary: A funerary epitaph for Quintus Nercinus Quirinus Victor, a soldier of the II Adjutrix Legion, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-38000306.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Ulpio Ianuario veterano legionis II adiutricis et Flaviae Valentinae eius et Saburtillae vernaculae Marcus Aurelius Vitalis heres faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-38000306.translation: To the spirits of the departed, for Marcus Ulpius Ianuarius, a veteran of the II Adjutrix Legion, and his wife Flavia Valentina, and Saburtilla, a household slave. Marcus Aurelius Vitalis, the heir, saw to it that this was made.
EDCS-38000306.summary: A funerary inscription for a veteran of the II Adjutrix Legion, his wife, and a slave, with the heir arranging the monument, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-38000308.text_edition: \n et Aureli-- Disa qu vixit nnos III mensem I dies V Aurelii Quintianus et V per libert D ia cum Aurelia --- Victorina ex testamento faciendum curaverunt \n
EDCS-38000308.translation: and Aurelia Disa, who lived 3 years, 1 month, and 5 days. Aurelius Quintianus and V... according to the will, saw to it that this was made, through their freedman.
EDCS-38000308.summary: A funerary epitaph for a child, Aurelia Disa, with the monument erected by Aurelius Quintianus and another person through a freedman, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-38000310.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelio Maximo d<e=F>curioni qui vixit nnis <L=I>XXX Aurelius Poludeuces sic cives sic pietatis gratia <f=E>ecit \n
EDCS-38000310.translation: To the spirits of the departed, for Aurelius Maximus, a decurion, who lived 80 years. Aurelius Poludeuces made this for him, out of civic duty and piety.
EDCS-38000310.summary: A funerary epitaph for Aurelius Maximus, a decurion, erected by Aurelius Poludeuces, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-38000311.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Publius Aelius Verinus |(centurio) legionis II adiutricis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-38000311.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Publius Aelius Verinus, a centurion of the II Adjutrix Legion, paid his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-38000311.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by a centurion of the II Adjutrix Legion, recording the fulfillment of a vow, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-38000312.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Valerio Sabiniano filio pio vixit annos VI et Valeriae Mamani matri karissimae vixit annos LXXX Caius Valerius Martinus cornicen legionis IIII Flaviae faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-38000312.translation: To the spirits of the departed, for Caius Valerius Sabinianus, his pious son, who lived 6 years, and for Valeria Mamanus, his dearest mother, who lived 80 years. Caius Valerius Martinus, a cornicen (horn-blower) of the IIII Flavia Legion, saw to it that this was made.
EDCS-38000312.summary: A funerary epitaph commemorating a son who died young and his mother, erected by a cornicen of the IIII Flavia Legion, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-38000313.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Cominius Cominianus eques alae Brittonum stipendiorum IIII vixit nnos XX hic situs est Cominius Celer fratri faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-38000313.translation: To the spirits of the departed, for Caius Cominius Cominianus, an equestrian of the ala Brittonum (Brigantes cavalry wing), with 4 years of service, who lived 20 years. Here he lies. Cominius Celer erected this for his brother.
EDCS-38000313.summary: A funerary epitaph for Caius Cominius Cominianus, a cavalryman of the ala Brittonum who died at age 20, erected by his brother, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-38000315.text_edition: \n Herculi Augusto Marcus Antonius Victorinus IIvir coloniae Aquincensium votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-38000315.translation: Marcus Antonius Victorinus, duovir of the colony of Aquincum, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Hercules Augustus.
EDCS-38000315.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Hercules Augustus by Marcus Antonius Victorinus, a duovir of Aquincum, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-38000317.text_edition: \n Titus Sulpicius Iustus Magni filius annorum XXV hic situs est\n
EDCS-38000317.translation: Titus Sulpicius Iustus, son of Magnus, aged 25, lies here.
EDCS-38000317.summary: This is a tombstone recording the death of Titus Sulpicius Iustus, a 25-year-old man, probably from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-38000318.summary: This is a brief dedication by Nonnus Busai, the purpose and recipient of which are unknown, but likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-38000319.text_edition: \n M Iovi Optimo Maximo Aurelius Setanus veteranus alae primae Thracum CEP CEORVM votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-38000319.translation: Aurelius Setanus, a veteran of the first cavalry regiment of Thracians, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.
EDCS-38000319.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Aurelius Setanus, a veteran of a Thracian cavalry unit, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-42700004.text_edition: \n Genio centuriae sacrum Titus Gramonius Lucilianus \n
EDCS-42700004.translation: Sacred to the genius of the century. Titus Gramonius Lucilianus.
EDCS-42700004.summary: This is a dedication to the genius of a century, made by Titus Gramonius Lucilianus, likely a soldier or officer, probably from the 1st to 3rd century CE.

EDCS-42700005.text_edition: \n Schola in ruinas collapsa reintegrata Licini Gaudenti Papia ex civitate Lyipias sumptibus! Paulini Domiti Nimesi Domiti Aureli Tauri Aureli Prisci Domiti nti Mon Licini Veri Supsi S Culumbula Stercoria B P Martiniano Tauro et Iu Hannibaliano et Asclepiodoto consulibus\n
EDCS-42700005.translation: The meeting hall, collapsed into ruins, was restored by Licinius Gaudentius Papia from the city of Lyipias at the expense of Paulinus Domitius of Nimes, Domitius Aurelius Taurus, Aurelius Priscus, Domitius Antimus, Licinius Verus, Supsius Columbulla, and Stercoria. [In the consulship of] Martinianus Taurus and Iunius Hannibalianus and Asclepiodotus.
EDCS-42700005.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a collapsed meeting hall (schola) funded by several individuals, including Licinius Gaudentius Papia and others, and is dated by the consuls Martinianus Taurus, Iunius Hannibalianus, and Asclepiodotus, likely in the 4th century CE.

EDCS-42700012.summary: This is a brief dedication to Asclepius and Hygieia, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-42700013.text_edition: \n Aesculapio Hygiae pro salute Imperatoris Silvano Domitius Victorinus |(centurio) legionis IIII Flaviae Dis Conservatoribus sacrum\n
EDCS-42700013.translation: Sacred to Aesculapius and Hygieia for the health of the Emperor. Domitius Victorinus, centurion of the IV Flaviae legion, [dedicates this] to the Gods Who Preserve.
EDCS-42700013.summary: This is a religious dedication to Aesculapius, Hygieia, and the "Gods Who Preserve" by Domitius Victorinus, a centurion of the IV Flaviae legion, for the Emperor's health, likely from the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-42700014.text_edition: \n Apollini Turmus Cudanei Baradatix votum solvit libens\n
EDCS-42700014.translation: Turmus Cudanei Baradatix gladly fulfilled his vow to Apollo.
EDCS-42700014.summary: This is a votive inscription to Apollo by Turmus Cudanei Baradatix, likely a local deity or person of provincial origin, possibly from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-42700015.text_edition: \n Iovi Granno Apollini Publius Aelius Priscinus optio legionis II adiutricis [Imperatore Commodo VI] et Septimiano consulibus\n
EDCS-42700015.translation: Publius Aelius Priscinus, optio of the II adiutrix legion, [dedicated this] to Jupiter Grannus Apollo, in the consulship of Emperor Commodus for the 6th time and Septimianus.
EDCS-42700015.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Grannus Apollo by Publius Aelius Priscinus, an optio in the II adiutrix legion, dated to the consulship of Emperor Commodus (VI) and Septimianus, likely around 175-180 CE.

EDCS-42700016.text_edition: \n Dianae votum Titius Faustinus beneficiarius consularis Sabino II et Venusto consulibus\n
EDCS-42700016.translation: A vow to Diana by Titius Faustinus, a benefited soldier of a consular governor, during the consulship of Sabinus II and Venustus.
EDCS-42700016.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Diana by a soldier under a consular governor, dated to the consulship of Sabinus II and Venustus.

EDCS-42700018.summary: This is a dedication to the goddess Epona, likely from a military context given her association with horses and cavalry.

EDCS-42800203.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Marcus Aurelius Ingenuus cornicularius legati legionis II adiutricis piae fidelis Severianae votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-42800203.translation: Marcus Aurelius Ingenuus, a *cornicularius* of the legate of the Second Legion Adjutrix, pious and faithful, Severian, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Best and Greatest.
EDCS-42800203.summary: This inscription records a soldier named Marcus Aurelius Ingenuus of the Second Legion Adjutrix fulfilling a vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, likely dating to the Severan period.

EDCS-42800204.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Aurelius Maximianus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-42800204.translation: Aurelius Maximianus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Best and Greatest.
EDCS-42800204.summary: This is a votive inscription recording Aurelius Maximianus fulfilling a vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.

EDCS-42800205.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Petronius Secundianus votum solvit merito\n
EDCS-42800205.translation: Petronius Secundianus fulfilled his vow deservedly.
EDCS-42800205.summary: This inscription records Petronius Secundianus fulfilling a vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus.

EDCS-42800206.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Septimius...
EDCS-42800206.summary: This is an incomplete dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by someone named Septimius.

EDCS-42800207.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Ulpius Victor pro salute sua suorumque votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-42800207.translation: Ulpius Victor gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Best and Greatest for his own well-being and that of his family.
EDCS-42800207.summary: This inscription records Ulpius Victor fulfilling a vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus for the health of himself and his family.

EDCS-42800210.summary: This is an incomplete dedication to the god Hercules.

EDCS-42800211.text_edition: \n Collegium cornicinium legionis II adiutricis piae fidelis Severianaes Primus cornicen in honorem domus divinae Imperatore domino nostro Aurelio [Severo] Alexandro? --- Kalendas Apriles dono dedit\n
EDCS-42800211.translation: The college of *cornicines* of the Second Legion Adjutrix, pious and faithful, Severian, Primus the *cornicen*, in honor of the divine house, on the Kalends of April, gave as a gift to our Emperor Lord Aurelius [Severus] Alexander.
EDCS-42800211.summary: This inscription records a gift made by the college of trumpeters of the Second Legion Adjutrix to Emperor Aurelius Alexander, dated to the Kalends of April, likely in the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-42900229.summary: This is a dedication to the rural deity Silvanus Domestus.

EDCS-42900231.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico votum solvit libens merito Aurelius Faladus Flavus\n
EDCS-42900231.translation: Aurelius Faladus Flavus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Silvanus Domesticus.
EDCS-42900231.summary: This votive inscription records Aurelius Faladus Flavus fulfilling a vow to the household god Silvanus Domesticus.

EDCS-42900232.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico Gaius Iulius Primus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-42900232.translation: Gaius Iulius Primus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Silvanus Domesticus.
EDCS-42900232.summary: This votive inscription records Gaius Iulius Primus fulfilling a vow to the household god Silvanus Domesticus.

EDCS-42900233.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico sacrum Titius Priscianus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-42900233.translation: Titius Priscianus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Silvanus Domesticus.
EDCS-42900233.summary: This votive inscription records Titius Priscianus fulfilling a vow to the household god Silvanus Domesticus.

EDCS-42900234.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico Primus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-42900234.translation: Primus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Silvanus Domesticus.
EDCS-42900234.summary: This votive inscription records Primus fulfilling a vow to the household god Silvanus Domesticus.

EDCS-42900236.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico Salutari Severus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-42900236.translation: Severus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Silvanus Domesticus Salutaris.
EDCS-42900236.summary: This votive inscription records Severus fulfilling a vow to the healing household god Silvanus Domesticus Salutaris.

EDCS-42900237.text_edition: \n Soli Deo Marcus Foviacius Verus decurio municipii Aquincensium IIvirum sic quinquennalis flaminicius votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-42900237.translation: Marcus Foviacius Verus, decurion of the municipality of Aquincum, quinquennial magistrate and priest, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Soli Deo.
EDCS-42900237.summary: This inscription records Marcus Foviacius Verus, a local official from Aquincum, fulfilling a vow to Soli Deo, likely around the mid-3rd century AD based on the imperial context and his titles.

EDCS-42900240.translation: Marcius Sura, son of Proclus, to Soli Soccio.
EDCS-42900240.summary: This is a dedication to the god Soli Soccio by Marcius Sura.

EDCS-42900245.text_edition: \n V Diis deabusque Genioque loci pro salute Im[peratoris] Cae]saris Publi Li[cini Gallieni Invicti Augusti nost[ri] Marcus Aurelius Valentinianus vir clarissimus legatus Augusti pro praetore\n
EDCS-42900245.translation: Marcus Aurelius Valentinianus, a most distinguished man, imperial legate with praetorian authority, [for the safety] of the gods, goddesses, and the Genius of the place, [for the safety] of our Emperor Caesar Publius Licinius Gallienus Invictus Augustus.
EDCS-42900245.summary: This inscription records Marcus Aurelius Valentinianus, a high-ranking imperial legate, dedicating a monument for the safety of the gods and Emperor Gallienus, likely in the mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-42900246.text_edition: \n Saluti sac(rum) Titus Repellius Commodus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-42900246.translation: Titus Repellius Commodus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Salus (Health).
EDCS-42900246.summary: This votive inscription records Titus Repellius Commodus fulfilling a vow to the goddess Salus (Health).

EDCS-42900248.text_edition: \n sacrum Aurelius Lucianus cornicen legionis II adiutricis Antoninianae pro salute sua et conlegorum sic suorum Messala et Sabino consulibus\n
EDCS-42900248.translation: Sacred: Aurelius Lucianus, a horn player of the II Adjutrix Legion Antoniniana, for his own safety and that of his comrades, under the consulship of Messala and Sabinus.
EDCS-42900248.summary: This inscription records Aurelius Lucianus, a horn player in the II Adjutrix Legion, dedicating something for himself and his colleagues during the consulship of Messala and Sabinus, likely in the late 2nd or early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-42900250.text_edition: \n Aurelius Ianuarius Savaria tubicen inpendiis suis posuit Imperatore domino nostro Marco Aurelio Severo Alexandro cons(ule)\n
EDCS-42900250.translation: Aurelius Ianuarius, trumpeter of Savaria, dedicated this at his own expense during the consulship of our Emperor Lord Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander.
EDCS-42900250.summary: This is a dedication by Aurelius Ianuarius, a trumpeter, to Emperor Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander.

EDCS-42900251.text_edition: \n Aurelius Vitalis miles legionis II adiutricis piae fidelis Severianae votum solvit merito\n
EDCS-42900251.translation: Aurelius Vitalis, a loyal and faithful soldier of Legio II Adiutrix Severiana, fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-42900251.summary: This records a votive offering by Aurelius Vitalis, a soldier of Legio II Adiutrix.

EDCS-42900253.text_edition: \n tesserarius legionis II adiutricis piae <f=B>(idelis) Antoninianae pro se et suis votum solvit libens merito \n
EDCS-42900253.translation: The tesserarius of Legio II Adiutrix Pia Fidelis Antoniniana fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly for himself and his family.
EDCS-42900253.summary: This is a votive inscription by a tesserarius of Legio II Adiutrix Antoniniana.

EDCS-42900254.text_edition: \n veterani et milites et pagani votum solverunt libentes merito Fusco II et Dextro consulibus Septimius Teca Iulius Potnitanus Aurelius BIsanus emeritus Aurelius Respectus armamentarius Aurelius Mercurialis optio\n
EDCS-42900254.translation: Veterans, soldiers, and villagers willingly and deservedly fulfilled their vow. Septimius Teca, Iulius Potnitanus, Aurelius Bisanus, the retired soldier, Aurelius Respectus, the armourer, and Aurelius Mercurialis, the optio, [made this offering] during the consulship of Fuscus II and Dexter.
EDCS-42900254.summary: This inscription records a collective votive offering by veterans, soldiers, and villagers, listing several individuals, during the consulship of Fuscus II and Dexter.

EDCS-42900258.text_edition: \n Silvano votum solvit libens merito Claudius Severus\n
EDCS-42900258.translation: Claudius Severus willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Silvanus.
EDCS-42900258.summary: This is a votive inscription to the god Silvanus by Claudius Severus.

EDCS-42900259.text_edition: \n Deo Silvano s(acrum) fecerunt Marcus Claudius Lucilius et Aurelius Festus votum solverunt libentes merito\n
EDCS-42900259.translation: Marcus Claudius Lucilius and Aurelius Festus willingly and deservedly fulfilled their vow to the god Silvanus.
EDCS-42900259.summary: This records a votive offering to the god Silvanus by Marcus Claudius Lucilius and Aurelius Festus.

EDCS-42900260.translation: To the god Silvanus, favorable deity.
EDCS-42900260.summary: This is a short dedication to the god Silvanus.

EDCS-42900262.text_edition: \n Fonti Dei Iulius Severus pro se et suis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-42900262.translation: Iulius Severus willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to the god of the Springs for himself and his family.
EDCS-42900262.summary: This is a votive inscription to the god of the Springs by Iulius Severus.

EDCS-42900263.translation: Silvanus fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-42900263.summary: This is a votive inscription by someone named Silvanus.

EDCS-42900264.summary: This inscription simply records the names of Symphorus and Marcus.

EDCS-42900266.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo deo Soli Crescens ex voto\n
EDCS-42900266.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, the god Sol, Crescens fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-42900266.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Sol by Crescens, likely from a private votive offering.

EDCS-42900267.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Iunoni Reginae Minervae pro salute domini nostri Imperatoris Marci Aureli Antonini Augusti Aurelius Domitianus et Titus Asininus Florianus bbenefficiarii votum solverunt libentes merito consularis Imperatore A[nt]onino Augusto II et Sacerdote consulibus\n
EDCS-42900267.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Juno Regina, and Minerva, for the health of our lord Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, Aurelius Domitianus and Titus Asininus Florianus, beneficiarii, gladly and deservedly fulfilled their vow, during the consulship of Emperor Antoninus Augustus II and the priest consul.
EDCS-42900267.summary: A dedication to Jupiter, Juno, and Minerva by two beneficiarii for the health of Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, dated to his second consulship and an associated priesthood.

EDCS-42900269.text_edition: \n Libero Patri et Liberae Augustae Lucius Seranus Erotius \n
EDCS-42900269.translation: To Liber Pater and Libera Augusta, Lucius Seranus Erotius.
EDCS-42900269.summary: A dedication to the deities Liber Pater and Libera Augusta by Lucius Seranus Erotius, likely a private offering.

EDCS-42900276.text_edition: \n Invicto Deo sacrum Caius Iulius Ingenuus scriba civitatis Aquincensium\n
EDCS-42900276.translation: Sacred to the Unconquered God, Caius Iulius Ingenuus, scribe of the community of Aquincensium.
EDCS-42900276.summary: An inscription dedicating a site to an Unconquered God by Caius Iulius Ingenuus, a scribe of Aquincensium.

EDCS-42900277.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute Severi et Antonini August--- \n
EDCS-42900277.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus for the health of Severus and Antoninus Augusti...
EDCS-42900277.summary: A partial dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus for the health of Emperors Severus and Antoninus.

EDCS-42900279.summary: A very brief dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by someone named Vius.

EDCS-43000395.text_edition: \n <D=T>eo Sil(vano) Domestico Hermes votum libens solvit \n
EDCS-43000395.translation: To the Household God Silvanus, Hermes gladly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-43000395.summary: A votive offering to the household god Silvanus by Hermes.

EDCS-43000399.text_edition: \n NGOIus centurio legionis Victorino et Severo consulibus\n
EDCS-43000399.translation: ... Ngoius, centurion of the legion [VI] Victrix, during the consulship of Victorinus and Severus.
EDCS-43000399.summary: A dedication by a centurion of the VI Victrix legion, dated by the consuls Victorinus and Severus.

EDCS-43000400.text_edition: \n eques legionis II adiutricis piae fidelis pro salute sua et suorum votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-43000400.translation: A cavalryman of the II Adiutrix Pia Fidelis legion fulfilled his vow for the health of himself and his family, gladly and deservedly.
EDCS-43000400.summary: A votive offering by a cavalryman of the II Adiutrix legion for his and his family's well-being.

EDCS-43000401.text_edition: \n ntus Calatu(s MT legionis II adiutricis piae fidelis votum solvit\n
EDCS-43000401.translation: ...ntus Calatus of the II Adiutrix Pia Fidelis legion fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-43000401.summary: A partial inscription recording a fulfilled vow by someone named Antus Calatus from the II Adiutrix Pia Fidelis legion.

EDCS-43000403.text_edition: \n elpi votum fecit libens Atius Eumer\n
EDCS-43000403.translation: Elpi gladly fulfilled her vow, Atius Eumer.
EDCS-43000403.summary: This inscription records Elpi, possibly a freedwoman of Atius Eumer, gladly fulfilling a vow, likely on a personal monument.

EDCS-43000410.text_edition: \n simus pro salute sua votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-43000410.translation: Simus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow for his own safety.
EDCS-43000410.summary: This inscription records Simus fulfilling a vow for his well-being, likely on a votive monument, probably dating from the imperial period.

EDCS-43000411.translation: F. Nice, his daughter, erected this.
EDCS-43000411.summary: This inscription records F. Nice erecting a monument, likely a tombstone, for a male relative.

EDCS-43000421.translation: Lucius Septimius Gaianus, for himself and his family...
EDCS-43000421.summary: This inscription is the beginning of a dedication or funerary text by Lucius Septimius Gaianus for himself and his relatives.

EDCS-43000422.text_edition: \n NA tatus optio legionis II adiutricis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-43000422.translation: Natus, opto of the Second Adjutant Legion, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-43000422.summary: This inscription records Natus, an optio in the Legio II Adjutrix, fulfilling a vow, likely military in origin.

EDCS-43000423.summary: This inscription records Theocetis fulfilling a vow, likely on a votive or funerary monument.

EDCS-43000429.translation: M. Valerius... by order of the God...
EDCS-43000429.summary: This inscription records M. Valerius acting under divine command, possibly related to a religious dedication or oracle.

EDCS-43000462.summary: This inscription records Blanda fulfilling a vow, likely on a personal votive monument.

EDCS-44200269.text_edition: \n Vivat Simplicia VII Nocidius Amanti Nepotia vivis VI Nepotia Ursa Valentia matrona Vitaliani vivatis Argutio feliciter Munidus ALANCO Calandinus vivus Ave\n
EDCS-44200269.translation: Long live Simplicia! Nocidius Amanti and Nepotia (lived) seven (years). Nepotia Ursa Valentia, a respectable matron, (lived) six (years). May you live, Argutio! May you live happily, Munidus! Calandinus (lived) while alive. Greetings!
EDCS-44200269.summary: This is a complex funerary inscription recording the ages at death of several individuals, including children and adults, likely from a family plot.

EDCS-44200270.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Silvano Tertullinus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-44200270.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, to Silvanus, Tertullinus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-44200270.summary: This inscription records Tertullinus fulfilling a vow to Jupiter Optimus Maximus and Silvanus, likely on a religious monument.

EDCS-44200272.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute Cai Iuli Agathopi conductoris ferrariarum Pannoniarum itemque provinciarum transmarinarum Gamicus arkarius votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-44200272.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, for the safety of Caius Julius Agathopus, contractor of the Pannonian iron mines and also of the overseas provinces, Gamicus, an arkarius, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-44200272.summary: This is a votive inscription to Jupiter, dedicated by Gamicus for the well-being of the contractor of mines, likely from the late Roman Empire.

EDCS-44200273.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Luciae Apuleiae Candidae coniugi carissimae quae vixit annos XXV pro pietate posuit Hermoix bene merenti\n
EDCS-44200273.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed, for Lucia Apuleia Candida, dearest wife, who lived 25 years; Hermoix erected this for her deserving merit.
EDCS-44200273.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to Lucia Apuleia Candida by her husband Hermoix, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-44200274.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription dedicated to the god Silvanus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-46700072.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Fulguratori votum solvit libens merito Septimius Severus veteranus\n
EDCS-46700072.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, the Thunderer, Septimius Severus, a veteran, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-46700072.summary: This is a votive inscription to Jupiter, dedicated by a veteran named Septimius Severus, possibly from the late 2nd or early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-46700083.translation: To Septimius Viator, husband, Spirits of the Departed.
EDCS-46700083.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription dedicated to Septimius Viator, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-48300012.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription that likely records a name and possibly a location or action, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-48300013.translation: To Aurelius Auxilia, parents, for his deserving merit.
EDCS-48300013.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription dedicated to Aurelius Auxilia, possibly by his parents, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-48300018.summary: This is a very brief inscription that likely records an age and military rank (optio), from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-48300051.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription mentioning the office of duoviri in the Roman city of Aquincum, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-48300055.summary: This is a highly fragmentary inscription that possibly mentions the emperor Commodus and a cohort, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-48300062.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Terrae Matri Titus Ta\n
EDCS-48300062.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, and to Mother Earth, Titus Ta...
EDCS-48300062.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter and the Earth goddess, likely from a private individual, Titus Ta, with the rest of the name lost.

EDCS-48300066.text_edition: \n Silvano Magno Aelius Amandus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-48300066.translation: To Silvanus Magnus, Aelius Amandus fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-48300066.summary: This is a votive inscription by Aelius Amandus to the god Silvanus Magnus, indicating the fulfillment of a promise.

EDCS-48300069.text_edition: \n Silvano Silvestri Septimius Orfius pro se et suis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-48300069.translation: To Silvanus Silvestris, Septimius Orfius, for himself and his family, fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-48300069.summary: This is a votive inscription by Septimius Orfius for his family to Silvanus Silvestris, the god of woods and wild places.

EDCS-48300073.text_edition: \n Silvano Silvestri Aurelius Frontinianus AEBV et Aurelius Frontinus et Aurelius Pronus filii votum solverunt libentes merito\n
EDCS-48300073.translation: To Silvanus Silvestris, Aurelius Frontinianus AEBV, and his sons Aurelius Frontinus and Aurelius Pronus, fulfilled their vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-48300073.summary: This is a votive inscription by Aurelius Frontinianus and his sons to Silvanus Silvestris, fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-48300076.translation: To Sol Invictus, Publius Aelius Brinco.
EDCS-48300076.summary: This is a dedication to the Unconquered Sun god Sol Invictus by Publius Aelius Brinco.

EDCS-48300077.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Medulius Utvivunis annorum L hic situs est Appianus et Otiorix memoriam heredes faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-48300077.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld, Medulius Utvivunis, aged 50, is buried here; Appianus and Otiorix, his heirs, saw that this monument was erected in memory.
EDCS-48300077.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Medulius Utvivunis, erected by his heirs Appianus and Otiorix.

EDCS-48300086.translation: Sacred to Minerva the Protectress, by Flavius Titianus Cerialis, a speculator (scout/guard), son of Titus, and Marcus Aurelius Iulianus, a speculator of the First Legion Adiutrix Pia Fidelis, son of Marcus, on the Kalends of March, when Civica Pompeianus and Lolliuanus Avitus were consuls.
EDCS-48300086.summary: This is a sacred dedication to Minerva by two military men, Flavius Titianus Cerialis and Marcus Aurelius Iulianus, dated by consuls to 157 AD.

EDCS-48300093.text_edition: \n stipendiorum XVII hic situs est Fronto stip<e=A>ndiarius et Felix libertus titulum memoriae posuerunt \n
EDCS-48300093.translation: Fronto, a stipendiarius (soldier serving for pay), and Felix, his freedman, placed this inscription in memory after 17 years of service.
EDCS-48300093.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Fronto, a soldier, and his freedman Felix, commemorating their lives or service.

EDCS-48300094.text_edition: \n secundus heres et amicus faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-48300094.translation: Secundus, heir and friend, saw that this was done.
EDCS-48300094.summary: This is a brief statement indicating that Secundus, who was both an heir and a friend, arranged for the erection of a monument or the performance of a funerary rite.

EDCS-48300097.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld, Salluvia...
EDCS-48300097.summary: This is a tomb inscription for Salluvia, with the rest of her name and details lost.

EDCS-48300098.translation: To the departed spirits and to my wife.
EDCS-48300098.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the Manes and the deceased's wife, likely from a tomb.

EDCS-48300099.text_edition: \n ina SEESATVTES filia gener Flavius Seccio\n
EDCS-48300099.translation: Ina Seesa Tutes, daughter, and her son-in-law Flavius Seccio.
EDCS-48300099.summary: This fragmented inscription likely records names, possibly related to family and inheritance.

EDCS-48300105.text_edition: \n Valerius pater filiae dulcissimae faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-48300105.translation: Valerius, the father, had this made for his dearest daughter.
EDCS-48300105.summary: This inscription records a father dedicating a monument or memorial to his beloved daughter.

EDCS-48300106.summary: This inscription is a dedication to a beloved wife, likely part of a funerary monument.

EDCS-48300109.text_edition: \n Dasius RaT eques legionis II adiutricis piae fidelis annorum XLIII stipendiorum X hic situs est Publius Aelius Dasimius vet(eranus) legionis eiusdem fratri pientissimo fecit\n
EDCS-48300109.translation: Dasius RaT, a cavalryman of Legio II Adiutrix Pia Fidelis, aged 43, with 10 years of service, is buried here. Publius Aelius Dasimius, a veteran of the same legion, made this for his most devoted brother.
EDCS-48300109.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Dasius RaT, a soldier, and commemorates him through his veteran brother.

EDCS-48300110.text_edition: \n Aelio Valenti decurioni coloniae Aqquincensium IIviro vica SVI P si \n
EDCS-48300110.translation: To Aelius Valens, decurion of the colony of Aquincense, duovir.
EDCS-48300110.summary: This inscription honors Aelius Valens, a local official in the Roman colony of Aquincense.

EDCS-48300111.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aeliorum --- Severo nnorum et Anai coniugi annorum V et Aequatae bruti annorum e annorum VI et Res \n
EDCS-48300111.translation: To the departed spirits, to Severus of the Aelii [family], aged [..] years, and to his wife Anai, aged V years, and to Aequata, daughter of Brutus, aged [..] years, aged VI years, and to Res...
EDCS-48300111.summary: This fragmented funerary inscription lists several deceased individuals, including members of the Aelii family and their relatives.

EDCS-48300112.text_edition: \n Aelia Valent<i=L>na memorian sic posuit filiis suis Aelio Vitaliano et Aeliae Faustinae annorum XII Aelio Valentiano annorum VIII Iuliae Opta<t=C>ianae nnorum VIII et Aelius Vitalianus annorum XI \n
EDCS-48300112.translation: Aelia Valentina set up this memorial for her sons Aelius Vitalianus, aged 12, and Aelius Valentianus, aged 8, and for Julia Optatiana, aged 8, and Aelius Vitalianus, aged 11.
EDCS-48300112.summary: This funerary inscription records a mother, Aelia Valentina, setting up a memorial for her deceased children, listing their names and ages.

EDCS-48300114.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Aemilius Teretina C<a>mpanus domo Viminacio miles legionis II adiutricis adiutor principis praetorii stipendiorum XVI nnorum XXXVI hic situs est Marcia soror fratri pientissimo posuit\n
EDCS-48300114.translation: To the departed spirits, Marcus Aemilius Teretina Campanus, from Viminacio, a soldier of Legio II Adiutrix, assistant to the commander of the praetorium, with 16 years of service, aged 36, is buried here. Marcia, his sister, placed this for her most devoted brother.
EDCS-48300114.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Marcus Aemilius Teretina Campanus, a soldier, and was erected by his sister.

EDCS-48300116.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Arriae Severae quae vixit nnos LX Aelia Martina filia et heres et Aelius Crescentianus immunis legionis II adiutricis faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-48300116.translation: To the departed spirits, for Arria Severa, who lived 60 years. Her daughter and heir Aelia Martina and Aelius Crescentianus, an immunis soldier of Legio II Adiutrix, had this made.
EDCS-48300116.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Arria Severa and was commissioned by her daughter and another individual, likely a soldier.

EDCS-48300117.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Atilio Flavia Sirmio Vitali veterano legionis II adiutricis nnorum LX hic situs est collegium fabrum et centonariorum posuit\n
EDCS-48300117.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Gaius Atilius Vitalis, veteran of the Second Legion Adiutrix, sixty years of age, lies here. The guild of builders and furnishers erected this.
EDCS-48300117.summary: This funerary epitaph records the burial of Gaius Atilius Vitalis, a veteran, and states that the guild of builders and furnishers erected it.

EDCS-48300118.text_edition: \n Aurelius Mucatralis candidatus legionis II adiutricis et Obsenia Opes coniuges pientissimi sibi vivi fecerunt\n
EDCS-48300118.translation: Aurelius Mucatralis, candidate of the Second Legion Adiutrix, and Obsenia Opes, his most devoted wife, had this made for themselves while alive.
EDCS-48300118.summary: This epitaph records that Aurelius Mucatralis and his wife Obsenia Opes commissioned their tomb while they were still living.

EDCS-48300119.text_edition: \n Aurelio Augusti liberto Paoni sic praeposito lecticariorum Aurelia Eupraepia sic coniugi carissimo et inconparabili sic bene merenti et Paulinus libertus patrono bene merenti faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-48300119.translation: Aurelia Eupraepia commissioned this to be made for Aurelius Paon, freedman of Augustus, formerly superintendent of the litter-bearers, and for herself, his most cherished and incomparable wife, who deserved so well, and also for Paulinus, his freedman, who deserved so well of his patron.
EDCS-48300119.summary: This epitaph records a commission for a funerary monument for Aurelius Paon, his wife Aurelia Eupraepia, and his freedman Paulinus, indicating their respective statuses and relationships.

EDCS-48300121.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus et perpetuae securitatem sic Aureliae Tertullae quae vixit nnos XVIIII mensem I dies XVIII Aurelius Tertullus equo publico pater filiae dulcissimae faciendum curavit ea qua sic perlegeris et dices obiter Tertulla dulcis vale\n
EDCS-48300121.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed and for everlasting security. Aurelia Tertulla, who lived 17 years, 1 month, and 18 days. Aurelius Tertullus, of the equestrian order, had this made for his most beloved daughter. As you read this, say, 'Farewell, sweet Tertulla.'
EDCS-48300121.summary: This epitaph honors Aurelia Tertulla, detailing her age at death and is a dedication from her father, Aurelius Tertullus, including a final farewell.

EDCS-48300122.text_edition: \n D(is) M(anibus) Memoriae Aureliae Priscillae quae vixit annos XI menses XI dies XXI Aureli-- Pri \n
EDCS-48300122.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. In memory of Aurelia Priscilla, who lived 11 years, 11 months, and 21 days. Aureli-- Pri [---]
EDCS-48300122.summary: This incomplete epitaph commemorates Aurelia Priscilla, stating her age at death and likely initiated by a paternal family member named Aurelius.

EDCS-48300123.text_edition: \n Aurelius Maximus vae coniugi carissimae et filiis dulcissimis \n
EDCS-48300123.translation: Aurelius Maximus [erected this] for his most cherished wife and his most beloved sons.
EDCS-48300123.summary: This epitaph is a dedication by Aurelius Maximus to his wife and sons, indicating a familial memorial.

EDCS-48300124.text_edition: \n coniugi karissimo et Avidia Frontosa filia heredes faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-48300124.translation: Her husband and Avidia Frontosa, his daughter, the heirs, had this made.
EDCS-48300124.summary: This epitaph records that a husband and his daughter, Avidia Frontosa, as heirs, commissioned the monument.

EDCS-48300126.text_edition: \n XXIII V XXXXIII Claudius tianus custos armorum legionis supra scriptae secundus heres iuxta volu<n=MP>tatem testamenti faciendum curavit \n
EDCS-48300126.translation: Claudius tianus, second custos armorum of the legion mentioned above, had this made in accordance with the terms of the will.
EDCS-48300126.summary: This epitaph records Claudius tianus, a custos armorum of a legion, commissioning a monument as stipulated by a will.

EDCS-48300127.summary: This brief inscription identifies Tiberius Claudius Victor as a custos armorum.

EDCS-48300128.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Claudia Ingenua Ingenuo filio viva sibi\n
EDCS-48300128.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Claudia Ingenua [erected this] for her son Ingenuus while she was alive.
EDCS-48300128.summary: This funerary epitaph records Claudia Ingenua dedicating a monument for her son Ingenuus during her lifetime.

EDCS-48300130.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Dubitatio pia Thyce sic \n
EDCS-48300130.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Pia Thyce, devoted, thus [speaks].
EDCS-48300130.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the Divine Spirits, likely by a woman named Thyce.

EDCS-48300132.text_edition: \n CVS hic situs est Titus Flavius DOG LM testamento fecit Flavius Potens ex protectore defunctus est qui vixit annos L cui collegass sic fecerunt et heredes\n
EDCS-48300132.translation: Titus Flavius Dog, LM, is buried here. Titus Flavius Potens, formerly a bodyguard, made this by his will. He died having lived 50 years. His colleagues and heirs made this.
EDCS-48300132.summary: A tombstone for Titus Flavius Dog, commissioned by Titus Flavius Potens, a former bodyguard, and dedicated by his colleagues and heirs.

EDCS-48300133.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus M A Dis Manibus Flavius Ursus biarchus ex numero equites sic Dalmatas sic degentium Cirpi qui vixit annos LI menses IIII dies XII Aurelia Munnia coniugi dulcissimo et Aurelia Ursina filia faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-48300133.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Flavius Ursus, a biarch from the Dalmatian equestrian order residing in Cirpi, who lived 51 years, 4 months, and 12 days. Aurelia Munnia, his wife, and Aurelia Ursina, his daughter, had this made for their dearest husband and father.
EDCS-48300133.summary: A funerary inscription for Flavius Ursus, a Dalmatian equestrian, who died at 51, commissioned by his wife and daughter.

EDCS-48300134.text_edition: \n Titus Flavius S faciendum curavit opto sit tibi terra levis\n
EDCS-48300134.translation: Titus Flavius S had this made. May the earth lie lightly on you.
EDCS-48300134.summary: A short funerary epitaph commissioned by Titus Flavius S, wishing peace to the deceased.

EDCS-48300137.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Iovine fili quantos mi reliquisti dolores in plenius doleor quotumi in fato periti Aurelia Gemellina filio posui qui bixit sic annos XXV menses X dies XV\n
EDCS-48300137.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Iovine, my son, how much grief you have left me! I grieve all the more because I die before you. Aurelia Gemellina set this up for her son, who lived 25 years, 10 months, and 15 days.
EDCS-48300137.summary: A funerary inscription by Aurelia Gemellina for her son Iovine, who died at 25, expressing her grief.

EDCS-48300138.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Iulio Sabino civi Campano domo Capua quondam militi legionis II adiutricis adiutori officii rationum militum <q=O>ui vixit nnos XXVIII mensem I dies XVIII Aurelia Carianae c<o=I>niugi piissimo opto sit tibi terra levis \n
EDCS-48300138.translation: To the Divine Spirits. For Gaius Julius Sabinus, a Campanian citizen from Capua, formerly a soldier of the II Adjutrix legion, assistant in the office of military accounts. He lived 28 years, 1 month, and 18 days. Aurelia Carian, his most devoted wife, had this made. May the earth lie lightly on you.
EDCS-48300138.summary: A tombstone for Gaius Julius Sabinus, a soldier of the II Adjutrix legion and assistant in military accounts, who died at 28, commissioned by his wife Aurelia Carian.

EDCS-48300139.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caio Iulio Valerio veterano ex immuni legionis II adiutricis vixit nnos XXXX Aelia Ingenua uxor et Iuliae Valeria et Valentina filiae curantibus Iulio Valerio speculatore nepote et Flavio Antiochiano beneficiario consularis genero faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-48300139.translation: To the Divine Spirits. For Gaius Julius Valerius, a veteran and immunis of the II Adjutrix legion. He lived 40 years. Aelia Ingenua, his wife, and his daughters Julia Valeria and Valentina, with the care of his grandson Julius Valerius, a speculator, and his son-in-law Flavius Antiochianus, a beneficiarius consularis, had this made.
EDCS-48300139.summary: A funerary inscription for Gaius Julius Valerius, a veteran of the II Adjutrix legion, commissioned by his wife, daughters, grandson, and son-in-law.

EDCS-48300143.text_edition: \n Memmius nus |(centurio) eiusdem heres et Popilia Primitiva soror faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-48300143.translation: Memmius's [---] (centurion) and his sister Popilia Primitiva had this made.
EDCS-48300143.summary: This inscription records that a centurion named Memmius and his sister Popilia Primitiva commissioned a monument.

EDCS-48300144.text_edition: \n Quqo Resulae filius nnorum LX et Pana Garvonis filia viva marito hic sita est titulum posuit Vibius filius et heres\n
EDCS-48300144.translation: Quqo, son of Resula, aged 60, and Pana, daughter of Garvo, while alive, is buried here. Vibius, her son and heir, placed this inscription.
EDCS-48300144.summary: A funerary inscription for Quqo and Pana, placed by Pana's son and heir, Vibius.

EDCS-48300146.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. In memory of Septimius.
EDCS-48300146.summary: A funerary inscription dedicated to the Divine Spirits in memory of Septimius.

EDCS-48300147.text_edition: \n Titus Tullius Titi libertus Heraclia Abascantus \n
EDCS-48300147.translation: Titus Tullius Heraclia Abascantus, freedman of Titus.
EDCS-48300147.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a freedman named Heraclia Abascantus, dated to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-48300150.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Ulpiotero duplario legionis II adiutricis nnorum L domo Aquinco Marco Ulpio Vibiano avunculo eius Marcus Ulpius Marci filius Vnilis Poet<o=E>vione V coniugi VO ENTI H curav \n
EDCS-48300150.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Marcus Ulpioterus, duplicarius of the II Adjutrix legion, aged 50, from Aquinum, by his uncle Marcus Ulpius Vibianus, and his nephew Marcus Ulpius Vnilis Poetovionus, for his wife VO EN H, who had him cared for.
EDCS-48300150.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Ulpioterus, a soldier of the II Adjutrix legion, set up by his uncle and nephew, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-48300152.text_edition: \n Marcus Valerius Fronto strator consularis legionis II adiutricis Valeria Eutychie liberta pientissimo fecit\n
EDCS-48300152.translation: Valeria Eutychie, freedwoman, lovingly made this for Marcus Valerius Fronto, consular strator of the II Adjutrix legion.
EDCS-48300152.summary: This inscription records a funerary monument dedicated by a freedwoman to her patron, Marcus Valerius Fronto, an official in the II Adjutrix legion, during the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-48300153.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Valerius Luci filius Natalis veteranus ex signifero legionis II adiutricis vixit annos XLVII hic situs est Lucius Valerius Crispinus frater et Publius Decimius Constans veteranus legionis supra scriptae ex tubicine heredes et Iulia quae et Decimia Constantis coniux et Iulia Maxima socrus pientissimo posuerunt libertique manumissi Valeria Prima et Valerius Primitius\n
EDCS-48300153.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Lucius Valerius Natalis, veteran from the staff-sergeants of the II Adjutrix legion, lived 47 years, is buried here. Lucius Valerius Crispinus, his brother, and Publius Decimius Constans, veteran of the aforementioned legion, a trumpeter, his heirs, and Iulia who is also called Decimia, wife of Constans, and Iulia Maxima, his mother-in-law, placed this for the most dutiful. And also his freedmen, Valeria Prima and Valerius Primitius.
EDCS-48300153.summary: This extensive funerary inscription for Lucius Valerius Natalis, a veteran of the II Adjutrix legion, details his age, burial location, and lists the numerous family members and freedmen who erected the monument, likely in the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-48300154.text_edition: \n Valeria Severa Valeri Crispi liberta annorum XXX et Valeria Sibulla Crispi filia annorum V hic sitae sunt heredes monumentum faciendum curaverunt Flavius Ingenuus et Valerius Aquila\n
EDCS-48300154.translation: Valeria Severa, freedwoman of Valerius Crispus, aged 30, and Valeria Sibulla, daughter of Crispus, aged 5, are buried here. The heirs arranged for the monument to be made by Flavius Ingenuus and Valerius Aquila.
EDCS-48300154.summary: This funerary inscription records the burial of a freedwoman, Valeria Severa, and her daughter Valeria Sibulla, with the monument erected by their heirs, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-48300159.text_edition: \n Valentinae Flavius Geminus filius faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-48300159.translation: Flavius Geminus, his son, arranged for this to be made for Valentina.
EDCS-48300159.summary: This is a dedication or funerary inscription for Valentina, commissioned by her son Flavius Geminus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-48300162.text_edition: \n S aris legionis II adiutricis piae fidelis AVN nis faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-48300162.translation: Arranged for the tomb of the II Adjutrix legion, pious and faithful, aged [---] years.
EDCS-48300162.summary: This inscription appears to be part of a funerary monument for a member of the II Adjutrix legion, with the specific name and age largely missing, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-48300164.text_edition: \n D legionis eiusdem nsium nnonia inferiore Septimia Flora infe atque pientissimo faciendum curav---\n
EDCS-48300164.translation: Septimia Flora from Inferior Nennianis of the same legion, and for the most dutiful [---], arranged for this to be made.
EDCS-48300164.summary: This inscription records Septimia Flora from Inferior Nennianis arranging for a funerary monument, likely for a member of the same legion as indicated by 'eiusdem', dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-48300165.text_edition: \n E eius qui vivi sibi sarcofagum faciendum curaverunt et Aureliae Iuliae nepti eorum qui vixit annos VIIII menses VI\n
EDCS-48300165.translation: They who while alive arranged for this sarcophagus to be made for themselves, and for Aurelia Iulia, their granddaughter, who lived 9 years and 6 months.
EDCS-48300165.summary: This inscription indicates a self-funded burial arrangement for a couple, who also provided for a sarcophagus for their granddaughter Aurelia Iulia, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-48300166.text_edition: \n P S IM annorum LX Marcus Ulpius Firmus libertus et heres faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-48300166.translation: Marcus Ulpius Firmus, freedman and heir, aged 60, arranged for this to be made.
EDCS-48300166.summary: This funerary inscription records Marcus Ulpius Firmus, a freedman and heir, who commissioned the monument, likely for himself or another relative, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-48300169.text_edition: \n us eques ex st<i=E>pendiorum XX annorum \n
EDCS-48300169.translation: Lucius, a cavalryman, from his pay of 20 years.
EDCS-48300169.summary: This inscription seems to record the financial savings or achievements of a cavalryman named Lucius, possibly related to his service.

EDCS-48300173.text_edition: \n secundus heres corpus concontubernalis monumentum faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-48300173.translation: Secundus, the heir, took care to have a monument made for his tent-mate.
EDCS-48300173.summary: This is a funerary inscription where the heir, Secundus, commissioned a monument for his deceased tent-mate.

EDCS-48300175.text_edition: \n Aiiuco Veneriae annorum L hic sit-- us Magi filius adfinis a heres \n
EDCS-48300175.translation: To Aiucus, of Venus, 50 years old. Here lies..., son of Magius, relative and heir.
EDCS-48300175.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aiucus, who died at the age of 50, identified by his relative and heir.

EDCS-48800014.summary: This inscription is very brief and appears to be a reference or attribution, possibly related to a work or decision by someone named Maximinus.

EDCS-48800037.translation: To our Lords Diocletian, Maximian, and Constantius, the most noble Caesars.
EDCS-48800037.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the Roman Emperors Diocletian, Maximian, and Constantius, dating to their joint rule.

EDCS-48800135.summary: This is a personal inscription, possibly a graffito, expressing a sentiment about someone named Cupidus.

EDCS-51000058.text_edition: \n Deo Soli Invicto Claudius Neronianus votum solvit libens merito in templo Mucaporis sacerdotis\n
EDCS-51000058.translation: To the Unconquered Sun God, Claudius Neronianus willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow in the temple of Mucapor, the priest.
EDCS-51000058.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Sol Invictus by Claudius Neronianus, who fulfilled a vow in the temple of the priest Mucapor.

EDCS-51400172.translation: By the hand of Adiutor of Masculus.
EDCS-51400172.summary: This inscription likely indicates the maker or scribe of an object or document, identifying Adiutor working for Masculus.

EDCS-51400176.summary: This inscription marks the work of Attus from the TFSE workshop, likely indicating a craftsman or business.

EDCS-51400195.translation: By the hand of Calvinus Marius.

EDCS-51400210.translation: By the hand of Iulianus Surius.
EDCS-51400210.summary: This inscription records work done by Iulianus Surius, likely a craftsman or builder.

EDCS-51400211.summary: This inscription is too fragmentary to determine its specific purpose, but likely names individuals.

EDCS-51400214.translation: Ulpius Ingenuus, living in the Via Britannica.
EDCS-51400214.summary: This inscription likely records the name and residence of Ulpius Ingenuus.

EDCS-51400232.summary: This inscription records that [...]lnerius made something, likely a monument or structure.

EDCS-51400243.translation: Damini factory, skilled in the art of the XV [...]
EDCS-51400243.summary: This inscription refers to the workshop ('fabrica') of Damini, highlighting their skill in a particular craft.

EDCS-51400271.summary: This inscription appears to be a simple marker of ownership or a tally, possibly related to a person named Damini, with a numerical value of XV.

EDCS-51400273.summary: This inscription records a tally or mark of ownership by Coccillus, indicated by the number XII.

EDCS-51400274.summary: This inscription indicates a count or ownership mark by Priscinius, with the value XI.

EDCS-51400279.summary: This inscription states that Marcus created the object and includes the notation X NM, possibly a quantity or date.

EDCS-51400280.summary: This inscription likely represents a count or ownership mark by Montanus, indicated by the number X.

EDCS-51400297.summary: This inscription seems to be a record of ownership or a tally by Seniia, with the marking ANE.

EDCS-51400300.summary: This inscription appears to be a count or ownership mark by Moxi, indicated by the letters AV.

EDCS-51400302.summary: This inscription likely records a tally or ownership mark by Musici, with the notation BHH.

EDCS-51400336.summary: This inscription indicates a count or ownership mark by Gippius, noted by ME.

EDCS-51400341.summary: This is a simple dedication by Carus, likely a tombstone or memorial.
