EDCS-51400434.summary: This inscription records ownership or association, likely of a monument or property, belonging to Maximini Fortis.

EDCS-51400444.summary: This inscription records the name of Petilius Aquincus Secundus, likely a dedication or funerary marker.

EDCS-51400446.text_edition: \n ius Petilius Provincialis feci Petili Petilii\n
EDCS-51400446.translation: Ius Petilius Provincialis made this, [son of] Petilius Petilius.
EDCS-51400446.summary: This inscription records that Ius Petilius Provincialis made this monument, likely a funerary or dedication marker.

EDCS-51400450.summary: This inscription records the names Pulchellus and Hermocrates, likely a funerary or ownership marker.

EDCS-51400459.summary: This inscription records the name Anmamatus, likely a funerary or ownership marker.

EDCS-51400464.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely recording the names Tiberius and Florentinus, possibly related to a funerary monument.

EDCS-51400466.summary: This inscription appears to record ownership or familial relationships, with the names Longus and two possibly abbreviated terms (AIICHMFIL, ALI).

EDCS-51400467.summary: This inscription records that Longus made this monument, likely a dedication or funerary marker.

EDCS-51400468.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription recording a dedication to the god Mars.

EDCS-51400472.summary: This inscription records that Mercator made this monument, likely a dedication or funerary marker.

EDCS-51400474.text_edition: \n Legionis II adiutricis piae fidelis Panphilus sic fecit\n
EDCS-51400474.translation: Panphilus of the Second Adiutrix Legion, pious and faithful, made this.
EDCS-51400474.summary: This inscription records that Panphilus, a member of the Legio II Adiutrix, made this monument.

EDCS-51400475.summary: A brief inscription indicating that Resatus was the maker of the object, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-51400476.summary: A brief inscription indicating that Resatus was the maker of the object, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-51400477.summary: A brief inscription indicating that Resatus was the maker of the object, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-51400479.summary: This inscription likely refers to a workshop named Similis, possibly indicating the origin or manufacturer of an object, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-51400491.summary: A brief inscription indicating that Rauracus was the maker of the object, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-51400494.summary: A brief inscription indicating that Toccinus was the maker of the object, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-51400498.summary: This fragment mentions Avitus and possibly a gentilicium like Casia, but its meaning and context are largely lost, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-51400500.translation: Julianus, Princeps prior of the Cohort.
EDCS-51400500.summary: This inscription records Julianus, who held the rank of Princeps prior in a cohort, likely a military inscription from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-51400501.text_edition: \n |(Centuria) Iuli Apici Claudius Dubitatus cohortis VII hastatus |(contrascripsit\n
EDCS-51400501.translation: Claudius Dubitatus, hastatus of the 7th cohort, wrote this in the century of Iulius Apicius.
EDCS-51400501.summary: This military inscription identifies Claudius Dubitatus as a hastatus in the 7th cohort, with a note about the century of Iulius Apicius, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-51400508.text_edition: \n Legio II adiutrix pia fidelis Priscinus II pro \n
EDCS-51400508.translation: Legio II adiutrix pia fidelis. Priscinus, for [...]
EDCS-51400508.summary: This inscription mentions the loyal and faithful Second Auxiliary Legion (Legio II adiutrix) and the name Priscinus, likely a military record from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-51400512.summary: This inscription appears to be a dedication by Septimius Caenetus to Nestus, likely a deity or a deceased individual.

EDCS-51400513.text_edition: \n Cohors VII Breucorum Anotininiana Smaragdus X\n
EDCS-51400513.translation: Smaragdus, decurion of the VII Breucorum Anotininiana cohort.
EDCS-51400513.summary: This inscription records Smaragdus, a decurion of the VII Breucorum Anotininiana cohort, possibly indicating his rank or a benefaction.

EDCS-51400537.summary: This inscription mentions the I Ulpia Pannoniorum cohort, likely part of a military monument or record.

EDCS-51400609.summary: This inscription seems to be a dedication from Quietus to Amo, possibly a deity or a distinguished person.

EDCS-51400615.text_edition: \n Iulia Nissa et Gaius Mutilius ne possit facere contra Oceanum contra Amoenem sic ne possit Gaius contra Felicionem facere respectes sic lingua ne possit adversus conservos Ammionis lingua ne possit facere Eunici Suri lingua ne possit adversus Oceanunu(m) Asellionis lingua et nomen ne possit atversus sic Ocianum sic facere loqui ne possit Gaius aut Iulia adversus Annia Anniani lingua ne possit num facere et Decibali sic lingua et nomen ne possit adversus Oceanum facere eo modo hoc ego averso graphio scribo sic linguas sic illorum aversas ne possint facere contra hos LCO supra posivi ne Gaius aut Iulia Nissa et Eunicus Surus adversus Oceanum linguas ob AE lingua ECCIGNISN contra Amoenem sic \n
EDCS-51400615.translation: Iulia Nissa and Gaius Mutilius, so that he may not be able to act against Oceanus and against Amoena; thus may Gaius not be able to act against Felicio; respect this; thus may the tongue not be able to act against the fellow slaves of Ammionis; thus may the tongue not be able to act against Eunicus Surus; thus may the tongue and name not be able to act against Oceanus Asellionis; thus may he not be able to act, speak; thus may Gaius or Iulia not be able to act against Annia Anniani; thus may the tongue not be able to do this; and Decibalus, thus may the tongue and name not be able to act against Oceanus in that manner; I write this with the stylus reversed, thus their tongues turned away, so that they may not act against these; I have placed LCO above, so that Gaius or Iulia Nissa and Eunicus Surus may not use their tongues against Oceanus, for the sake of AE and the 'ignis' (fire?), against Amoena; thus.
EDCS-51400615.summary: This is a complex curse inscription, likely a legal or magical document, invoked by Iulia Nissa and Gaius Mutilius against several individuals and possibly supernatural entities, intended to prevent them from speaking or acting against certain people or principles.

EDCS-51400620.summary: This is a simple inscription, likely a name, possibly referring to a freedman or a person depicted.

EDCS-51400622.summary: This inscription records the centuria of Urbanius Philippus, indicating a division within the Roman army or administration.

EDCS-51400650.summary: This inscription identifies Cadgatis as a craftsman, possibly on a tool or a work produced by him.

EDCS-51400651.summary: This inscription states that Caratilius made the object, likely identifying the maker of a tombstone or a piece of art.

EDCS-51400652.summary: This inscription indicates that Gippius made the object himself, likely on a tombstone or a work of art.

EDCS-51400654.summary: This inscription records the name Laxtucis, possibly a dedication or owner's name, with illegible text following.

EDCS-51400655.summary: This inscription records that Lutevos made something, likely a monument or object.

EDCS-51400658.summary: This inscription records the name Materninus, possibly as a dedication or owner's name, with illegible text following.

EDCS-51400664.summary: This inscription records the name Romogillus, possibly a dedication or owner's name, with illegible text following.

EDCS-51400667.summary: This inscription records that Tittius made something, likely a monument or object.

EDCS-51400670.summary: This inscription records the name Attianus, possibly a dedication or owner's name, with illegible text following.

EDCS-51400689.summary: This inscription records that something was made by the hand of Ateclonis, likely indicating the craftsman or artist.

EDCS-51400690.summary: This inscription records that Attillus made something, likely a monument or object.

EDCS-51400691.summary: This inscription records that Celsus made something, with illegible text following.

EDCS-51400696.summary: This inscription records that someone whose name ends in -tius made something, with illegible text at the beginning and end.

EDCS-51400733.summary: This inscription records that Quartinus made something, possibly a grave marker or a building component, and indicates a quantity of 20.

EDCS-51400736.summary: This inscription records that Doccalus made something, possibly a grave marker or a building component, and indicates a quantity of 30.

EDCS-51400738.summary: This very fragmentary inscription likely records the maker's name, Victorinus, possibly related to a construction or dedication.

EDCS-51400740.summary: This fragmentary inscription records that someone with a name ending in -us made something, possibly a building or monument.

EDCS-51400755.summary: This inscription indicates that Aventinus worked on or produced something, with the number 8 possibly denoting quantity or size.

EDCS-51400758.summary: This fragmentary inscription indicates that Priscinius was responsible for the work, likely related to construction or a monument.

EDCS-51400760.summary: This inscription records that Cottalus made something, possibly a building or a monument.

EDCS-51400762.summary: This inscription records that Habilis made something, possibly a building or a monument.

EDCS-51400776.translation: By the hand of Panta. Bato.
EDCS-51400776.summary: This inscription notes the work of Panta and possibly the recipient or owner, Bato.

EDCS-51400781.summary: This inscription identifies Flavius Primanus with the title 'duplarius', likely a military or administrative rank.

EDCS-51400790.summary: A funerary monument or dedication made by Florentinus, likely from the Imperial Roman period.

EDCS-51400796.summary: A fragmentary inscription, possibly recording a personal name, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-51400836.summary: An inscription that likely accompanies a portrait or bust of Iulius Hilarius, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-51400837.translation: Legio II VII [made this]; Marcius Adiutor.
EDCS-51400837.summary: An inscription potentially related to the Second Legion VII, possibly mentioning a soldier named Marcius Adiutor, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-51400840.summary: A dedication or commission by a person named Respectus, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-51400850.translation: From the centuria of Pollus Verus.
EDCS-51400850.summary: An inscription indicating ownership or origin from the administrative or military unit of Pollus Verus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-51500035.text_edition: \n Memoriae Aureliae Severae quae vixit annos XXII menses VI dies XV Marcus Aurelius Asclepiades veteranus et Ulpiae Ursae dulcissimae\n
EDCS-51500035.translation: To the memory of Aurelia Severa, who lived 22 years, 6 months, 15 days; Marcus Aurelius Asclepiades, veteran, and Ulpia Ursa, his dearest.
EDCS-51500035.summary: A funerary inscription honoring Aurelia Severa, set up by her husband Marcus Aurelius Asclepiades and possibly Ulpia Ursa, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-51600805.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Hadriani filius divi Traiani Parthici nepos divi Nervae pronepos Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate VIIII imperator II cons(ul) IIII pater patriae equitibus et peditibus qui militaverunt in alis V et cohortibus XIII quae appellantur I Flavia Augusta Britannica |(milliaria) et praetoria civium Romanorum et I Brittonum civium Romanorum et I Thracum sagittaria veterana et I Augusta Ituraeorum et I Thracum civium Romanorum et I Alpinorum equitata et III Batavorum |(milliaria) vexillatio et III Lusitanorum et II Augusta Thracum et I Montanorum et I Campanorum et sunt in nnia inferiore sub Fuficio Cornuto quinis et vicenis item classicis senis et vicenis pluribusve stipendiis emeritis dimissis honesta missione quorum nomina subscripta sunt civitatem Romanam qui eorum non haberent dedit et conubium cum uxoribus Imperator Caesar Parthici nepos divi Nervae pronepos Hadrianus Antoninus VIIII imperator equitibus et peditibus qui militaverunt in nnonia inferiore XXV item classicis honesta missione quorum nomina uxoribus quas tunc habuissent cum iis quas postea\n
EDCS-51600805.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power for the 9th time, Imperator for the 2nd time, Consul for the 4th time, Father of the Country, to the cavalrymen and foot soldiers who served in the five alae and thirteen cohorts which are called I Flavia Augusta Britannica (milliaria) and Praetoria Civium Romanorum, and I Brittonum Civium Romanorum, and I Thracum Sagittaria Veterana, and I Augusta Ituraeorum, and I Thracum Civium Romanorum, and I Alpinorum equitata, and III Batavorum (milliaria) vexillation, and III Lusitanorum, and II Augusta Thracum, and I Montanorum, and I Campanorum, and are in Pannonia Inferior under Fuficius Cornutus, having completed twenty-five years of service, and the regulars, having completed twenty-six or more years of service, dismissed with an honorable discharge, whose names are listed below, granted Roman citizenship to those among them who did not possess it, and the right of marriage with the wives whom they then had. Emperor Caesar, grandson of Parthicus, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, Hadrianus Antoninus, for the 9th time Imperator, to the cavalrymen and foot soldiers who served in Pannonia Inferior for twenty-five years, and the regulars, with an honorable discharge, whose names [are listed below], and to their wives whom they then had, and with those whom they later [married]...
EDCS-51600805.summary: An extensive imperial rescript issued by Antoninus Pius concerning military service and citizenship for soldiers in Pannonia Inferior, likely from the mid-2nd century CE.

EDCS-55701999.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Aelius Marcianus pro se et suis votum posuit libens merito\n
EDCS-55701999.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Aelius Marcianus placed this vow for himself and his family, willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-55701999.summary: A votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Aelius Marcianus on behalf of himself and his household, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-55702001.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Tiberius Naevius V L I IL votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-55702001.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Tiberius Naevius paid his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-55702001.summary: A votive inscription to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, where Tiberius Naevius fulfills a vow, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-55702002.translation: To Silvanus Domesticus, Flavius Ingenuus [dedicated this].
EDCS-55702002.summary: This is a dedication to the god Silvanus Domesticus by Flavius Ingenuus, likely a votive or funerary inscription from the Roman period.

EDCS-55702003.text_edition: \n pro salute ddominorum nnostrorum Imperatorum Publi Licini Valeriani Pii Felicis Augusti et Publi Licini Egnati Gallieni Pii Felicis Augusti et Corneliae Saloninae Augustae Aurelius Victor et Aurelius \n
EDCS-55702003.translation: For the safety of our lords the Emperors Valerian, Gallienus, and Salonina, Aurelius Victor and Aurelius [dedicated this].
EDCS-55702003.summary: This inscription records a prayer for the safety of Emperors Valerian, Gallienus, and Salonina, dedicated by Aurelius Victor and Aurelius, likely from the mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-55702004.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Culminari et Genio huius loci sacrum Publius Aelius Antoninus cornicularius posuit et dedicavit\n
EDCS-55702004.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Optimus Maximus Culminaris and the genius of this place, Publius Aelius Antoninus, a cornicularius, placed and dedicated [this].
EDCS-55702004.summary: This is a religious dedication to Jupiter Culminaris and the local genius by Publius Aelius Antoninus, a cornicularius, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-55702006.text_edition: \n Publius Aelius Cassius vixit annos LXXX heredes eius posuerunt\n
EDCS-55702006.translation: Publius Aelius Cassius lived 80 years; his heirs placed [this].
EDCS-55702006.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the life and death of Publius Aelius Cassius, erected by his heirs, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-55702007.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publius? Aelius? atus vixit annos XXXV Publii Aelii Scun et Victor fratri bene merenti posuerunt\n
EDCS-55702007.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Publius? Aelius? [Atus?] lived 35 years; Publius Aelius Scun and Victor placed [this] for their well-deserving brother.
EDCS-55702007.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated to the deceased Atus (name uncertain), who lived 35 years, by his brothers Publius Aelius Scun and Victor.

EDCS-55702009.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caesernius Marcellus vixit annos L Caesernius Marcellinus miles legionis II adiutricis vixit annos XXV Caesernia Marcellina vixit annos XXV Domitius Domitianus miles legionis II adiutricis vixit annos XXIII Domitius Severinus vixit annos VIII coniux posuuit ET\n
EDCS-55702009.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Caesernius Marcellus lived 50 years; Caesernius Marcellinus, a soldier of Legio II Adjutrix, lived 25 years; Caesernia Marcellina lived 25 years; Domitius Domitianus, a soldier of Legio II Adjutrix, lived 23 years; Domitius Severinus lived 8 years; his wife placed [this] AND...
EDCS-55702009.summary: This is a complex funerary inscription commemorating multiple individuals, including two soldiers of Legio II Adjutrix, with their ages at death, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-55702010.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Claudio Proculino centurioni legionis ? adiutricis et Llaurinae coniugi quae vixit annis XLV et Secundino filio qui vixit annos XXV Claudia Proculina filia quae vixit annis XV Claudii Ursio et Ursus et Ursinianus ex praecepto patroni posuerunt inpendiis sic libertorum sit tibi terra levis\n
EDCS-55702010.translation: To the spirits of the departed, for Claudius Proculinus, centurion of Legio ? Adjutrix, and his wife Llaurina who lived 45 years, and for their son Secundinus who lived 25 years; Claudia Proculina, his daughter, who lived 15 years; Claudii Ursio, Ursius, and Ursinianus placed [this] by the instruction of the patron, at the expense of the freedmen; may the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-55702010.summary: This funerary inscription honors a centurion, his wife, and children, set up by other family members and freedmen, likely from the Roman Imperial period and mentioning the Legio II Adjutrix.

EDCS-55702013.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Septimius Florentinus Lucio Septimio Floriano patri vivus vivo posuit et Proculeiae Valentinae coniugi eiiusdem et Septimiae Quintae coniugi suae et sibi et P nepot\n
EDCS-55702013.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Lucius Septimius Florentinus placed [this] for his father Lucius Septimius Florianus while alive, for his wife Proculeia Valentina, and for himself and his grandson P[...].
EDCS-55702013.summary: This is a funerary monument initiated by Lucius Septimius Florentinus for his father, wife, himself, and grandson, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-55702014.text_edition: \n ------ nepo]tibus suis io Secundo posuimus et n nostris\n
EDCS-55702014.translation: [...] for our grandsons and [for us] and ours.
EDCS-55702014.summary: This fragment appears to be part of a funerary inscription, mentioning grandsons and the dedication by 'us', likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-55702015.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus s Vitalis vivus fecit sibi coniugi ae nnorum XL E\n
EDCS-55702015.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Vitalis, alive, made [this] for himself and for his wife of 40 years E[...].
EDCS-55702015.summary: This is a funerary inscription where Vitalis created the monument for himself and his wife while alive, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-55702016.summary: This is a short funerary inscription identifying Deupo as a slave, likely from the early Imperial period.

EDCS-55702017.summary: This is a brief inscription recording the name Candidus, identified as a slave, possibly from the Imperial period.

EDCS-56900076.translation: May you prosper and be happy.
EDCS-56900076.summary: This is a general good wish inscribed on a monument, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-59500054.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Publius Spe Sem P \n
EDCS-59500054.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Publius Spe Sem P.
EDCS-59500054.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Publius Spe Sem P., likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-59500055.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo aeterno Caius Equitius Artemidorus Augustalis coloniae Mursensium pro Aelia Brutulla coniuge et Caio Equitio Victore filio votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-59500055.translation: To eternal Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Gaius Equitius Artemidorus Augustalis of the colony of Mursenses fulfilled a vow for his wife Aelia Brutulla and his son Gaius Equitius Victor, willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-59500055.summary: This is a votive inscription by a local official for his family, dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-59500059.text_edition: \n Marcus Aurelius Aelianus hic situs est annorum X Marcus Aurelius Mogetmarus veteranus alae I Thracum et Aurelia Dumnomara filio pientissimo et sibi vivi posuerunt\n
EDCS-59500059.translation: Marcus Aurelius Aelianus, aged ten, is buried here. Marcus Aurelius Mogetmarus, a veteran of the I Ala Thracum, and Aurelia Dumnomara set this up for their most devoted son and for themselves during their lifetime.
EDCS-59500059.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a young boy, Marcus Aurelius Aelianus, erected by his parents, one of whom was a military veteran, likely from the late 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-59500060.text_edition: \n Libero Patri et L<i=L>berae pro salute sua et sua et suorum Aurelius Valens miles legionis II adiutricis <Imperatore Volusian>o II et Maximo consulibus \n
EDCS-59500060.translation: To Liber Pater and Libera, for his own health and that of his family, Aurelius Valens, a soldier of the II Adjutrix Legion, under Emperor Volusianus (cos. II) and Maximus.
EDCS-59500060.summary: This is a votive inscription by a soldier to the gods Liber and Libera for his well-being and that of his family, dated by the consuls to 250 CE.

EDCS-59500064.summary: This is a brief, possibly humorous or topographical, inscription, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-60600080.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription recording the fulfillment of a vow, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-64100110.summary: This inscription likely refers to the virtue of 'Fides' or perhaps a dedication to Emperor Constantine, dating to the 4th century CE.

EDCS-64100111.text_edition: \n Silvano sancto Pantheo Alexander pro se votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-64100111.translation: To the holy Silvanus, Alexander gladly and deservedly paid his vow for himself.
EDCS-64100111.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Silvanus by Alexander, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-66400001.text_edition: \n Deo Invicto Mithrae Augusti nostri dispensator pro se et suis vivis votum posuit libens merito\n
EDCS-66400001.translation: To the Unconquered God Mithras, the procurator of our Emperor erected this vow for himself and his family while alive, gladly and deservedly.
EDCS-66400001.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Mithras by an imperial procurator, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-67300137.text_edition: \n Silvano Domestico sacrum Abascantus Encolpi Augusti dispensatoris vilic(us)\n
EDCS-67300137.translation: Sacred to Silvanus Domesticus; from Abascantus, steward of the Emperor's procurator.
EDCS-67300137.summary: This is a dedication to Silvanus Domesticus by a steward, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-67400417.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tito Claudio Apollinari medico domo ICO\n
EDCS-67400417.translation: To the departed spirits of Titus Claudius Apollinaris, physician, from Ico.
EDCS-67400417.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Titus Claudius Apollinaris, a physician, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-67400418.text_edition: \n Caius Acellius Marronius ? praefectus? cohortis I Cretum IIvir Claudialis ob honorem duas porticus fecit summam honorariam XX\n
EDCS-67400418.translation: Gaius Acellius Marronius, possibly prefect of the I Cretum cohort, as duovir of the Claudian tribe, built two porticoes for the honor of the consulship, at the cost of 20,000 sesterces.
EDCS-67400418.summary: This inscription records the construction of public works by Gaius Acellius Marronius, likely a local magistrate, during the Imperial period.

EDCS-67400423.text_edition: \n cura agente Tiberio Claudio Claudiano legato Auggustorum pro praetore\n
EDCS-67400423.translation: Under the administration of Tiberius Claudius Claudianus, legate of the Augusti pro praetore.
EDCS-67400423.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription recording the administrative actions of a legate of the emperors, Tiberius Claudius Claudianus, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-68000047.text_edition: \n <Dis=ITO> Pater <Ae=H>racura Mer<c=Q>curis C<y=V>lleni ea nomina tibi dicto tradas dir<is=OV> canibus di Manes Tartaris Marcum Marciam Charitonem Secu<n=M>dum <quicum=QVIQVIT>que adversarius sur<r=G>exerit <q=C>ui tibi antepistulam adferet Muta et Tacita <q=C>uomodo Manes muti et taciti su<nt=M> seic <q=C>ui tibi ant<e=C>pistula<m=N> a<d=T>ferent muti et taciti <s=C>int adversarios Bellici a<c=T>cipit<e=I> Tricerberi et ret<i=E>net<e=C> illu ios \n
EDCS-68000047.translation: To Dis Pater, to Mercury, to the Manes, to the underworld, may you deliver the names of Marcus, Marcia, Charito, Secundus, and whomever else an opponent shall raise against you, and whosoever shall bring you this letter: may they be mute and silent, just as the Manes are mute and silent. And whosoever shall bring you this letter, may they be mute and silent. May Bellicus accept and hold back the adversaries, Cerberus, and the three-headed beast.
EDCS-68000047.summary: This is a complex magical text, likely an inscription on a tablet or amulet, intended to bind or silence enemies, dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-68600009.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo dis deabusque Aurelius Dubitatus veteranus legionis II adiutricis votum posuit pro salute sua et suorum VI Calendas sic Maias\n
EDCS-68600009.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, and to the gods and goddesses, Aurelius Dubitatus, a veteran of the II Adiutrix legion, erected this vow for the safety of himself and his family on the sixth day before the Kalends of May.
EDCS-68600009.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter and other deities by a military veteran, Aurelius Dubitatus, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-68600015.text_edition: \n Tito Cominio Titi filio Quirina Secundo in honorem et solacium Titi Comini i patris \n
EDCS-68600015.translation: To Titus Cominius Secundus, son of Titus, of the Quirina tribe, in honor and solace of his father Titus Cominius.
EDCS-68600015.summary: This is a funerary inscription honoring Titus Cominius Secundus and offering solace for his father, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-69200436.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Verecundinio Verino optioni legionis II adiutricis vixit \n
EDCS-69200436.translation: To the departed spirits of Verecundinius Verinus, centurion of the II Adiutrix legion, who lived...
EDCS-69200436.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Verecundinius Verinus, a centurion in the II Adiutrix legion, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-69200437.text_edition: \n Vercombera Vercomberi filia nnorum LXXX hic sita est Cobromara filia matri titulum memoriae posuit\n
EDCS-69200437.translation: Vercombera, daughter of Vercomer, aged 80, lies here. Cobromara, daughter of [Vercombera], placed this tombstone in memory of her mother.
EDCS-69200437.summary: A tombstone recording the death of Vercombera and the placement of the monument by her daughter Cobromara, likely from the Roman period in Britain.

EDCS-70100012.text_edition: \n Deo Soli Invicto Mitrhae sic Caius Iulius Italicus decurio coloniae Sirmiorum templum vetustate conlabentem restituit\n
EDCS-70100012.translation: To the Invincible Sun God Mithras, Caius Julius Italicus, a decurion of the colony of Sirmium, restored this temple, which had collapsed due to age.
EDCS-70100012.summary: An inscription from Sirmium recording the restoration of a temple dedicated to Sol Invictus Mithras by a local decurion, Caius Julius Italicus.

EDCS-70100013.text_edition: \n Deo Soli Invicto Mitrhae sic Caius Iulius Italicus decurio coloniae Sirmensium templum vetustate conlabenta sic restituit a solo\n
EDCS-70100013.translation: To the Invincible Sun God Mithras, Caius Julius Italicus, a decurion of the colony of Sirmium, restored this temple, which had collapsed due to age, from the very foundations.
EDCS-70100013.summary: An inscription from Sirmium recording the restoration of a temple dedicated to Sol Invictus Mithras by a local decurion, Caius Julius Italicus, emphasizing a complete reconstruction.

EDCS-71100090.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Valerius Primani(?) or Disanis(?), veteran exarch from the number of Syrian archer cavalry, lived 79 years, and his son Valerius Bar[---], exarch from the number of Syrian archer cavalry, lived 30 years. [---]na to her dearest husband Disani and her son, [she] herself, while alive, placed [this monument].
EDCS-71100090.summary: A funerary inscription honoring Valerius Primani (or Disanis), a veteran exarch of Syrian archer cavalry, his son, and his wife Disani, with a surviving portion indicating the wife placed the monument.

EDCS-71300225.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Marcus Aurelius Martial, guardian of the walls, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-71300225.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Marcus Aurelius Martial, identified as a guardian of the walls, likely from a military context.

EDCS-71300226.summary: A fragment of a funerary inscription recording the name Septimius and his lifespan.

EDCS-72200005.translation: ------ LIOLMIO, deposited in [---], to her husband.
EDCS-72200005.summary: A fragmentary inscription that mentions a person named LIOLMIO and refers to a husband.

EDCS-72200008.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Lusius votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-72200008.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Lusius fulfilled his vow gladly and deservedly.
EDCS-72200008.summary: A votive inscription to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Lusius.

EDCS-72200009.text_edition: \n -us obitis et Gresae Dasmeni vivae patronis bene merentibus Masti liberta posuit\n
EDCS-72200009.translation: --- having died, and to Gresae Dasmeni, while alive, Masti, his freedwoman, placed [this monument] for his well-deserving patrons.
EDCS-72200009.summary: A funerary inscription where a freedwoman named Masti dedicates a monument to her patrons, including Gresae Dasmeni.

EDCS-72200012.text_edition: \n ------ A]ureliae Ursae Oqu-- vixit cum Ssus \n
EDCS-72200012.translation: ------ to Aurelia Ursa, Oqu--, lived with Ssus.
EDCS-72200012.summary: A fragmentary funerary inscription mentioning Aurelia Ursa and another individual named Ssus.

EDCS-72200013.text_edition: \n li qui vixit annos et Aurelia Rustica quae vixit nnos Aureli-- Mu et Aureli-- \n
EDCS-72200013.translation: Here lies [name] who lived X years and Aurelia Rustica who lived X years, Aurelius Mu and Aurelius...
EDCS-72200013.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription likely recording the ages of deceased family members.

EDCS-72200014.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Titus Flavius Potiumenus votum solvit\n
EDCS-72200014.translation: Titus Flavius Potiumenus fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Best and Greatest.
EDCS-72200014.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Titus Flavius Potiumenus.

EDCS-72200015.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo sacrum Aurelius Licinius signifer\n
EDCS-72200015.translation: Sacred to Jupiter Best and Greatest, from Aurelius Licinius, standard-bearer.
EDCS-72200015.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Aurelius Licinius, a military standard-bearer.

EDCS-72200102.summary: This is a dedication to the god Silvanus Viator, likely by a traveler or messenger.

EDCS-72200107.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Caio Aurelio Valerio Diocletiano Pio Felici\n
EDCS-72200107.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Gaius Aurelius Valerius Diocletian, Pious, Fortunate.
EDCS-72200107.summary: This is a dedication honoring Emperor Diocletian, likely part of a larger inscription or monument.

EDCS-72200109.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Felici qui vixit annis mensibus diebus XV et ano qui vixit annis diebus XXIII MAS AVG \n
EDCS-72200109.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead, for Felicis who lived XV months and X days and [name] who lived XXIII days...
EDCS-72200109.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the short lifespans of two individuals dedicated to the Dis Manibus.

EDCS-72200110.text_edition: \n posuit iosa coniux optimis suis posuit\n
EDCS-72200110.translation: Iosa, his wife, set up for her excellent husband.
EDCS-72200110.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording a wife's dedication to her deceased husband.

EDCS-72200112.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Antonino Pio Felici Augusto divi Antonini Marci Pii filio divi Severi nepoti pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate patri patriae consuli proconsuli curante Pontio Pontiano consulibus legato eius pro praetore ab Aquinco milia passuum XLVIII\n
EDCS-72200112.translation: For the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, son of the deified Antoninus Pius, grandson of the deified Severus, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power, Father of the Fatherland, Consul, Proconsul, under the supervision of Pontius Pontianus, Consuls, his legate pro praetore, 48 Roman miles from Aquincum.
EDCS-72200112.summary: This is a milestone inscription dating to the consulship of Pontius Pontianus, marking a distance from Aquincum and honoring Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus (Caracalla).

EDCS-72200113.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Caius Iulius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus pontifex maximus tribunicia potestate II imperator IIII consul pater patriae proconsul et Imperator Caius Iulius Verus Maximus cons(ul) I SSMTE\n
EDCS-72200113.translation: The Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power for the second time, Imperator four times, Consul, Father of the Fatherland, Proconsul, and the Emperor Gaius Julius Verus [Maximus], Consul for the first time...
EDCS-72200113.summary: This is a titulature inscription for Emperors Maximinus Thrax and his son Maximinus Junior, likely from a public monument.

EDCS-72200114.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Antonio Gordiano Pio Felici Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate consuli patri patriae proconsuli ab Aquinco milia passuum XLVIII\n
EDCS-72200114.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Antoninus Gordianus Pius Felix Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power, Consul, Father of the Fatherland, Proconsul, 48 Roman miles from Aquincum.
EDCS-72200114.summary: This is a milestone inscription dating to the reign of Emperor Gordian III, marking a distance from Aquincum.

EDCS-72200116.text_edition: \n restituerunt cura agente legato Augusti pro praetore ab Aquinco milia passuum XLVII\n
EDCS-72200116.translation: They restored it, with the governor, an imperial legate, overseeing the work, 47 miles from Aquincum.
EDCS-72200116.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a road, commissioned by an imperial legate, approximately 47 miles from Aquincum, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-72200118.text_edition: \n ------ Marco] Iulio Philippo nobilissimo Caesari principi iuventutis et Mar[ciae ------\n
EDCS-72200118.translation: To Marcus Julius Philippus, most noble Caesar, prince of the youth, and Marcia.
EDCS-72200118.summary: This is a dedication to the imperial prince Marcus Julius Philippus and Marcia, probably on a public monument in the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-72200195.translation: To Gaius Valerius Fronto, veteran of the II Adjutrix Legion, decurion of the colony of Aquincum.
EDCS-72200195.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Gaius Valerius Fronto, a veteran, decurion, and citizen of Aquincum, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-72200197.translation: Severina, who lived 52 years. Flavius Mas[...], ordinary centurion of the II Adjutrix Legion, erected this monument for his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-72200197.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Severina, erected by her husband, a centurion in the II Adjutrix Legion, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-72300002.text_edition: \n Tiberius Claudius Magiatus eques cohortis I Alpinorum \n
EDCS-72300002.translation: Tiberius Claudius Magiatus, an equestrian of the I Alpine Cohort.
EDCS-72300002.summary: This inscription likely records the name and affiliation of Tiberius Claudius Magiatus, an equestrian serving in the I Alpine Cohort, possibly on a military monument.

EDCS-73000029.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Publius Aelius(?).
EDCS-73000029.summary: This is a fragment of a funerary dedication to the gods of the underworld (Dis Manibus) for Publius Aelius(?), dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-73400468.translation: Here rest two innocent ones: Petrus, who lived one year, one month, and 8 days; and Victorinianus, who lived 4 years, 7 months, and 15 days.
EDCS-73400468.summary: This epitaph records the deaths of two young children, Petrus and Victorinianus, noting their ages at death, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-73400469.inscription_type: inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-73400469.translation: To my dearest children. Surus, son of Salama, erected this monument for his daughter Ticia, who lived 10 months and 23 days, and her brother Ma[...], who lived 7 months and 8 days; they rest here in peace.
EDCS-73400469.summary: This funerary inscription records the premature deaths of two young siblings, Ticia and her brother, and was erected by their father Surus, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-75600249.translation: Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Pius, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan the Parthian, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, Pontifex Maximus, holder of tribunician power.
EDCS-75600249.summary: This is a formal titulature of Emperor Antoninus Pius, listing his divine ancestry and imperial powers, likely from an official monument during his reign (138-161 AD).

EDCS-78300212.translation: To Silvanus Augustus, sacred. Gaius Avius Epicurus fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-78300212.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Gaius Avius Epicurus to the god Silvanus Augustus, likely from a rural or provincial sanctuary.

EDCS-78300213.translation: To Silvanus the Great Augustus. Tiberius Claudius [dedicated this].
EDCS-78300213.summary: A fragmentary inscription likely dedicated to the god Silvanus and an emperor, possibly erected by Tiberius Claudius, with an uncertain date.

EDCS-78300214.translation: Sacred to Silvanus the Forest-Dweller. Agathopus [dedicated this].
EDCS-78300214.summary: A short inscription dedicated to Silvanus, possibly by a freedman named Agathopus, with an uncertain date.

EDCS-78300215.summary: A very brief dedication to the household god Silvanus Domesticus, with an uncertain date.

EDCS-78300216.translation: [He lived for ...] years. Marcus [ ... ] from the household of [...] dedicated this monument, along with [...]
EDCS-78300216.summary: A heavily damaged funerary inscription that records the name of the deceased (Marcus), their lifespan, and the person who set up the monument, with an uncertain date.

EDCS-79600011.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Adiutor and Dulli-bolus set up this altar in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-79600011.summary: An altar dedicated to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Adiutor and Dulli-bolus in fulfillment of a vow, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-79600012.translation: To Mercury. Aulus Julius Exomnius willingly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-79600012.summary: A dedication to the god Mercury by Aulus Julius Exomnius, who fulfilled his vow, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-79600038.translation: Aurelius [...] arranged for this to be made in memory of his wife Aelia Perpetua.
EDCS-79600038.summary: A memorial monument arranged for by Aurelius for his wife Aelia Perpetua, with an uncertain date.

EDCS-79600104.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. Iulius Aprilis (?), a retired soldier of the II Adjutrix Legion, willingly and deservedly paid his vow for himself and all his family for the safety of our Emperors.
EDCS-79600104.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Iulius Aprilis, a retired soldier of the II Adjutrix Legion, for the safety of the emperors, likely dating to the 3rd century AD.

EDCS-79600252.translation: Basuca, daughter of Clavonis, aged 90 years, and Vallauna, daughter of Ingenuus, aged 20 years, lie here. Ingeus set up this memorial stone.
EDCS-79600252.summary: A funerary inscription commemorating two women, Basuca and Vallauna, with their ages and parentage, set up by Ingeus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-81300031.translation: Silvanus, centurion of the II Adiutrix Pia Fidelis Legion, willingly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-81300031.summary: A dedication to Silvanus by a centurion of the II Adiutrix Legion, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-81300032.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, to Juno Regina, and to Neptune Silvanus, Marcus V...
EDCS-81300032.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter, Juno, and Neptune by Marcus V, likely from the imperial period.

EDCS-03000768.translation: In this place lies Ursicinus the exorcist; he made this commemoration for Laurentia, a chaste consecrated woman.
EDCS-03000768.summary: This is a tomb inscription from an unknown location, recording the burial of Laurentia and the commemoration made by Ursicinus, identified as an exorcist.

EDCS-08901101.translation: To Pan and Silvanus, the August gods.
EDCS-08901101.summary: This is a short dedication to the gods Pan and Silvanus, likely from an altar or small shrine.

EDCS-09300479.translation: Exempt in account of the infirmary of the Second Legion Adiutrix.
EDCS-09300479.summary: This inscription likely relates to financial or administrative matters concerning the military hospital of the Legio II Adiutrix.

EDCS-09800277.translation: May you live, Innocent, with your family in God.
EDCS-09800277.summary: This is a Christian blessing or epitaph, wishing well-being in God to someone named Innocent and their household.

EDCS-10000353.translation: Here are placed Venatorinus and Martoria, the innocent ones.
EDCS-10000353.summary: This is a tomb inscription, stating the burial place of two individuals named Venatorinus and Martoria.

EDCS-10000357.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter by Marcus Aurelius Eutychianus, likely from an altar or votive offering.

EDCS-10000358.translation: Marcus Avitus Asclepiades made this for Jupiter.
EDCS-10000358.summary: This is a votive dedication to Jupiter, made by Marcus Avitus Asclepiades, probably from an altar or statue base.

EDCS-10102035.translation: He nourished himself from the top... not... item from... Ursicinus... years
EDCS-10102035.summary: This fragmented inscription seems to be a personal note or record, possibly mentioning someone named Ursicinus and a duration of time, with unclear context.

EDCS-13900027.translation: Frigeridus, a man of highest rank, duke, by the provision of Valentinus.
EDCS-13900027.summary: This inscription records Frigeridus, a high-ranking military official (dux), likely dedicated or provided for by Valentinus.

EDCS-16700020.translation: To recall the right to justice and to protect the equitable is the law given by Dalmatio, which clear faith holds, written twice six times, and the entire volume of the praetor, composed by the learned and by holy princes. The same interpreter of laws and minister of laws, as wisely he knows, so well he carries out. For many merits, Valerius, most just governor, for many merits this image is set up for you, showing the public vows from afar, we send them into the bosom of the fatherland, hence we venerate the highest honors of the prefecture. Rejoicing, we accompany you with this omen. Whoever wishes to know by whose love you are celebrated, let him take this indication from the inscription: Dalmatio, governor, set this up for his grateful client, the third province of Lugudunensis, for his patron.
EDCS-16700020.summary: This lengthy inscription, likely from the late Roman period, is a dedication from Dalmatio, governor of the third province of Lugudunensis, to his patron, referred to as Valerius, praising his justice and administrative virtues.

EDCS-18300331.summary: This is a military inscription identifying a specific cohort, the Third Cohort of Batavians, likely part of a larger unit or monument, possibly dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-20900019.translation: To Iovius and Ianuarius, their living sons; from the will of Vens and Vicius, their patrons, they arranged for this to be made.
EDCS-20900019.summary: This funerary inscription records the commissioning of a monument by sons for their parents, who were patrons, and specifies it was done according to a will, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-24500526.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. I, Gaius Valerius Papirianus, tribune of the Second Legion Adiutrix, born in the city of Palaestina, military service brought me here, hoping so long that I, a tribune, would be released guiltless. See me, my two dear pledges. Wife, take them and live well, traveler. I, Gaius Valerius Turbo, and Lucius, his son, Roman knights. My homeland is the plain of Syria. I lie in Paeonia, an empty vessel for men, having been the leader of a legion whose name is Papirianus. His sons, knights, for a good father. (Greek section: My homeland is the plain of Syria. I lie in Paeonia, an empty vessel for men, having been the leader of a legion whose name is Papirianus. His sons, knights, for a good father.)
EDCS-24500526.summary: This is a complex funerary epitaph for Gaius Valerius Papirianus, a Roman tribune of the Second Legion Adiutrix, with Greek verses by his sons, highlighting his Syrian origins and military career, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-26600590.translation: I am he, once well known on the shores of Pannonia, the first among a thousand men, strong, who was able to swim across the vast waters of the Danube under Hadrian, with all my arms, and having shot an arrow which, while it hangs in the air, I struck and broke with another arrow as it returned. Whom neither Roman nor barbarian could ever conquer, not with a javelin, not with a bow, not even the Parthian. Here I lie, here with this memorial stone I have consecrated my deeds, that someone after me might follow my example. I am the first who achieved such things.
EDCS-26600590.summary: This is a self-aggrandizing funerary inscription from Pannonia, detailing the exceptional athletic and martial feats of the deceased, who claims to be the first to achieve them, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-27300185.summary: This is a very brief inscription, possibly a dedication or a record of testimony, naming Aurelius Speratus as a witness, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-27700306.translation: From [for the sake of] security, Flavia Calvena, born in Ulmus, lived 22 years, lingered 27 days. Aurelius Propincus, soldier of the Second Legion Adiutrix Pia Fidelis, had this made for his most beloved wife Flavia Cara, and for his most sweet daughter. Her mother also, out of piety for her most sweet daughter, arranged for this to be made. (Greek section: Light, Life.)
EDCS-27700306.summary: This funerary inscription records the ages of Flavia Calvena and her daughter Flavia Cara, dedicated by the soldier Aurelius Propincus and the mother, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-28500855.translation: Legio XIII Gemina Antoniniana, Decurion Fe-
EDCS-28500855.summary: This is a fragment of a military inscription identifying a decurion of the Legio XIII Gemina Antoniniana, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-28500908.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription recording the maker of an object or monument, possibly named Crummus, with a partial name following, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-28600082.translation: Aelius Vales and Pontius Sabinus, tribunes, [dedicated this] for the parade grounds.
EDCS-28600082.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by two tribunes, Aelius Vales and Pontius Sabinus, for the training grounds (campestres), likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-28600084.translation: To the goddess Peace, by Avitus.
EDCS-28600084.summary: A dedication to the goddess Peace, likely by an individual named Avitus.

EDCS-28600086.translation: For the safety of Augustus, by the villagers of Basoreta, by decree of the decurions, to Priscus, father of the city.
EDCS-28600086.summary: A decree by the local council of Basoreta for the safety of the emperor, likely honoring Priscus.

EDCS-28600088.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, son of the divine Antoninus Magnus Pius, grandson of the divine Severus, Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the second time, consul, father of the country, proconsul, the I Dquingenarian Antonine Cohort (was responsible), with P. DO. TA. IOI in charge.
EDCS-28600088.summary: A lengthy titulature inscription for Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus (likely Commodus), dedicated by the I Dquingenarian Antonine Cohort.

EDCS-28600094.summary: A fragmentary inscription mentioning a Roman knight.

EDCS-28600096.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. For Septimia Iudea, who lived 18 years. By Actia Sabinilla, her mother.
EDCS-28600096.summary: A tombstone inscription for Septimia Iudea, set up by her mother Actia Sabinilla.

EDCS-28600241.translation: Gnaeus Flavius Aelianus, prefect of the Praetorium.
EDCS-28600241.summary: An inscription identifying Gnaeus Flavius Aelianus as the prefect of the Praetorian Guard.

EDCS-28600247.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Pilargus, soldier of the II century.
EDCS-28600247.summary: A tombstone inscription for Pilargus, a soldier serving in the II century.

EDCS-28701113.summary: This names the First Cohort of Pannonians, Ulpia, likely a military unit designation on equipment or a monument.

EDCS-28701131.translation: Frigeridus, a most accomplished man, governor, with the equipment of Valentinus.
EDCS-28701131.summary: This records Frigeridus, a high-ranking official (dux), perhaps indicating his role in a military supply context or a building project associated with Valentinus.

EDCS-28701150.translation: Terentius, a most accomplished man, governor.

EDCS-28701194.summary: This inscription records the name and office of Flavius Macianus, who served as a tribune.

EDCS-28701211.translation: For 346 miles from Atrante to the river Sava, he made paved roads and repaired bridges, having restored the republic, having gained the victory, the 31st tribunician power, the 30th acclamation as emperor, the 7th consulship, father of the fatherland, proconsul, Emperor Caesar Flavius Julius Constantius Pius Felix Augustus, greatest victor, eternal triumphant one, son of the deified Constantine, best and greatest, grandson of the deified Maximian and Constantius, great-grandson of the deified Claudius, chief priest, Germanicus, greatest Alamannicus, greatest Germanicus, greatest GothTicus, greatest Adiabenicus.
EDCS-28701211.summary: This extensive inscription from the reign of Constantius II (mid-4th century AD) records his achievements in infrastructure, military victories, and imperial titles, documenting his vast construction and repair projects across Illyricum.

EDCS-28701231.summary: This inscription refers to the Third Cohort of Batavians, likely indicating their presence or a dedication made by them.

EDCS-28701232.summary: This inscription refers to the Third Cohort of Batavians, likely indicating their presence or a dedication made by them.

EDCS-28701240.summary: This inscription records Frigeridus, a high-ranking official (vir perfectissimus dux), associated with the equipment for Valentinus, likely from the late Roman period.

EDCS-29300012.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Luci Savarensis Ioni.

EDCS-29300093.translation: I, Aurelia Aminia, have set up this monument for my husband, Flavius Sanctus, formerly one of the Jovian protectors, who deserved well, who lived more or less 50 years and died in the city of Aquileia. I have set up this monument at the place of Saint Synerotus the martyr and his infant daughter named Ursicina, who lived for 3 years.
EDCS-29300093.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Aurelia Aminia for her husband Flavius Sanctus and their infant daughter Ursicina, near the shrine of Saint Synerotus, likely dating to the 4th century CE.

EDCS-29300094.translation: I, Artemidora, made this memorial for myself while alive, near my lord Synerotes, on the right, between Fortunata and Desiderium.
EDCS-29300094.summary: This is a private funerary inscription set up by Artemidora for herself.

EDCS-29400004.translation: Marcus Aurelius Apollinaris, decurion of the municipality of Murselenses, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Jupiter Best and Greatest of Doliche and the god Paternus of Comagena.
EDCS-29400004.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated by Marcus Aurelius Apollinaris to Jupiter Dolichenus and Paternus of Comagena.

EDCS-29601452.translation: Adonarl Valeria dedicated this sacred offering to Augustus Silvanus.
EDCS-29601452.summary: This is a dedication to Silvanus, likely by a woman named Valeria.

EDCS-29601454.translation: To the Spirits of the departed, Sextus...

EDCS-31000012.translation: From the strength of Claudius, the overseer.
EDCS-31000012.summary: This inscription names Claudius, an overseer, possibly indicating ownership or a dedication.

EDCS-31001222.summary: This inscription simply names Gaius Pellicius Zmaragdus, likely a person's funerary or ownership marker.

EDCS-31001227.summary: This inscription likely indicates ownership or is a funerary marker for Aurelius Menander.

EDCS-31001262.summary: This inscription identifies the workshop belonging to Bassio.

EDCS-31001268.summary: This inscription records that Primitivus was the maker of the object or monument.

EDCS-31001272.summary: This inscription records that Victorinus was the maker of the object or monument.

EDCS-31001283.summary: This inscription identifies the workshop belonging to Caturus.

EDCS-31001289.summary: This inscription records that Dubitatus was the maker of the object or monument.

EDCS-31001291.summary: This inscription records that Marcus Flavius Vitalis was the maker of the object or monument.

EDCS-31001309.summary: This is a simple owner's mark, likely from a workshop or a product, possibly dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31001311.summary: This is a maker's mark or an inscription indicating the craftsman, Saturninus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31001314.summary: This is a maker's mark or an inscription indicating the craftsman, M. Sentius, possibly dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31001330.translation: Publius, son of Gnaeus, made it for Emertis.
EDCS-31001330.summary: This inscription records a dedication or a commission made by Publius for Emertis, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31001332.summary: This inscription likely refers to an object or property belonging to Lucius Cornelius Sabinus, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31001334.summary: This inscription likely refers to an object or property belonging to Quintus Murcius, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31001512.summary: This inscription records a dedication or a commission made by Comitialis for Ioventus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31001518.summary: This is a maker's mark or an inscription indicating the craftsman, Venicarus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-31700419.summary: This inscription identifies the Fourth Flavian Legion, likely marking a military structure or equipment, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32300501.translation: Under the supervision of Tiberius Claudius Claudianus, legate of the Augusti for the province, two miles from Aquincum.
EDCS-32300501.summary: This inscription records a milestone erected under the authority of the governor Tiberius Claudius Claudianus, indicating a distance of two miles from Aquincum, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32300529.translation: The association of veterans erected this for Gaius Marcius Collina Polion, a veteran of the Second Adiutrix Legion, aged 60.
EDCS-32300529.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by a veterans' association for one of its deceased members, Gaius Marcius Collina Polion, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-32300530.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Publius Nonius Firmus made this for his most devoted mother, Pomponia, daughter of Sextus.
EDCS-32300530.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a son for his deceased mother, Pomponia, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-32300576.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Gaius Cominius Cominianus, a cavalryman of the ala Brittonum, who served for 4 years, lived 20 years, lies here. Cominius Celer had this made for his brother.
EDCS-32300576.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gaius Cominius Cominianus, a cavalryman of the ala Brittonum, erected by his brother, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-32300591.summary: This is a simple funerary epitaph for a woman named Asanutis, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-32500261.translation: For the Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, pontifex maximus, greatest Parthian, with tribunician power, consul, father of the fatherland, proconsul, and for Marcus Julius Philippus, the most noble Caesar, and for Marcia Otacilia Severa, most holy wife of our Augustus, mother of the camp and armies, the Second Adiutrix Pia Fidelis Philippiana Legion, under the command of A[---] V[---] pr[---].
EDCS-32500261.summary: This is a dedication by the Second Adiutrix Pia Fidelis Philippiana Legion, honoring Emperor Philip the Arab, his son, and his wife, likely from the mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-32701053.translation: For Gaius Nonius Marcellus, quaestor designate, son of Caius Nonius Marcus, decurion, aged 20, and for Gaius Nonius Priscianus, quaestor designate, nephew, aged 9. Caius Nonius Marcus, decurion of the colony of Bassianensium, and Ulpia Valentina, his mother, erected this monument while alive.
EDCS-32701053.summary: This is a funerary monument erected by parents for their two sons, both quaestor designates, likely from the late 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-39500056.translation: Tharsus, son of Naum, no mortal is immortal. Thirty years.
EDCS-39500056.summary: This is a brief funerary epitaph recording the name, parentage, and age of the deceased, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-39500070.summary: This is a maker's signature, likely for a statue or a sarcophagus, by Rufus of Tarsus.

EDCS-39500077.translation: Pistonas from Kaprobylon, while traveling abroad, died here. Krispos made [this].
EDCS-39500077.summary: This epitaph records the death of Pistonas, a traveler from Kaprobylon, with an attribution to Krispos.

EDCS-39500083.translation: Lucius Valerius Onesimus dedicated this as a thank-offering to the Saviour Gods, Asclepius and Hygieia.
EDCS-39500083.summary: This is a votive inscription by Lucius Valerius Onesimus thanking the gods Asclepius and Hygieia.

EDCS-39500089.summary: This is a simple owner's mark or dedication, likely from Athens.

EDCS-39500090.summary: This inscription is a short memorial, possibly for a person named Diza.

EDCS-39500092.translation: Grave marker of Herakleia, without children. Kallipone, a light, completed [this] with [her] beloved in nine years.
EDCS-39500092.summary: This epitaph marks the grave of Herakleia, who died childless, and was set up by Kallipone.

EDCS-39500100.translation: Christ, Lord, help the city and protect Abarys and guard Romanian and the one who wrote, Amen.
EDCS-39500100.summary: This is a Christian prayer for protection of the city, Abarys, Romanian, and the scribe.

EDCS-42900265.summary: This is a dedication to the god Mithras.

EDCS-43000431.summary: This is a fragment of a dedication to a deity, possibly initiated by Titus C.

EDCS-46700081.summary: This is likely an owner's mark or a simple inscription identifying Gnaeus Pompeius.

EDCS-48300078.translation: To the memory of Aurelia Severa, who lived 22 years, 6 months, 15 days. Marcus Aurelius Asclepiades, veteran, and Ulpia Ursa [dedicated this] to their most sweet daughter.
EDCS-48300078.summary: This is a funerary monument dedicated by Marcus Aurelius Asclepiades and Ulpia Ursa to their daughter Aurelia Severa, detailing her lifespan.

EDCS-48300087.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Quintus Fidius Aper, son of Quintus, a veteran of the Legio II Adiutrix, and Marcus Fidius Kasper, son of Quintus, a speculator of the Legio Adiutrix, and Publius Fidius Quirinus, son of Marcus, a speculator of the Legio I, made this tomb by common consent. In front 10 feet, behind 13 feet.
EDCS-48300087.summary: This tomb inscription records its construction by three veterans and speculators of Roman legions, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-48300088.translation: Here lies Tullia, daughter of Cicero.
EDCS-48300088.summary: This is a simple tomb inscription identifying the deceased as Tullia, daughter of Cicero, likely from the late Roman Republic or early Imperial period.

EDCS-48300089.summary: This inscription lists several names, likely funerary or commemorative, possibly of soldiers or individuals of note, without further context.

EDCS-48300090.translation: Hadrianus, consul. To the spirits of the underworld.
EDCS-48300090.summary: This inscription likely indicates a dedication made by someone named Hadrianus, who held the office of consul, probably during the reign of Emperor Hadrian.

EDCS-48300091.translation: Dorus, guardian of this river Laro.
EDCS-48300091.summary: This inscription records Dorus, identified as the guardian of the river Laro, possibly a local official or priest.

EDCS-48500229.summary: This is a very brief inscription, likely a price or inventory notation for 'excellent sweet black olives', possibly from a market or shop.

EDCS-48500232.summary: This is a short dedication or inscription found on an object, wishing happiness to the user, and mentioning the name Exsuperius.

EDCS-48500236.summary: This inscription appears to be a name, possibly identifying a person or a property owner, with the cognomen 'Bar'.

EDCS-48500245.summary: This inscription records Vencarus, son of Silvinianus, as the maker of the object or monument on which it is inscribed.

EDCS-48500247.summary: This inscription indicates that Marinus created the object or monument as a dedication or gift for Flavius.

EDCS-48500251.translation: Catullus made this. I moved on the tenth day before the Kalends of October.
EDCS-48500251.summary: This inscription records an individual named Catullus who made something and notes a date of relocation.

EDCS-48500268.summary: This is a simple dedication by an individual named Carugenus.

EDCS-48500318.summary: This is a simple dedication by an individual named Comitialis.

EDCS-48800015.translation: Made by the hand of Axami, Buccio.
EDCS-48800015.summary: This inscription attributes the making of something to Axami, with Buccio also mentioned, possibly as a patron or collaborator.

EDCS-48800040.summary: This inscription identifies Ario as a retired soldier ('emeritus').

EDCS-48800047.translation: The workshop of Ar. Maxentius Ar.
EDCS-48800047.summary: This inscription identifies a workshop belonging to someone named Ar. Maxentius.

EDCS-48800062.summary: This inscription appears to be a dedication from Materninus to Aurelia Respecta.

EDCS-48800065.summary: This inscription is likely ownership or dedication by Aurelius Posinnianus.

EDCS-48800067.summary: This inscription identifies Titus Sergius Primus, who held the military rank of sesquiplicarius.

EDCS-48800078.summary: This inscription contains a partially legible text, possibly related to funerary or commemorative purposes, with the name 'aterus' at the end.

EDCS-48800121.summary: This is a dedication by Petilius Quinciles, likely for a monument or object he constructed.

EDCS-48800134.summary: A short inscription wishing Secundine a long life, possibly on a tomb or as a general blessing.

EDCS-49000060.translation: Marinus Junius Volcamicus, soldier of the I Adiutrix legion, a beneficiary of the provincial governor's office, who lived 51 years, made this for Priscus.
EDCS-49000060.summary: This inscription commemorates Priscus, a soldier and official, and records his service and age, likely on a tombstone.

EDCS-51400321.summary: A simple dedication indicating Sacirus as the maker of an object, possibly with an age or measurement indicated by 'LV'.

EDCS-51400360.summary: A brief inscription stating Victorinus as the maker of an object.

EDCS-51400410.translation: To Concordia, may the emperors be fortunate!
EDCS-51400410.summary: This is a dedication to the goddess Concordia, invoking good fortune for the emperors, possibly from a public or military context.

EDCS-51400612.summary: A hopeful inscription for Exsuperius, possibly on a gift or a personal item.

EDCS-51400630.translation: Lucius marked this with 2117 of something, possibly units or a date, at Karnuntum.
EDCS-51400630.summary: This inscription from Karnuntum seems to record a measurement or quantity marked by Lucius, with some numerical and possibly date-related information.

EDCS-51400661.summary: A simple inscription indicating Melutta as the maker, possibly with a quantity or age denoted by 'X'.

EDCS-51400779.summary: This inscription records the workshop of Melutta and names Eutychus, possibly indicating ownership or a craftsman within the workshop.

EDCS-51400851.translation: The rear rank of the century of Pollus Verus.
EDCS-51400851.summary: This inscription identifies a military position within a century, likely belonging to Pollus Verus.

EDCS-51400852.translation: The rear rank of the century of Pollus Verus.
EDCS-51400852.summary: This inscription identifies a military position within a century, likely belonging to Pollus Verus.

EDCS-51400853.translation: The rear rank of the century of Pollus Verus.
EDCS-51400853.summary: This inscription identifies a military position within a century, likely belonging to Pollus Verus.

EDCS-51400854.translation: The rear rank of the century of Pollus Verus.
EDCS-51400854.summary: This inscription identifies a military position within a century, likely belonging to Pollus Verus.

EDCS-51400861.summary: This inscription records that Caratilius made something, possibly indicated by the Roman numeral X.

EDCS-51500034.summary: This inscription identifies a workshop belonging to Gaianus.

EDCS-55702008.translation: The last... of the municipium of the Brigetiones.
EDCS-55702008.summary: This inscription refers to a position or boundary marker within the municipium of the Brigetiones.

EDCS-55702020.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Riesus to the god Silvanus.

EDCS-60600065.summary: This inscription dedicates a sacred object or place to the god Silvanus Domesticus.

EDCS-67400419.translation: To my lord and brother Maximus, greetings. Valerius Ianuarius [sent this].
EDCS-67400419.summary: This inscription is a friendly greeting from Valerius Ianuarius to his superior and brother, Maximus.

EDCS-67600179.summary: This is a two-part name, likely of a person or perhaps a dedication to two muses.

EDCS-67600180.summary: This inscription lists four names, likely of muses, possibly for a dedication or artistic context.

EDCS-67600183.summary: This inscription contains a Hebrew greeting and name, potentially an unusual or syncretic dedication.

EDCS-68600013.translation: and Eucarpus of the colony of Sirmium
EDCS-68600013.summary: This fragment likely identifies Eucarpus as belonging to the Roman colony of Sirmium.

EDCS-72200019.translation: Macedonius, together with his wife Mammete Zevenati, placed this memorial in the basilica of our Lord Ereneius.
EDCS-72200019.summary: This is a funerary memorial placed by Macedonius and his wife in a basilica, likely dating to the Roman or late antique period.

EDCS-72200023.translation: Claudia Flavia Zosimus asks and begs Aeracura, and pleads with her through Jupiter the Father, to give her the names of Titi Alexander Candidus, Mamanus Marcellinus, who was also Attanius Marcianus, and whoever else may oppose them, whether slave or free, and whoever may rise up as a new opponent. We beg Aeracura the Mother, that you would cause them, like the letters given to you, to speak with the same muted silence of the Manes, and that the Manes themselves would be against Beroena, against Zosimus, and against whoever delivers letters to you. We beg you, Manes, tomorrow.
EDCS-72200023.summary: This is a magical curse text, likely from the Roman Imperial period, where Claudia Flavia Zosimus invokes the goddess Aeracura and the Manes to silence her enemies.

EDCS-72200196.translation: Caio Cornelius Claudia Eutyches, of Phrygian origin, aged 50, is buried here. Cornelius Marcianus [placed this].
EDCS-72200196.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Claudia Eutyches, a 50-year-old Phrygian, set up by Cornelius Marcianus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-72300001.translation: For the safety of Marcus Ulpius Marcianus, a veteran of the First Legion Adiutrix Severiana, Marcus Ulpius Eutyches Augustalis of the colony of Aquincensium, his brother, and Ulpius Tigrinus, his son, and his grandsons, fulfilled their vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-72300001.summary: This is a votive inscription from the Roman Imperial period, recording the fulfillment of a vow by the family of Marcus Ulpius Marcianus, a veteran of the I Adiutrix legion stationed in Aquincensium.

EDCS-72900284.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription with missing context, making its purpose and date uncertain.

EDCS-73700045.translation: Claudius Candidianus, a soldier of the first cohort of Mauri, willingly and deservedly paid his vow to Jupiter Best and Greatest.
EDCS-73700045.summary: This is a votive inscription from the Roman Imperial period, dedicated to Jupiter by Claudius Candidianus, a soldier serving in a cohort of Mauri.

EDCS-75300258.translation: Marturius the butler of Emperor Constantius, who lived ninety years, together with his wife Man...
EDCS-75300258.summary: This is a funerary epitaph fragment for Marturius, a butler to Emperor Constantius, likely dating to the late Roman Empire.

EDCS-75600224.translation: ...placed by Ioma, wife of the tribune Lupu.
EDCS-75600224.summary: This is a fragmented funerary epitaph, likely from the Roman Imperial period, recording a dedication by Ioma for her husband Lupu, a tribune.

EDCS-75600226.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Aurelius Frontinus, his most devoted son, placed this for his dearest mother, Aurelia Frontina, who lived 40 years.
EDCS-75600226.summary: This is a funerary epitaph from the Roman Imperial period, erected by Aurelius Frontinus for his mother Aurelia Frontina.

EDCS-75600230.translation: ... placed by Veturius Martianus and Iul...
EDCS-75600230.summary: This is a fragmented inscription, likely funerary, from the Roman Imperial period, initiated by Veturius Martianus.

EDCS-75600231.translation: To the spirits of the departed.
EDCS-75600231.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the Manes, likely from a tomb, with the name of the deceased lost.

EDCS-75600238.translation: He lived as a freedman in peace.
EDCS-75600238.summary: This epitaph records the peaceful life of a freedman, indicating the deceased's status and final state.

EDCS-75600242.translation: In peace, to Stinus, son of P... [restored name].
EDCS-75600242.summary: This inscription, found in peace, is likely a funerary dedication to a man named Stinus, with his parentage partially preserved.

EDCS-75600245.translation: To Flavius Aurelius, son of Arescus, and to Crescentina, who lived for XXIII years and...
EDCS-75600245.summary: This fragmentary inscription dedicates a monument to Flavius Aurelius and Crescentina, with Crescentina's lifespan recorded as 23 years.

EDCS-76200039.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aurelius Vitalis, a soldier of the first Praetorian cohort, lived for X years.
EDCS-76200039.summary: This funerary inscription honors Aurelius Vitalis, a soldier of the first Praetorian cohort, who died at the age of 10.

EDCS-79100140.translation: Flavia Festa erected this for her patron and herself while alive. A veteran of the VIII Raetorum cohort, aged LXX years.
EDCS-79100140.summary: This tombstone was set up by Flavia Festa for her patron and herself, commemorating a veteran of the VIII Raetorum cohort who lived to 70.

EDCS-79600253.translation: Deusa, daughter of Clavius, aged XXXX years. [restored name] son of Lucanus, V.
EDCS-79600253.summary: This inscription records the ages of Deusa, daughter of Clavius (40 years old), and possibly another individual, Lucanus' son.

EDCS-81400017.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aurelia Asclepiodota lived for XIIII years. Aurelius Alexander erected this for his dearest sister. [commissioned by] his father's instruction.
EDCS-81400017.summary: This funerary inscription honors Aurelia Asclepiodota, who died at 14, and was erected by her brother Aurelius Alexander, with a note about the commission.
